title: Halloumi, Peach and Cucumber Spoon Salad Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: This recipe would be greatly improved if it suggested the yield quantities (e.g., 1 cup of diced etc) in addition to the number of whole cucumbers and peaches. Neither ingredient comes in a standard size.
Added arugula, mint and grilled chicken. Subbed pepitas for almonds as that was all I had. Very simple and refreshing.

Tasty, easy, crisp, and attractive early summer salad. I used torn fresh basil and it really went well. The trick is to get the Halloumi browned and crisp. The exact proportions of peach, cucumber, and onion really don't matter; what matters is that they are fresh.
this was a very much needed simple summer-salad and was published just in time for the heatwave that struck europe this past weekend. had it with bread on the side and it was perfect as it is!
I added a little hot honey to the dressing. The tiny bit of heat was perfect.
I make this salad with cubed watermelon or peaches too, but I add toasted fennel seed to the dressing.

Maybe my peaches weren't sweet enough but this was only okay! I added toasted sunflower seeds instead of almonds which worked well but the halloumi is soooo salty it doesn't rlly blend with everything.
@Hayley a salad will only ever be as delicious as the ingredients you add to it
I made this recipe as written, but doubled. It is refreshing and really delicious. Perfect for the hot DC weather we’re experiencing!
This could not be easier to make and is the ultimate crowd pleaser!
this was a delightful salad! tossed in some chopped romaine hearts and served with grilled chicken thighs.

title: Skillet Pork Chops With Buttered Corn Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: The corn, butter, and shallot combo is a winner, even by itself (with a little black pepper).

Excellent recipe. I had boneless chops but I’m sure it would have been even better with bone-in. I thought it needed more vegetables to push it over the top, so I added a zucchini and more butter along with a little cream for saucier sauce.
@Karin, did the cooking time differ? I’d like to try with my boneless ones
@Terrierdem  Recipe says to cook to 130 degrees. Do that.
Made this tonight exactly as written. Easy and delicious is.
Made this as is except adding a thinly sliced jalapeño with the shallots. It was delicious and so easy,
@Mark Jalapeño and Rosemary? Seems an unusual flavor profile, but to each his own…
@Mark -I did the same and added a bit of fresh basil. So good!
@Mel H:  Google jalapeno + rosemary. It's not an "unusual" combo. There's even lemonade with jalapeno and rosemary!
If you pull pork chops at 130 they’ll carry over to about 140.  You’ll have a nicely dried out, overcooked pork chop. Pull at 120 (even 118).  You’ll get a nice medium rare pink, juicy pork chop.
Absolutely delicious! Quick and easy, just as written. I used frozen corn and dried rosemary; could not believe how good this was.
This was stellar. Made as directed (though I was out of rosemary) and we gobbled it up. I’ll include the rosemary on the next go-around but the dish wasn’t lacking without it.

Do I boil the corn first, cut it off the cob, and then put it in the skillet? Or do I cut it off the cob first and then let it cook in the skillet? I read the recipe several times but didn’t see where it answered this question.
Best pork chops ever!  Moist, juicy and flavorful.  Yum.
Absolutely 5 star.   Added a little bit of cream at the end.
Does 1 can of corn equal 2 ears? Yes of course fresh is better. But does 1 can = about 2 ears?

I find blending cows milk yogurt with feta really detracts from the feta's flavor. Make sure you use yogurt made with sheep or goat milk, traditional Greek yogurt, or just us a little sheep's milk instead of the yogurt.
@Jim Johnson Or use plain coconut yogurt.
@Jim Johnson I tried the sheep’s milk yogurt and it was way too thin. Ruined it. Next time I’ll put in a little at a time.
Would blueberries added to this work? For a red, white and blue fruit salad?
@Johnson It would look lovely.

@Jim Johnson wouldn't have thought of that but thank you. I now buy sheep's milk yogurt in addition to my usual Fage Greek. My local market carries bellwether farms, sheeps and goats milk, yogurt, and trader Joe's has goat milk yogurt as well. I like having the two really different flavor profiles at hand and I can see that cheap or goat's milk yogurt would be really Superior with this recipe. it's also thinner and I bet the texture will be perfect. thanks for posting this!
I love watermelon sprinkled w kosher salt and lime or tajin. I may try this w replacing lemon w lime and the crushed red pepper w tajin.
has anyone made the whipped feta ahead of time?
@Trixie I am whipping my goat cheese mixture tonight but leaving it in the food processor sobo can buzz it again tomorrow.
We followed the recipe and put the feta and greek yogurt in a blender. It was delicious but came out soupy. What did we do wrong?
I had this issue too! Not sure what happened. But treated it like a dressing and it tasted good- just seemed to be a different dish!
Could chèvre be substituted for the feta?  I have an excess of goat cheese on hand.
@Mara I bet it would be good!
Super helpful comment.  I hope I can find sheep or goats milk yogurt locally.
I only had non fat yogurt so I add a generous tablespoon of mascarpone. The feta flavor is coming through loud and clear!
My first batch is coming out two liquidy. More like a thick cream than whipped cream. Suggestions?
very delicious but make sure you actually buy SEEDLESS watermelon. Was no fun spitting seeds out while enjoying an otherwise great dish.
By accident I put the lemon juice into the yoghurt mix instead of the watermelon. It was delicious! I also use only mint and lots of it.

title: Greek Salad With Sardines and Beans Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: This was delicious with smoked trout and warmed pita bread. Eating it tomorrow over leftover farro, which will soak up all the juice.
@yush smoked trout. Great idea.
Excellent recipe. Subbed 1/4 cup red onion for shallot and used green olives. Used sardines packed in mustard sauce.
Delicious! I found the dressing a bit heavy so added some chopped romaine. Other than that, no notes!
Don’t discard the olive oil from the sardine tin. That’d be a waste. Use it as the olive oil in the dressing. If there isn’t enough, supplement with EVOO.
This was very good. I’d double up on the sardines…if you’re a sardine lover! Great protein boost for a Greek salad. Officially on the summer rotation—yum!
A high-quality can of yellowfin would be good.
Has potential.
There will be haters…BUT add a bit of brown sugar or molasses and garlic powder or grated fresh garlic to the vinaigrette.

Hmmm. I’ve never seen this adaptation to the traditional Greek country salad, but then I’ve only been in a Greek (from Greece) family for 45 years! And we eat the traditional salad EVERY day in Greece all summer long!
@Donna Macris I don’t think the author is claiming this recipe is authentic - she’s just suggesting a way to adapt a Greek-style salad.

@Donna Macris but this is confusing - the recipe doesn’t say nor does it need to be a traditional Greek salad you grew up with. It’s nice to have variations and try new things and if you don’t want to then don’t, but allow others to enjoy it who haven’t been 40+ years eating the same salad and enjoying it - others may enjoy something different🙏🏽
What does "shake the tomato mixture of any liquid" mean?  Drain the liquid?
@Judi Riva Confusing wording, but it’s referring to shaking the colander holding the ingredients to make sure excess liquid has been drained from it.
Thank you B! I was totally mystified by that as well.
Going to try this with some poached gulf coast shrimp.
Delicious…. Chickpeas work well too (had no white beans). Served sardines on the side. Perfect for the weather and one’s health!
Dumb question but: What does "Shake the tomato mixture of any liquid" mean?

Stu, the tomatoes, cucumbers and salt are in a colander w/ beans, after sitting for the 10-15 mins the salt will draw water out of the tomatoes and cucumbers, so give the colander a little shake over the sink to drain the water out.

Delish. Followed someone’s tuna suggestion. Made it a cross between tonno e fagioli and Greek Salad. Would use less tuna next time. (One tin between 2 of us was too much). Also more feta and olives. But they were green olives. Less salty than Kalamata.
Very good but used anchovies on the side.
Next time, we'll lay this lovely salad on a bed of greens for a nutritional umph, but overall, a delicious mix of colors, textures, and flavors.
I am a single person who lives alone so, of course, leftovers...gave the original a 5.. with garlic toast, hot sauce and parsley, 5++++

Made with gigante beans and tuna, and served some stuffed grape leaves on the side. Great mix of textures and tastes, and pretty filling too. Good recipe that can adapted to what you have in the pantry. Next time I think I'll add some green pepper. Don't forget to serve with warm pita or crusty bread to sop up the extra dressing at the bottom of the bowl.
Any thoughts on subbing anchovies for sardines?
@A much saltier I’d imagine..,
@A Should have specified fresh white anchovies. I’ve heard the canned variety are quite salty.
@A I wondered about anchovies too. White anchovies (boquerones) are mild, delicious, and not overly salty.
I don’t mess with Greek salad. They’re perfectly good as-is.
Gigantes instead of cannellini.
Not a sardine fan. Could I sub in White Anchovies (Boquerones)?
I used white anchovies and they were excellent. Honestly, I think you could make this with just about any tin of fish you found in your pantry and it would be great.
Preparation 15 minutes. Cook time 20 minutes? Just what is being cooked here? Do they mean assembling and getting to the table? If so, why not say so?

This recipe sounds great!  However, I am a person who does not digest cucumbers well.  Does anyone here have ideas for a suitable substitute for them.  Thanks in advance for any advice that you are willing to share!
My mother had the same issue. She bought "regular" cucumbers and peeled them, seeded them if they were large> Problem solved.
@Lori M.  Thanks!  I’ll try it out!
This sounds like a great combination of flavors and textures.  I wouldn't necessarily have thought of it, so:  thanks.
Nutritious, looks fancy, yet easy. Put mine on a bed of baby spinach.

Just curious - who rates recipes?  I had always assumed it was cooks who commented, but this recipe has 5 stars & only one comment, which is actually a question.  I’ve just made this & I think there’s too much dressing.  It’s tasty but a little too salty & gloppy. I like it overall, but think next time I’ll cut way down on the dressing.

@YiaYia any user can leave a rating regardless of whether they also add a comment or not. I leave a rating more for myself (as a reminder that yes, I did cook this one, and I should do it again!) and only leave comments if I have something to add to the conversation that may be useful for others.

@Morgan at the end of each recipe, there’s a spot next to “rating” that’s says “Mark as cooked” that you can check off as well! I always forget that feature is there, but it’s a helpful feature to track what you’ve made if you can remember to tick it off. :)

I liked these! I did make a few changes to the dressing after tasting it - a pressed clove of garlic, a healthy glug of olive oil, and a teaspoon or so of agave nectar. 

Pretty easy summery veggie side. Would make again.

I haven't made this recipe, but I love green beans, and cook them often. I strongly recommend that you start checking the green beans for doneness around 3 to 3 1/2 minutes,and keep checking every 30 seconds. In my experience, except for the rare excessively fibrous types, most green beans get to tender crisp in around 4 minutes, so 5 or 6 minutes could overcook them.
Would this dressing work with Aspargus?
I have the same question about trying on broccoli!
Not a big fan. The miso makes it taste almost sweet. Would lean more toward the mustard and lemon juice.
Trying this tonight, I have all the ingredients in the fridge, and Korean fried chicken for the main! Perfect side!
So we’re serving these cold?
@GeorgeSlater. I served them warm, they were delicious!

I made this according to the recipe and like another comment mentioned that the dressing was too much for the amount of green beans. I thought the dressing was delicious, but I think more green beans added next time.  And maybe some olive oil too.
Made exactly as written, delicious! Especially considering that it’s almost embarrassing how easy it is.
So simple and totally delicious. Will be on rotation in my house.

@karen I tried this on broccoli (blanched) and it worked. I added a glug of honey to the dressing. It really sung when paired with lemon dressed potatoes. I've been enjoying the leftover green beans and broccoli all week.

Per Laura B, I used asparagus.  The miso sauce as written is too much for asparagus. Make half ( or even a fourth) of the miso mix in the recipe.  Use half of that while mixing in asparagus.  Add more miso sauce as needed so as not to overpower the asparagus.  Less is more. Lovely.

Instead of blanching and chilling the green beans I just steamed them in the microwave. They were a bit watery when I mixed them with the whisked dressing which created a perfect texture. I agree with others that you could use the same amount of dressing for a bigger quantity of green beans- it was quite thick and I had a bit left over. When I first tasted the dressing I blinked a few times- it is very high intensity flavor. But then it was absolutely irresistible to keep eating.
Made this using Trader Joe's Dijon Mustard which is relatively spicy and this dish turned out excellent!

title: Seared Zucchini and Halloumi in Red-Wine Vinaigrette Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: What a surprise. I made this as a main dish lunch for two - - mostly because I had all the ingredients on hand and it sounded good. Also, let's face it: the prep is simple. Well, it was surprisingly good. It will be a go-to for this summer, both as a lunch and a side dish. I used mint as the herb addition, which resulted in a bright, summery taste. I did not use all the oil called for in the recipe, and agree with the comment that this amount of dressing was on the heavy side. My solution for that next time will be to use three, not two, zucchini. Enjoy.

This was absolutely delightful with fresh garden herbs. So simple to make but such elevated summer flavor. I did not add the additional oil to the zucchini in the dish, as I felt the seared zucchini and halloumi brought enough oil with them. My other tips would be to take care not to overcook the zucchini. A few of my thinner ones were on the mushier side but they taste much better with some bite to them. Also don’t add too much salt, I forgot how salty halloumi already is! Will absolutely be making again.
Cooked the zucchini and halloumi on the grill and served this over Charlie Bird’s Farro Salad with grilled pita on the side. Great summer meal
Really tasty. I'd cut the amount of dressing in half next time - just a little too much liquid in the finished dish for my taste.

This was really delicious!  No notes.  We had it with some grilled chicken and toasted pita but it could easily go over rice.  I cut the halloumi too small so I didn’t get as much browning but that’s my own mistake.  Will definitely cook this again.

This dish has fantastic flavors but the texture of the crisped halloumi and marinated garlic truly make it! I used Dodoni Authentic which caramelized well in the pan and a little over half the vinegar and oil that was called for. Turned out great. Will make again!
I wish ALL recipes had nutritional info.  It helps those of us watching calories or other things.  When I make it, I will drizzle balsamic glaze.

@zoya Hey! Are you on the app or are you using a browser? I have seen this comment come up before, and I think maybe there is a different user experience between the two. I only use the app, and can always see the nutrition information. Hope that helps!

@soya it’s kind of hidden in very light gray text at the bottom of the recipe when viewed from the app. That’s one of the first things I like to look at if a recipe looks good. I wish it was a bit less hidden.

I prepped the platter where I placed the pan grilled zucchini with a bed of baby arugula. I think this addition really completes the dish. Placed the dressing on top, but did not mix-yet. Grilled shards of Queso de Freir (that’s what was available), placed on top of the zucchini and then I tossed the entire mixture in the platter. Topped with chopped mint and cilantro. Perfecto!

Run, don’t walk, to make this! Delicious, bright, a gorgeous addition to the table, and easy. What more could one ask for? I cut and salted the zucchini ahead of time to reduce some of the moisture and they fried up easily.
This was terrific. I used burrata rather than halloumi, as it needed to be eaten. Delicious. But halloumi is up next. Really a simple way to add great flavor to zucchini.

Fantastic flavor, but the texture of the crispy halloumi and marinated garlic really make this dish! I used Dodoni Authentic which caramelized well in the pan and a little over half the vinegar and oil that was called for. Turned out great. Will make again!

I'm usually not drawn to zucchini, have a prejudice that it will be boring and mushy. This is my new favorite recipe. I forgot to add the olive oil to the platter and that ended up making the dressing perfect for me. I only added parsley because I didn't have anything else on hand and found that it was plenty flavorful just like that.

This dish was as simple and delicious as everyone commented. My husband and I voted it into the regular meal plan.  I did finish the dish off with 2 Tbsp of red wine vinegar and 2 Tbsp olive oil tossed with salt and finely chopped mint from the garden.  Next time I will add more mint and let the dish set for a few minutes to let the flavours co-mingle before serving.   The tastiest bits were the ones left sitting at the bottom of the dish that I ate while cleaning the kitchen.
Will try with feta (uncooked, of course), less salt, and less liquid as recommended).
Delicious!  Definite keeper. I put some different types of lettuces on the bottom, added the zucchini and halloumi on top. Wonderful salad, but could be ramped up with grilled meat.
Could you grill all the components?
Could this work with tofu? (dairy allergy)
@Marlea Please give it a try and report back! I really like this recipe: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1026876-baked-tofu?unlocked_article_code=1.nVA.XUmF.1ILctJHgGnEe&smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share

I have tried to sear zucchini so many times and it does not work. I have a high BTU gas stove, I crank the thing to the max, and still water pours out of the zucchini and it does not brown. I tired salting it like I do with cucumbers and squeezing out the liquis but that was even mushier. What are people doing to make this work?

Treat for the tastebuds!
Followed the recipe. Took me over 30 minutes. Made it with fresh mint. The sweet zucchini marinade with the warm salty halloumi was a treat for the tastebuds!
My family rated this as meh. It wasn't as good as the sum of its parts--we ended up eating the zucchini, and then the halloumi. It was also fairly oily.
Incredibly simple and delicious. After making it as written a couple times, I tried adding farro and chickpeas to turn it into a meal. Made for an excellent lunch!

This was meh… needed some more crunch and something to brigyten the flavor. If Inmake it again, Id add pomegranite or oranges possibly sauteed briefly with some red onion and toasted pistachio hazelnut or sliced almonf as a topping. I also served on a bed of mixed greens. The idea was good but the recipe is seriously missing balance and texture.
Any way to do some of this ahead of time?

This was great! I used the small amounts of herbs I had on hand (chives, dill and parsley) and reduced the dressing by half as other commenters noted. I agree with the commenter that said to keep the zucchini on the thicker side to keep some bite to it.

How can something this easy be this delicious?! Ali Slagle is a magician! I had zucchini from the CSA, lots of herbs from our garden, and conveniently some halloumi in the fridge. Serves 2 as a main, and 4 if other dishes available.
DELICIOUS.
Divine! I used an extra zucchini and a few sliced mushrooms, and grated the garlic. Cold leftovers were also delicious.
Good, but needs something else, like an extra herb or a punch spice…
I should have drained the halloumi before adding it to the hot oil. Water hitting the oil splatters!
Absolutely delicious but cooks beware: if you have a regular sized pan, you'll spend a fair bit of time browning the zucchini in batches. This is a tremendous side, but not a quick one.
Any advice for substituting the vinegar here?  I usually opt for citrus.  Wondering what would pair best overall.  Lemon?
@One Human I used 1 TB of red wine and 1 TB of apple cider vinegar.  Delicious!
I love halloumi but I find it difficult to brown without melting. Any suggestions?

@NNYer oil and heat promotes browning. If that doesn’t work, it could be the package of halloumi you’re using (manufacturing error e.g. I bought vanilla yoghurt that was thick on top and soup on the bottom. I can’t imagine that’s normal)
Saw the recipe today and had all of these ingredients. What a hit!
Added 2 cans of butter beans, amazing!!

Wow! Delicious. Grilled the zucchini and halloumi in thick strips on the grill then chopped and assembled with a big handful of mint. Mint is a must! Didn’t have garlic but honesty didn’t miss it. 10/10!

I just finished licking the bowl…too delicious!  Super easy and quick.  I had some cherry tomatoes that were languishing, so I halved and roasted them in the toaster oven, to toss in at the end.  As others mentioned, be judicious in salting, and I was happy I’d heeded the advice to skip the additional oil when dressing it at the end.  Can’t wait to have this again…and again.
Honestly this took 15 minutes. Was amazing for a light lunch.
This was just ok for me. I think someone mentioned adding a little apple cider vinegar which would have helped with the bitterness for me.

i thought I was getting zucchini adn squash in my farm share, Got broccoli instead. I cooked that with garlic scrapes, onion, olive oil in a Dutch oven - everything else the same - plus farro - and wow!! Delightful.

Wow! So easy and yummy! I used one zucchini and one yellow squash. I made the dressing in a little bowl using minced garlic & two teaspoons each of the red wine vinegar and olive oil. Put the browned veggies in a shallow bowl, seasoned with salt and pepper, and drizzled with some of the dressing. Added the browned halloumi and topped with the remaining dressing. Served as dinner for two with pita bread. No leftovers. A definite do-over!

I liked this recipe immensely. The salty fried halloumi is a revelation and if thin enough not squeaky. As usual, having made it as the recipe, I have tried variations and have subsequently added finely sliced radishes which add a texture and found that sliced (not too thin but so they are still slices and not chunks) and quartered cucumber can do the same.
So yummy! Used 6oz block of garlic bread cheese and skipped the garlic. It’s quite a bit cheaper at my store and really similar. Added mint, lemon thyme, and pineapple sage from the garden.
Absolutely delicious.  Cut the recipe in half because there are only two of us but regretted it!

Mild is a euphemism.  Bland. Zucchini is just bland.  And it’s too bland to be as ubiquitous as it is this time of year.  This recipe is fine. And, I suppose it doesn’t seem too much like one of the countless recipes existing only to find new things to do with the ridiculous bounty of zucchini that marks this time of year.

Pretty meh!  I wanted a grain base and used toasted farro. The base was not the issue. The zucchini halloumi part was very oily and combined with the farro base it was not a stellar combo even after letting it sit and mingle for a while.  Bleah!
Can it be served at room temp or because of the cheese does it need to be served hot?

Tasty but zucchini, no matter how seared will always be mushy. Perhaps patting dry and salting beforehand can result in some crispiness?  Used a combo of parsley and dill and some left over green spring onion.
I had the same issue-it all tasted fine but the zucchini was mushy even though browned.
Delish
I almost never cook anything like this, frying in hot oil, spattering my stovetop, clothing, nearby floor.  But this was absolutely worth it.

Loved this - wow. I never comment on recipes, but this was so wonderful I am compelled. I used ghee instead of olive oil for the cooking of the courgette and cheese, which worked well. I added lemon zest at the end, which gave a nice lift. We served with a rotisserie chicken that we bought (from Harley’s in Hampstead, for anyone in London!). Anyway, it was a lot of bang for the effort here. And a surprising summer meal (while watching the World Cup). I was initially concerned about the amount of dressing - but I added half at the point called for in the recipe), and then half at the end. I think it was the perfect amount. I loved letting some of the cheese bits crisp more than others; the different textures made it even more interesting in the mix. This will become a staple recipe!

After trying with paneer because that's what I had, and then halloumi in another occasion, I have to say it's the halloumi that makes this dish. Without it, it's not nearly as exciting. The zucchini is just zucchini.

I made this exactly as written, aside from soaking and drying the halloumi prior to cooking to remove some salt and help it brow. It was delicious. I could've eaten the whole bowl myself. I didn't, but I sure was tempted!
This was excellent. I paired it with the chicken. mint meatballs with tzaziki from Bon Apetit and my family pilaf recipe. it was a delicious dinner.

title: Chicken Salad Slaw With Peanuts and Nori Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: Not a fan of nori, so I'd leave that out, but I've made salads like this for ages. It's great on pasta also. Use any peanut butter you happen to have. You'll be adding peanuts anyway, so why not use crunchy? I also recommend adding red bell pepper for that little sweet + color.

Tried and found it made enough for good-sized dinner four nights in a row. I switched it up every night: added a bit of extra cabbage the first night, tossed in a bit of Chile crisp the second night. The third night, I added fried shallots. And more cucumbers, some basil and a little extra rice vinegar. Each night was great… and slightly different. Tons of fun!
Your several versions are very inspirational -- THANKS!!!

Didn't use Nori (family member won't eat) and reduced garlic but otherwise made as directed.  Absolutely delicious.  Used squeeze ginger (1 TBSP) and minced most of a large garlic clove.  Had smaller bag of slaw so will use rest of dressing in other ways (happily).  Hoping extra dressing keeps in the refrigerator for around a week - will add warm water to loosen if needed.

This was delicious but missing something nonetheless: sesame oil? (I think.) Easy enough to put together, I followed the recipe precisely. But couldn't taste the nori, it was nice and crunchy, but...salted peanuts, perhaps would have helped.? The sauce just needs something. Guess I said that already.
@Carol S. Fish Sauce - just a dash (or 2)

@Carol S. Yes - definitely needed tweaking.  I doubled nearly everything in the dressing (except for the peanut butter), plus added a couple dashes fish sauce, 2 T maple syrup, a couple tablespoons of lime juice and half a teaspoon of gochuchang paste for heat.

Our family loved this salad. I agree with other reviewers to 1.5 x the dressing. I also agree that the dressing needs a little extra kick - I added juice from half a lime, a rough 2 tsp of fish sauce, 1 tbsp honey, and 1/2 tsp of chile crisp. I also swapped in a tbsp of thinly sliced mint for the nori (it gets soggy once the dressing hits it). It changes the flavor profile to something  more on the Thai side of things, and it was tasty….!

Loved this dish - I would up the sauce a little, 1.5x the recipe, add a little sesame oil and fish sauce, and I doubled the cumbers, used slaw blend, and used salted peanuts (why use unsalted? makes no sense). Absolutely delicious!
This recipe needs cilantro and mint in the salad and a pinch of sugar in the dressing and a squeeze of lime at the end and then it is perfect and would eat it for every meal.
@SR agree with this - remembering for next time I make it
OK, good recipe but needs a few tweaks. Not enough sauce-could easily double. Add 1-2 tablespoons of maple syrup.

I agree that a few somethings were missing.  I doubled the sauce and the salad was still very dry (yes, I measured all the ingredients and followed the directions).  There was so much leftover that I made another double batch of the sauce to deal with it.  Probably won't make again.  Disappointed.

A note for those looking for a peanut butter substitute - as someone with a lifelong life-threatening peanut allergy married to someone who loves peanut butter, sunbutter has been our house alternative! It “tastes just like peanut butter” and nobody dies - win win!

I used regular soy sauce, as that's what I had on hand. I started out using 1.5 TBS, but the sauce was too bland, so added in the remaining .5 TBS. Used 2x the garlic/ginger. Added thin sliced red bell pepper. Excellent!

Great recipe. Couple tips after making it twice:

-Double sauce quantity
-Add 1/2 tsp toasted sesame oil to sauce
-make the sauce in a nutribullet if you have one (just add the water before you blend)

Great cold/room temp salad that's still hearty. Had it with cold quinoa, which didn't take over any of the flavor profile or texture of the salad. Suggest making extra sauce in order to adjust before serving, especially if prepping in advance or storing overnight (salad is drier the next day). I increased the ginger and vinegar to get the right balance in the sauce, as well as chili crisp for a bit of bite. I wondered if making the sauce in advance would help it the flavors come out more.

I made this tonight without the nori (sorry). Very good, similar to Martha Rose Schulman's Indonesian-Style Chicken Salad, also on this site. For this one, I felt like it needed a small hint of something sweet to balance the vinegar. Maybe a a bit of brown sugar or something. I served fresh mango on the side, which also helped.
Great base for a salad slaw but in addition to sesame oil & a splash of Fish Sauce this definitely needs sliced Green onions!
@Maya scallions are in the recipe.  scallions= green onions

so so good.  tetris meal for me. had everything here.  love the way the warm poached chicken wilted the napa further.  used peanut butter powder and the sauce is drinkable!   thank you, ali, for creating and sharing with us.

Great recipe.. Make a little more sauce than is called for.  Yes, it keeps well in the fridge. If you poach the chicken, massage some sauce into the warm slices before tossing with the salad... Oh, strain and save that poaching liquid! Make rice or soup with it.
I loved this recipe! Would have grated the garlic and ginger into the sauce instead of chopping so it blended better but otherwise great.
@Mariel this is such a good and helpful tip!
@Mariel GREAT idea!

Had high hopes for this, but it came out so bland. The dressing on its own is fab (definitely making again to use in other recipes!) I think this is a situation where the core ingredients of chicken, cabbage, and cucumber have so little of their own flavor that they suck up the dressing's, in a bad way. I even stirred in a few spoonfuls of chili crisp at the end, which helped, but not enough for this to be a repeat. That said, highly recommend adding fish sauce + lime juice to the dressing!

I have to agree. The dressing tasted fabulous before it was added to the salad... but then, once mixed in, all the flavor seemed to go right down the flavor drain. (The only component that really came through... and in not a pleasing way... was the raw garlic.) I did serve with lime wedges, which helped somewhat. but I wondered if more of the dressing (like maybe double the recipe), possibly with the modifications you suggest, would help?

Took some shortcuts and it was still delicious - bought packages of Applegate Naturals Shredded Chicken Breasts instead of shredding chicken myself, used 1 T of ginger paste instead of fresh ginger, and used a bag of slaw blend. No mini cucumbers available so used an English cucumber.
Nice to also sprinkle with toasted sesame seeds.
Plus, serve with a wedge of lime.
Great summer salad and great for meal prepping/making lunch ahead of time. Added fresh herbs like basil for added flavor.
I also thought sauce was missing something.  Sesame oil?  More soy sauce?  Might try adding to leftovers.  Couldn’t taste the nori, but it looked pretty.
It was good and I will definitely make it again. We didn't care for the nori and will leave that out next time. It is a good base recipe.
I made it exactly as instructed. Very good!

Really good and very simple. Squeezed a little lime over the top. Next time I think I’ll add some red bell pepper and Fresno chile, and maybe use furikake seasoning instead of the Nori flakes. Will definitely make again!

This is very good, though it was not good the next day, so plan to finish it the day it's made! Though I like Nori, I don't think its addition added much here. Instead, I would add a splash of lime juice and chopped cilantro and/or mint before serving. I used 1.5 lbs. of chicken thighs marinated in equal parts olive oil, vinegar, soy sauce plus garlic and baked and sliced thin.

Great recipe. When I tasted the dressing it was overpowered by vinegar (could have been my TJ’s rice vinegar) so I added a few drops of fish sauce as recommended here. That did the trick. Next time I may add red pepper flakes or a little sriracha. Delicious salad, easily came together with a bag of Wegmans precut Asian slaw.

Used napa cabbage and sliced it super thin in my food processor, and it kinda turned to mush. But I am dedicated to this recipe and will try again, either with non-Napa cabbage or not in the processor.

Subbed tahini for the peanut butter b/c that’s what I had in the pantry. Tasted great and I think matched the flavor profile of the recipe. Increased salt all around by using Korean gim, salted peanuts, and full salt soy sauce and increasing amount to 2 T. At the cabbage salting step, added a sprinkle of sugar. Served with chicken on top versus mixed in the slaw.
Nori gave it a nice umami flavor reminiscent of sushi. Best suggestions here were to add chopped red pepper and mint. I also added avocado. Low salt peanuts are better imho.
I liked the texture and flavor of this salad.  I used the poached chicken breast.  I also doubled the tamari (I used tamari rather than low-salt soy sauce) and rice wine in the dressing.

I served this at a casual dinner to my family and friends. It wasn’t well liked. When I asked for suggestions and thoughts they said it was bland, it didn’t have much flavor, needed more sauce, and  they couldn’t taste the nori. I’ve been trying to eat the leftovers and I’ve added more rice vinegar to give it more pep but I agree- we won’t be making this again.
I agree the sauce is missing some umami (and salt) so a dash of fish sauce, splash of sriracha and cilantro did the trick.

Easy and very good recipe especially with the nori - the more nori the better.  However, we felt it needed a bit more oomph and the next time I will add radishes for that little extra bite.  And I actually used "bought" cabbage slaw and less than called for in the recipe since I could only find a 10oz package and the dressing was a little too sparse.  Will go 1-1/2 times for the dressing next time.
A very healthy low glycemic salad especially for diabetics but it needs a lift as most reviews state. Next time I will double the sauce and perhaps add diced preserved lemon.
Hi,  We don't do well w peanuts or even almonds...  what  would yu sub?  I am wondering about Miso.  many thanks!
Tahini?
For a vegan option, subbing shredded tofu is absolutely enough

We didn't think the nori really went with the other flavors so we left that out and added fresh basil. We also added a little lime juice, sambal and fish sauce to the dressing.  We liked it on it's own, but also with glass noodles and as sandwich filling.
Double the dressing and cucumbers. Add lime?
Anyone tried it with tofu or a vegetarian version?

Yes, you can make this recipe with relatively firm tofu. First try to squeeze out the water by pressing the tofu gently between two plates + by blotting out water with plenty of paper towels (or a clean dish towel). Then, cut the tofu into cubes (no more than 3/4") and mix + coat generously with some of the dressing before adding to the salad. We also shred carrot into the slaw + top with B+W toasted sesames. If the tofu seems likely to crumble, just pile it on top, instead of stirring it in.
I was thinking tofu as well. Cubed and fan oven or airfry in similar seasoning.

I followed readers' suggestions by adding sesame oil and fish sauce to punch up the dressing, plus thinly sliced red peppers for color. Didn't have nori, so omitted it. Salad was delicious and a big hit at a potluck, but it took a loooong time to assemble (shredding cabbage, shredding chicken, grating ginger, chopping peanuts, etc.)
Followed various suggestions and added cilantro and mint, a dash of sesame oil, and finished off with a squeeze of lime. Yum.

A word about the raw GARLIC: 

If you don't mind totally raw garlic that will linger in your mouth long after the meal, then execute that part of the recipe as written.  

But we don't care for that experience...so, in the future, we will do what we typically do with raw chopped/pressed garlic in a dressing (or similar): Before mixing it in, we let it sit in a small bowl or cup for at least 10 minutes in enough vinegar to cover, mixed well with a good grind of salt, to "cure" it.
If you're not a fan of peanuts - - - -  has anyone made this with almond butter as a substitute, and if so, how did it come out?
just wondering if you tried the recipe with almond butter already. my son is allergic to peanuts
Are sheets of nori that one would use to roll sushi the same as nori snacks? if not, what are nori snacks?

@Susan B Yes, the same. I believe the suggestion of Nori snacks is because of the volume or smaller amount that comes in the snack pouch. Nori snacks are dried seaweed in small pouches. They can be flavored as sea salt, wasabi and such. My granddaughter loves them, we include them in her lunch daily. But, yes they are the same as nori sheets.
Does this hold up in the fridge for a few days or must it be eaten that day for best results?

As per the recipe, you can prep the slaw up to 1 day ahead. I can’t see any reason why the dressing couldn’t be made and kept for at least a few days (refrigerated) - though you may need to add a splash of water once it’s at room temperature to loosen it up again. I would wait to add the remaining ingredients (and dress the salad) until day of eating, to avoid it becoming too soggy.
Would this be ok with almond butter instead of peanut butter?
What are nori snacks, and where do I get them?
I was so excited to see this recipe until I saw the ingredient list - I hate peanut butter! (I know how odd that is.) Any ideas on a good substitute? Maybe just another "Asian" style salad dressing?
This one didn’t work for me. The sauce lacked zing, and the use of rotisserie chicken left the salad mushy.
Sorry. Worst thing I ever made. Had to throw the whole thing out. Love your recipes and won’t  let this shade my opinion of NYT cooking/recipes.
?Have you made this with salmon??

The chicken turned extra- mushy, not sure why. I used a rotisserie chicken, followed the recipe, and it was....ok. The consistency of the meat was the biggest issue. I wonder whether the liquid ingredients had anything to do with it.

Watch out for salt. Skip salting the cabbage, use unsalted peanuts and peanut butter. Use more ginger than recommended. Add lime juice and cilantro. Add something red (bell pepper) for looks. i didn't have nori on hand and didn't miss it.
Agree with other reviewers - a bit more sauce, maybe will try lime juice, fish sauce next time.  Big picture - a yummy recipe that can always be tweaked for your diners.

I was hoping for a fresh tasting, light summer dish. I found this to be very heavy and bland tasting. There was no zing to it and the nori made the entire dish taste fishy. I think this is the first recipe from NYT that I haven't liked.

This recipe was disappointing.  I do love how fast it came together and no oven, especially on a hot day.  Strange how I love all the ingredients, but they didn’t work well together for me.  As others have commented, I tried to kick up the flavor.  I added maple syrup, pochang sauce, lime juice; I also used apple cider vinegar instead of the rice vinegar.  But it still fell flat.  Definitely needed salt.  This recipe may also work with grilled shrimp or salmon instead of the chicken.
Allergic to peanuts any recommendations for substitutions?
So good! Serve it on toast with apple slices
Did anyone use almond butter and almonds?
Increase dressing by 50%, but keep PB around 1/4 cup. Roasted thin cut chicken breasts and cup up when making salad. Good meal prep and leftovers recipe

Followed all the suggestions and still found it was missing something. I have some leftover so I’ll try with  making more sauce ., more garlic, fish sauce, lime juice and maple syrup. Maybe better, maybe worse.

Nori is definitely an acquired taste (not mine!). I made 1.5x the recipe and glad I did. I also added an extra clove of garlic, a little more ginger than suggested, 1 Tbsp. maple syrup (I'm Canadian, eh), 1/2 orange and the juice of one lime. Oh, and salted peanuts and a bit of chili pepper. Will never eat Waldorf Salad again.

Tried and found it made enough for good-sized dinner four nights in a row. I switched it up every night: added a bit of extra cabbage the first night, tossed in a bit of Chile crisp the second night. The third night, I added fried shallots. And more cucumbers, some basil and a little extra rice vinegar. Each night was great… and slightly different. Tons of fun!
Great combination of flavors and textures. (I made it with packaged broccoli slaw. I agree with the previous commenters who said there needs to be more dressing and it needs to be more ... assertive.

I just took a star off (leaving 4) because the recipe says you can mix chicken, cabbage slaw, and cucumbers 1 day before serving. I found this NOT to be true.

 I live alone, so I made a half recipe, ate half of it, and refrigerated the above-described 3-ingredient mix for the next day, when I added the garnishes. Well, like the spearmint on the bedpost, it loses its flavor overnight. It was so blah that it lessened my enthusiasm for the recipe.

Moral: Eat it fresh.
Would this work with shrimp or salmon?
Delicious. I added a bit of sesame oil and still found it a bit dry, so I’ll increase dressing and cucumbers. Chili crisp is a great idea too!
How did the chicken get cooked without cooking?
Made without chicken and chopped the cucumbers made for a great slaw to top fish tacos.
The amount of dressing called for in the recipe is fine. I made it with extra dressing (1.5) and it was too much.
This dish just tasted very "meh" to me. It's any chicken salad with a twist, and in this case I thought the twist was OK but not especially impressive. I'll try it again with some minor tweaks.
Thank you for recipes that use a full box of pasta,
Loved this meal. Used impossible meat, which worked well. Makes a lot of food!

I made this for dinner this evening and thought it was delicious. I used fresh peas, not frozen, added some dill because I didn’t have basil, did not add the butter, and forgot the Parmigiana. Still, I don’t think it could have been better. I have enough asparagus and peas to make it again for tomorrow’s dinner—it was that good!

I made the recipe again for dinner this evening. I found morels in the market this morning, which I immediately bought. I added the morels after the sausage and onions were fried, along with the asparagus and peas. I remembered the cheese this evening, too—and the result was mouth-watering. It is a perfect recipe for spring.
@Margie Gibsonhow can you possibly say it could be better when you didn’t add three items in the recipe . It probably would have been better if you added those items.

@Nori I think you misread @Margie Gibson's original post.  She said "I DON'T THINK. it could have been better."  She wasn't disparaging the recipe -- she said it was as good as it could be.  You and she are on the same side!

Good, but not great. Leveling up options for next go round, wound maybe using a little white wine to deglaze the sausage and to double the lemon juice at the end. Added some red pepper flakes which added a needed kick. Will definitely attempt again.
I made this exactly according to the recipe and it was fabulous!! It will remain in my rotation.

Very solid but I found it lacked the flavour I was expecting. Might play around with maybe some capers or anchovies and some more citrus. Feels like it should work!
I also added chili's for some extra spice that helped also.
@Reg S.I added a cup of homemade pesto sauce and it really brought this recipe together.
PLEASE give us some weights for the asparagus. The asparagus in my garden does not arrive in bunches.

PLEASE use your best judgment for the amount of asparagus. Too much or too little will not affect the outcome of this recipe. If you like asparagus (why else would you grow it?), grab a big bunch from your garden. If you don't care for asparagus, grab less. The recipe calls for 1-1/2 cups of peas. The 1 inch pieces of asparagus could be about the same -- or more or less -- depending on your love for asparagus.
@Corlan Johnson Where I live, asparagus is generally sold in 1 pound bunches. Lucky you, having the fresh stuff!
A bunch of asparagus is typically 1 pound.
Made this for Mother’s Day. 10/10. Used about 2 cups pasta water. 1 was not enough.
Yummy delicious. I used half the called for pasta (8oz) and thought it was the perfect ratio. Also subbed the vegan sausage - added a bit more oil while cooking.

The joy of NYT Cooking website is those little bars that say recently viewed or similar recipes. That's what happened here. I planned to make this as written, then I saw Melissa Clarks recipe. Similar, but mint was a NO. Loved the idea of a creamy cheese sauce. Ultimately meat (sausage) wins the day. I combined the two recipes and it was a perfect marriage. Yes, I'm one of those people who complain about other people altering a recipe. Hmm. I guess that's why there's a comment section.

Perfect spring greens pasta salad! 
Seasoned Clonakilty traditional Irish sausages with sage, black pepper and thyme. 
Would increase basil and reduce the amount of pasta next time.
Excellent,but honestly couldn’t taste the basil. I think you would be 100% fine without it. Also only used half the amount of pasta, sausage, olive oil and butter and it was absolutely delicious.
Delicious and fresh tasting. Feels healthy and indulgent at the same time. Perfect spring recipe.
Please give a weight for the asparagus-our ‘bunches’ may not be the same size. Thank you
Use as much -- or as little -- asparagus as suits you.
It's a keeper.  No asparagus at the market, so I used sugar snap peas and mushrooms.  Just the right amount of garlic and lemon.

Agree with Bee, deglazing with white wine or stock would be great before adding the veggies. 
Made this with sliced snow peas and they were a great swap for regular peas. Added 4 oz of mascarpone with the pasta water and it helped everything come together. Also recommend serving with crushed chili flakes.

Decent but not stellar recipe on its own. If following the recipe exactly, it will taste a bit bland. Need to use the zest and juice of a very large lemon, and add additional spices like black pepper and more salt at the end. I also added half a teaspoon of MSG, which helped significantly.
This was so easy and perfect for spring produce. Used parsley instead of basil and spring onions instead of shallots and it turned out great.

Made this pretty much as written and it was a huge hit in our house. Spicy Italian sausage added kick without any additional pepper flakes. I look forward to making variations throughout growing season. We’ll be mixing up the sausage options, too.
to A and Corlan: I've noticed that asparagus bunches--at least where I shop--weigh a pound, or very close to it. I think you could assume that this recipe calls for a pound of asparagus.
Perfect dish to add garlic scapes.
Took advice of a few capers and used a little white wine to deglaze. Everyone loved it!
Didn't have basil; so I subbed in arugula.  Didn't have a fresh lemon either so I subbed lemon pepper - a goodly amount.  Delish!
I made this with the lemon pasta from Trader Joe’s. It was wonderful! I did add a little extra red pepper.
Just made this almost exactly as written. Used zest of an entire lemon and was liberal with the salt but not too much if topping with Parmesan. Everyone liked it!

I’m a very lousy cook. This recipe is fail proof and profoundly delicious.  Leftovers are just as tasty the next day.  

Be a bit careful with the timing of the pea and asparagus additions. It’s easy to overcook them losing their crunchiness and their bright green-ness.

You are getting better presenting fresh and accessible ingredients and simplicity in your recipes. Most importantly, those couples with young families who Door Dash regularly have a reachable  and cheap alternative. Easy healthy recipes for all. Please keep up your good work.
This was fairly bland, which surprised me given the list of ingredients. I think it would be better using half the amount of the pasta, but keeping the other ingredients the same.
Mine was not bland at all, and I used half the amount of pasta.
Super excited about all the new recipes being posted recently but please provide nutritional info as well for us! Love NYT Cooking!
Can I make this in a regular pan since I don’t have a Dutch oven?

Great Spring pasta recipe. Sautéed some mushrooms first, then put to the side. Followed the recipe from there and put the mushrooms in after the pasta was stirred back in. Skipped the basil and mixed in some grated parmesan. We loved it!
Made it tonight. Used a half pound of chicken Italian sausage for its lower fat content and so as not to overpower the vegetables. Turned out great. Will make it again.

Delicious! Though I did think the dish needed a creamy element. I used sugar snaps from the garden--fun to eat food that I grew myself. I chose the hot Italian sausage but also added a jalapeno with the veins and seeds removed, making it spicy but not too hot. I'll make this again.

I love this recipe. And yet, like some other cooks hopefully have done, after following this original recipe exactly as it is written, I tweaked it by adding mushrooms and cherry tomatoes. Both iterations, the original as well as the tweaked variety, are so satisfying and unctuously delicious that we make it twice a month.
Fast, easy, and tasty. Added cherry tomatoes because they were there. Deglazed the pan with white wine. Otherwise followed the recipe. Delightful!

Perfect dish for early spring. I forgot the basil, so used baby spinach with a pinch of oregano instead. I’ll try it with basil next time, but this was still very good. I appreciate that the recipe calls for using the peas frozen, so they’re not mushy.
Yum! So good! I added two cups of frozen sliced shiitakes with the green veggies and that was a great addition. Love Ali Slagle’s easy, family friendly weeknight recipes like this.

AMAZING! Finally a veggie-filled pasta that also contains meat and isn’t overly heavy. Going in my regular rotation for sure. 

Also, life is short. Use the whole lemon ;)
We found it bland. Perhaps because had to use gluten-free pasta.

We liked this more and more as we ate it and appreciated the pops of sweetness from the peas, the silkiness of the butter, and the accents of the basil.  I used a full box of Barilla Protein Plus farfalle, and it was fine.

The main flaw in the recipe is excessive pasta.  The flavor of the other ingredients is unnecessarily diluted.  Next time we’ll cut the pasta to 1/2 pound. We made the recipe as directed but substituted 1.5 cups of chopped leeks for the peas and added some hot pepper flakes.
Excessive pasta? It's a pasta dish. Go to Rome someday and order a pasta dish. Ask the waiter to "hold" half the pasta. Let us know the response!
The whole didn’t transcend the sum of its parts.
So much better reheated.  The pasta retains the sauce which is rich and creamy!

I thought I had the ingredients on hand for this recipe until I started cooking and I am not very good at improvising on recipes, yet, necessity is the mother of invention! Had almost zero frozen peas so searched for veggies.  Two very small tomatoes, dried tomatoes (small handful), handful of shredded cabbage, phew! I added pasta water earlier to hydrate the dried tomatoes. Added Aleppo pepper flakes. It was delicious!

I had lemon basil pork sausages in the freezer when I saw this recipe and thought they would go with it perfectly…and they did! We did not have asparagus, but substituted green beans in their place and it turned out wonderfully. The only thing I might do differently is to cut back on the amount of pasta used.

I made this tonight and followed the recipe with one exception: I used the rind of an enter large lemon. It is perfect for a rainy, dull spring day. The ample vegetables lighten it. Fusilli, not rotini, is essential. So good that everyone wanted seconds. Next time, I’ll drain the sausage fat off after crisping.
Didn’t drain it, but probably should have.

I made in the amounts suggested, but did sub one cup white wine for a cup ogthe pasta water, let it cook off and then added the cup of pasta water. Don’t skip the basil. It’s great with the lemon and is full of antioxidants. A little red pepper and a bit more salt at the end. This is a nice bright change from a favorite, more wintry orechiette, sausage and broccoli dish I make. Thanks, Ali Slagle
What can  I use instead of asparagus ?

@Sheila C 
Maybe a different recipe?
Broccoli?
@Sheila C I would try broccolini.

Recipe would work great if all asparagus were the same size. For larger ones you need with withhold the peas, since the asparagus may need twice as long to reach the point described. I learned the hard way, not the end of the world, just surprised I’m paying for a recipe that didn’t bother to offer a wide range to match our varying agricultural products.
Anyone know how much a bunch of asparagus typically weighs? I see big variations where I live.
Use whatever quantity you think you and your family will eat, allowing for leftovers (or not). When a recipe isn't precise, you can use your best judgement.
Attractive, simple recipe. I just need to grab some basil then I can give it a go.
I cook the pasta last!!
Delicious! I halved the amount of pasta per another reader and I needed just 1 C of pasta water . Family asked for second helpings.

Shallots helped.  Used True Lemon powder instead of expensive lemons - that citrus kick matters - or it can be a bit bland.  Also added some dark soy sauce, kitchen bouquet. Not bad; not the best on NYT culinary.  Basic.

This was very bland, which surprised me, given the list of ingredients. I had to add quite a bit of salt, more basil and even some pesto. I think there is far too much pasta - it should probably be cut in half, but keep the amounts of the other ingredients.
this was easy, quick and delicious.  Made it with chicken sausage because that is what I had.
Wow, this is good! Don't be afraid to be heavy handed with the amount of peas or lemon zest/juice used. Came together fast, makes a ton, and the amount of veggies is great!

Has anyone used chickpea noodles (like Banza, or the like)?

Giving this recipe a try later this week.

So simple, yet tasty - hearty and fresh at the same time.  It does make a lot of food. I used all the reserved pasta water, KEEP IT SAUCY & GLOSSY! Beyond brand veggie sausage worked really well in this
Great recipe! Threw the lid on the dutch oven for about 2 mins to move the asparagus along, cooked as written and my 5 year old told me this was "a great dinner, mama." Hooray!
This was incredibly bland. Maybe b/c I used hot chicken sausage instead of pork.

This recipe calls for a whole pound of pasta to make 4 to 6 servings. All my pasta containers say that a pound serves 8. We made the full recipe for two diners so we would have leftovers, and boy, did we have leftovers! Opted for  sweet sausage and otherwise stuck exactly to the recipe. We found it bland and unfortunately it did not reheat well. So much for leftovers - the asparagus flavor dominated the mild taste of the dish, especially after reheating. Oh, well, a rare meh dish from NYT Cooking.
My husband is allergic to green peas and green beans. What would be a reasonable substitute for the green peas?
I've subbed fresh corn for the peas and it worked well. You get the sweetness that peas would add with a little more texture.
Most past recipes don't, but I think this one could work with whole wheat pasta--a longer noodle though because a mouthful of whole-wheat fusilli might make me gag.
Try Delallo whole wheat fusilli.  Much better than other brands.
I'm eating this now with whole wheat pasta (reginetti from Sfoglini) and it's delish.
I only used one clove of garlic, a bit less pasta (8.8 ounce package), and no shallots. It tasted great!

If you are going to add fresh morels to this dish, please make sure they are cooked for at least 10 minutes. They are toxic when not cooked well and can send people to the hospital. Add to the sausages when browned for a couple of minutes to release their liquid. When liquid is gone, continue recipe with shallots and garlic.
Nice taste, a little bland. Might be zippier with spicy sausage.

I generally followed and my mods were adding cherry tomatoes (semi blistered) and chopped broccolini. Added peas a bit later so they didn’t overcook. 
This type of recipe is perfect for using veggies on hand. I also used 2 lemons and added ~1/4 cup Italian parsley.

Made this tonight and just loved it. I used orecchiette, as that was the pasta I had on hand. Also, as suggested, I used half a pound of pasta instead of the whole box. We will still have loads of leftovers for the two of us. Otherwise, I followed the recipe as written. It took a bit longer than 40 minutes, but preparation was still less than an hour with minimal cutting of vegetables - a plus! Highly recommend this lovely spring-ey pasta dinner.
It's good! Easy too. Didn't take the casings of the sausage, just cut it in smaller pieces.

We have an asparagus garden and I am always in need of recipes where a lot of asparagus can be used.  This recipe exceeded expectations, but for our preference, half a pound of fusilli was used. We bought the Rao pasta, from Italy with two ingredients only, and it is quite heavy. Hubby bought ordinary store brand hot sausages; I added two shakes of anise seeds and one shake of hot pepper flakes while it cooked. Other than that I followed the recipe exactly and this will be in our meal rotation.

Delicious and easy! I had made homemade pesto earlier today (thanks to my prolific sweet basil plant), so  then followed the recipe with shallots, garlic, and three links of Aldi’s organic Italian chicken sausage. 

I made this with penne pasta, saved the pasta water and added the knob of unsalted butter as directed, and then added about half a cup of wine wine. I swirled in several generous tablespoons of pesto and topped with red pepper flakes, oregano, and Parmesan cheese.

It was  divine and will definitely make again!

WHEN USING gluten-free pasta, it seems not "firm enough" after a while, even if cooked al dente, and looses it's shape and taste especially, when I add the cooked pasta back in to vegetables & sausage. Any suggestions?
Two thumbs up, even though I used chopped onions and dried basil instead of what was in the recipe. It is delicious.
Two thumbs up, very delicious, although I made it with chopped onions instead of shallots, dried basil, and bottled lemon juice instead of the recipe ingredients.
Great easy meal.  Since I had zucchini, I substituted that for the asparagus.  I also bought garlic-basil sausage instead of Italian.
Oh. This was a celebration on the tongue. I used about twice the garlic, but followed the rest of recipe as written. OMG.  Amazing.
I wish I could taste more of the flavors in this dish. I don’t taste the basil or the shallots — just a lot of sausage. Wondering how to deepens all the flavors — just add more?
we used beyond hot italian sausage for this and love it.

Didn't have any lemon, but I added a couple of spoonfuls of pesto at the end.  Delish!  Oh and I halved the recipe since there were just two of us (we measured out 3 servings of pasta instead of using half the box).  There's a serving left for lunch tomorrow - yay!
Hal, try Impossible's spicy sausage in the red tube...makes it even better!

Ditto--the salmon was overcooked yet raw after 8 minutes so I dropped it to 350 and moved it lower in the oven, and it was good after another 5-7 minutes. Next time I would season it. I made black lentils with some broth and leeks and used that instead of canned. Used just a couple tablespoons oil from the anchovy tin, and some extra anchovies. So, made some changes but the overall effect--crisped salmon, lentils, herby umami lemony sauce--was good!
Why not just sear the salmon, skin side down in a cast iron pan? The toaster oven will retain a fish smell.

This dish was superb but I made some changes. I used black beluga lentils instead of canned. I also pan seared the salmon. You want the oil hot enough to shimmer, then place the salmon skin-side down and immediately lower to medium heat. Press each fillet down for 10 seconds so the skin stays flat and crisps evenly, then cook about 7 minutes until nearly done. Flip for 90 seconds on the flesh side, then flip back skin-side down and give it one final minute over medium-high heat for extra crispy.

My salmon took much longer than 6-10 minutes.  I had to move it lower in the oven and adjust the broiler temperature a bit lower as well.  In the end the skin was a bit charred and crispy.  I served it skin side down, and just put the lentils on top of the meat.  It was truly delicious, it will be a keeper in my house.
I don’t use canned lentils. Are the lentils lentils de puy or are these lentils as used in dah?
It would be good with any sort of cooked lentil.

Made this tonight with plain, old green lentils from TJ's that I cooked myself. It only takes 20 minutes! I added a small carrot to the pot about halfway through, and then sliced it into the lentils once they were drained. 
 
You don't need a full 1/2 C of o. oil, but use a good one. Broiled the salmon until the skin was (a little too) crispy, then moved it to a lower rack and reduced the oven to 400. Total time about 13 mins. This was quite delicious, I'll make it again.
Instrad of anchovies can yiu use capers?

I used bigger fillets and it took longer but was delicious and very simple to make. I lowered the fish as recommended and it came out perfectly.  I roasted cherry tomatoes and served the fish over the tomatoes to increase acidity and sweetness. This will go into regular meal  rotation.

Four stars, as modified.  I really like lentils, but salmon is not my preferred fish.  I made this with arctic char, sautéed, an excellent choice for me.  I thought the lentil salsa verde was lacking.  I prepared lentils du puy and then sauced the legumes as directed.  I must have had a very juicy lemon as there was too much sauce for me.  More importantly, there wasn’t enough flavor.  I wound up adding some Dijon mustard to the lentils.  If I made this again, I’d double the amount of crushed red peppers and anchovies.

Such a wonderful and easy week night recipe! Followed the recipe as directed, however did season the fish with a little Dijon and salt and pepper before broiling. I think the cook time was a few more minutes in my oven. I served this with some sautéed Swiss chard with pistachios and dried cherries and it was the perfect meal.
Is there a substitute for anchovies?
Canned lentils, is that a thing? I don't think I have ever seen them.
Why not just grill the salmon.  So much easier.
What's hard about broiling fish? Not everybody has access to outdoor space and a grill.

@Miss the Redbird
Grilling with smoke adds another dimension to salmon especially if fresh and oily
@Maryanne De puy is my first thought, but if you do make it with whole urad dal/black lentils, I'd love to hear how it turned out!
Very quick and tasty recipe and good for both fish eaters and vegetarians in the family. I doubled the lentil salsa verde and subbed Worcestershire sauce for the anchovy.

This is my second note.  I made the Lentil salsa verde with modifications.  I doubled the crushed red pepper and added 0.5-1.0t of dried tarragon which goes well with lentils.  An improvement, but I may still add some poupon mustard.  Enjoy.
@drgonzo777. This note refers to me making a one-quarter batch.  That ‘sizes’ the tarragon amount.  :)
Delish - made black lentils instead of canned, cooked them in chicken broth, seasoned the salmon and seared in a cast iron and finished in the oven. Serve with plenty of lemon!

Love this idea! I adore sardines and find avocado toast bland and unappealing. The garlic rub in combination with the dressing is perfect, plus no more wasting of the packing olive oil. Forget the naysayers, it’s a delicious combination!

To me this was gilding the lily. Sardines — I used mild ones — are so distinctive that they overpowered and negated the avocado. The recipe was easy, and it tasted good, but the avocado was a superfluous waste.
I love this combination and I will be experimenting to get the perfect luscious combination for me. Capers and red onions? I wish I could find the nutritional information and calories per serving!

@Michael Feno
Just above the preparation section there is a nutrition link.

An easy, delicious meal for a warm night when you don't want to cook.  I used smoked mackerel since that's what I had and added capers, halved cherry tomatoes, and slivers of red onion for a sweet/salty supper.

Serving this on toasted artisanal whole wheat bread makes for a battle with knife and fork on the plate. Try going with a bruschetta approach: cut from a narrow long loaf to make several portions per person, small enough to pick up and bite into.
Drizzle hot chili oil on top.  It gives it a bit of umami flavor.
Wow, this sounds amazing and healthy. I can't wait to try. I do love sardines but never considered this method.
There lots of kinds of sardines packed in olive oil. What does this recipe recommend?
@Mitchel  I recommend Brunswick sardines in soybean oil. Tasty and not too fishy. I buy them on Amazon.

Honestly, at 5 am today I was thinking about how to use a ripe avocado and sourdough bread. A simple avocado toast? No maybe add tinned sardines I thought. Then, this recipe popped up. Thank you. Sounds wonderful!!!
This is perfect. Perhaps a squire of lemon to brighten it up and contrast with the avocado?
This makes an easy and delicious lunch. Since I’m only making for me, I used an entire mini avocado for one piece of SD toast. I also added the garlic to dressing. The sherry vinegar is key!

I squeezed a garlic clove and spread it on the toast. It definitely needs the garlic to give the dish a little punch. This is a quick breakfast or lunch and both the sardines and the avocado have good nutritional value. It's definitely on my breakfast/lunch rotation.
I love sardines. My family rolls their eyes. Mashed with a splash of apple vinegar and crackers is my favorite.
a surprisingly perfect dish.
I like raw garlic in moderate amounts, so I ground the garlic on a microplane and sprinkled on the bread. Beware of garlic breath though.
5/5 stars. I made this exactly according to the recipe and it was a divine breakfast. The sherry vinegar/sardine oil dressing is genius. I’ll be making this one again!

This was a tasty upgrade on avocado toast. I used smoked trout and it worked well. Definitely go with higher quality canned fish and a good sourdough. The tang of the sherry vinegar was a perfect contrast to the richness of the fish and avocado.
this worked... I didn't know what to expect, but add this to Girl-Dinner rotation...

Completely elevated the bland frozen dumplings we had at home. I can't wait to make this again! 
A few suggestions based on changes I made: 
- Try spinach instead of kale (about 500g for 20 dumplings)
- Double the ginger and garlic
- Before serving, I added sliced scallions and white sesame seeds, then drizzled with soy sauce, sesame oil, ponzu, chili crisp and a few drops of la-yu (Japanese chili oil)

@Taline
Definitely double the ginger and garlic! Great notes.

made the recipe exactly as written. It was tasty. A few things I would do differently next time.... hoison sauce instead of soy and then let everyone add soy if they want it...and additional veggies like carrots, celery and bean sprouts for some variety of flavor and texture.

I tripled this recipe this last night for a postcard writing crowd. It couldn’t have been easier. I used Trader Joe’s veggie dumplings and their bagged organic Tuscan kale. I added toasted sesame seeds at the end. It was a real crowd pleaser and postcard writing stimulant-we wrote over 400!
@Willa pardon my ignorance, but what is a postcard writing evening like? I truly dont know and would love to learn!
@Paige political… Sending to people to make sure they vote.
Good, quick, and easy. Buy precut kale if available, because you need a lot of it. Don’t forget to add the rice vinegar at the end.
@Jayne, not all precut kale is equal. The one I had was full of the tough stems. Learned my lesson the hard way. I will use lacinato and chop it myself after this.
@Sharon Thanks! Karl I use ends up like tough, wet newspaper. Will try Tuscan instead next time.
@Sharon absolutely!
Delicious! Used a mix of tuscan kale (I don’t like the curly variety) and shredded cabbage. Would add even more cabbage next time!

After deribbing the kale leaves, place the leaves in the freezer (bagged) for at least 24 hours.
When ready to use, remove from freezer and massage the frozen kale into small pieces. This process breaks down the tough cell structures and tenderizes the kale.

I ran out to buy dumplings as soon as I read this method. Used the bag of spinach I had & followed the directions for a quick @ fabulous dinner. This recipe & technique are a game changer for fast & healthy. Thank you.

Very good recipe. I used baby bok choy - a bit more by weight than recipe calls for in kale. Result was, surprisingly, too little bok choy. Still yummy. But I’ll use kale next time or at least 50% more baby bok choy.

@chrstrr
I used regular bok choi including all the white parts. Delicious but will use more next time.
@chrstrr baby big choy was great. But, definitely needs tons more.
@chrstrr Oh! Thanks for the vol choy idea. I have some that I want/need to harvest and this will be a perfect recipe for it tonight.
Very nice, but Chinkiang vinegar all the way, I wouldn't do rice.

Used Korean dumplings, adult spinach and extra garlic and ginger. Finished with a spoonful of hoisin sauce. Had some leftover salmon which protein complemented nicely. Cabbage and radish quick pickle completed the feast. Thank you for the the helpful comments, and the recipe, for a great meal.
@Sheila lol “ adult spinach.” That’s my name for proper spinach as well. I also happen to love “adult Lima beans.” Why do we live in a world that sacrifices them so young?

@Sheila you just turned on the light for me for many recipes with the radish quick pickle! There’s now a jar in my fridge of sliced radishes right next to jar of chopped red onion, happily soaking in Marukan seasoned rice vinegar.
Delicious easy recipe. Double the soy sauce and vinegar or it will eat a little dry.

I used kimchi dumplings and collard greens. I don’t care for kale and they were out of mustard greens.A bold choice for sure but it worked. You need to cook the  collard greens first for at least 10-15 with about 1 cup of water. I doubled the garlic, rice vinegar and I added hoisin sauce. Very tasty. 

Next time I’ll try napa cabbage.
Delicious, easy, and quick. Use broth instead of water to steam the dumplings for even more flavor.
@CLB great idea. This would be so good with Trader Joe's lemongrass ginger broth!
Tasty and easy, right up my alley. Subbed cabbage for kale. Added about 10 minutes to the cook down time to make sure the cabbage was cooked. Also added 1/4 teaspoon hot pepper flakes. Delicioso!
I used a bag of kale and broccoli slaw, and the vegetables shrunk so much that there was only enough for one serving! LOTS of precut veggies next time!
I've made this recipe several times now with (lots of) fresh spinach instead of kale, and a splash of toasted sesame oil. It's delicious! Will make again for sure.
Where do you find frozen dumplings?
My grocer carries them in the Chinese frozen food section. They’re labeled as dumplings, although restaurant menus in our area (northeast Ohio) refer to them as potstickers.
@Barbara M Trader Joe’s has 3-4 different kinds of Gyoza, all 1-pound bags. That’s what I’m going to use
I used the Costco Bibigo Mini Wontons with Chicken & Cilantro. I usually keep a bag in my freezer for wonton soup, and this recipe was a nice change of pace.
Chopped some bok choy instead of kale because I had it. Maybe took a bit longer for the stems to soften but perfect substitute.
Pre-chopped greens increase potential for contamination with Listeria, Salmonella, and E. coli. Just buy whole and wash & chop at home.
Top this recipe over the Miso Brown Butter Rice recipe from earlier in the week. Works well!
really tasty, but next time I will add more kale!

Amazingly good!  I agree with the comment about black vinegar.  Didn’t have kale so used gailan.

A keeper !

20 minutes to make -- Don't believe it!  It takes 20 to 30 minutes just to prep the kale, ginger and garlic. Once you have everything ready to go it takes another 20 to 25 minutes to cook.  A fine recipe but give yourself 50 minutes to make it.
@Paul, although they don't make this information especially clear, the time posted for NYT recipes is the actual cooking time, not the prep time (chopping, grating, etc).

@Paul I am a sometimes-impatient cook. I used pre-chopped bagged kale, and pre-minced (tubed) garlic and ginger. Everything turned out quite nicely. 

The only real prep I had to do was to wash and thinly slice three leeks, which added a nice flavor to the kale.

Our family LOVED this recipe! 

We used a bag of frozen chicken potstickers from Costco, which turned out just fine. Might try a bag of frozen Korean mandu next time.  

Also used a bag of pre-chopped kale, which was easy and turned out very tender.

We also added three halved and very thinly sliced (4mm food processor blade) leeks, which brought a lovely earthy tone to the otherwise bland kale.

As others have noted, we used more ginger and garlic than called for (probably about 50% more than the recipe suggested). 

I will probably double the recipe next time by using two large frying pans for the greens, because we all loved the greens so much.

I’m sure I’m going to cooking h-e-l-l because I used the tubes of pre-minced ginger and garlic, but the end result was still staggeringly good. 

I made the dipping sauce as directed, found it a bit bland. (However, that’s why chile crisp and hoisin sauce is for, in our home.) Served it with toasted sesame seeds for garnish, and they added a lovely nutty subtle crunch to the greens.

Next time we’ll add a bag of baby spinach, and maybe some baby bok choy.

We also decided that we’ll make just the greens some evening as a side dish, without the dumplings. The greens were just that good.

Very good. A one-pound package of dumplings (aka potstickers) was a bit too much food for my 2-person household. Next time I’ll save half for another meal and serve it over rice. By request, I will also substitute other greens for the kale.
Really good -- made with spinach as that's all I had. suspect kale would be better but spinach works well too! Serve over rice as the resulting sauce doesn't totally cook off and is really flavourful
Really quick and easy dinner! After I steamed the dumplings, I added the soy sauce and rice vinegar straight into the pan to get everything coated together.
This was a great recipe and so quick and simple. I love that you can add what ever you want and it only makes it better!
This is crazy good, especially in light of how simple and quick the ingredients and prep are.  I used a big bunch of chard instead of kale, which was good, but next time I'll double the greens!
Made it exactly as written and will do the same in the future.  Perfect quick Meatless Monday dinner!

We took another reviewer’s advice and subbed hoisin sauce for the soy sauce and it was delicious. Definitely a great way to elevate frozen dumplings and a breeze to make on a busy weeknight. Also a great recipe for a college kid who has a kitchen.
How is this a nytimes recipe. Frozen gyoza? I could have come up with this recipe as a starving college student.
@DF Maybe we are all really starving college students inside tired older bodies that need ideas for getting a quick dinner on the table.
@DF Starving college students need quick and easy recipes, too, without sacrificing yumminess!
@Amy S. Yes! This reminds me of the many many packets of ramen noodles with salvaged veggies and added soy sauce that I ate in college.
@KathyT Hi Kathy, you must have just missed it but the ingredients list specifically says 'unthawed' dumplings
Can I use frozen spinach?
Sure! Just thaw it and drain it well.
For something with kale, this has no business being as good as it is. It’s…eye-rollingly good. The dumplings are almost incidental.

Yikes. That was the worst thing I’ve made in god knows how long. Maybe it was the pan or the dumplings I used, but the dumplings stuck to the pan and came apart instead of browning when I tried to turn. Heat was medium high and there was plenty of avocado oil but it turned into a total mess. In the end I spent more time cleaning the pan than I did preparing the meal. Frustrating and dissatisfying.
I used shredded cabbage instead of kale, and served it with hoisin, as others suggested, along with soy sauce.  My husband and I ate all four servings!  So quick and easy for such a delicious meal.

I love this dish. This time I substituted spinach for the kale and added some sliced baby bella mushrooms. Did not add any water or salt and it was fine. Will keep doing this for an easy meal that is nourishing and filling.
