Quick and easy weekend lunch. Will definitely add more ginger and garlic next time and maybe some shelled edamame and carrots to the veggies. Great 1 pan meal!

Set a speed record with this recipe. I used kale and mustard greens from my garden with Trader Joe's mini chicken wontons. I used probably double the greens recommended. I put a little Hoisin on the side for a dipper but not in the sauce because it's too much. I topped it with scallion, Sriracha and sesame seeds.

This was absolutely delicious. 
However not all “bunches of kale are created equal. I used 6 packed cups of prewashed and cut (thankfully!) kale. The soy sauce and rice vinegar at the end is great.
This is delicious. The rice vinegar makes it. I used chard/silverbeet as it is more available down under. 5 minutes to cook kale??? Not in my experience.

This comes together super quickly, which I appreciate on a busy school night! Next time I'll be sure to use a slightly lower heat and my largest pan or wok, b/c I felt like I was under attack from the splattering oil when I added the dumplings - and my stovetop looked terrible! I may add more kale next time or serve atop rice as suggested to make it stretch a little bit more (as written, it served one adult and 4 kids perfectly). Kid-approved.

This was so good! I made as directed except I had chard instead of kale. I guesstimated quantities to make for 2 people. I had soy sauce and scallions to use as an optional topping. I may have overdone the garlic and ginger. Oh well! So glad to have a big bag of dumplings in the freezer so I can make it again. Recommend serving with some steamed rice.
All I have is frozen spinach. Not sure how to adjust to avoid soggy dumplings…?
Easy, fast and tasty emergency meal. Could sub in other veg for kale as needed. Will likely make this again!
Super easy and the soft crispy dumplings with the kale was a perfect texture experience! Threw in some mushrooms and doubled the garlic, ginger and sauce at the end. Great weeknight meal!
Easy. Used asparagus which is what we had. Good.
Fabulous. I used spinach vs kale. Great. Would use even more spinach.
I used collards because that’s what I had. I was concerned the dumplings would fall apart, so I waited to add them until collards were done. Turned out great! Absolutely loved this recipe!

Used TJ’s frozen dumplings and bagged kale for this easy, quick, and delicious lunch for two… made it today and we finished the entire pan! No rice was needed alongside, as this was filling enough. Will try it with chili crisp next time.
Really delicious. Used Spinach and kids happily ate it!
Quite good, easy and simple to make. Next time I will double the kale. I bought pre cut baby kale and it was perfect.

Yum! Used Costco dumplings on a busy school night. A big bunch of purple kale plus a beautiful bunch of bok choy from the farm stand. Served over jasmine rice with smoked salmon for added protein, though an egg would work well too!

I found that using a full box (1 pound) of dumplings was far too much for one bunch of kale, and also did not fit well in a 5 qt Le Creuset signature braiser- it was overstuffed. I would use 1/2 a box in the future. It also took longer to cook the dumplings and I needed 2x much oil, and used 2x soy sauce.

Why thaw the dumplings? Just a question because I don’t know. I usually cook them frozen (same method, but steam before removing from pan initially) because they tend to stick together in the package if you thaw them first.

@KathyTthe 
The recipe calls for cooking frozen dumplings.

Easy, tasty and super flexible - this recipe works with any sturdy green (bok choy, chard, etc.) and yields a heartier and more flavorful meal than steamed or pan-seared dumplings alone. I doubled the ginger and garlic and added cilantro, green onion and chili crisp at the end.
@KathyT ...  Not sure which recipe you read, because this one calls for UNthawed dumplings.

Was alright. Easy to make, which is nice. I added carrots, per someone else’s recommendation. However, my family wasn’t a fan of the “slimy kale” as the vegetable bed. Your texture preferences may vary. Probably won’t make again.
Very fast and delicious. Chili crisp (Momofuku’s, with black truffle, no less!) is a plus. LOVED that cooking techinique. Thank you Ali!
Has anyone tried this with pierogi?

Made this tonight…had frozen dumplings our daughter had left and spinach I had in the refrigerator…used more garlic chopped carrots that needed to be used. Hoisin sauce at the end as someone suggested, rice vinegar, chopped scallions, sesame seeds  for serving and soy sauce  easy and quick..

What a great tasting surprise! Tried this even though the combination of kale and dumplings seemed odd and it was great! Perfect meal to cook when you’re hungry and don’t feel like making a long drawn out recipe. Even my sister who always seems to have notes said it felt like she had just finished a meal at a restaurant! Highly recommend.

Excellent recipe. Used spinach instead of kale and premixed soy sauce, rice vinegar, chile paste, sesame seeds and spring onions.  Used half immediately after cooking and put the other half of the mix on the table.

Such a great recipe. Followed the recipe, but doubled the garlic and ginger. Massaged the kale in olive oil before adding to pan. Trader Joes dumplings make this easy peasy. Be sure to add the vinegar and chili crisp (I diluted mine with olive oil in order to make it easier to drizzle).
How much kale by weight?

I just made this using the sugar snap peas that just arrived at my farmers market,  instead of kale. Fantastic! Used a bag of TJ vegetable dumplings and topped with both white and black sesame seeds and sliced scallions.
So easy and so good!

*Step 2 does not include how long to “Add the dumplings on top of the kale and partially cover the skillet with a lid or baking sheet.”  before continuing to “Pour ¼ cup water into the skillet, cover completely”
*The flavors are WOW!
*For tenderizing kale - remove kale stems , wash & chop leaves then allow to dry on towels/racks. Bag & freeze chopped kale. When ready to use, remove lfrom freezer and immediately begin kneading and massaging the still frozen bag of kale
Love this recipe!!  Easy and delicious.
Kale was unavailable due to being out of season, so I substituted one head of Savoy cabbage, shredded. Delicious!

I'm not much for hyperbole but this was one of the best dishes I've ever eaten. Next time I'll double the recipe. I just picked up some kimchi potstickers from Trader Joe's that I use next time. The vinegar and soy added to the greens was the chefs kiss.
Easy and tasty!  Made as described but I love everyone’s comments—will definitely make this again and will try your suggestions!
Made it with rainbow chard and TJ's frozen pork dumplings. Looking forward to making it with par-cooked mustard greens.
This was excellent.  My husband loved it so much he made it three days later.  Definitely elevated our “emergency “ midweek stash of frozen dumplings.

Quick pantryish dinner. Definitely triple the kale or green of your choice along with extra garlic and ginger.  Go lightly with the salt on the greens because of the salt in the potstickers, soy, and chili sauce.
How can a serving just be 149 calories unless you only wave the frozen dumplings over the meal?

Super easy and quick weeknight dinner.  I used kale that I massaged first to tenderize.  I would probably use spinach next time.  Also used tofu, mushroom and ginger dumplings from our local dumpling shop. Per other reviews, I doubled the ginger and garlic, served the dish with toasted sesame, green onions and chili crisp with a drizzle of toasted sesame oil. My husband and I both loved it!
I used some asian cabbages all to good effect.  Excellent dish!  Very simple.  Dumplings were cooked perfectly!
Never hurts to add some oatmeal; 1/8-1/4 cup to keep you satiated!
@NancySF I like the fluffiness I get when adding a few tablespoons of oatmeal to this.
@NancySF raw or cooked? I’m usually not an oatmeal fan due to texture, so I’ve never considered this idea… seems like a perfect solution to the texture problem!
Added 1/2 cup froz avocado, and 2 tsp matcha powder, skipped nut butter. Consistency like thick milkshake. Delish!
@Sharon I would’ve never thought to add avocado. Thank you for the tip! I love peanut butter by itself, but don’t really care for it in anything.
@CDGlad you liked the avocado tip. I use avocado as it is healthy fat source, does not have a strong flavor and added a consistency to this drink that I like!

@Sharon I love avocado in my smoothies! I will use 1/2 avocado and peel the seed, cut it in half and blend that in, too, for a little extra nutrition. Yum! They do have some compounds that might inhibit nutrient absorption so I only do this about once a week.
The chia seed did not blend well . They were like clumps in my smoothie
@Maya j 2 Tablespoons is a lot in my experience.

@Maya j Mix the chia seeds with your milk of choice and stir until it starts to thicken into a pudding-like texture, about 5 minutes. Don’t let the chia seeds settle at the bottom. You want them to absorb the liquid evenly and avoid clumping. Once thickened, add the chia mixture to your shake ingredients and blend.

Clean your dishwasher filter weekly (or more than your usual once in a whenever). Chia seeds easily get trapped in the filter and clog it, and then you have an unwanted unintended consequence. Speaking from experience

I like the focus of ditching the protein powders which are ultra-processed.  My favourite ingredient in protein shakes is egg whites. You can buy pasteurized egg whites and 1/3 cup has 10 gm of protein.  Top up with soy milk, throw in a bit of nut butter or hemp hearts and whatever fruit you like and you have 20 gm of protein. You can’t taste the egg.
If you add cottage cheese, you'll also want to add a sweetener as the former really dims the flavor.

@Smoothie drinker if it dims the flavor,
Don’t do it

Just made this - delish! I used a thick Greek yogurt, plus peanut butter and a smoothie mix of berries and mango. I eyeballed everything but I think my thick ingredients meant I used more milk. The cinnamon really does tie it together like a dessert!
@Sally the recipe intro says it's "flexible based on what you have". You have protein powder you want to use. Congratulations on your protein shake adaptation! I hope you enjoy it!

Tip for frozen bananas to make blending much easier: slice up your bananas when they ripe and put them in a ziplock in the freezer with the round pieces arrayed kind of like a line of fallen dominoes. When ready to make your shakes, pull out a ziplock and they should easily break apart and be much easier blend than a whole banana. Rinse and reuse the ziplocks.
@Butter Cup Love the fallen dominoes image:)

Don't see Protein ratings.   
Not sure why your other stuff tasted like cement.  Maybe too much powder. 
Try frozen bananas, blueberries.   This isn't your only source of protein, so you don't need to overdo it.

I’ve read info & my Dr. also told me as well to not add fruit to my smoothies b/c:
Blending fruit for a smoothie breaks down plant cell walls, releasing trapped natural sugars and turning them into "free sugars". While fiber remains, this mechanical breakdown makes the sugar metabolically similar to added sugar, often causing a faster spike in blood sugar compared to eating whole fruit
I add a bit of vanilla extract or a FEW blueberries .. then eat my fruit in it’s natural form.
Used what I had. Oikos Key Lime, mango, soy milk, pb fit powder, cinnamon. Sounds like it would be gross but tastes great!

Sixteen grams of protein is a pittance. My daily shake
(homemade) is packed with almost 60 grams of protein. This is kind of a nothing burger. Also if you have frozen berries and even 1/4 of a frozen banana, you do not need any dairy or dairy substitutes.
@Chaz would u share ur recipe? I could use 60g!!

@Chaz
One’s body cannot metabolize more than 20 grams of protein at a time. The rest gets burned off as energy or gets excreted.
heads up that many grocery stores sell powdered peanut (and other nut) butters! Should be in the same aisle as the regular peanut butter. This has been a great addition to my smoothies.
@searching I agree!

I usually do a variation of this:

1 cup unsweetened almond/nut milk
2 Tbsps Greek yogurt (whatever fat content you want)
1 Tbsp chia seeds (soaked in the nut milk for at least 15 min so they become like little jelly balls)
1 Tbsp flax meal (yay fiber!)
1 serving protein powder (I use flavored, but you can substitute with whatever protein source you want)

That’s my go to base. Then I add either a banana or a couple slices dehydrated mangos and about half a cup of frozen fruit. I’ll throw in a scoop of peanut butter if I’m hungry and out of frozen fruit. Frozen blueberries thicken the shake a lot so I use more nut milk when I do blueberries.

I don’t track my macros or anything, but just generally want a tasty and pretty healthy shake. This hits the spot every time.
i like cookies.

Lots of comments about how fat is good for you, however, it is not good for everyone. My cholesterol will rocket if I include that in my diet. Live and let live;-). Agree that I might add a little oatmeal and maybe a handful of greens.
Consider kefir as the added dairy option! So good with a tangerine, some ginger and a teaspoon of cocoa powder.
What's the protein content in this? I don't see the nutritional breakdown on this recipe.

That’s a lot of calories for only 16 g of protein, and only half the size of my regular smoothie. Also, if you’re using tart fruit like frozen mango tends to be (at least the stuff I find around here) you’re going to need a sweetener of some kind. I put about a tablespoon of agave syrup in it and it was — ok. I think I’ll go back to my regular.
High carbs for diabetic.  Maybe nonfat greek yogurt and non fat milk? Also, reduce the amount of fruit especially if banana.
Nonfat yogurt and milk don't have less carbs than the full fat versions - in fact they often have more sugar / carbs.
@Sally Yes I wish people would stop thinking of low-fat foods as healthy…

@Meladee. Low fat versions of dairy are carb bombs and bad for diabetics and those with insulin resistance.  Fat slows down the absorption of the carbs, and removing the fat leads to insulin and glucose spikes from the now “naked” carbs.  

Fat is also a macro nutrient our bodies need. Fat is not the enemy:  sugar and ultra-processed foods are the enemy.
add frozen avocado for a healthy fat
A weak tasting recipe. Go ahead, use organic plant based protein powder. Add a whole sectioned lemon too. Flavor with a bit of bourbon.
Add a shot of espresso and you've got yourself a complete breakfast! 💪🏻

I try not to lump all fats together.  The evidence is quite strong that consumption of land raised animal fat contributes to arterial disease.  See e.g. Esselstyn's work at Cleveland Clinic.  The consumption of relatively small amounts of fat from nuts, seeds, (and perhaps fatty fishes) appears protective for arterial disease.
Yes, Tom! You’re so right!
Adding raw whole oatmeal adds beneficial nutrients, helps with cholesterol and adds that full feelin.
I think chia seeds need to soak for 20 to 30 minutes before eating (or drinking them).
Mine stays really thick even though I add a bit more milk than the recipe. Anybody has a tip that could make a bit smoother without using too much milk? Thanks :)

I can't stand the texture protein powder gives my fruit and kale smoothies, but I add a chunk of tofu (about 1/5 of the standard package) instead of the peanut butter or yogurt in this recipe. It adds protein and a bit of thickness without changing the taste.
If using, chia seeds need to be soaked a few hours ahead of time or overnight to create a chia "pudding" then blended. Otherwise they clump.
Though overnight pudding is lovely, I've found that soaking chia seeds for about 10-15 minutes does the trick well enough when you're tossing them in a blender.
Very filling & satisfying, however not quite as sweet as i was hoping. Next time i might add a little honey or maple syrup.

Why skip the protein powder? Many of us eschew dairy as lactose intolerance becomes prescient.  I swim, jog, lift weights at an advanced age; this gives me energy: Handful of chopped fresh spinach, 4-5 chunks each of pineapple, avocado, banana, all frozen) 1 T. 3-seed mix (hemp, chia, flaxseed), scoop of vanilla protein powder (35-60 grams), 1c. SF coconut milk, stevia, dashes-nutmeg, ginger, cinnamon. Can add 1/2 c. canned pumpkin, SF brown sugar, SF maple syrup, pumpkin pie seasoning.

Many protein powders contain stuff you don't want to put in your body, and that's why many homeopaths and naturopathic doctors will steer you away from them in favor of nuts, nut butters, and/or seeds. These powders can include heavy metals and other junk, all in the name of healthy eating.
Try buttermilk! Easier to blend and keeps beneficial probiotics.

Bananas are particularly high in PPO (Polyphenol Oxidase). Adding bananas to smoothies introduces this enzyme, which can reduce the beneficial effects of polyphenols from berries like blueberries.  I imagine that a high percentage of protein smoothies sold or homemade contain berries and bananas.  Bananas add thickness and sweetness, but to benefit from the best nutritional component of the shake, you may want to avoid this combination (86 the bananas). Just a thought.
27g of protein: 2g/milk + 8g/peanut butter + 17g/Greek yogurt. Perfectly fine amount of protein.
@Sharon, I often add 1/2 a fresh avocado to my shakes.  Love the creaminess it adds.
I love there smoothies and appreciate that cow milk is used. The last time I told someone my recipe had milk, they looked at me like I had just kicked a kitten. Delicious.
If using cottage cheese, try one with lower sodium. I found the shake to be far too salty when I substituted yogurt for cottage cheese when I ran out.
Made for my kids, so I added 2 dates and a banana for sweetness. The tang of the yogurt is still detectable, but there were no comments of “Mom, why does this taste different?”.

Purple Skies nailed it…too many low fat items are very high in sodium. Not good for diabetics nor anyone else. To emphasize, "FAT is not the enemy: SUGAR and ULTRA-Processed Foods are the enemy.”  NancySF has the correct idea as oats are good for everyone including those with sugar issues.

@Meladee Or, you're never going to believe this, you have the option to buy low fat dairy without any added sugars! Skyr is naturally fat and sugar free. You can get plenty of low fat greek yogurt without any added sugars.
Sorry, this want meant for @Purple Skies
There is too much yoghurt, and I recommend if using mango to add more. I don’t know what it tastes like with other fruits, but with mango I only tasted yoghurt.
Where is a list of the ingredients for the variations seen in the photo? I somehow missed them
@AnneD I think the photos are of smoothies using the different fruits listed in the ingredients, judging by the colors.
Delicious! Running the chia seeds through the spice grinder helps with clumping, as well more fully digestible.
Instead of milk, what can I use?
@L. I used to water.
Very good and smooth

I've been making this for breakfast for years... 1/2 c Greek yogurt, 1/2 banana or mango, strawberries, blueberries, soy milk, cinnamon, a pinch of tumeric, ground flax and frozen fruit blended together.  Delicious and nutritious!
almond protein, one ingredient, clean, if you want more protein that is.
Did I miss it, or did you not tell us how much protein is in your Protein Shake recipe?

Very creamy and tasty. Only complaint is it was a little too salty. I do like salty + sweet as much as the next guy, but not in a fruit smoothie. I could try next time with greek yogurt, but I used cottage cheese - is there such as thing as low sodium cottage cheese? I don't know how else to cut back on the salt.

Made this with cottage cheese. Do not recommend. Maybe it work work better with low sodium or salt-free cottage cheese (is that a thing?). Maybe recipe should specify that regular cottage cheese will make it taste like liquid cottage cheese :P

Friendship makes a salt-free cottage cheese. It's awful by itself (bought it once by mistake). Maybe blended with other flavors it would...blend? If I were to make this, I would use Greek yogurt and leave the cottage cheese, which I actually do like, for more savory uses.

I made the recipe with one cup of non fat Greek yogurt, 1/2 room temperature banana, 2 Tbs black chia seeds, 15 drops of stevia, 4 ice cubes and 1/4 cup of water. I blended for one minute in the magic bullet. Tasted great with no chia clumps.
My favorite combo: milk, frozen banana, cocoa powder, peanut butter, chia seeds.
Mastered these at last! Baked for 28
I added flax seeds and it tasted yum
I use 1/2 cup of yogurt and 1/2 cup of cottage cheese; I really enjoy the taste of real food in this recipe

My version was: 1/4 cup macadamia milk, 1 tb chia (soaked in the macadamia milk for a few min), 1/2 cup plain kefir, 1/2 cup lowfat milk, 2 tb peanut butter fit powder, 1 tb flaxseeds, 1 cup berries. It was excellent
add cacao nibs … they add a nice crunch!
I cannot be titled protein shake if the grams of protein multiplied by 10 are less than the total calories.
O think that chia seeds need more time to soak in the liquid. iI only use it for overnight oats etc@Maya j

438 calories for 16g protein? This is not an efficient way of getting protein. Blend 3/4 c nonfat Greek yogurt, a peeled orange, 1 T flaxmeal, 1 T hemp hearts, and a 30g chocolate protein shake (I like fair life) w/ ice. You get a hydrating heart healthy shake for roughly 405 cal and 53g protein. Sub different fruit or shake flavor if you like.
Protein powder…

title: Creamy Cottage Cheese Basil Pasta Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: Made this last night following the recipe to a T. It was delicious - and a really cool shade of green! We would not suggest making any changes or adjustments. Since it was just two of us, we halved the recipe and it was more than enough. We LOVE Ali Slagle’s pasta recipes!
@paulpap Halving the recipe is an adjustment! But good to know the proportions work.  I’ll halve it for me and the Mrs when we give it a whirl.
@paulpap How lemony is it and do you think it would taste ok without? I don’t care for citrus in my savory dishes.

@Katie80
Lemon is definitely an important part of the recipe.

Do Not try to grate Parmesan in a food processor or blender. I almost ruined my high powered cuisinart. I had to mail it to the repair center. I begged them to fix it because I have all of the disc blades. But they had to break the bowl to dislodge it. 
Just grate the cheese by hand. It’s not that hard.
@NHMAAZTXCA I’ve literally done this hundreds of times with my $100 cuisenart food processor. Just cut it into ~1in cubes first. Works like a charm.
@NHMAAZTXCA. AGREED! I have an older machine too with all the blades. I nearly destroyed it. Had to search high and low for a new work bowl. Hand grate only!

@NHMAAZTXCA The recipe calls for grated cheese. It doesn’t suggest using the food processor. Personally, I grate it either by hand with a metal grater or or with one of the crank graters, which are perfect for Parmesan.
Please don't jump on me, but what would happen if you combined cottage cheese with a very good store bought pesto?
I think it could be absolutely delicious and it’s certainly worth trying.  Go for it!

@Old guy who doesn't do much cooking - You should be fine and that should take care of all the oil, garlic, herbs/green leafy veg. Check the container to see what it has in it.  You may still want/need to add more garlic, parmesan (I use the pre-grated store bought kind found in the cheese section, not the cheap kind in a cardboard can), and/or a little lemon to boost and brighten the flavors. (Consider also adding some walnuts, chickpeas/garbanzos, or chicken to the pasta for protein after it is all mixed.)
@Old guy who doesn't do much cooking I bet it would be delicious!

I didn’t have basil on hand so I used parsley from my garden instead. Was fantastic! I also added a handful of walnuts into the sauce to add a little extra protein and healthy fat to the sauce because I was making this on the fly and didn’t have a separate protein prepared to serve with it. The family devoured and the entire dish came together in exactly as much time as it took to boil the pasta (11 minutes)!

@Valarie

cottage cheese has a ton of protein which is helpful
@Jen. Exactly. The calorie to protein ratio on walnuts is quite low/poor (great ingredient for other reasons).  Protein content in the cottage cheese and parm is what makes this great.
Try this with Trader Joe’s Gigli pasta. Delicious. So many cracks and crevices for the sauce.  Also kale works great instead of spinach.
@Anon O. Miss. Thank  for the tip! We used the Trader Joe’s Gigli and the sauce found its way into all the right spots!! YUM!!
I roasted chopped portobello and asparagus and tossed it in as the last step for a nice addition! This was great and a hit with toddlers.

The recipe does not suggest that you put chunks of Parmesan in your food processor.  It says to use grated.  I have a handheld crank grater that I use exclusively for Parmesan, it was a game changer for our household where the king of cheeses is added to almost everything

@Jacki the tip at the bottom literally says to.

“Chop the Parmesan into big chunks, then pulse in a blender until ground. Take some out for serving, then add the rest of the sauce ingredients and proceed.”
@Jacki The Mouli grater, just for grating mouli!
I added a strip of preserved lemon before blending - it gave it an extra little pop of lemon and salt and I would do it again!

Growing up in the 50s, my family was poor, although as a kid, we didn't know it. One dish my mother made frequently was pasta with butter, cottage cheese and the grated Parmesan from Kraft that came/still comes in the green tube. Occasionally she would throw frozen green peas in at the same time as the cottage cheese. It remains comfort food for me

I am bemused and encouraged to find the NYT version of this. It is definitely going into the rotation.
@PEllen Yes!! We called it Noodles and Cheese when I was a kid, and it was cheap and easy for Mom and comforting.
I call cottage cheese the “Lazy Girl’s Bechamel.”  I’m actually classically trained, but, as in this recipe, cottage cheese is an ideal substitute.
Can't eat pasta becvause of tyupe 2 diabetes. What would substitute as a carrier for this interesting sauce. Would it work as a sauce over vegetables, say, "zucchini noodles" or "cauliflower rice"?

@Charles Kruger zucchini noodles would be good but try shirataki noodles (Miracle Noodles) — basically all konjac fiber and no carbs.  There are also some varieties that are part tofu (House Foods brand) which I actually prefer for a slightly better texture and taste plus a little protein.  The important thing in preparing these noodles is to rinse well then boil for about 5 minutes and drain well. They are packed in a solution with an unpleasant smell but this will remove it. I usually find shiratake noodles in the tofu section of the store.
@Charles Kruger Zoodles will definitely work.
@Charles Kruger I’ve been thinking about that too… maybe steamed cauliflower and some sliced chicken breast?
Would it be bad to use 2% cottage cheese instead of full fat?
@AEC That’s what I had so that’s what I used. I added a bit more olive oil. I liked it.
That's what I plan on using!
Also delicious over boiled potatoes if you want a change from the usual mayo or vinegar based potato salads.
Just a thought :  Halve small red, orange and / or yellow heirloom tomatoes at the end -- colorful and adds a bit of zip to the mix !  Enjoy!

I’m a basil pesto fiend. So I’m wondering why not use 100% basil leaves, 
5 1/2 oz basil, skip the spinach? No chance it’ll be too intense mixed in with 1 Cup bland cottage cheese and spread over 1 lb spaghetti.
@AnnaB I’ve long added spinach to my pesto. It helps keep it a vibrant green (and maybe ups the nutrients?).
NHMAAZTXCA how did you attempt this?  Didi you use a shredding disc, or did you throw chunks into the moving blade?
@AAC I’ve done it many times in my Cuisinart, just put small chunks in with the blade, few at first and keep adding the rest. Use the pulsating button.
@Bakingmom yes, this is the way.

My kid had no idea this had cottage cheese or spinach in it. Loved it!  No adjustments necessary but I did make the full recipe and will plan to freeze half the sauce since we only ate 1/2 lb of pasta.

@Rachel

I’ll be interested to hear how the texture is after freezing.
When the nutrition list says 4-6 servings are the values listed for 4 servings or 6 servings?

To those that want to sub in ricotta or just don’t like this recipe-there is a huge difference in taste and texture amongst cottage cheese brands!  Read the label.  Many have unnecessary additives and are watery and tasteless.  I like Daisy and Good.  Ingredients should number 2-3 and be basically milk, cultures and salt.
For those of us with homemade pesto in the freezer (or even local fresh store bought), what is the ratio of pesto to cottage cheese?
@Alice B. That’s what I’m going to try. I have lots of frozen pesto from my garden. I’m going to just estimate and adjust until I like it.

Yummy and with a lot of food value, too.  Made as written, weighing everything as suggested. We added a bunch of freshly ground black pepper and we liked the suggestion of chopped walnuts.   Loved the color!  Perfect for a Shrek party.
This is incredible
I used my Vitamix for the whole process and it worked great.  I’ve used my Cuisinart to “grate” Parmesan but find the Vitamix works a lot better. The texture is very fine and it’s fast and easy.
Which Vitamix disc did you use?

@Diana I don’t know about your Vitamix, but ours didn’t come with various blades. I believe the instruction manual said to cut the Parmesan in small cubes, freeze it, then procede as Ali said. I haven’t tried it yet. I would assume a Zyliss hand grater would work with cold Parmesan, too.

I’m not a huge fan of cottage cheese, consistency- and sodium-wise, although the consistency will not be a factor in this recipe. Still, I think I’ll try it with Eastern European “farmers’ cheese”, called tvorog in Russian. It’s sold at Slavic/Russian grocery stores, of which there are several in the DC Metro area where I live. Possibly also in Whole Foods, although not sure about that (also easy to make from scratch with milk that is starting to turn, or simply by adding some vinegar to fresh milk - then heating it until the curds separate from the whey and straining it). This type of cottage cheese is lovely for breakfast, a snack, etc., and much lower in sodium.

@SarahDC
Farmers Cheese is ALWAYS my substitute for cottage cheese. I can usually get it at Whole Foods. Use it in cheese cakes, pancakes, lasagna and will use it in this sauce.

@MJoyM I finally had the chance to pick up some tvorog/farmers’ cheese and promptly made the recipe! Haven’t even cooked the pasta yet and am ready to dive in to the food processor bowl with my spoon. So Yum!!
Has anyone tried subbing Ricotta cheese for the Cottage cheese?

I recently made a different recipe from an Italian chef using Ricotta for the arugula pesto and it was delicious so I would say, most definitely, yes you can use Ricotta.  However, the chef made the pesto, added to pan, thinned with some pasta water, then added the Ricotta, mixed and then pasta.

Give it a try!!
@NY Guy I will always substitute ricotta for cottage cheese. The quality difference between the two is huge.

The Nutritional Analysis gives figures "per serving" for "4 to 6" servings, but doesn't specify whether it is counting a single serving as 1/4 or 1/6 of the recipe.  That would make a big difference; the analysis isn't very useful without this information.
I suggest adding the salted water earlier - with the spinach and basil - to help with mixing.  You can add it before the pasta is finished cooking.  Without the additional liquid it is hard to blend.
Slice the Parm and you'll have no problem.  It's chunks that get stuck on the blades.
I make and freeze my own pesto, so can make this an even speedier meal by blending the cottage cheese with the pesto!!

I grew up eating cottage cheese and pasta. Easy recipe. When you’ve drained the pasta, put it back in the pot. Over small fire, add cottage cheese and whatever else you’d like, parmesan cheese is good. Stir and you’re finished.

I’ve been grating parmesan in a Cuisinart for roughly 50 years.  I usually do a fairly large quantity at one time, put some in a jar in the frig, and freeze the rest.  I cut the cheese into digestible chunks, start the machine and drop a few chunks at a time through the feed tube.  It works with the disk grater, but I often just use the steel blade.  The process is noisy, the Cuisinart sometimes bounces around a bit, and stay bits of cheese occasionally fly out, but it works fine.
@GarryG how did that freezer portion fare? Have you tried it yet?

Well, this was fun - such a bright green pasta! I used grated parm (the kind without cornstarch) which worked fine. Had leftover sauce as I halved the sauce recipe for 5 oz pasta (8 oz is too much for the two of us). Maybe I’ll broil some shrimp for it? Next time I’ll add some toasted pine nuts on top for texture.
Thought this was rather bland. Will add some red pepper flakes and maybe some chopped anchovies.

really good, easy meal! I agree with other commenters that you don’t need that much pasta water, but I’m saving the rest to use with the leftovers. It could use a little bit of pop- maybe black pepper or crushed red pepper flakes. I’m going to experiment with the leftovers and see if either of those help

We made this with blanched Nettles from our garden and it was DIVINE.....added a touch more garlic and a little arugula....oh and some chicken thigh cooked in marsala and onion.  Will definitely be making this again

This recipe popped up Saturday afternoon and I wanted to try it immediately! I had to raid the cupboard and fridge, swapping in kale for the spinach and jarred Barilla pesto because I didn’t have enough basil. I was unsure how much Parmesan to use, but can you ever use too much? This was still delicious—such a forgiving recipe! I sautéed shrimp from the freezer to go with. Such a keeper!
Works with full fat Greek yogurt, too. (And for that matter, with spring mix rather than pure spinach)
Three quarters of cup of pasta water made my sauce too loose.

One of the best NYT ever & I’ve done many.  Incredibly creamy.  Do yourself a favor & don’t shortcut with jar pesto.  Do the deal with very fresh basil & spinach.  Use only real grated Parmesan, not the shaker kind.  3/4 c pasta water is a bit much cuz it turns out a bit soupy but use a slotted spoon.  No need for long pasta.  I used Trofie pasta from Trader Joe. I think you could use this (before adding pasta water) to dress salad, veggies, fish, chicken.  Out of this world with possibilities.  And it’s cottage cheese yay!flavor bomb!

This recipe is very forgiving and adaptable with other fresh herbs. I had spinach but no basil so used tarragon, dill, and chives. It was bright and zippy! Everyone from toddler to grandparents loved it.
WAY too much work for an imitation pasta with pesto.  Needs some red pepper and walnuts or pine nuts to make it more interesting.
Came here to also recommend this. I roasted halved cherry tomatoes at 375 for 11 min, and the extra sweetness and color played very nicely with the slightly tart sauce. @kkm

I made this tonight, and it's very good! My only suggestion would be to use a medium lemon rather than a large one. mine was quite large, and just on the edge if overwhelming the other flavours. Definitely a keeper!
This was excellent, definitely going into the rotation, but honestly I think the amount of sauce could have dressed twice the pasta.
Loved this recipe but felt it needed something more so I roasted walnuts and rough chopped them which added color, texture and protein.  Think hazelnuts would work well too.
Nutritional yeast instead of Parmesan works just as well. Blend with a little almond milk. It's my go-to cream for all pasta and soup dishes.
Cuisinart has a grater attachment that works like a charm. My Cuisinart is 40 years old and works perfectly. I always grate a large volume of parmesan with it and never had an issue.
@Nancy same here.
@Nancy Yes, the grating disc for the Cuisinart does a great job with Parmesan.
AEC - Will it be as creamy if you use 2% cottage cheese, possibly not but, it's what I'm planning to do!
Re: Using food processor for grating cheese.....cube the parmesan, turn processor on and add a few cubes at a time as it is running. I've done this for years and never had a problem.
For those of us who pay "some" attention to the nutrition numbers, PLEASE don't show them as per serving (4-6 servings) Which is it?

The is a great recipe. A nice way to get a creamy sauce for pasta without the heavy cream. I amped up the garlic (to the equivalent of 5 cloves) and was generous with the olive oil. Next time I will use all the pasta cooking water recommended in the recipe so that it would loosen up more. Also, I should add that I used the Beta version of the recipe (I made only a half) which resulted in leftovers for two people. The Beta version of the recipe was spot on.

This will now become a staple for me.  It's outrageously good!  I was low on fresh basil so filled in with some fresh parsley.  It has the prefect amount of creaminess cut with the lemon zest/juice and garlic.  Divine and a great weeknight reheat.

Delicious!! wow. Used Fresh local spinach, in lieu of basil, garlic and olive oil I added a home made batch of petso from the freezer, which equated to 2 cups of basil, ect.  Tasted great, so assuming it was an okay substitution.  The sauce tastes so good it would be a great dip as an appetizer too!

This was meh. I had to add a lot of pepper and more parmesan It also wasn't particularly filling, which was surprising for something with that much cottage cheese and parmesan. It was fine, but I wouldn't make it again.

I've made this again and again and again. I serve it with a side of seared chicken sausage, sliced, for my kids. The leftovers are even better. This is a killer receipe and my kids love it after practice when they are starving and just out of the shower. I serve it to them in a big bowl, with a towel on their laps. I've shared it with a lot of people. A keeper.

5 stars all around. This recipe f l e x e s. Our basil plants are busting, so we harvested from 4 basil plants, (finally) used a 12oz bag of frozen spinach bc that’s what we have, a tub of Violife vegan cream cheese, vegan Parmesan cheese, half a block of silken tofu (boo is lactose free intolerant), olive oil because we are lacking dairy fat, and 2 juicy, fat garlic cloves which I recommend to everyone following Ali’s recipe to a T. Flavor is off the charts, easy to make and adapts h a r d. I have 3/4 pint of additional sauce due to my adaptations. I regret nothing.
Would it be possible to make this with Farmer’s cheese? I have some to use up.

I use farmer cheese or pressed cottage cheese all the time. It should be fine; just break up the cheese into chunks. Since farmer cheese is dryer than curd cottage cheese you might need to add more liquid (more pasta water or lemon juice or a bit of white wine).
Has anyone tried adapting this using store-bought pesto?

I made this with roughly equal parts cottage cheese and my frozen and thawed homemade pesto. Morning olive green in color, although my pesto did not contain any spinach. Topped it off with leftover breadcrumbs from the eggplant parm pasta recipe. Easy riff on pasta pesto and kids liked it.

I would not try to great a hard cheese in a food processor. it's just going to get caught on the blades and spin around and jam it up. I do however great hard cheese in my original Vitamix. it has an extraordinarily strong motor and it's probably the only appliance I would use for something like this. Otherwise it's probably best to just hand grade it using a box grater
I’m confused about the lemon.  The recipe sounds like you toss the whole lemon into the high speed blender.  Is it the zest of one lemon and the juice of one lemon?  Please explain.
It sounds like you zest and juice one lemon. Both things can be done to one lemon. The whole lemon does not go into the blender.

The instructions say "zest and juice the lemon into a high-powered blender or food processor." So you first zest the lemon and put that into the blender, and then squeeze the juice from the lemon into the blender. You don't put the whole lemon in.
This is a fantastic sauce! I used pecorino romano instead of parm.  I served it over small raviolis.  It was a hit with the family.  Next time I will serve it over tortellini.

All we could taste was lemon. If I could go back in time, I’d start with 1/4 lemon juice zest and gradually add in more. I ended up having to throw most of it away because my family refused to eat it. Even an extra cup of cheese couldn’t save it

Definitely not going back to  days of cottage cheese and cantaloupe /pineapple  which everyone ate to loose weight.  And spinach ? The 80’s and 90s  “it salad”.  No thanks .  Let’s update to delicious Ricotta cheese and arugula or kale or baby anything greens.
I would love to make this tonight but I have no basil yet. I do however have a garden full of gorgeous mint. maybe mint and parsley would work?
Do you taste the spinach?

We called this "green spahetti" and it was one of my kid's favorites. We made it pretty much as the recipe says, except we used cream cheese instead of cottage cheese, and we didn't have a blender. it is truly a perfect sauce.
Has anyone tried this with jarred pesto? I use Trader Joe’s all the time.
I have lots of frozen homemade pesto. Think I can substitute pesto for the fresh basil leaves? I don’t see why not, but haven’t made this yet. Thoughts?

This is reminiscent of a much simpler recipe that my mother made when we were kids: macaroni, sour cream, and cottage cheese. It was served with salt and pepper and whatever green vegetables were in the fridge like peas, or chopped up scallions. I think basil was a little too sophisticated but this sounds great and I'll definitely give it a try.
Old guy who doesn't do much cooking:  Try it!  The "what ifs" are the mainstay and great joy of cooking!

I, for one, I know I am in the minority, don't particularly like too much lemon. For me, it cuts through the other flavors, so I would do the zest.  I also think a handful of nuts would be nice, for a little texture.
For the Parmesan:  I run pieces through the processor with the shredding blade, then switch to the regular blade and chop up those shredded pieces.
OMG. This is genius. Made as written. Glorious! Bonus is protein from cottage cheese!

Used 16 oz tub of cottage cheese and a 5oz tub of spinach. We like our pasta saucy. 

Not sure if it’s because I used whole wheat pasta, but the sauce didn’t really seem to stick to the noodles. Loved the flavor and texture if the sauce though, 

Next time I make it, I’ll use a penne or other tube shape to get more sauce per bite.
Yum! Made as written and topped with grilled chicken. Easy 30 minute meal. It was a hit with the whole fam. Will definitely make again.
Just Delicious and super easy! Will be my new go-to for an easy, healthy and yummy pasta sauce!

I’m a fan of cottage cheese because of the protein,but this recipe was a big bore. I prefer Eric Kim’s Spinach Egg Bites if we’re combining cottage cheese and spinach. Otherwise I’ll forget trying to get my protein intake in a pasta recipe.

What a great recipe.
April is the end of the winter growing season here in Miami and our veggie garden is full of all kinds of greens that are starting to go to seed. So besides snipping the basil and spinach the recipe calls for, I also grabbed a bunch of kale, mustard greens, Swiss chard, and arugula. 
Because they were weeks past baby tender, I removed the thicker center spines and blanched the leaves quickly in the pasta water before throwing them into the food processor.
The dish was fresh, floral, and very delicious. I even have a nice container of sauce left that I’ll use for something else tomorrow.
Thank you for a great suggestion. We’ll be making this again soon.
Added pine nuts…yum!
Tasted great! I didn’t have fresh spinach so I used frozen, and I didn’t have fresh Parmesan so I used some packets from pizza delivery, and it tasted great anyway.
Who knew that grating cheese in the food processor with the blade was so easy?! It was good but will definitely add less lemon next time.  Like half.
Halved it and made it as is with Thai basil since that’s what my farmers market had. I added some red pepper flakes and it’s so good.
Made this exactly as written and it was delicious.  It produced enough for 4 generous servings.  I used my immersion blender and it took 5 minutes to reach the correct consistency.  Will make again.

I enjoyed preparing this dish (much prefer weighing ingredients for accuracy).  But my immediate reaction was disappointment in how lemony this was, as in much too much.  I'll admit that the lemon I had on hand was large and very juicy.  But if everything else in the ingredients list here is measured, why not the lemon as well?  Will try again, but will really dial the lemon back next time.  P.S. Used whole wheat pasta, which worked fine.

Made this last night exactly by the recipe - including weighing all ingredients -  and did not love it.  It was too much basil for my taste.  Also needed more acid.   I threw some fresh cherry tomatoes in while eating, and that helped a bit, but still wasn't great.  Tasting for salt is a good tip - I felt like it needed a good bit of salt added.

So good! Wouldn't change anything except add lemon to your taste. Half a lemon plus zest from one full lemon was the perfect amount without being overwhelming. Also, I like my pastas with more sauce, so I'd use this much sauce for half a pound of pasta.
Great recipe!  Being unable to be faithful to any recipie, I did add double the olive oil and a bit more (1/3) of the greens, cottage cheese, and parm, plus 1/2 cup cream cheese.  This is  keeper.

Hello,  I was really hoping to love this but it was a bit to vividly herbaceous for me.  It was likely user error - I got regular spinach rather than baby spinanch by mistake and just eyeballed the measurements which likely caused me to add too much of the green.  My lemon was also a bit long in the tooth.  Am posting so you can avoid my errors.  I think I will give it a second try though, maybe.

Was so excited about this recipe. Everything seemed to come out great. I added the pasta water to the creamy sauce, and tossed it with the pasta, all was well. It was beautiful in fact. Great recipe. I recommend freezing the tofu ahead of time and then thawing (and wringing it out like a sponge) which helps give it a better texture.
@Excellent - do you drain the liquid from around the tofu before you freeze it?
@Betsy nope! Just throw the whole container in the freezer
@Excellent another method would be to tear the tofu into chunks and boil it for 5 minutes in salted water.

This comment applies to ALL of your recipes featuring tofu: The best way to drain tofu is using the container it comes in, turned upside down on a plate. It has grooves/ridges that help the water move downward toward the plate. Place the slab of tofu on top, covered with a piece of wax paper or facsimile. Place a plate with weight on top of that. Wait for the weight to work its magic - about an hour or more. Perfect. No towel necessary. The slab can be easily sliced into perfect cubes.

I just want to say that if you use “super firm” tofu, which is AKA “high protein” tofu (14g vs 9g per serving), all this fussy pressing, drying, freezing that folks recommend is not at all necessary. I go way back with cooking tofu, and there is no need to do all this.

@JenScan I concur. Our local supermarket just started carrying super firm tofu, which still has some liquid, but it amounts to less than three tablespoons. It also crisps really nicely in the pan, or in the oven to roast. Forget extra firm.

@JenScan Agree. Also, trust the recipe if it doesn’t say you need to press. Make it without pressing it or freezing it and see how it is. I am an inconsistent presser and find the difference negligible, even with extra firm.

I did half in the air fryer, half in the oven. Air fryer for the win! They just didn’t crisp up in the oven.  Subbed rice flour for the AP flour. How long did you cook them for in the air fryer?

@Kay I did the air fryer at 400 for 25 minutes which was perfect for the bigger pieces but too much for the ones smaller than an inch. I also added a little corn meal in the coating and that was great too.
@Kay thanks for the info! I came to the comments to see if anyone had tried these in the air fryer. :)

Ehhhh… these still have that raw tofu taste. I followed the recipe exactly except I added a dash of corn starch. I am vegan so no stranger to tofu but I was hoping for more flavor. I think I’ll try this again but “marinade” the tofu in the hot sauce or something else. They were nice and crispy though.

Added 1 1/2 tsp. Old Bay and used cornstarch instead of flour. Also used 1 tbs. oil, 1 tbs. chile crisp and 1 tbs. of a spicy lime mayo sauce I had to make the cornstarch adhere. This is a great recipe, super easy, flexible and fast to throw together. We squeezed lemon juice on top (the best) and also opened up whatever fast food sauce packets we had in the junk drawer. Delicious! Thank you, Ali.
Extra hot sauce and old bay did the trick. Partner loved it alongside rice, broccoli and mushrooms. Nothing left!
I have a tofu press which works beautifully in the recipe. Such an amazing recipe! Thanks again NYT Cooking for another winner!

Thank you, Rich for the suggestion to sub ranch dressing. I first made it with half the Frank's Red Hot (and added an extra tablespoon of oil), but the flavor was still too strong for my kids, even though it wasn't that spicy. They definitely like the ranch version better.

It was with this recipe that I discovered that Ali Slagle's all about recipes that aren't too time-consuming or complicated. Turns out I have made other recipes of hers, and they have been great! Keep 'em coming!
Made as written. A nice easy recipe that results in a perfect blend of complimentary addictive flavors. No need for improvements.
Can these be cooked in an air fryer?

These are very tasty. I try to always make a recipe exactly as written the first time, but I didn't have Frank's on-hand, so I used Tapatio instead. It was delicious. I will be making this recipe *a lot*.

Surprisingly good and quick. I used about 1/3 of the hot sauce and wheat flour. I didn't bother pressing the tofu. That seemed like an unnecessary step and they turned out almost a little dry. Might be a tad much on the garlic
Too spicy
roasted crispy tofu!
What can I use in place of the hot sauce for extremely spice averse toddlers? All of the subs listed in the comments would still be too spicy I think
So easy and delicious. We paired it with a malt vinegar based slaw. Celery seed is indeed key for that part. Will cook this one again for sure.
Delicious! A unique take on tofu that easily comes together.

Public service announcement: Old Bay is predominantly celery salt, so if you don't like celery salt, might be a good idea to swap out that particular seasoning for something else.  The tofu preparation is great (binder, seasoned flour, baking instructions).  I'll be using it often.
It was dry and somewhat bland. Not sure if this tofu needed marinating…
@shaunaleee - I couldn't tell the difference at all!

Hmmm, my tofu did not turn out crispy at all.  I did follow the instructions as I like to do when trying out a recipe for the first time.  It could use more flavor, maybe a dash of soy sauce in the mixture.
Thank you for not telling us to press the tofu first! An often repeated but often necessary instruction.

Following other suggestions I used half flour half cornmeal, increased the Old Bay to 1.5 teaspoons, and used a combo of hot sauces (one homemade, one maple habanero acquired in Canada). My pieces were somewhat smaller than 1 inch so a few seemed a bit dried out with 30 minutes cook time; will adjust sizes or timing next time I make it. We lived in Maryland for a few years in the 80s and I have a recipe in the 50th anniversary cookbook of 1989, so I have a soft spot in my heart for Old Bay.

I can second prior comments that this is truly delicious.  One of the best tofu recipes I have tried, honestly. A nice change from stir fries, curries, etc.  I have made this with extra firm and super firm tofu, and super firm is the way to go.  The nuggets or "sticks" hold their shapes better.  Turned out crispy and very tasty. Served with simple homemade tartar sauce and lemon wedges. Two big thumbs up!

I thought as written this didn't work -- the hot sauce was overpowering, and I couldn't taste the Old Bay or any other flavors. I used Cholula; maybe that was an issue. It also didn't get crispy in my oven. Would reduce the hot sauce and try the air fryer if I made it again. I do agree that the leftovers out of the fridge were weirdly addictive.

I did not have Franks so I used three tablespoons of Earth Balance stick butter, melted, with two teaspoons of gochujang paste mixed in as the binder (had a little leftover). I also used high protein tofu. Anyway, it was delicious.
So delicious! I’ll be experimenting with this method to make other flavor combinations of tofu nuggets.

Made once as written and liked a lot. Made again using potato starch (same amount) instead of flour and baking at 375 convection mode on the middle rack and loved them!

So nice added to a salad or cut up cold and put into a chicken salad style situation the next day. 

Looking forward to trying the cornmeal recommendation.
These are good but need 2x the old bay and garlic powders. I also threw in some nutritional yeast for good measure. Bakes well in air fryer.
These are supposed to be crispy. They did not come out crispy.

I forgot to "tear" the tofu instead of just cutting it. It was fine. Not evenly coated though.  didn't have any red hot sauce so used some other habanero sauce that we had on hand, doubled the spice amount.  I cooked it for a total of about 26 minutes to make it just a tad crispier.

I love most Ali Slagle recipes, but this one was a miss for me. Not terrible, but not great. There are better tofu recipes in the NY Times Cooking section, including Ali's wonderful Lemon-Pepper Tofu and Snap Peas recipe.

Loved this recipe! But two words of advice: potato starch. It really does the trick, getting the torn pieces perfectly crispy and not gluey. Also, advice to put on the bottom rack is spot on. 30 mins at 425 in gas oven was perfect.
Do these freeze well?
We loved these little nuggets. Made the NYT tartar sauce recipe to dip. Fantastic combination.

Ah been making these for years for breakfast.. much easier wisk eggs in small bowl. Add cheese and mix.  Melt butter in frying pan. Place tortilla in buttered pan and top with egg mix (and perhaps some canned refried beans as extra). Place second tortilla on top. When bottom tortilla starts to brown flip and cook other side to golden flip on plate and cut in half and serve hot plain or top with salsa, cilantro, fresh diced tomato, sour cream, chopped green onions, etc.
@Geri.  Thank you!
If you don't want to grab the tortilla from the skillet, you can slide it onto a plate, put a second plate over that, flip and then slide it back into the skillet. Or just use a spatula!
This has been a breakfast staple of my family for years and we call them armadillos.
@Los armadillos.  Love the name!
Whole wheat tortillas and olive oil.  Aged sharp cheddar.
@John MD That’s a quesadilla only a bolillo would eat.

Actually, one can and should omit the "dexterity required" flipping technique.  When the cook "presses the tortilla to adhere", it sticks to the cheese and egg.  Use a rigid metal restaurant-type 5" long spatula and you can easilly flip like a pancake.  Then on to Step 4. as described. Easier.  Safer. And even the dexterous challenged among us can do it successfully.
@Andy Hess a metal spatula is not too kind to non-stick pans. But other than that, you are spot on!

Whisking the eggs in a bowl, adding some cilantro, cumin powder, and paprika will take it to a new level. Instead of holding the tortilla by hand, simply cover the skillet with a plate, flip it, and then slide the tortilla back into the skillet.

I used the technique as written (normal sized tortilla, not big burrito size), and it was super easy and not tricky or dangerous in the least. Heed the directions to use a pan that fits your tortilla snugly, and you’ll be fine! I added fresh jalapeños, cilantro, and chopped green scallions in the filling and served with avocado and Peri Peri sauce for dipping. Easy and delish!

This is one of my favorite breakfasts.  One small addition that I cannot resist is some crumbled Mexican style chorizo sprinkled over the egg and cheese before folding.  I also like to put a bit of a smokey char on the tortilla by toasting it for a few seconds on over the gas burner.  Finally a big spoonful of pico de gallo goes on top of the finished quesadilla.

Made it as written except used cooking spray instead of butter and a low carb tortilla (I need the fiber, sorry!) Turned out perfectly and I will be making this regularly for a quick lunch. I can see myself adding a squirt of kewpie mayo or whatever veggies are currently going bad in my fridge in the future, it’s very riffable.

There are lots of ways to make this. You don't have to engage in the act of grabbing a hot pan and turning its contents upside down. Just cook the egg first then put on a plate. Lay the tortilla in the hot pan and sprinkle with cheese. Replace egg back on top, give it a minute or two to brown the tortilla, fold, and you're done. This is not complicated cooking here.
I make this Chinese-ish, sort of a breakfast bau. Add sliced scallions to the egg. Instead of cheese, I put a smear of hoisin sauce and maybe a drizzle of chili crisp. Delicious!

I don’t add any fats to the pan for this or other grilled sandwiches because I like the toasted surface to be dry and crispy (I love butter and oils, just not in this instance) - it still works well - each to their own!

Geez, I'm 80 and have been making this since high school. Born and live in CA, so....I usually scramble the eggs in a separate pan and I almost always add chopped scallions, sometimes canned diced chilis. Butter the down side of a tortilla  and place on hot flat skillet. Put cheese on half, dump the eggs on,  (diced avocado !) add more cheese, fold the tortilla in half, flip to the cheese side for a minute to melt. Cut in half and serve w/ salsa or not. A squiggle of sriracha is fast instead.

When I make this, I use only 14 grams of cheese to one egg. I always use corn tortillas rather than flour tortillas. Always tasty but even better if I also add some sort of vegetable, anything from lettuce to broccoli or eggplant.

My granddaughter love these, which she calls 'tias. I usually add a slice of deli ham or turkey before the cheese. When I flip it over, instead of folding it I let it cool briefly on a cutting board before slicing it into small squares for her.
I'm going to try this with 2 halves of a pita.
This is called and “egg-adilla” in my house and I top it with avocado and pico. I use small tortillas (like the mini Trader Joe’s ones) and a single egg and it is very easy to flip with a spatula.

It's interesting that so many NYTC recipes call for non-stick pans when most people I know don't own them anymore.  Many people are opting out of coated pans. I used a high quality stainless pan for this and it worked perfectly.
Old cast iron flat skillet, well seasoned, works really well. If making for others, I use my cast iron pancake griddle, fits across 2 burners.
This recipe is a go-to when I need something quick. I add some Sriracha on the eggs before adding the cheese. If I want to be fancy, I add sliced avocado too.
Subbing the tortillas for matza as Passover comes to a close!
Staying at my boyfriend’s house and the only ingredients in his man fridge are quesadillas, eggs, and Mexican cheese. NYT saved me today by making this the featured recipe :) yummy and easy
Fun for a change! Added spinach.

Very easy and good. 
I used two slices of cheddar rather than grating.
For a little extra flavor I spread a thin layer of sun dried tomato pesto on the tortilla before placing it on the eggs.

Very good breakfast! I made a few changes: Add a few grinds of white pepper to the eggs along with the salt, and about 1 tablespoon of snipped chives. As others have suggested, I placed a small plate face-down on the eggs in the pan and (using pot-holders), flipped the eggs face-down onto the plate, then slid the eggs (tortilla-side down) back into the pan to cook. I'll make this often.

I make something (it may even be a NYT recipe, though I'm not quickly finding it here) with similar effect but I'd say easier technique—start by toasting a flour tortilla in a hot pan on just one side. Then basically follow this recipe, (toasted side up) but no need to cook after flipping, you can just flip it onto a plate. I do end up heating two pans (cast iron for the tortilla toasting, nonstick for the eggs), but only one gets dirty.

Made as written except used sliced American cheese and found it too cheesy. Prefer the Cooking section's Scallion Egg Wrap, which I know is a whole different direction but in case you haven't tried it, look it up! Otherwise, this was fine for breakfast though half was enough.

I just soft scramble an egg and put it on one side of a tortilla (which is in my counter).  Sprinkle on cheese, roll it up burrito style or fold in half quesadilla style, and return to pan to crisp on all sides in some additional melted butter.
We used to call these eggsadillas-quick breakfast for the boys when they were young.
Breakfast burrito in quesadilla form.
Like a breakfast burrito, just not folded the same. Could add breakfast meats or veggies to your liking.

I recently discovered that a key ingredient for amazing quesadillas is queso Oaxaca (Oaxacan cheese).  If you have a Mexican grocery store nearby, I suggest you give it a try.  It may be behind the counter rather than with the prepackaged cheeses.
Like an omelette in a tortilla. I added chopped garlic chives w the cheese and topped it with avocado chunks and chopped tomato at the table.
It's also a 2am favorite! basic recipe that other ingredients can be added.
Been making these for years! My daughters know them as "Eggy Cheesy Tortillas". The perfect lunch!
Just what I needed this morning
I am totally confused by your directions. Step two refers to a skillet and a pan. I do not understand which is which or are they the same. Please elaborate

Really easy, even the hot pan /hand flip!  It took me about 15 minutes of cooking to make 8 of them. 
I did add mayo and scallions , but I could say that about any recipe,,,
Quesadilla are super easy to make, but I found the build process for this recipe to be fun and the results were an easy to work with and eat egg cheese sandwich.
I call these "eggadillas".  Delicious!

When our daughter was an infant our babysitter would make this with egg, cheese and refried black beans, with corn tortillas.  Thole family ate them up! This reminds me of those quesadillas and for certain, add black beans or refried black beans, or really any other bean, smashed if you prefer, and then its more protein!

As someone whose breakfast diet is heavily dependent on egg & tortilla combinations, I am grateful to Ali Slagle for developing this ingenious technique. It is nearly impossible to keep the eggs from falling out of the tortilla any other way! (the eggs do kind of get loosey-goosey during the last bite, but stay in place much much better). Also, the proportions are good. Make sure you cook the eggs enough before you put the cheese & tortilla on, or the trick won't work).
Made this last night. Easy to do, flip and all!! Served with black beans and Hatch green chilis and a dolop of sour cream. Yum and satisfying!!

This works great with a single egg + smaller "soft taco" tortillas, too! 

In my large pan, I just eyeball the size of the tortilla to size my egg pour, then after adding the tortilla, I gently press the egg towards the edge of the tortilla so it's fully covered. This is also much easier to flip/handle for me. 

So far my favorite inclusion is a few frozen seasoned curly fries, cut small with my kitchen shears after a minute or so in the microwave to soften. I also like adding in hot sauce (Cholula!) for some extra flavor. 

Great with a side of grapes for a quick lunch.

title: Gochujang Tofu, Squash and Brussels Sprouts Bowls Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: tasted the sauce as-written and it was a bit too acidic for my liking. I added a bit of soy sauce and toasted sesame oil to take it closer to a traditional bimbimbap sauce (referencing a recipe from Meera Sodha's East cookbook) and that helped.

I really liked this. The dressing was the perfect sweet-sour combo. I used 4 tablespoons of gochujang and a teaspoon of red chili flakes, and it was not too spicy. I didn’t skimp on the salt either. The veggies and tofu were nice and browned after roasting. Served over short grain brown rice and included sesame seeds and cilantro. Yum!
The dressing was really delicious. Next time I would use less vinegar and maybe add some sugar to thicken it.

My husband and I loved how this recipe turned out. Served with pearl couscous. I used a mandoline to get the apple and shallot sliced very thin - having both raw adds a wonderful texture variation. I thought we had ACV in the pantry, but we did not, so I ended up just using white wine vinegar and didn't have any complaints. Also skipped the gochugaru (sorry!). I had a butternut squash on the larger side, and ended up with enough for 6 decently sized servings. Great ingredient combo!
pretty good, but I wish I had used a little less vinegar. it was the most prominent flavor and I am a vinegar lover. grated the apple instead of slicing. I used sweet potatoes instead of squash.

Had this last night and then again for lunch as leftovers. I think it was a little better last night but maybe because the flavors were new and interesting. 

I used about 3 T gochujang and 2 t crushed red pepper. It was not spicy, so if you want spice, you might try turning it up a notch. I also only used 1/2 of a green apple in the sauce and that felt like enough. Served it over farro. I don't know that this would go into a regular rotation but maybe a once a year kind of recipe?

This turned out really well, save for the sauce which we also found to be overly vinegary. Next time will reduce the vinegar and add sesame oil/soy sauce as suggested by other commenters. Doubled the recipe to feed family of 6 and ended up with a ton of leftovers. I also added Italian sausage, taken out of the casing and broken into pieces, on the baking sheet at the same time as the Brussels sprouts, it cooked fantastically. Ground chicken might also be great if you want a little meat in this.

I cooked 2 cups of pearled barley, only added one tablespoon gochujang and skipped the gochugaru.

The dish was OK but the flavor combination was a little bewildering. Not sure exactly how things are supposed to work together. I can imagine taking the sauce in a different direction and with the same vegetables could be a winning dish. 

Tofu, brussel sprouts and squash worked well together. Maybe a different fruit or dressing would brighten the dish or complement the heat of the gochujang better.
A little too vinegary for me, and I love vinegar. Maybe a little soy and sesame and a small amount of sugar will help.
The dressing was alright - after halving the vinegar and adding a little sugar - it just didn’t pair well with brussel sprouts and squash and apples. I’d skip the dressing or make your own
a great base of a recipe but after reading the comments i put extra gochujang in the sauce, added maple syrup and toasted sesame sauce and did a really saucy bowl and it was delicious !

So delicious--my brussels overcooked slightly, perhaps because I put on convection, but otherwise perfect! I used seitan, as I discovered at the last moment I didn't have tofu, and it was great although again perhaps needs a bit less time. Endorse the addition of some sesame oil for extra umami and aroma, and I used persimmon vinegar. Served with my favorite organic short-grain brown rice from Massa Organics.
Just made this and overall good though I agree that sauce is too acidic   Next time would add less vinegar.   Adding soy sauce as suggested in earlier comment really helped balance out the flavors :)
Pretty average recipe.   The dressing needed a lot of work to make it taste good.    As noted by others the dressing was too acidic with a pretty one dimensional flavor profile.

Is there a reasonable substitute for gochujang? I don't want to keep so many ready-made sauces in the fridge, as I don't use them often. I'd really like to try this with crushed red pepper as a sub for the gochugaru.
We ended up eating roasted veggies and tofu which was perfect. Before tossing them into marinade I tasted and decided not to mix them. Too much vinegar.
100% agree, these flavors were not gelling well. Might have been decent with just a dash of vinegar but it was so overpowering it completely killed it.

Made this as written and will not make again. Way too much vinegar. We were disappointed, as we are always excited to see (and game to try) a one-pan vegetation sheet dinner with protein. Not worth the fuss for this one.

delicious! i disagree with others who said that the vegetables don’t go with the dressing. i did add a tablespoon of maple syrup to the dressing, thx to comments that it was a little too acidic. but i would put this in the regular rotation for sure.

I have to respectfully disagree with most of the comments I've seen here. We loved this dish and didn't have any issues with the combination or the dressing, in fact, I wished I'd made more! The apple and onion added lovely freshness and the brown rice soaked up all the tangy residue. We didn't use gochugaru as we didn't have it. I also used a buttercup squash as it's what I had but defo think butternut would be better texturally. Be sure to add the sesame seeds if you have them. Very nice addition.

Very tasty! I used generous tablespoons of gochujang, not accounting for the gochugaru so it definitely had some heat but that didn’t stop us from readily spooning this in. I did sweeten the sauce a bit for our taste. Also, I air fried our tofu as I have a personal preference for crispy, chewy tofu cubes, It was an extra step worth doing. Served over some leftover farro so this was a perfect dish to use up odds and ends in the fridge.

LOVED the dressing. So simple and good - I plan to use it for salads. This was a great dish! The tofu won't get crispy as written, so keep that in mind if softer tofu bothers you. I think next time I'll use sweet potatoes instead of butternut squash, but that's personal preference for the overall flavor profile.
@CL I used sweet potato instead of butternut squash and it was excellent!
Easy, interesting, great payoff for effort. Use the full 4 tablespoons gochujang!
Do the squash and tofu stay in the oven with the Brussels sprouts? Or do they just roast 10 minutes on their own and come out?
Great flavor!   The tofu was really spongy tho.   I would omit the tofu next time and add other veg.

I love vinegar and agree with others that this sauce was a little too vinegary. Added some honey to temper it which helped but it needed less vinegar to start. Otherwise, a straightforward and satisfying tofu bowl meal
Followed 100%. Really yummy! Fingers crossed leftovers are delish too.
Took a pointer from other commenters and halved the vinegar. Perfect balance! Will definitely make again, perhaps with other proteins to switch things up.

Mouth-puckeringly tart! But also the description for how to cut the tofu seemed unnecessarily specific yet unclear: “3/4 inch cubes” would work. I love gochujang and will be giving this another go, but will halve the vinegar and pre-season the tofu.

I chose this to use up some winter vegetables I still had on hand. I loved it! I wouldn't recognize it as bimbimbap, which for me is all about the egg--and it's possible that actually egg would make a nice addition. What I liked was the combination of textures and bold, contrasting flavors. Contrary to one commenter, I did not find the dressing too acidic at all. I wish I had checked on the vegetables after 20 minutes, since at 25 the sprouts were quite brown, though not burnt. Glad I tried it.
Unlike some comments I’m seeing, I love the tart taste of the apple cider vinegar! I add a splash of soy sauce and I crumble the tofu instead of cutting it to help it get crispier.

I hate to be that guy, but as written, this isn't a vinaigrette (and thus tastes too vinegary) because there's no oil in it. You need a bit of fat to cut the acid and help emulsify the dressing! 

Similar to Mehak, I added a drizzle of fish sauce and a few teaspoons of sesame oil. This made it feel much more balanced!

title: Sheet-Pan Chicken With Black Beans and Squash Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: New family favourite! My two-year-old loved this so much that in her bedtime prayers she said, “Thank you, God, for the new dinner.”
@Ashley Best review ever!
@Ashley this is adorable! I think she’s a girl after my own heart.

Another great Ali Slagle sheet pan dinner that'll be going into the rotation at our house! I subbed in sweet potatoes for the squash because I find them easier to work with and my family likes them better anyway. For toppings we had cilantro, lime, red onion, and feta. I doubled the recipe for our family of 4 (2 adults, 2 elementary-aged kids) hoping for leftovers. But everyone liked it and we only had one thigh leftover, so I think we would have been hungry if I hadn't.

@Kate C. I took your suggestion and used sweet potatoes. Also prepped veggies early and tossed in large bowl with spices. Spice wimp here so only one 1t. of chili powder. Added chicken after an hour. Delicious!

I was attracted to the combination of butternut squash and black beans, and it was definitely a winner.  We used skinless-boneless thighs, and marinated the chicken and the vegetables in the seasoned oil for about an hour in advance before roasting. Although the tip for using boneless thighs suggested starting the vegetables first, I did everything together and both the chicken and the vegetables were perfectly done in just under 30 minutes. We garnished with cilantro, sour cream and pumpkin seeds. My one recommendation is to lightly toast the pumpkin seeds—it added a delicious crunch. Overall, a great dish that we will make again.

Used smoked paprika, pinch of cayenne, and a little extra cumin instead of chili powder. Also, mixed all the ingredients in a large bowl before spreading onto sheet pan, ensuring that the chicken was well coated with oil and spice. Absolutely delicious, and so easy.

NO comments yet? Made this last night, pretty easy, very good, was a hit (used less chili powder, we don't do spicy well). Curious what others have done to adjust the spice mix, but it was a hit as written (didn't need that much raw red onion at the end...)

@Jack from Joisey For those that have issue with nightshades (chili powder comes from nightshades) try subbing Achiote aka Annato. It’s used in many regions of Mexico as a substitute and compliment to dried chili powder. Been using it for years in this capacity when we discovered in our household that nightshades upset and bloated our digestive system.
@Jeff in HMB I am allergic to all nightshades and I love your suggestion! Always looking for new spices to substitute for nightshades. Thank you!

@Jeff in HMB
I did not know that this spice came from the deadly nightshade. Thank you, I always love learning new things

Spectacular! Took 5 mins to  make on a busy weeknight and was absolutely delicious!
I used skinless boneless chicken breast and cut them in 1/3's. No onion at the end.
Served on whole grain farro
This is a winner!
@JennieTXS. Like the farro idea, will def try!
Like the marmalade one, this is excellent. I used curry powder instead of chile powder gave it more of an India taste.  So easy loved it.
@Nicole The marmalade one? Going to have to look for that!

This was surprisingly delicious for how simple it was! Only downside is I used frozen butternut squash and it got really mushy. Flavors were so tasty. I would not use bone in next time — more trouble than it’s worth IMO. Also I added pomegranate seeds because I had some and thought it was a great addition.

Excellent return for the amount of efforts.  I used chickpeas, because that's what I had, and spices were more of a morrocan mix;, cooked the whole sliced onion, had boneless skinless chicken thighs only. No lime nor fresh avocado on hand. Did not matter, dropped dollop of guacamole and showered w cilantro. Delish. Side of steamed kale. Great leftovers as well.
Very good! Super fast and easy especially if you use pre-cut squash. And very high flavor to effort ratio. Served with cilantro, lime, avocado, and crema.

Delicious, easy, fast, and healthy! This is immediately on rotation at our house. I added smoked paprika, which pairs really nicely with the cumin (season your chicken thighs with it!). And I used frozen cubed butternut squash, thawed for a few minutes in the microwave. It's almost too easy! It's a full meal, but benefits from a side like the lemon-garlic kale salad: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1015707-lemon-garlic-kale-salad

Made this with boneless, skinless chicken breasts. I tossed everything into a large bowl and added the ingredients that way to ensure the chicken breasts were adequately coated with the oil.  I used frozen cubed butternut squash from the freezer section at the grocery and if you do this, I highly recommend opening the bag and dumping into a collander to allow to thaw at least slightly and drain any potential moisture before adding to the dish.  Super easy prep and tasty meal and leftovers!
@KC thank you - could only find frozen squash!
Fantastic flavor! And really easy to make. Will be going into the easy weeknight dinner rotation!

This was delicious.  I tweaked a bit based on what I had on hand. I added Tajin, 1 minced garlic clove and smoked paprika in addition to listed spices. I used 2 boneless chicken breasts, each cut into 3 tenders. I mixed all ingredients in a bowl to marinate while my (horribly slow) oven preheated.  Baked at close to 425 for 20 minutes. Topped with cilantro, crumpled feta, sliced red onion, tzatziki, and squeeze of lemon juice.
@Jill B that sounds delicious with the feta and tzatziki!

This was a delicious, easy meal that transforms chicken and veggies into something new and exciting. Based on what I had, I used boneless skin-on chicken breast and sweet potatoes.
