I have had that problem with leaky spinach and they fell apart so I used a bit more binding the next time in the meat. Also squeezing the spinach to death of course. Make the spinach mixture w a bit more cheese.

@Spinach and meatball connect I place thawed spinach in metal colander and press hard with a large bowl. Then wrap in paper towels and dishtowel and roll to the point I sometimes have to pick apart as it’s so dry.

Reviewers are complaining about the meatballs falling apart. I'm not surprised at all. Skip the liquid. I was dumbfounded when I read that a 1/2 c of spinach water is add to just one pound of (fatty) meat. Holy mushy meatballs!! Yuck. Also, cut the panko to 1/2 c. A full pkg of frozen spinach + 3/4 c bread crumbs = too much filler.
any thoughts on using ground turkey or chicken here?
@Michelle Great idea!  I have come to prefer turkey in meatloaf and meatballs.  Sometimes on its own, sometimes in a mixture with other ground meat.
@Michelle I was thinking the same thing, but worry they will be too dry. Please post back if you try it and let me know how they turned out!
That was my first thought! My husband is supposed to avoid red meat, so I'll use a pound of ground chicken along with the pound of ground pork and halve the exposure.
Why hasn't someone invented a frozen spinach that does NOT have to be squeezed???
@Max is this a serious question? Frozen spinach is minimally processed and highly nutritious.

Not sure how the nutrition reading for this one ended up with nearly your entire day's supply of sodium in one serving... looking at the ingredients it shouldn't be nearly that bad. I've come to believe that the NYT nutrition estimates are way off, unfortunately. Anyway, this should be easy to make as low-sodium, just look for canned tomatoes with no salt added, because the rest of the ingredients look pretty sodium-friendly.
@trinkd i’ve found that many Italian brands of canned tomatoes, tomato sauce and tomato paste contain very little sodium.
Any reason not to bake the meatballs? I am partial to a firm meatball that keeps its shape in the sauce.

@Deb I have not made this particular recipe yet, but I’ve made very similar dishes where I baked the meatballs instead of frying as called for in the recipe, and it’s worked well if not better. It may mean dirtying a baking sheet, but it results in a firmer, better formed meatball overall. In the case of this recipe, I would bake them at 350° for about 30 minutes (slightly under baked, in the case of my oven) then add them when making the sauce, just like the recipe advises, along with whatever drippings or fond has rendered off on the baking sheet. They will finish cooking and add their flavor to the sauce without falling apart.

@Deb when you cook the meatballs in the pot, some of the meat’s drippings wind up being the base for the sauce… yum! That doesn’t happen when you bake the meatballs. (It’s definitely a personal preference thing, so you do you! But I like keeping the fat in the sauce. And thought I’d share since you asked.)

Not sure why there isn't a separate recipe for making the sauce.  A can of crushed tomatoes with parmesan, while OK, should be jazzed up with the traditional ingredients (IMO). Regardless, will try the meatballs in this manner.  Assuming substituting ground turkey will work as well?  (Yes, I understand that it's not a traditional Italian meatball!)

I put them in a 400 ‘ oven first for about 10 minutes to set them up-when they come out, I pour the juices/fat into the pan then sauté them in the pan to crisp them and to render more fat. They never fall apart!
@Minette Love this idea!

I keep some cheesecloth available to "wring out" cooked spinach - fresh or frozen.  If the recipe calls for liquid, I will supplement with the water that was strained out to capture any benefits that might be lost in the process!
Do you think the recipe would work if I used "Impossible" sausage and meat in place of the beef and sausage?
I very much appreciate calling out Diamond Crystal vs. coarse Kosher salt. It makes a difference and so few people specify.

Disappointing. I usually love Ali Slagle’s recipes and have made many of the NYT meatballs recipes but this one didn’t work for me. The meatballs formed together well while shaping but they disintegrated while frying in the pot. I see many comments saying the same. I’m guessing it’s because of the water in spinach (although thoroughly squeezed out) coming out when heated? I don’t know how this passed the recipe testing.
I used to make this over 40 years ago to get more veggies into my kids. It was one of their favourite dinners. Used all beef then; but I like the idea of adding ground pork too.
Instead of going through all this trouble, I just add spinach (fresh or frozen) directly to the sauce. I usually just use frozen meatballs.

I do this as well, but I go a step further: I don't bother with meatballs. Just brown the crumbled meat and throw it in the sauce with the spinach. Then you don't need the panko at all, and if you use pasta made from chickpeas, you have a super-fast, low-carb, high-protein meal. You can sub seasoned, pressed tufu crumbles for some or all of the meat if you like. Cuts the cost way down considering the current cost of beef.
The best binder is a bit of coconut flour. It's sticky - holds meatballs, meatloaf and so on. . . together really well. Just mix in a Tablespoon or so. You won't notice a change in flavor.

I love spinach and often add some to meatballs and meatloaf. When thrift is an issue, frozen is better, and for the same reason, I buy inexpensive "Wonder" type bread, which I wouldn't eat as bread, but makes perfectly acceptable breadcrumbs, and is much cheaper than panko. Onion is a big plus. If you need more bulk to hold the meatballs together onion powder is preferable.
I like to add pulverized mushrooms, too, but that isn't particularly thrifty.

Could anyone advise on why panko, specifically, is called for here? I make my own breadcrumbs as part of my bread-baking routine. Buying breadcrumbs usually seems wasteful and unnecessary, but I realize that a recipe occasionally depends on a specific texture. Has anyone who's used whatever breadcrumbs they've had lying around comment on how that worked? (And NYT: could you stop specifying panko when it's not really necessary, please?)
@Su panko is just crustless bread made into crumbs and I make my own (many packaged ones have junk in them) - there are recipes out there but I doubt you need one. That pioneer woman has one.

Of course use your own bread crumbs - it's a recipe not the law. If you read the NYT comments you will find a plethora of commentary with so many substitutions it doesn't even resemble the original recipe. Go for it!!
The trick is to NOT flip the meatballs while they simmer. Just leave them be and they will hold together.
I’m considering baking the meatballs instead. Has anyone tried this with success?
@Laura please read the intro to the recipe please

Winner with Ground Turkey Breast.  Lite and Lower Fat.   Also baked meatballs in a FAN ASSIST Oven for 30 Minutes.
Stuffed the meatballs with fresh mozzarella pearls. Yummy

My husband is a gourmet foodie and he, along with the rest of my family thought this one of the best meatball dishes EVER! I carelessly didn’t read the directions correctly and dumped all 6 cloves chopped garlic into the meatballs….oh well! I just added 4 more to the crushed tomatoes. Also used fresh basil and parsley and rigatoni. This recipe is a KEEPER! Thank you!
My husband says there is no such thing as too much garlic. If the recipe says 2 cloves, he will double (or sometimes triple) the garlic.

I read all the notes and comments before starting this recipe. Here are my comments: first, my husband wanted to know when the rugby team was coming. 2-4 servings? More like 6-8 or even 8-10! 15 minutes prep? Not likely!  My 12 oz package of frozen spinach turned into 5 oz once thawed and squeezed, but it was plenty for the meatballs. I had no problem with using panko - no broken meatballs. I let the meat balls rest as suggested in the frig for 10 min. Then I used the oven method including broiling.  They stayed nicely shaped and firm, even after simmering in the sauce for ~20 min. After tasting it, we added red pepper flakes.

I had fresh spinach in the house, so while I read the comments about frozen being more nutritious and moist, I went with fresh, used more chopped garlic, probably 8 cloves and a "meatloaf" mix of ground beef/pork/veal.  We did them on the stovetop, gradually browning each side.  I use two forks to turn them.  Tomato sauce doesn't sit well with my husband, so since we were using the spinach, I served  with pesto.  Very tasty and ours didn't fall apart (only used the 1 egg and panko as suggested).
Wondering if these can be done with ground chicken instead? Or if there is a reason that wasn't suggested as an option.
Has anyone tried baking the meatballs in the oven?

@Shelagh I have not tried it with this recipe, but I regularly make a similar recipe that bakes the meatballs on a cookie rack over cookie tray (so they do it just boil in the juices) and it’s delicious. If I make these that’s how I’ll do it.  I think it’s 20 min at 350. No need to turn. 
I often substitute pork sausage for ground pork, to up the seasoning, and add onions and peppers to the sauce.

This recipe is absolutely delicious and will become a favorite in my house. I made minor changes with the seasoning, but everything remained the same. I added dried oregano, basil, and crushed red pepper to the sauce and it was perfect.

My meatballs were delicious and firm; you must really squeeze out all the water from the spinach to ensure a firm meatball. However, the sauce was 'meh'. I would make the meatballs again but use my own sauce style which is full of flavors.
Using 2 eggs helped properly bind the meatballs. Added basil and parsley to the sauce. Made it in one pan and then baked at 350 for an hour. Delicious. Can’t hide the spinach from my husband though;)
Also good to add frozen spinach to meatloaf (I do turkey not beef).

I doubled this but followed as written and it ended up becoming a meat sauce. The majority of the meatballs held together through browning but fell apart once put in the sauce with some stirring. I’d recommend baking the meatballs like others have suggested.

Several noted the meatballs fell apart when they browned them, but my experience was they held together well.  For size context, I got 16 meatballs, the recipe spec's 18, so close enough.  I made these per recipe except for subbing in finely chopped cooked kale for spinach.  These are tender and I wonder if cooks who report them falling apart didn't let them sit long enough on the first side when browning, that is tried to turn them too soon.  I used tongs to turn and it worked well.
This was so delicious. No complaints, no fixes. Loved it.

I made this last night and it was soooo delicious! I left out the spinach liquid and only used ½ cup of panko, and added some chilli flakes. I fried the meatballs for about 5 minutes on each side to get a really nice crispy edge. Perfection! 😋🍝
Sounds great. Has anyone frozen the meatballs for later use. Just two in our household.

The fix for meatballs that fall apart is to cook them in sauce in a large frying pan.  Arrange them similar to making rolls - no meatballs on top of each other. Don't stir - just gently turn over halfway through cooking.
We used to get a "Joe's Special" somewhere between Sacramento and the Bay Area - ground beef, spinach, and eggs.

Really, really delicious. Cooked 12 oz of fresh spinach instead of frozen. Sub mild Italian ground pork sausage instead ground pork. Generous 1/2 cup of Parm. Skip the parsley. Fridge is key to holding them together. Recommend baking at 425 15 min flip another 5 min and boom.
Costco had huge packages of brats on sale, so I took them apart and used them for the meat. worked out well.
Metric equivalents as well please!  I am an international subscriber, as are many others, so please provide a metric toggle on all recipes, not just some.  Thanks

I added some garlic powder and powdered oregano to the sauce, which kicked it up on par with some of the best sauces I have enjoyed from various Italian families who have invited me to dinner.

For my spaghetti sauce, I’ll add:
1 large onion chopped, 1 red bell pepper, chopped, 1 lb mushrooms sliced, sautéed for aprox. 5 minutes.  Then add an additional 1/4 cup of chopped basil, the remaining garlic and crushed tomatoes. 
Continue with recipe by adding meatballs and drippings to sauce, then simmering till meatballs are cooked about 25 minutes.
After reading the other notes, I made the meatballs a little smaller and baked them in the oven a bit to firm them up before adding to the sauce. Delicious!

Delicious and and they didn’t fall apart. I added an extra egg, skipped the spinach water and used a few tablespoons of milk instead. Air fryer the meatballs about 8 minutes before adding to my own sauce which I  cooked for awhile before adding the meatballs

i use my potato ricer to dry the spinach. Awesome results. Squeeze over separate container to save the liquid. (I know seems obvious but I have found out may people have no idea what I am talking about (such as friends of grandkids - no experience in kitchen)

Meatballs came together fairly easily.  However, they tended to break apart during the Browning and even more so after added to the sauce. Speaking of the sauce it was kinda bland. I'd give the recipe a 'C' , 'C+' at best. Won't make it again.

I made the meatballs and they were great! I baked them before adding to sauce and they held together beautifully.  Much moister and tastier with the spinach added.  A keeper for sure and I froze some for the future.

I wanted to like this, getting protein and veggies in one. But the steps were baffling. Remove the spinach water and then… add it all back? 56 Oz of sauce?? My meatballs held okay when browning and I tried to keep them together in the sauce. But I checked after 25 minutes and there was still raw pork. As Wendy B already noted, there's really no need to make meatballs of the meat/spinach/garlic/parm mix.  I chopped up some chicken thighs (instead of using beef and pork), then browned that with the aforementioned ingredients.  Added a jar of our favorite spaghetti sauce and served over noodles. For those who don't like the simplicity of the sauce or don't have the time to make a more nuanced or complicated one, I suggest using your favorite jarred marinara sauce. I used Rao's. It is OK not to be perfect! I also cooked the meatballs in the oven and then put them in a large saucepan with the already-heated sauce, adding the pan juices from the baking. Next time I would pump up the flavor of the meatballs with some dried onion, maybe. Make again.

I used one reader's suggestion to bake the meatballs for 10 minutes @ 400 to firm them up a bit before browning. Ignore the "don't overwork" advice -- I pack them tight as golf balls. They hold together just fine as long as you're fairly gentle with them. The frozen spinach makes the mix very moist and keeps the meatballs tender.

The nutritional information is bogus. The yield is "6 servings" and the nutrition guide is for "2 to 4 servings." So which is it?

A delicious recipe, but because I felt it needed some help I added a little flour to the meat/spinach mix and that helped keeping things together. To lift the taste a bit, I added a little pepper flakes and fennel seeds. To break the sharpness of the sauce, I added 1/4 cup of sour cream and a little tomato paste - and it was really good.

I used 1 lb. Italian bulk sausage + 1/4 lb. ground beef (because I had it left over). Whole Foods didn't have a 10 oz. package of spinach so I used about half of a 16 oz package of whole leaf spinach that was in the freezer. I chopped it up while it was still partly frozen, microwaved it and squeezed out the water. I did not add back any of the spinach water to the meatballs.  Meatballs did not crumble but I was careful with them. Made 24 meatballs. Delicious with the sauce and pasta.

@Coletteim sure you can but it has to be cooked to get the water out. Microwave or sauté it and then squeeze and chop
If fresh spinach keeps the meatballs from falling apart, that's an improvement in my book even if there is a slight difference in nutritional value.  Does it help?
Can the meatballs be frozen after cooking and saved for later?
Can you use fresh spinach?
@Spinach and meatball connect did you add more bread crumbs or egg or both?

I will use this recipe to make vegan "meat"balls by substituting TVP or lentils for the ground meat. Seasonings and the addition of spinach will make all the difference. I make similar ones without spinach, so I am eager to enjoy them with spinach, one of my favorite vegetables.
Any thoughts on trying this with vegan ground meat substitutes???
Can these meatballs be frozen if prepared in the oven? I love the idea of extending the nutritional side of meatballs by adding the spinach!
Could you use canned spinach?
May we sub ground turkey or chicken?
Five cloves of garlic is overkill.  In fact saute' the garlic for flavor then get rid of it.  This is what my mother from Calabria used to do.
As a garlic Lover , I say the more the better!! I will be adding another 5 cloves to my sauce with more herbs as well! ;-)
Can I use fresh spinach?
Maybe, but that would definitely be more labor-intensive, since you would have to clean, cook and chop the spinach first.
The sauce needs a lot more. I think a jarred marinara would be much better. The meatballs also did fall apart but were yummy.

I found this to not be so good.  It's edible, sure, and it will be consumed, but by *following the recipe exactly*, I got a sauce that was one note (tomato) and meatballs that basically fell apart so the one note sauce became a meat sauce.  I found the meatballs bland, too, so the sauce wasnt helped when they fell apart.  Justine below also had this issue.  And someone else talked about the tomato sauce blandness, too.

We added onion and mushrooms to the sauce. I felt that the sauce was too acidic so we added some sugar. We didn't have any trouble with the meatballs, but I used ground pork only. Also, we browned the meatballs on all sides so that might have helped keep them together. In the future we may add some anchovy so give some more depth to the sauce.

These were delicious but a little more labor intensive than practical on a week night. We made these for a weekend lunch with the following comments:
1. Didn’t take my spinach out early enough for it to thaw so I ended up using a double boiler method on the stove top to get the spinach to thaw. 
2. Uses a combination of Pomi chopped tomatoes and some chopped fresh cherry tomatoes I had on hand. I added some leftover Pinot noir that has been open for awhile and a couple of Tsps Pomi tomato paste with splash of good balsamic vinegar. 
3. I ended up with 15 decent sized meatballs that are quite filling and make great leftovers.

My meatballs fell apart too. I bought a packaged ground meat blend (beef pork and veal) and wish I’d stuck to just beef and pork. I also should’ve minced the garlic and parsley to avoid larger pieces that disturbed the structural integrity of the meatball. 
The sauce and pasta need sufficient salt. 
At the end of the day I’d prefer ground meat and veggies in a sauce, over attempting this recipe again.
Did you chill the meatballs before adding to sauce? Helps to firm up. I have used this method for years.
The spinach is good for you, and much easier to use when it's chopped and frozen. Just make meatballs and add the spinach to the sauce. Works with just garlic and oil as well.

These are delicious! Super tender. I made them in the oven as my toddler was getting a bit hangry and we couldn’t wait for a 35 min simmer. Also used Raos sauce but next time will try the homemade sauce option. Toddler and adult approved!

We had half an onion and some mushrooms leftover from another meal so we added them to the sauce, along with a tablespoon of sugar.  The meatballs come together easily and quickly. This is a great weeknight meal.
Really -- two 28 ounce cans of tomatoes. I wanted to check but since no one has said anything I guess it's correct. This is a time consuming recipe with a lot of prep.

These meatballs are really great for a week night dinner but like someone commented, are a little bland. I added soup stock to the sauce, which improved the flavor. Next time I will add sautéed onions to the batter for more umami. 
Regardless, our family enjoyed the leftovers in the form of meatball sandwiches.
Made this tonight. Like the concept but flavor wasn't there. Next time we will sub Italian sausage for the pork. Served it with polenta instead if pasta and that was a good decision. Bery comforting.

The meatballs fell apart for me and the whole dish wasn’t that tasty.  I’d cut the spinach in half, or maybe just used chopped mushrooms instead, possibly with ground chicken. Add milk, substitute plain bread crumbs for panko, increase the parmesan cheese.   Keep the parsley and garlic and egg.

I should have read the comments before attempting this. Same issues with meatballs breaking apart while cooking.  I added my own herbs to the sauce as I usually do, so that part tasted very good. We did not eat the meatballs because they weren’t even meatballs. They were particles 😆  
Figures the first time not reading comments…. Dummy me.  But I’ve had great experiences with many other recipes of Ali’s.

The meatballs are good. I used regular bread crumbs and the mis seemed dry so,I added 1/2 c of ricotta. They held together nicely and tasted good. 
The sauce is very bland and not worthy of the meatballs.
Meatballs fell totally apart even thoughI chilled them as suggested.   Ended up eith s beef/ spinach ragu
very good. very tender meatballs. added a bit of Worcestershire sauce and ketchup  to boost the sauce
Tastes fine, but very wet and is more like a meat sauce than meatballs

I loved the idea of having somethng green in my meatballs.  But my store didnt have frozen spinach, so I took 14 oz. frozen kale instead.   I was too lazy to squeeze water out of it or wait for it to thaw.  I threw in 2ibs of ground pork and turkey to absorb the excess water along with all the other ingredients listed.  I baked about 50 meatballs at 400 for 25-30 min.   Then, I simmered diced onions and garlic, threw in 6 cans of tomatoes and red wine, and added the meatballs in.  Yummy!

In the frying, the panko in the mix burned the first side of the first batch of meatballs so I had to pick off the burnt stuff.  My meatballs are in the sauce right now, mostly falling apart. I have them on low and are just letting them simmer, no stirring.  Should still taste ok.

Would definitely recommend good whole canned tomatoes (crushed by hand) instead of crushed tomatoes.  In order to completely cook the meatballs it needs to be cooked a while and by the time they're cooked, the "sauce" is more like a paste.  Also, the sauce is very bland.  I would brown some onions, then the garlic, then add the tomatoes and spices.
Could this be made with plant based meat???
@Lisa M. I made with 2/3 impossible beef and 1/3 beyond breakfast sausage patty. Worked great. Omit extra liquid in first step.

I took Jojo's advise and left out the spinach water, reduced the panko to .5 cup. It was perfect. Meatballs held together fine. Two cans of crushed tomatoes, one with no salt and basil. I didn't make any other changes. Delicious.
This was delicious!

If you want to get exactly 18 meatballs, they should be around 47 grams instead of 50 grams. A #24 disher / red cookie scoop will get you pretty close, but you might need to add a few extra grams worth to each scoop.

I wanted to like this. I read many of the reviews and took note. In particular, the comments about the meatballs falling apart. I thought I had this with minor alterations, to avoid the meatballs falling apart. But alas, they did. Though not entirely. My assessment is there’s too much spinach in this for it to be tasty and to hold together.

I think I will try this recipe but use all beef and basil pesto in the meat mix and use no canned tomatoes. Instead of canned tomatoes I think I will use one tomato peeled and seeded, chopped and simmered in butter.
Really good- modifications I made was I added mozzarella cubes. It froze really good in my Souper Cubes and I brought to work

Soft, but kept their shape. Challah crumbs; no panko. Put only a little squeezed-spinach liquid into the meat mixture. Made the sauce in a large frying pan by browning salted onions, adding garlic, then a dab of tomato paste, deglazed with about 1/2 cup pinot noir, stirred in the tomatoes and remaining spinach juice, then basil, oregano, and red pepper flakes. Roasted the meatballs in a rimmed sheet pan lined with parchment, which absorbed the grease, before adding them to the sauce.

Not bad, but overall pretty bland. Made as directed, but  the sauce and the meatballs both felt like they were lacking in flavor.  Only one meatball fell apart for me, so no issue there with making them in the pan rather than baking first. And while I am a huge spinach fan, I almost felt the meatballs had a tad bit too much spinach. If I can figure out how to give it all a bit more depth I would make it again. My husband, not a spinach lover, liked it a lot though so that was a win!
Tripled recipe with turkey, pork and bison. Roasted meatballs at 400 on an oiled sheet pan. Also used matzo meal instead of panko. Came out great.

We really liked this! Used 1 lb ground beef. 1 x 28oz diced tomatoes + 2 cups beef stock. They didn't fall apart. Served over sautéd mushrooms, peppers and zucchini noodles. Spinach in the meatballs - genius!
I followed the recipe exactly and my beautiful delicate meatballs instantly stuck to my le creuset and were destroyed…did my pan get too hot?? Spinach too moist?

delicious. we mis-read and also put chopped basil and parsley in the meatballs which was great. also cooked the tomato sauce down for an hour before adding brown balls back and added chopped onion, anchovy paste, wine, worcester. really good and would cook for friends. meatballs did not fall apart but we were extra nice to them :)

Added two eggs to help with binding and used only beef ~1.3lbs. Meatballs still kinda fell apart.
Delicious and moist meatballs with a subtle spinach flavor. We had to put the lid back on the Dutch oven in order to get the meatballs up to temp (165F).
Made almost exactly as written, sooooooo delicious and a great toddler friendly meal! Baked the meatballs per the note and added pan drippings to the sauce. Also added 1/2 tsp sugar to the sauce

Made just the meatballs with a few  modifications—used ground turkey and fresh chopped spinach, and put them in the oven at 425 for 20 minutes, rotating halfway. Moist and flavorful meatballs! Great way to use up some spring spinach!

I make this recipe with ground chicken and I use fresh spinach.  Much easier that way and I don’t have to squeeze out the water.  The spinach is finely chopped.  Results are yummy. I also add more Parmesan cheese.

As others have stated, the meatballs fall apart.  They are so delicious, what does it matter.  I added  chili flakes, bay leaf, oregano, and cumin to increase the flavor based upon comments of others.

Don’t know what the raves are about. Meatballs didn’t fall apart but they were bland and 2 28oz cans of crushed tomatoes made abundant sauce that lacked flavor or depth. Would not make again (from someone who likes Ali’s recipes 99% of the time).

Surprisingly (to me) good. I used the drained frozen spinach and all beef and when fashioning the balls, dipped my hands in cold water between balls. That way you get a nice rounded meatball. I cooked them in a large frying pan: heated the oil, placed the balls in the hot oil, and then put them into the oven. It worked. Then poured a 15 1/2 oz jar of marinara around them. Heated up on the stove and poured over the spaghetti. A crowd pleaser.

Used only half the called-for frozen spinach and no liquid but kept all else the same, except used turkey sausage instead of plain ground turkey (cut back a bit on salt as a result). Mix made 15 meatballs. Baked meatballs at 400F for around 25 min and skipped the simmer. Also made Julia Moskin's classic marinara (on NYT) instead of this one--called for only 1 28oz can of tomatoes. Made 12oz dried pasta rather than an entire pound, to feed three people.  Tasty; would make again.
Any ideas for substituting the egg? Will it hold up with extra panko/breadcrumbs? Egg allergy in our house

The recipe was good, but needed a little hel to not be too bland. And the time estimates were pretty off for me… it took way longer and was more of a project than expected from the instructions. I’d give this three stars.
How long am I supposed to preheat a 12” stainless steel sautee pan and how many cups of oil do I need so the meatballs don’t stick? This became a bolognese.

I just made this with a tweaks. I used ground chicken, and added tabil spice blend to the meatball mixture. I baked for 30 mins at 375°. I added a heaping tablespoon of Onion Achaar to the crushed tomatoes. I used orercciette instead of spaghetti or other pastas mentioned. Delicious.

My toddler and I loved these. Used a whole box of fresh spinach (wilted in olive oil then chopped), garlic powder instead of fresh, and simmered in Rao’s marinara with a sprig of basil. Get a good sear on them first and they’ll hold together. Finishing them in the sauce is key! Makes them so tender and flavorful.
Made with cottage cheese since I had no ricotta. Wonderfully moist meatballs which stood up to simmering. Very good, simple sauce using Muir Glen crushed tomatoes. Definitely a keeper of a recipe.

Wonderful meatballs and sauce. I used fresh spinach and 1/2 cup 4% fat cottage cheese instead of ricotta since that’s what I had. Opted for my homemade bread crumbs vs Panko. Did not need to add liquid. Muir Glen Organic crushed tomatoes used for a delicious sauce. I will be making this again.

The sauce is a little bland as it is. I added some more herbs: rosemary, oregano etc. aka “Italian seasoning” and probably should’ve sautéed an onion too to really pump up the flavor. Overall a good dish. Would also recommend baking the meatballs (I believe Smitten Kitchen does this for many of her recipes) to avoid them falling apart in the sauce.

I liked the meatballs with the thawed frozen spinach really tasty.  I used the .5 recipe widget and added dry basil and a touch of oregano in the beginning to the sauce as well as a small chunk of parm. used barilla protein spaghetti as I couldn't find any dreamfields.
Meatballs fell apart in the sauce but flavor was good.

I made the ssamjang from scratch, and boy, was it good*!  

As was the entire dish.  For so little effort, it was an impressive hit for my dinner guests.  I expect it would also be terrific subbing shrimp for salmon . . .

Served it with Eric Kim's miso soup, which also got rave reviews from my guests.

*so good, in fact, I'll be making another batch to keep in the frig.
so simple. so satisfying. so ssamjang.

My spouse had bought too much salmon. So I turned to this recipe and invited two good friends over for a Friday night dinner. One of our guests remarked that he had to slow down eating so that he could savor every bite because it was just that good. I made a quick cucumber salad with rice vinegar, sugar, and ice, cold water and had some kimchi on the side along with cheater version of gamja jorim. Such a super easy meal and so rewarding.

A new favorite way to make salmon! This is one of those recipes where the "whole is greater than sum of its parts." The rice vinegar in the marinade truly cuts that sometimes strong salmon taste.  Super easy to make, went excellently with the rice and lettuce leaves. I had mine with perilla leaves too, and mixed some ssamjang+sesame oil+rice vinegar+green onions into a dipping sauce.

So simple and fast to make. I baked the salmon at 375 for 10 minutes because I don’t have a broiler and it was perfect. You can really decorate your lettuce wraps with condiments, too, which is fun — furikake, sriracha mayo, soy sauce, etc — in addition to the ssamjang
amazing recipe. so good.
Yummy and easy to make and customize for a family with lots of different food preferences.
I liked this, but my husband didn’t. Will try same recipe with chicken.
So Flavorful for such little  effort!
really, really tasty! definitely worth making the ssamjang yourself. very easy and quick. i would recommend using a full tablespoon of gochujang in the ssamjang if you like spice!
Wow. Super easy and beyond good!
Has anyone tried using an air fryer instead of a broiler? I don't trust my broiler. How did it turn out for you?
Used the air fryer 400F for 8 minutes. Great easy recipe

We enjoyed this very much.  When we watched the video, there was a flat circular thing on the lettuce that the salmon and rice, etc were placed on.   There was no mention of that in the recipe?  Seems like that would make eating this recipe easier.  Anyone know what it is?  Rice paper or something?
Broiled on 2nd rack for 4 minutes and skin burned. Next time will bake or air fry per other comments.

Two questions, please: In the recipe video, what is the white disc that sort of looks like a small tortilla, sitting inside the lettuce wrap? I don't see it mentioned in the recipe itself or the introduction. And, are we supposed to leave the salmon skin for eating?  Thank you.
@Ashley daikon or similar radish
it's been asked before, but why do these pasta recipes seem to always call for 12 oz?
@WTR because 12 ounces is four servings. (Technically, 2 ounces is a serving, but almost everyone eats 3 per serving.)

Because so many NYT Cooking readers don't seem to like pasta, so they always says 16 oz is "too much pasta." Also, the full bottle of wine will work best with 12 oz. Otherwise, you would be asking why the recipe calls for 1-1/3 bottles of wine.

I always scale the 12 oz. recipes down to 8 for just the two of us and up to 16 for a guest or two. The calculation for this one is a little trickier than most to get the right consistency. We don't always have a suitable white on hand but do have Noilly Prat vermouth in the fridge that works well for this.

I thought it was really terrific. I used dried pasta and cooked it a bit longer so the sauce would thicken up. I added scallops and the resulting dish was full of flavor. Will definitely make it again. (Might squeeze a lemon over it for some brightness).
@David C Scallops are a great idea!
Don’t use the best wine in your cellar but there’s a rule: “If you wouldn’t drink it, DO NOT cook with it.”
@Peter W.  Ha!  I like a good wine but I save a lot of money cooking by being happy to drink almost any old plonk.
@Peter W. Maybe I’m just a philistine, but I often make beef stew with old open bottles of red wine I wouldn’t drink, and it turns out great!
@Jo I recently watched something about cheap wines having sugar, pesticides, etc.  might be worth it to up your game.
Didn’t have anchovies so used several tablespoons of Vietnamese fish sauce with a little Worcestershire sauce for good measure.  Tasty pasta.
Made it without any modifications.  It is horrible.  A mucky mess of highly reduced wine and gloopy pasta.  Don't bother with this recipe.
@Michael, I agree. Made it tonight and it was tasteless.

@Michael
I also followed recipe to the T, and while my pasta came out quite nice in texture and the sauce was creamy, the flavor was acidic and bland. I added sautéed garlic and grated cheese after tasting how bland it was, and while that helped, the acidity from the wine was off-putting. I think adding some wine while deglazing makes sense and adds some nuanced flavor, but boiling pasta in that much wine is gimmicky and not for me. That said, cooking pasta with much less water than the standard process is a good method and one that I have been using for a while now.
@Michael same. I threw it all away!

This this is the craziest thing i’ve ever heard. Cook the pasta in a whole bottle of wine? No. There are so many better ways of doing this. Firstly, if you really must cook the pasta in wine, then use a wide shallow pan instead of a pot, you’ll be able to use 1 cup of wine and 1 cup of water and get the same effect. But it’s completely unnecessary anyway. Just follow the same basic recipe (which is just a typical midnight pasta recipe), cook the pasta in water as always, then move the  very al dente pasta to the pan, add a half cup wine and let it simmer until soft enough to eat.
I think I will try it your way!
could you substitute something like capers for the anchovies
@Pat In a word - NO. There’s no “taste” of anchovies left - only the umami which they contribute when used in this fashion.
@Pat I wonder though how a white miso as the umami contributor would do with the white wine
@Pat i’m going to try a good amount of garlic instead of anchovies to make it vegetarian.

I don't understand this recipe at all - cook 12 oz of pasta in 5 cups of liquid for 8 to 12 minutes and if it's looking dry, add more water? How can it possibly look dry? Five cups of liquid isn't going to boil away in that amount of time. What am I missing? I'm imagining spaghetti and anchovy soup.
@CS Wine reduces much faster than water, because the alcohol evaporates into a gas state much faster than water.

@CS The water/wine isn't boiling away as much as it's being absorbed into the dried pasta. When the eight minutes have elapsed the liquid has transformed the dried pasta into the pliable pasta you eat.
@Darby Doo wine is only between 10% and 14% alcohol, so that’s not going to affect the amount of liquid that much, reducing it by only ~90 ml if all the alcohol boils off. I
Number 11 Speghetti works best
@Mónica Thank you for this tip off! I completely concur!

@Pat anchovies are for saltiness, they will be mostly dissolved in the sauce. If you want to skip them, my advice would be toadd more salt, or something like worcestershire. Capers or olives in a pasta like this would be very tasty too, but not as a substitute, more like a bonus.

It may be anathema to some, but I added a handful of minced garlic and a good amount of finely minced kale to the pan with the red pepper flakes with the foaming butter. So good, even greater depth of flavor — and some healthy veg to boot!

I often make red wine spaghetti, so I used the same preparation technique. Boil the pasta until a couple of minutes before the doneness you prefer. Drain the pasta and save some of the pasta water. Melt the butter and anchovies (I use a wok but you can use the same pot). Toss the pasta with the anchovy butter. Add the wine and cook/boil until absorbed by the pasta. Add some of the water if more liquid needed. More of the flavour of the wine is absorbed this way. A nice dish. (LOVE anchovies.)

Since the pandemic began in 2020, I've been faithfully cooking the recipes from "What to Make Next Week" from the Saturday Times.  This was the worst of 6 years of cooking those recipes.  Not sure how to fix it.  Sorry
Way way too much anchovy flavor. Really took away from the quality of the dish
Wasn’t crazy about the anchovy fishy aroma. Not a bad pasta dish but I’ve had better. We weren’t crazy about it.

I made this as directed and served with brown butter seared scallops with garlic and lemon. It benefited from a squeeze of lemon over the full dish. I used a very good wine, and agree that it would have been a much less interesting dish with a cheaper selection.
One of the worst things I've ever tasted. Tasted like wet earth only not as good. If you want someone out of your life for good, this is the meal to cook!

This recipe looked so unusual I had to try it without any modifications.  It was in a word, inedible.  I have never made anything in my entire life, and I am an official old fart, that was so awful.  It is a gooey, gloppy hideous tasting mess, and a waste of good ingredients.  I threw the entire pot out.  Don't bother, this one can't be salvaged, and that's saying a good deal.
I always wonder about recipes that ask for unsalted butter and then add “a big pinch of salt.”
@Mary, that’s fair. I can only speak for myself, but I use unsalted butter for many, many things; so it tends to be the only kind of butter I buy.
It's so you can control the amount of salt to be added rather than having the salt manufacturer determine this.   Saltiness of a dish is a personal taste and  that taste varies widely among people.
Sorry to say, we hated this. It just tasted like wine and fish, and not in a good way. We love all these ingredients, but together, it just didn't work.
For vegetarians, could miso be a substitute for anchovies?

@CaraSoup I used lots of garlic and a little bit of bouillon powder. Really very pleased with the result. Huge hit with everyone here. I had some excellent spaghetti on a cheese fondue sauce due base in Venice last year; I think the pasta was probably cooked in this way.
For us, it turned out to be a big pot of sour mushy gloppy pasta.  @lwiedenf

To those who wonder why the recipe calls for 12 ozs of pasta (rather than the 16 oz pasta comes in) it is because 3 oz is a generous serving of pasta and the recipe is for 4 persons. It is a conundrum - rather like why hot dogs come in packages in different numbers than rolls. Go figure.

I’m sorry to say this goes into the “never again” bin. Aggressively rich and unpleasant flavor and it got gloopier the longer it sat on the plate. Maybe a better bottle of wine would have made a difference but I don’t think it’s worth it to experiment further to figure it out. Echoing another commenter, the video shows two late ingredients being added that aren’t in the recipe. Maybe those do some heroic heavy lifting but I’ll never find out.
Very much a bagna cauda, but with a whole bottle of wine!
Bagna cauda takes a lot of garlic, and there is none in the ingredient list for this.
What a great idea! I needed something new, thank you!
I don't like the idea of putting pasta into cold liquid and letting come to a boil. It's just wrong.

@CS  "I don't understand this recipe at all... Five cups of liquid isn't going to boil away in that amount of time."

You've never made risotto, have you?
Not good! Gah! Too thick and not a good taste.

I was so excited to try this and really wanted to like it but unfortunately found it almost inedible.  In retrospect, it might have been a good idea to start with less than the specified number of anchovy fillets. It turned out incredibly fishy.  A complete waste of a bottle of sauvignon blanc!  If I try this again, I'll use white miso as suggested by other commenters.
I'm just confused that the dry pasta is cooked in 2 cups of water???
@Ung and one whole bottle of white wine.
Do you think that increasing the liquid by 1/3, even if it was just plain water for the extra, and then being able to use a full normal sized portion of pasta (1lb), would work?

Made as directed. Very disappointing.

I don't understand all the complaints & alterations. I thought it was great - especially for nights when I don't feel like real cooking. I always have anchovies & white wine on hand. Maybe my taste buds have aged (I'm old), but anchovies weren't too strong... as others have said, just a good background flavor.

I am curious enough to do what you did;make the recipe as is with no alterations... It still bothers me, this new idea of cooking an entire box of dry pasta with just 2 cups of water... (I wonder if they mean 12oz fresh pasta?) .. I'm glad you enjoyed it. Perhaps I will too.
@Ung 2 cups of water PLUS a 750ml bottle of wine (which is more than 3 additional cups of liquid).

This was a total failure! The only sketchy thing I did was to use capelini (sic) instead of spaghetti. Despite continuing to add water, the thing turned to glue. The wine never really had a chance to evaporate. Had to toss.
Trust me, I am with you on this.  Complete waste.  Just drink the wine and forgo the pasta

Followed this to the T, with a go-to table white, and now concur with many of the 1/0 star comments on both approach and result. 

There are plenty of excellent other uses for the ingredients, so many simple pasta recipes to enjoy. This, sadly, yields a dish with no redeeming qualities.

I've made this recipe twice now and it's always a hit. I do agree with the commenter below who questions why NY Times recipes always seem to call for 12 oz. of pasta, when dried pasta is ALWAYS sold in 16 oz. packages, at least in the US. You end up having to either do complicated math to adjust the recipe for 16 ounces of pasta or, more likely, end up with a bunch of open packs of pasta with 4 oz. leftover, an awkward amount that's too little for a single serving.
Assume this is dried pasta and is it chilli pepper or red pepper

If it were fresh pasta, the recipe would say that. Wikipedia says: "Crushed red pepper or red pepper flakes or chilli flakes is a condiment or spice consisting of dried and crushed (as opposed to ground) red chilli peppers."
Made this for 3 people. All 3 thought it wasn’t worth it and would not try it again.
I made it with no anchovies but substituted chopped garlic and some small Parmesan rinds which made it nice and salty tasting.
Very disappointing.

Good golly this was a hit over here. Made it as written (including measuring out the 12 ounces of pasta) and paired it with garlicky chicken under the broiler and roasted veggies. I think shrimp or fish would suit beautifully... or just the veggies; lovely flavor and perfect for that box of wine on the fridge door! :)

Echo the earlier comment about 12 oz of pasta?  Also, 8 anchovies which come in tins of 12??  We used fettuccine, and it turned out fine, but because it's flat, it takes a bit more stirring to keep from clumping.  The flavor of the finished dish was sort of unusual, not bad, just unusual.  My wife thought it had sort of a cheese flavor.  I was a little surprised that the anchovies didn't seem to add any "fishy" flavor.  Bottom line, it was ok, but not  sure it's worth a bottle of  wine!
BTW! Worcestershire has anchovies in it.
I did a half batch of this last night. Pretty darn tasty.

This was delicious.  Used Miso in place of anchovies and fettuccine instead of spaghetti.  Cut recipe in half.  Accidentally added 2c water vs 1c.  It worked out just fine though.  No excess liquid, just a nice creamy sauce. Also used 1/4tsp red pepper for more kick.

The move from the once 16 0z standard pasta boxes to 12 oz boxes...is being "sold" as a convenience to consumers, as some don't use the entire 16 oz box, however...the skeptical pragmatist in me knows that it's because the pasta makers simply make more money selling less pasta for almost the same price as the former larger size. Word, simple as that.

Halved the recipe and added the meat of 5 chopped quahogs(clams) that I had just dug. Added a small bit of the resulting clam broth and the dish was wonderful. Used 1 cup of water and 1 cup of  Sauvignon Blanc.

Did anyone find that the cooking time was too short? I really loved this pasta but the cooking time was longer than 8-12 minutes and so I did have to increase the amount of liquid to get to true al dente. Thinking perhaps my pan was to wide for the 1/2 recipe.
Love the idea of cooking pasta in wine! For vegetarians, could someone suggest a tasty alternative for anchovies?

Why use unsalted butter to sauté only to season with a "big pinch" of salt? Does unsalted butter brown or sauté better or differently from salted? Given the anchovies, presumably preserved in oil and salt, this is already a pretty high-sodium recipe.
Different brands of salted butter contain different amounts of salt. Using unsalted butter allows you to control the amount of salt.
Cook 20 hrs?

@Rich Makes it kind of complicated as a  weeknight dish, right? If you plan to serve it at 7pm Tuesday I suppose you have to get started at 3pm Monday. Not to mention that after 20 hours it will no longer resemble food…
If you don't like anchovies, or want to open a can just for one recipe, use fish sauce or straight up MSG.

Not sure if I missed something, followed recipe more or less exactly. Pasta ended up tasting strongly of wine and not much else, was soupy at first but did end up thickening up. I don’t feel that the wine fully cooked off in the time it took to get to al dente.

So good! Used half a bottle of white wine that I opened a week ago and cut the recipe in half. Used miso instead of anchovies bc they were on hand but anchovies would have been good. Honestly this was so good for how little effort it is
Made it exactly as specified, but to me, the anchovies overwhelmed.

This is an easy 5 star dish for me it's incredibly easy to make, I made it as directed. The flavor intensity is off the charts the combo of acid from wine, umami from anchovies and richness from butter is incredible.
And now that I've made it variations seem endless I bet an egg or two cracked in there would be great.

I must have made it wrong because the wine and anchovies taste was too strong. It mellowed out after an overnight in the fridge, so leftovers were okay. My 16yr old banned the recipe family dinner rotation. If I try again, I'd use less wine and substitute miso for the anchovies.
I only had a 1/2 bottle of white wine on hand, so used a 1/2 bottle of dry vermouth to make up the difference.  It came out beautifully.  Really great dish.
The video shows something white, maybe flour added towards the end if cooking. There is also a lot of a red powder added too. Neither are listed in the ingredients.  Any thoughts as to what is added?
@Peter W. I use inexpensive dry vermouth. it makes ZERO difference, unless you are using corked or oxidized wine.  We are just looking fot the acid balance.
This is the white wine version of spaghetti all'ubriaco, a Tuscan classic.
Can I eat the entire pan?
I used 12 oz pasta and 3 c wine, 3 c water. Substituted white miso instead of anchovies. Added grated fresh Parmesan, squeeze of lemon, fresh parsley abundantly on top. 6.5 out of 10

This recipe needs major adjustments. 
I would replace th ebutter with extra virgin olive oil and add a few cloves of garlic.
The liquid amount is fine.

We will never make this again. Weirdly, aggressively rich from the wine, very gloppy, and got gloppier as it sat on the plate. No ideas for how to rescue it, and not willing to risk the same outcome in the future.
I followed the recipe but the end product was disappointing. The wine simply overwhelmed all other flavors.

I am not in a position to scientifically test this, but at least based on some quick and dirty Internet searching this cooking time could easily leave as much as 40% of alcohol remaining in the sauce. Not a lot, but also misleading to suggest that all the alcohol is gone.

Worst headache ever. I followed the recipe precisely, save for replacing the anchovies with garlic and a teaspoon of spices. I chose a good New Zealand white wine and was careful cooking the past so it wouldn’t stick or gloop together. It looked great even but it tasted like I was eating wine! It even burned a little as I swallowed. I let it rest thinking it would get better and made myself eat a portion only to be left with a terrible headache. The wine never had a chance to fully evaporate though I followed the timing exactly. I would have had a better meal just drinking the wine and cooking the pasta in broth. This is a never again recipe.

You had me at glug glug glug.  Unfortunately this devolved into an inedible glutinous hot mess.  My daughter then made a simple butter anchovy sauce with onion and garlic that she deglazed with white wine. She then added cooked pasta.  Don’t be fooled by the promise of a one pot wonder.

This did not taste good at all. I was excited to try this and thought the idea behind the recipe was interesting. I went to taste the pasta for doneness and it had the worst aftertaste. I’m usually down for an anchovy-heavy dish, but something about this amount of white wine in pasta just made it really unpleasant.
Is there any substitute for the anchovies?
Do you actually add dry pasta and wine to the same pot with the suace? Do you ever need to drain it before you serve?

@Brother Bluedream you shouldn’t need to drain it. This is one of many “one pan pasta” dishes. The proportion of liquid to dry pasta is usually something like 1 cup of liquid to 3-4 ounces of pasta, and once the pasta is cooked you have a fully cooked pasta dish without the need to cook the pasta in a separate pan of boiling water, saving you time and clean up. 
This one is 5 cups (2 water, 3 wine) for 12 ounces of pasta, so it’s probably intended to be a bit saucey once the pasta is fully cooked.
I wonder if you can switch out the white wine with a red?
@Ed i feel like the taste would be fine but the visuals would be a little weird
@Ed Came here to ask if rose worked!
I’m assuming the parsley mentioned is Italian parsley?
@ScottI can’t imagine it makes a difference in flavor. Texture? Maybe.
I detest anchovies, so would sardines be an acceptable substitute?
No I don't think so - anchovies add umami to a dish and dissolve in a sauce.  Sardines would likely just be weird bits of fish in the sauce.
For this dish better to use an unbaked Sauvignon blanc than an oaked one; and def not New Zealand.
I live in ridiculous Montgomery County Maryland and cannot buy wine in a grocery store. Can I use a bottle of nonalcoholic wine in this recipe, or do I need to make an extra stop at a liquor store?

@Megan I would not use non-alcoholic wine, because I don't consider that to be wine. If I am going to spend the time and money to prepare this dish, I will make the extra trip. But, I agree, the ways states control the distribution is ridiculous.

I'm considering going with fresh pasta, but the cooking time would be reduced to a couple of minutes. 
Should I rather use dry pasta here, to allow more time for the sauce? Or let the sauce cook on its own for 10 minutes, and add the pasta at the end?
Dry pasta will take longer to cook, giving the wine time to flavor the pasta and for the alcohol to simmer away. Fresh pasta won't do either of those things.
Use dry pasta you want it to absorb the sauce
Would a chardonnay work?
Probably not, but go ahead and try it. Chardonnay has a distinctive flavor that will remain. Sauv Blanc does not have the same taste.
@Richard X And, IMO, Chardonnay has a distinctively unpleasant flavor.

Is there a way to make this with angel hair pasta?  My husband doesn't like thicker pasta, but since it only take 2-3 minutes to cook angel hair, I don't think this sauce would come together in that short of a time.
Has anyone tried this with GF pasta?
Simple and delicious
Is this made with canned anchovies or "real" anchovies like one would find at a fishmonger?
Definitely ‘canned’.
Made pretty much like the recipe, except subbed two tbsp. anchovy paste and bucatini, and it was phenomenal.  Will definitely be making again.

As for the question of whether the quality of a cooking wine matters... 
I used to make Marsala chicken with a pretty low-end Marsala... then I switched to something far more drinkable, and it was transformative, totally improving the dish.  

But... I have also prepared other dishes with unimpressive, long-opened bottles of wine... and the results have still been delicious.  Could they have been even better?  Maybe.

Since I didn't have an enlongated pot that could hold the length of  my pasta, rather than insult Italians world wide by breaking it, I opted on using a  tall pot to give the pasta a short time to soften. Sorry but using two pots, one very easy to wash and rinse, didn't stop me from enjoying a great dish of pasta. Oh yeah, the addition of shrimp and some grated Parm at the end of the prep made for a wonder dinner!
Made this tonight. Next time would use two tbsp of butter and add some lemon juice and maybe clams+broth.
re: why 12 oz of pasta - 2-3 oz per person

Hey there @Ung, it's 2 cups of water and 750 ml of white wine -- which is about ~5 cups of liquid. Which seems like a small amount compared to the large volumes called for on the pasta packages, but I often cook pasta in similar volumes of water, and it works fine -- you save time because it comes to a boil quicker, and the water gets much more starchy, which is great for making sauces.
Substitute shrimp for the anchovy fillets.
Would Chardonnay be okay?
I used Pinot grigio. Any inexpensive dry white wine would work fine
I added both parsley and dill, sardines and sliced olives, garlic and crushed red pepper.  The wine I used was a pinot grigio from Italy.  Very flavorful--no salt added.
The wine bottle I used was small--half size.  That might have helped.
I made this as written. Love the method, but I found the amount of anchovy to be overwhelming. Otherwise excellent, and it lends itself well to cacio e pepe.

I replaced the 2c water with chicken broth, added 5 tablespoons chopped garlic with the better and anchovies, and at the end sorted in 1/2 cup of grated gurye and Swiss cheese blend. Also mixed in 1/4 c chopped walnuts. Superb!!
I am a frequent cook and have made hundreds of NYT recipes. This was the worst meal I have ever made. Gloopy, acidic mess. Totally inedible. It should be removed from the app.

Didn’t really turn out. The sauce took on a weird texture from all the starch in the pasta, and I found that the wine came through way too strong. Ended up using a Pinot Grigio from Costco, which I love. It felt like I was just drinking the wine while eating plain pasta. Maybe it was just something I did wrong.

Excellent recipe !!

Just cooked it this evening (we live in France). We loved it, that's the first thing ! Now, a few (positive) comments !

1/ Prepare more broth : we prepared 5 cups of broth, because some types of pasta soak up more water than others and you don't want to find yourself with a dry meal. Add more broth if you feel it's necessary.

2/ We added carrots and celery. We diced 3 carrots and minced 3 celery sticks. Boil for 15 minutes, rinse with cold water. Prepare ahead of time, so

Axel, thanks for your suggestions! As so many soup recipes start with mirepoix, I wondered why this one didn't have carrots, celery, and onions. I will add them, and leave out the bacon or pancetta. Then the recipe can be vegetarian if one prefers.

When making a big pot of soup, I cook the pasta separately. That way, when there are leftovers, you can add cooked pasta to the leftovers so it stays al dente. Or, add another grain the second day.
@ Karen Lee: the headnote to this recipe explains why we don't start with diced carrots and celery.

@Richard X My local store (like many others) sells prechopped mirepoix, which makes it easy to boost the flavor and nutrition. Or you could just chop an onion, which takes two minutes and is a basic kitchen skill. Or you could toss in a bag of frozen onions (either chopped or pearl$.

I basically lived on this for months in a difficult time and place (don’t ask) and still make it regularly. If I have time I simmer a half dozen peeled garlic cloves and a chopped celery stick or two in olive oil to add in at the end; and if I get “fancy” I use chopped pancetta instead of bacon.  Like others, I will also cook the pasta separately as time and space permit.

This what we grew up calling "Pasta Fazoole". Sweat some onions, add a can or 2 of beans, including the liquid, maybe a cup or so of water, season with oregano and/or whatever else you like for flavor, add 5-6 tablespoons of spaghetti sauce, simmer until it thickens a bit. Cook the macaroni separately, then add to the soup, finish with grated cheese. Here's the most important part: Put it in the fridge, and eat it the next day. Nothing tastes better than leftover Pasta Fazoole.
@Jim that’s what we called it, too! Grams always had a pot on when we’d come to visit.
I made yours today Jim. Thanks for the recipe, it was yummy.  I saved half to eat tomorrow.

Instructing anybody to add 1/4 tsp of any seasoning to a dish with 6-8 cups of liquid should constitute a violation of the Geneva convention.

Please do yourselves a favor and 12x the amount of seasoning in this recipe.
I find that letting garlic brown produces a bitter taste. I add the garlic with no more than 1 minute before I add liquid.

@crabby kotzmann
I do that too. I also smash my garlic before mincing it up. Something about breaking down the fibers on the cloves makes it sweeter as well. I always mash my garlic into a paste for salad dressings.

Made this first of all, there’s no such thing as “Italian seasoning”. Italians use herbs such as oregano, rosemary, thyme in most savory dishes. Secondly the base of onion, celery and carrot is a MUST no matter if it’s a slow cooking soup or a quick 30 minutes weeknight soup. Never skip it. Always short pasta such as ditalini are what my Italian in-laws always use. Fresh or canned tomato can be used. Either is fine.

@Lost In A Space I totally hear what you are saying but some cooks (novices, those who have limited kitchen space, or those who are trying to get dinner on the table in a hurry) prefer to use seasoning blends such as Italian seasoning, which are made by most of the large spice companies and usually contain all the herbs you mention). Personally, I prefer to stock individual herbs and spices but there are a few blends I like that contain seasonings that I would probably not use in enough quantity before they’d get stale, making them a more economical choice.

Skip the carrots, celery, onion.  Just use lots of garlic. A quick tomato sauce.  ditalini  goes best with the bean size.  Add some water from the pasta and you have a dish as soupy as you like.  Yum.

I make a very similar version but use a 15 can of tomato and more broth. This is such an easy recipe and you can use what you have in your pantry or freezer.

For a more American version use 1 lb Italian sausage. I like to add onions, carrots and celery. Frozen veggies work well and most grocery stores carry  mirepoix in the produce section.

You can cook the pasta separately and add it separately to each serving so that the leftovers don’t soak up too much of the broth and get mushy.

My Italian grandmother's depression era recipe.  Crush a garlic  clove, fry in olive oil till golden, add 1 qt. crushed tomatoes (home canned).   Cook elbow macaroni.  Add navy beans (canned or cooked) to either sauce or pasta before draining.   reserve some pasta water.   add pasta to sauce with enough water to thin to taste.   grated romano cheese.   bread.   salad.   dinner.  My father refused to eat it as he said he had his fill in the depression.

Great recipe, make it your own! I added celery and onion, would have used fennel, carrots if on hand. A little tomato paste in the soffrito adds body. Definitely liked whole tomatoes with a little hand crush. Drizzle with best fruity olive oil to serve.
Just get a can of Progresso Macaroni and Bean .Enough for 2 .Prep time 2 minutes.Cook to a simmer and stir often to prevent sticking.Sprinkle some Parmesan !Enjoy

And sometimes those of us who cook mostly from scratch (me, 55 years of cooking and counting) welcome short cut recipes. So there’s room for everyone, I think.
@Bobby G. Ick. Canned processed food with tons of salt. Making your own tastes much, much better and is actually good for you.

The photo shows a more hearty pasta like cavatappi. Elbows and ditalini are much smaller/delicate and don’t work as well in my opinion. Like other comments, better to cook pasta separately and then add to soup at the end. Otherwise pasta soaks up too much liquid. I do this with minestrone soup too.

In my opinion, the traditional ditalini works just fine. It's what my family always uses. The pasta soaks up flavor as it cooks. We only use as much pasta as we will eat. For leftovers, we cook more pasta... also in the soup.

Excellent recipe !!

This was delicious! I did take the time to add a mirepoix (a cup total) and swapped orzo because it was in the pantry, and added 3oz  baby spinach. Wonderful!  Thank you!

Easy! Cut recipe in half except for the broth, used not quite 4 c. I also did the mirepoix of 2 small carrots, 2 celery, small leek, sautéed in the bacon fat and a little olive oil for 7 min before 1 min for the garlic. Simmered more like 11 min. Served with a swirl of pesto but it wasn't really necessary, this is so comforting and good.

Learn from my mistake: do NOT make this with whole wheat pasta for your kids and spouse. No one will like the taste and you'll be stuck eating it for a sad office lunch all week. One of those instances where traditional semolina elbows are the best way.

Made this exactly as written. It's a fast, satisfying weeknight meal. I cut the whole tomatoes in half while they cooked with kitchen shears. It seems a little flat to us. Will double the Italian seasoning next time. Maybe try ground Italian sausage?
While Pasta e Fagioli often starts with a bit of pancetta, it traditionally does not include meat.

@SBGo I followed the recipe. I thought it tasted very “light”. I’m used to a more robust taste. My husband suggested some red pepper flakes but on the whole, it needs some umami flavor. I used pancetta and very good tomatoes, seasoning and added spinach at the end. Not sure what else to add!
For the vegetarians out there: replace the bacon with onion and smoked paprika fried in butter. EXCELLENT weeknight meal.

Wonderful! We are vegetarian,  so I substituted some chopped mini sausages for bacon. We had celery and scallions so I chopped one of each and added it to a Tb. of olive oil along with the chopped garlic and vegetarian sausage. Amazing flavor.

The 20-minute "reddy in" time for this recipe is misleading. For example, it calls for "3 slices bacon, chopped (or 4 ounces diced pancetta)." This means you chop or dice BEFORE the method begins. Since that takes a few minutes, we're up to 22 minutes. Ditto for "thinly sliced" garlic. Another few minutes, maybe 3. Now we're up to 25 minutes. (And. why slice? You get much more garlic flavor from finely chopping. Also,why not a can of chopped tomatoes?) 
Sam Gugino "Cooking to Beat the Clock"

Time estimates do not include chopping, peeling, etc. Also, the recipe already calls for a can 0f tomatoes. (Good San Marzano tomatoes are always sold whole.) Why chop garlic? We want a hint of garlic, not a slap in the mouth of garlic.
To follow the recipe as printed (1/4 teaspoon seasoning?), ends up with little flavor to lift with the vinegar.  I will start with a mirepoix next time.
Nice, easy recipe!  One hint: I would brown the bacon first and then add the garlic. Otherwise your garlic might burn.
I followed the recommendation of adding mirepoix while my diced guanciale browned. I also added much more Italian seasoning. Yummy and quick weeknight meal.
I made this today for lunch. I will cook  it again. I followed recipe exactly. It goes into rotation of soups. Next time I will try it with the mirepoix
Nice and quick, but needs more depth. Next time I’ll add a Parmesan rind, smash in a few anchovy filets and toss in more herbs, dried and fresh.
Don't need chicken broth,use pasta water instead.Dont drain the beans,it will thicken the broth,This is an simple soup don't over do it.
We use chicken broth to add the flavor that was sacrificed by omitting the sautéed celery, onions and carrots. We don't drain the beans because bean juice is yukky.

Pasta fagioli is my favorite soup, so I was excited to try this recipe. I followed the recipe exactly (except added more oregano, basil, rosemary and dried hot pepper) and it’s delicious. I ate 3 bowls and my husband is on his 4th. Thank you NYT!
It’s either Pasta Fagioli or Pasta Fazool - depending on which region of Italy you are from.
For "pasta fazool" the region is probably Brooklyn.
This was really easy and so delicious on a cold winter night. Made as directed except added 4 oz baby spinach with the vinegar after taking off heat.
Fantastic and quick! Perfect for a rainy winter night. Will definitely add half cup more broth next time,

I probably won’t make this again and I don’t say that often for NYT recipes. It’s basically adult Mac n cheese. It really needed a TON more veggies (we did 5oz spinach and it still ended up light), less pasta, and the sauce was lacking flavor
Do you put vegetables in macaroni and cheese? Are you maybe thinking of minestrone?

I inadvertently added TWO cans of tomatoes and so had to double the recipe. However, instead of doubling the pasta ( elbows) I added only 8oz and found this to be more than enough. I also added hot chicken sausage  (  1 package)  and a few pinches of raw sugar. We like this soup a lot, and obviously will have plenty of it  for the freezer! Today is Blizzard Day in CT, and the soup is perfect for it.

Trader Joe’s has chopped Pancetta and adding some of it to the chopped onions etc. some like using will bring another note to the dish with little effort.  Trader Joe’s also has frozen garlic cubes as well (and also ginger). A quick stir of jarred Pesto wouldn’t hurt, either.

I like the idea of adding a mirepoix but that makes it less easy. It is delicious. 

I added extra vinegar to combat the bacon fat, red pepper flakes and some celery seed. Let the seasonings cook in the fat to bloom. I also used orecchiette which cupped the beans so well!! Next time I’ll add kale or spinach for a nice pop of green & veg.
Added spinach and onion. Served with bread. Very good!
My momma , first generation American w a southern Italian mother,  always put chopped celery in her pasta beans as we called it . I do as well and feel it’s an essential ingrédient. Try it

Not great - 4 cups of broth is not enough at all.  Taste was bland and would strongly suggest adding onion, celery and carrot for texture and taste.  This simplified version just doesn’t work.  Stay traditional.
Traditional vs Quick: on a busy weeknight, Quick will win.
Really great recipe! Quick and easy! Hope you have some leftovers for lunch!!!

I thought about adding onion and celery but decided to try to stick to the recipe. I used prosciutto and added 2 tbsp olive oil. I also increased garlic to 6 cloves. Delicious! Will definitely make this again.

Just returned from vacation - no food in the fridge.  This recipe popped up on the NYT Dinner Tonight newsletter in my email while I was on the plane  this afternoon thinking about dinner.  This was perfect! Such easy home cooked comfort food using pantry ingredients.  Made as written.  Topped with lots of parmesan, chilli flakes and flaky salt.  Nothing to complain about here - thanks NYT!
Delicious!  Don’t forget to add a Parmesan rind - extra lovely flavor!!

This has way too much tomato in it.  My Italian mother in law made this probably once a week.  No meat at all.  A few cherry tomatoes, or one large one, or maybe a couple of spoons of leftover sauce.  Celery leaves instead of chopped celery.  In Italy it's the leaves that are valued for flavor.  This soup should be almost white, not too garlicky, always with a parmesan rind and some peperoncini for a touch of heat. Peasant food at its best.

Pretty simple recipe for an authentic tasting pasta fagiolo. I would make a couple minor changes: 1) Cook the pancetta a little longer before adding the garlic - the garlic crisps up so quick and the fat on the pancetta didn't even render 2) crush the tomatoes by hand before adding to the pot - adding them whole made the soup a little too chunky for my liking.
Bobby G. As everybody knows, canned soups usually have way too much sodium. I like shortcuts too, but not if it's going to send my blood pressure up.
Add about 1 tablespoon of anchovy paste, for a serious yet subtle umami boost.  While you’re at it, crumbled Italian sausage goes well, too.
Do you drain the can of tomatoes?
Recipe does not say to do that, so no.
Meh. Lacked flavor.
Is the bacon cooked first before adding?
The bacon is cooked in Step 1. It isn't "added"; it's the original ingredient.
@Jim  you have some good shortcuts, like using spaghetti sauce that has the seasoning already built in, and using the bean liquid for a nutrition bump that also thickens the broth. Nice!
@Pbasile you need chicken broth for flavour! to compensate for missing soffritto at the beginning!

This is a delightful recipe to follow and it yields a scrumptious, soul-warming dish! In addition to dried oregano and basil, I added some freeze-dried chives as well as a pinch or three of Burlap and Barrel’s Silk Chili. The chives imparted their lovely mild oniony flavor and some pop of color, and the Silk Chili added some extra depth and a bit of heat to the dish. Really delish!
I used smoked sausage instead of bacon/pancetta. And carrots and celery. This is wonderful.
I add some chopped chard leaves.
Try it with ceci cooked or canned and drained with rotini or cavatelli.
Pasta e fagioli is best when the beans and pasta are about the same size.
I've been making a version of this once a week for over 40 years. Now I add some carrots and spinach, possible making it more like minestrone. Try it with baby shell pasta!

No matter how you make pasta fazoole,  I can only add that for me,  I always try and use a
ham bone with some ham on it. If i dont have one handy I can  always go to Honey Baked Ham and buy one. Last resort is Italian sausage or even Andouille! Skip the carrots etc

I make something like this but I use chicken sausage - maybe 2 or 3 removed from skin and browned with garlic. Also do Mirepoix, a can of fire roasted tomatoes, small can tomato sauce, & some homemade (or jarred) pesto. I cook the pasta separately and store leftovers separately so it doesnt suck up all the broth & get soggy.

This was quicker and good. I did add a mirepoix after sauteeing the bacon and garlic slices, stirred and sautéed five minutes more, then proceeded with the rest of the ingredients. Garnished the soup with parsley and parmigiana, I don’t think that’s optional. I have a favourite Pasta e Fagioli soup recipe that’s delicious but this one is really good too and I’ll make it again.

I made this this afternoon for lunch  with a few changes and it turned out well. I used turkey bacon and added small dice carrots and celery to brown with it before I added the garlic. I had made some sauteed Tuscan kale for dinner last night and I dumped most of the leftovers into this dish. I also put in a pinch of dried red pepper, enough to just give a warm feeling as the dish goes down your throat, but not be too spicy. It was altogether delicious and I'll definitely make it again.

What a fantastic recipe! Followed as written with the addition of a sautéed onion at the beginning. Questioned the need for two cans of beans and am very glad I followed the recipe. One of the benefits is multiple meals. Expect to add more water on day two and three. Looking forward to making this again.

Please use an extra cup/can of chix broth… this way you have enough liquid in 2nd day and you are not watering down. 
Remember - one CAN (15.5oz) of broth is two cups!
This was delicious just as is!

This is a baseline soup. It needed flavor so I added: one-half lb. of cooked, crumbled Italian sausage; one diced onion; 1/4 c. red wine; 1 tsp each dried basil, Italian seasoning, and oregano, plus 1/2 tsp. each red pepper flakes and fennel seed; and a sliced and quartered zucchini. Perfetto!
I can’t eat legumes… any substitute suggestions?
Really appreciate a quick recipe that IS quick! Very hearty & delicious, especially when you use a small amount of duck pancetta!

Made this as written except that I added what was probably close to 2 TBS of dried Sicilian oregano because I agree with a previous comment that 1/4 TSP would be meaningless.  Delicious and filling.  Will echo another comment about having more stock on hand if necessary.  I used Whole Foods' brand of elbow pasta.  The dish turned out closer to a stew than a soup.  When I went back for seconds, it had thickened even more.  Will reheat leftovers with another cup of stock.

Made with a mirepoix, a splash of white wine, lots of fresh grated parm and two nice parm rinds. Took others’ suggestions to follow the mirepoix with 4 crumbled chicken sausages (removed the casings). Delicious weeknight meal.

We found this so bland. I was trying to make it vegetarian so I did the mirepoix, no bacon. I added a potato… we ended up adding a bunch of lemon and additional seasoning stuff. Not sure I would make this again. Extremely hearty though.

Quick, easy, and delicious! This recipe also works well with chickpeas. And add arugula at the end.

I'd omit the bacon and use olive oil instead. My favorite pasta for a recipe like this, where the pasta cooks in the sauce, is Brami protein pasta curly macaroni. And if I know there will be leftovers, I cook the pasta separately so it doesn't get mushy when reheated.
Growing up, we also called this "Pasta Fazoole"...very, very thick, never noticed tomato, and my grandmother would give it to us in Mason jars.
This was so easy to make on a cold rainy day. I did substitute wheat berries instead of pasta because they keep their firmness, but you do need to simmer it a lot longer. Tasty, quick, a keeper!
Solid base recipe.  Needs more spices.

Delicious as written!

I used boccoli, like in the picture.

Cooked as written and it was delicious!  I'd probably add a little more broth next time - it wasn't very soupy (I know other reviews said the same thing but I generally make the recipe as is the first time).  Definitely a keeper!
What a great recipe!  I love making a nice dish with items already in my pantry.  I upped the garlic and spice to my taste.  I used parmesan packets from my last take pizza.  It was delicious!
This soup turned out so beautifully!  I followed the recipe exactly, and I'll definitely include this in our rotation of dinners that come together easily.

I adapted this recipe to being a vegetarian - so no bacon or chicken broth - and what I had in the pantry. The liquid was two, 14-ounce cans of stewed tomatoes with onions, celery, green peppers and 2 grams added sugar, and 4 cups vegetable broth. Celllentani, the pasta joined by 2 cans of low-sodium Great Northern Beans and 1 teaspoon of apple cider vinegar. Quite tasty.
Easy to prepare and very yummy.
Good enough, but my bf certainly didn't love it.  Tbh neither did I. I even added carrots, celery, onion, and some parmesan rind - but it still lacked some level of complexity and umph.

Very nice! Like others, I added some carrots and onions while frying the pancetta. I also added 300g of Italian sausage. I needed closer to 5 1/2 or 6 cups of broth. Will definitely make it again. Lots of possible variations.

In step 3, when the recipe says to mash some of the beans against the side of the pot, I instead used a handheld immersion blender to puree some of the contents (which included carrots, onion, and celery), just as I do when making minestrone.  That thickened it and made it more to my taste (though I see from other readers' comments who added more broth, that they must prefer a thinner, soupier product).
