added 1/2 lb of sweet italian sausage, used full 16 oz of pasta, pre-cooked the pasta to al dente which worked out great, went cray-cray with spices, fresh-ground fennel, cumin, oregano, thyme. used onion and celery. next time (sans 20-month-year-old grandson) will add some red pepper flakes. diced sweet peppers would be a nice addition. the toddler loved it for dinner AND lunch the next day, parents loved it because the toddler loved it.  big hit all around. and yeah, i liked it too.
Savoury dish! Made a mirepoix as I had the time. Did a 50/50 on chicken and beef broth. Added laurel, thyme and rosemary. It really gained a ton of flavor after two days in the fridge.
Solid recipe. Easy to make and nice flavor. I did add some mushroom I had lying around and some bok choy to add more vegetables. I would make again.
Excellent, forgiving base recipe and perfect for a rainy day. Added broccoli and tofu. Will make again and again.

Simple and satisfying! Omitted the egg but would probably add next time, along with more veggies. Used homemade chicken broth instead of water for the soup, and I think that helped provide more flavor/body. Did not need the gochugaru. The gochujang and kimchi provided enough spice as is.

Super easy & fast. I used 2.5 c of chicken broth (next time I’ll use 2) instead of water and went light on the soy sauce. I didn’t cut up the kimchi, I like a substantial chunky broth. I added both the egg and tofu. Five stars! Can’t believe it had such great flavor but came together in minutes.
DO NOT DISCARD THE SEASONING PACKET. Shin Ramyun packets specifically (and ramen packets generally) make an insanely good popcorn seasoning. You're welcome.

Broth was a little thin, even with the addition of oyster mushrooms, bok choy and eggs as recommended by Greta.  Will try adding beef or chicken broth in the future to round out the spice.  Otherwise quick and flavorful.

Really fantastic recipe—I used way more soy sauce than directed and added sesame seeds, garlic, and mushrooms. The broth was great for me but I plan to add a bit of corn starch to thicken if necessary in the future.
The temperature dropped 30-40 F here in 2 days.  This recipe is fixing all of that. It's perfect for this day!  Will add to the rotation.  Yum!

Very good base of a recipe. I added Shrimp, carrots, mushroom and garlic as well and it came together great. I'd recommend first sauteing the shrimp in the pan and then pulling them once cooked. Then the carrots for 2 minutes, then the mushrooms and garlic, then starting step 1.

Good. But not any better than regular Sapporo Ichiban with egg and kimchi added as a topping. Doubled the recipe. No need to cut up kimchi. Add egg as soon as noodles separate from their form or they'll be overcooked by the time the eggs are done. Added bok choy cooked w noodles and then tofu and salad greens in the bowl w ramen poured over. Didn't use gochugaru, didn't need it for spice level. Mushrooms would be a good add.
Veggies is a must, not an option
Has anybody made this with rice noodles to make it gluten free?
Delicious! Definitely will make this again! I did not cut the kimchi, which added some body to the broth.

Comes together quickly and tastes excellent. I got a rotisserie chicken and shredded up the meat to add as a protein. Also, feel like I easily doubles all the spices. We had sliced limes at the end and dipped some baguette bread in the broth. 10/10 kid review and the adults liked it, too!
gochugaru is red pepper flakes in Korean. When I was growing up, my brother and I debated a lot over whether egg yolks should be stirred into soup or left whole.
What would be wrong with doubling (or more) this recipe?

Delightful! I was fortunate to have  a quart worth of homemade kimchi from a friend (I tripled the recipe). I tripled the recipe, adding a full bunch of scallions + garlic + many shitakes—and followed users' recommendations for chicken broth over water, leaving no need for soy sauce. Add baby bok choy added alongside the tofu cubes for a 10 min simmer, so the tofu becomes somewhat saturated with flavor.

Condiments I lined up: more fresh scallions, sauteed corn, soft boiled eggs, nori sheets!

Fantastic - 30 minutes start to finish and excellent flavor. My only suggestion would be to back off the water a little - 2.5 cups was fine, but 2 would have made for a slightly more intense broth. Add fresh vegetables toward the end if using.

This one wins the Old Person Hippie Food prize for the month of May! Made as written with egg, Britt's Back Market Kimchi and a handful of broccoli florets, but with this one parlor trick: cooked a small fillet of salmon in the toaste,r oven and poured the noodle soup over it to serve. OUTSTANDING.
Substituted de-stemmed chopped Kale for chard and used chili crisp oil with the coconut saute instead of just red pepper flakes.
Delicious! Cooked exactly as written. Substituted kale for the chard as mentioned in the note. Don’t forget to squeeze a little lime on the finished dish. Will definitely be making this one again.
Pleasantly surprised! I used Tuscan Kale, but otherwise followed the recipe. Served it with wild rice. Will make again.
Outstanding!  Doubled the chard and used steelhead trout.  This will be a "go to" recipe for me.
Farmed steelhead is a great budget-friendly swap for salmon. It’s always available at our local co-op for a lot less than wild salmon.
Any substitute for chard?  It's not available around here except during the summer.
Recipe note suggests kale
I used lacinato kale and it worked really well.

We used the chard, my husband didn't really care for the chard and next time I will try the kale. The recipe was really good. Followed the instructions and it came out perfect. Served it with roasted sweet potatoes.
Forgot to get shredded coconut at the store so used unsweetened coconut mild we had on hand.  Still very delicious.

Very good! 
I found that 10 minutes skinside down and a quick flip for two minutes was the right formula for my desired doneness. I might try popping the lid on next time to get a little bit of surface steam and avoid flipping.

I made this with kale and followed the recipe as recommended. I did end up sprinkling a bit of water over the kale to soften it up and steam a bit. The coconut kale was really flavorful and delicious. I found that the salmon was a bit overcooked in the oven - I may consider steaming it next time? Or maybe cook it in parchment? I think a silkier texture would go well with the chewy kale.
Would it work to cook it in a covered pan the whole time?

This is an outstanding recipe I will put in heavy rotation. I made it with white chard and doubled the chard, and used canola oil instead of olive oil. Otherwise I followed the recipe exactly and wouldn’t change a thing.
This was really delicious. I used kale instead of chard because that’s what I had in the fridge. Otherwise, I followed the recipe exactly. Love Ali Slagle’s recipes!

I used the Swiss shard recipe on its own since my garden is heavily producing it now. I made one batch exactly as is and it was great. Made the second batch and added some yellow raisins for fun. Love the coconut.

title: Crispy Braised Pork and Peppers Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: This turned out fantastic. Many readers and cooks have shared how they made adjustments to recipes in the past. I’ll say I’m in that camp as well to this recipe. What I realize about recipes is they are a base unless one wants to follow to the rule. Isn’t that what cooking is all about. Anyhoo, I modified this by adding  a few dry spices to the pork and added Spanish sofrito and in the end finished it off with cilantro (parsley too can add a touch of green freshness) and lime to cut the heat.

Delicious. I added an onion and browned some sausage I had on hand and added for the last hour. I also stirred the pot a few times during the process to brown more bits and wouldn't change a thing. A keeper.

Delicious, layered flavors, with a delightful array of different textures! Scaled down for just two, used some white wine to supplement fluid while braising, otherwise followed recipe. Served it with German spaetzle pasta (dried), a fine pairing. Will be making it again.

Tasty and versatile. Served with Caribbean style red beans and rice, but can see this with pasta/in a taco/on mashed potatoes.

I also let it sit overnight to remove the fat cap. Maybe was just my pork but it was crazy fatty. Must have removed at least a full cup. The residual broth was fantastic and not too rich.

I added an onion and used beef broth instead of water to give it some depth. This could be done in the slow cooker but you lose out on some of the roasted components by baking with the lid off. Ate this over polenta which was good, but made a sandwich the next day on soft French bread that was the best version of this.

Loved this! I made a big pan of slow roasted tomatoes that I mixed in at the end which was an amazing addition - could be substituted with sun dried tomatoes. Ate with some homemade garlic bread. Next time I might double the amount of bell pepper because the cook down a lot and are so good. Perfect Sunday dinner.
Easy & delicious; perfect for a cold winter day!  And it doesn't break the bank.

I made this as directed (I usually try that first unless a recipe looks off or else later goes sideways in the cooking process).  This one worked, was easy and delicious. I used 2 large fresno peppers and I like the combination of heat and sweetness (from the peppers).  Given all the meat juices and vegetable (garlic, peppers) rendering into the sauce I don't think any extra broth addition is needed.  Like the French bread idea and my son is looking forward to leftovers on tacos.

Can someone explain to me how “prep time” is defined???? I usually add about 15 minutes to the recipes in this app. But this one? It took me 45 minutes to prep the peppers and pork. Browning in batches, four, took about 20 minutes. Dinner is going to be eaten  pretty late tonight.
@Chris-SFO i agree! If you don’t have your own sous-chefs, add 15 minutes for every three ingredients listed. 😂

Delish!  Husband who made it (yeah!) put only one pepper in as I can’t take spicy food.  Next time we’ll add two to try it out - one added a hint of great flavor w low heat.  Looking forward to tomorrow’s leftovers!
Could this be made in a crock pot?  Asking cause my husband really dislikes crock pot meals.
@Lee Greenwald and you want to make him unhappy?🤣🤣
@AC Wisner. Thanks for asking!  No, I don’t.  And this is delicious!
Great flavor on top of polenta. Made as directed and is now one of my favorites.

I ended up adding 1 jalapeno and 1 yellow onion, skipping all the stovetop steps and throwing it all in the crockpot on high for 6 hours. Very tasty and tender! Even if there was fat that wasn’t trimmed it didn’t end up chewy or stringy. Put it on Hawaiian rolls for sliders. Very easy for the Super Bowl party!

Super delish! I’ve got a lot of weak taste buds in my house so I only added like 10-15 jalapeño rings from a homemade jar of maple jalapeño cowboy candy and it gave just a TINY bit of spice. Still too much for my kids apparently. 😆 It looked too watery so I let it go another 30 mins. Don’t do it! Once I shredded the pork there was basically no liquid left. 😩 I will definitely make again but stick to the 2.75-3 hour cook time. I can totally see myself eating this in a taco, rice, noodles, on top of cooked veggies… yum! Makes a bunch and probably freezes great.
The peppers and braising are game changers for what is otherwise just a pulled pork recipe. Delicious and so easy!

I would make this again but reduce the liquid and add onion. Made as written except added some Jamaican/Louisiana spices. Used bell peppers and Pablano peppers. Also added roasted tomatoes. It does need more depth of flavor. Makes a lot of liquid as written. Used beef broth instead of water, but would reduce to 1 cup.
Very similar to pork chilindron, a Spanish classic: to convert, char and peel the peppers, add paprika and sauteed onions, and use olive oil rather than a neutral oikl
This seems ideal for a slow cooker, too. How would one go about converting it to a slow cooker?

I haven't done this yet, but am going to try it in the slow cooker. I'll reduce the water a bit, maybe to one cup, and will cook it on low for 6-8 hours. Once it's done and shredded, I may transfer it to a sheet pan and pop it under the broiler a bit to crisp it up.
Delicious! I resisted the urge to add onions and was pleasantly surprised with the depth of flavor without them.

@Portia R IKR? Since I found out, I actually need to avoid onions, I’ve been doing a ton of cooking without them. I thought they were a secret ingredient to everything but it turns out most recipes are just fine without.

Don't add any extra liquid (you'll be tempted). When I first put it in the oven, there was very little liquid and a pile of peppers / meat just sitting on top. Within about 30 minutes, both the meat and peppers released enough liquid that it was properly braising. 

At the end, we didn't have crispy pork, but we had a really delicious sauce.

I made this today and followed the directions exactly. OMG this is so good! I was worried about all that vinegar but it really smoothed out. I served it as a hot sandwich but looking forward to serving it with mashed potatoes.  I added 2 hot peppers- next time I will use 3. So good, make this and thank you Ali.

My lumpy mashed potatoes worked really well with this.  I used a 4 lb pork butt instead of shoulder as that is what the butcher had. Used white wine vinegar.  3 hours was perfect.  So so good. Will repeat this for sure.

One of my new favorite meals in the Dutch oven. Added an onion, replaced the water with beef broth, and added a few chipotle peppers in adobo sauce to give it a smokey heat flavor. Poured over Zatarain’s dirty rice to finish it off. Incredible.

Had to make this almost immediately after seeing the recipe and it didn’t disappoint. Next time would kick up the heat with more chilis. Since the pork gets shredded anyway, I’d sear in larger pieces as that’s the most tedious part. Otherwise super easy, flavorful and versatile!
It’s a keeper, but next time I’ll add onions.
Has anyone successfully done this in a slow cooker?  Absolutely deliciously.  In steady rotation here!
This was great! Followed the recipe with the addition of parsley before serving.  I don't think it needed anything else. Terrific depth of flavor.

title: Chicken and White Bean Stew Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: I love recipes like this but I would sauté the chicken with the onions first, wilt the greens in same, then add liquids & beans. Due to dietary issues I need greens like kale & chard to be well cooked. And I like the added bit of flavor that comes from browning the meat first.
Give it a squeeze of lemon too!

Had chicken in my fridge that I didn't want to deal with and then found this - all I had to do was throw the chicken in the pot which was exactly what I needed.  Super easy prep.  Made as written, with plenty of salt and pepper, minus the herb sprigs (none on hand) and used spinach instead of kale.  Smashed beans made it somewhat creamy.  Served over some leftover rice with a shred of parm on top.  Delicious.

Wicked Good.  If I had made it as-is, it would’ve been perfect. But…. I had a sick wife that wanted extra broth. I doubled the broth to two quarts.  I also added 4 sliced up medium carrots. It was perfect. Oh, I also added in a parm rind for the last 1/2 of the cooking.  She loved it.
@Joe Pro Adding the parm rind! Thank you for this tip. Never would have thought of it and it is a great way to not waste that last but of parm AND flavor soup.
@Joe Pro was just going to comment that a parm rind would be perfect here.
@Joe Pro parm rind is always delicious!

Added 2 tsp grated ginger.  Lovely lighter flavor, a great alternative to traditional chicken soup. Can easily adjust other add-ins, add spice, chilies, as desired. Next time might add 3rd can of beans and more as it there was a lot of chicken.
@Liberty adding grated ginger was great!
look up the NY Times recipe "Braised White Beans and Greens With Parmesan" for a more tasty version
@KM Ah, yeah, but that recipe does not have chicken.
@KM When I saw this I thought “hmmmm, think I just made this!” But yes, it was the one you posted. That was very good, maybe just add the chicken, if you want.
@KM thanks for the tip! I used this same recipe as a base then added in rotisserie chicken  and more chicken stock afterward. It smells and tastes fantastic.

Delicious and incredibly easy. I used ingredients on hand and made some modifications: I didn’t have fresh herbs so used a heavy hand with dried rosemary, probably twice as much garlic for extra flavor, and a bit of red pepper flakes. I used the four cups of broth and probably added at least half that again (or more) in water. I used four cans of Great Northern Beans and after removing the chicken I dipped out and smashed probably a can and a half’s worth to thicken the broth up and transform from soup to stew. I added several handfuls of shredded collards instead of kale, which cooks up beautifully and went well with the flavors, I added more red pepper flakes at the end which really punched it up. I think, for me, the recipe as originally written could lean towards a bit bland if you don’t add a bit more zing in the way of spices to the cooking or a drizzle of olive oil and/or lemon, etc to the finished bowl. Start to finish in under an hour and a great recipe where you can pull chicken from the freezer and don’t have to wait for it to fully thaw out first - just have to adjust the cooking temp and time a bit to compensate.

I’m no cook but I wanted to try making this. Unfortunately, I misread the ingredient list and only bought one can of cannellini beans instead of two. Being snowed in, I couldn’t just run out to the market. Luckily, I asked my new buddy electronic Alexa if I could add a can of corn which was in my pantry. She said go for it but it would change the taste (sweetness) and texture (crunchiness). OMG was it fabulous. Added to make it again!
@Ken AI is the best when it comes to helping with recipes, or creating new ones! Glad you found a new friend!

Really great easy dish, super warm and fuzzy for a cold night. Used some dried cracked rosemary because I had all the other ingredients. I also used frozen chicken tenders, just defrosted in the microwave. Half recipe was perfect for two.

It was a bit thin; I thought it could have used another can of beans, allowing for more mashing. And, just judging from the picture I decided to throw in some casually chopped baby Bellas. I took the suggestion to add the chicken early, but it still took a long time to get the chicken up to temp. And the breasts resisted shredding. We agreed that next time we’d add the beans, keep the Bellas, use thighs, and not hold back on the spinach.

This was very easy and delicious. I made a few changes:
I had chicken thighs on hand so cut those up and browned them with the onion. I let the onion cook for five-ish minutes first. 
I didn’t have any herb sprigs so I added some dried rosemary and sage to the onion. 
I added a few sprinkles of crushed red pepper with the broth. This really added a nice hint of heat and flavor. 
I used spinach as that’s what I had on hand.
Really nice for a cold night.  I used an immersion blender to mash up some of the beans and added a Parmesan rind as suggested by another reviewer.  Plan to freeze half.
This was super easy and delish!  I used sage sprigs, low salt broth, and generously added salt and pepper

Like KCartire I had chicken in my fridge and I was uninspired last night so I made the recipe as stated subbing baby spinach for the Kate and using fresh sage from my garden. Despite the five star rating i feared it would be bland.  It wasn't.  I was deiighted with it.  There are lots of suggestions here for how to improve it, add lemon, browin the chicken first, add red papper flakes, and I'm sure they would be good. However, this is delicious as is.
This was super yum! I wanted to make it a bit more peppery so I added lots of black pepper and tumeric for color added flavor and hopefully some anti-inflammatory benefits. Will be making this again!
Would love a filter on the comments where I could choose between "made as written" and "made with modifications."
@Teresa very few people actually make the recipe

Sorry bur I always find this hilarious, especially the way this is worded:
melt the (unsalted)butter over medium. When foaming, add the onion, garlic and herb sprig and season with salt
If I used dry beans, how would I change this recipe?

Used both fresh sage and rosemary. Added crushed red pepper flakes with onions and garlic, further to suggestions from others.  Next time I will roughly chop/slice garlic. Also, further to others’ suggestions, I added Parmesan rind in last 10 minutes.  I don’t know that I will do that again. Also squeezed in half a lemon right before serving. I agree with others that it would be advisable to add a third can of beans. Served with warm baguette. A real hit with the whole family. Five stars!
One of, if not THE, best soup/stew I've ever made.  Added a parmesean rind with the liquid.  So comforting and delicious, rich without being heavy.

Made almost as directed, but low butter had me opt to render two thick bacon strips, saving crumbles for garnish (highly recommend) and no fresh herbs made me sub a rosemary sea salt which worked well with both unsalted broth and low sodium beans. Satisfying comfort food but more brothy than expected. Next time more beans, and immersion blender to partially puree before adding kale. And more garlic. Measurements for kale, garlic, and onion would have helped.
This was a really yummy dinner, very clean tasting.  I added a teaspoon of red pepper flakes, cuz it was cold here yesterday.
I added carrots and mushrooms the 2nd time I made it. It added to it. Also, it's better the 2nd day (like most soups, stews, & chilis).

made this almost as written, the only alterations I made, and method where I browned all of my chicken first, and then cooked just the onions and the butter for a while until they had really begun to caramelize, then I added the garlic. I did need some acid, so I add a couple teaspoons of soy sauce as well as white wine vinegar, and just a dash of Worcestershire sauce. I didn't have any fresh herbs on me so I wound up using time, rosemary, and a few Bay leaves. all of this made inside of a homemade chicken stock. I double the amount of kale because we enjoy kale. this is great on night one, however this made even better leftovers night two.
How to modify for a pressure cooker?

I think for me I would reduce the broth by a cup and add an extra can of beans. But I enjoy a thicker consistency. I also butterflied the chicken breast and seared it in a pan before adding it to the pot. All in all it was an excellent winter dish that will be making again!

After reading through the comments, I added chopped carrots to the onion garlic mixture.  The grocery store only had a pkg of "poultry herb blend".  Several people said the stew was a bit bland so I used 2 sprigs each of rosemary, sage, and thyme.  As suggested, I used 4 cans of cannellini beans, mashing 1 1/2 cans. I added parmesan rinds from the freezer with the spinach and about a  tablespoon of lemon juice.  Served with parmesan cheese.  Delicious and easy.
This was great! I added a parm rind at the same time as the broth and removed it with the chicken and it made it a little extra creamy. Nonna tricks for the win!

Very nice on a chilly, rainy winter night. I used boneless thighs, added two sliced carrots with the onions and a Parmesan rind with the broth as suggested in the comments. A couple shakes of hot flakes suited us. Other than that followed the recipe. Fast, easy and good. Served with salad.

Delicious recipe! The squeeze of lemon to the bowl of stew was a nice touch.  I added some chili crisp oil because I love spice.  Some sourdough bread is great with this stew too.

I think next time I’ll add some ginger because #1. I love ginger!
Yummy but a little bland. Unlike other bland things, it still really had the warm yummy rich feel. Agree that adding some zing with some chili oil and lime helped quite a bit for me.

Don’t trash those chard stems.  Dice, sauté in a little olive oil or butter and sauté till soft about 15min.  I made the recipe as printed and tasted - very good. Then added the sautéed stems - added a nice crunch and did not diminish flavor.  A keepers.  Would make a ice breakfast meal too.

I had bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs that I needed to use so I made this recipe. When I threw the chicken in I kicked myself that I hadn’t thought to brown the chicken first and use the fat for cooking the onions, but I was still amazed at how much flavor this soup had with basically no spices. I also used dried great northern beans cooked in the Instant Pot—I can’t find dried cannellinis around here to save my life (and yeah, I could have used canned, which I often do, but I had the time to make dried). I cooked them to the slightly mushy stage, which gave the soup a nice creamy texture without having to mash the beans. Will make this one again. Nice comfort food for a blizzard!
@Natalie cooking time for instant pot please?

I used olive instead of butter and used three can of beans instead of two. Whenever I use canned beans I soften them up a bit before adding by boiling for 5 minutes with a half tsp of baking soda. I love this recipe and will make it again.
Super easy, delicious and even better with a parm rind!
Will try that next time, thanks!
Add a cored and sliced fennel bulb, and forget the chicken breast. Use a quqrter-pound of Italian sausage for flavor. The small amount of fat in the sausage will be offset by the kale and fennel.
@Mary Quirolo thanks for the fennel suggestion! Such an amazing addition!!!
Title says "stew"; blurb/instructions say "soup."  Which is it, please?
Definitely a winner. Strong ROI compared to the effort required.

Quite good! Even the 9 and 13 year old approved.
1. Used chicken thighs. Browned them in the pot first. Removed to plate.
2. Cooked the onions, garlic, and butter in the chicken fat.
3. Did not add herbs until chicken, beans, broth were added to the pot. Let herbs (sage) infuse the broth.
4. Simmer longer to add depth and reduce liquid. 
5. Serve with hot sauce. I liked it with Tobasco, my partner liked it with Dave's Gourmet Carolina Reaper Hot Sauce. And of course bread and butter.

Disappointed. I didn’t love this like I thought I would. It seemed to be missing something—maybe some spices other than the sprigs of sage or rosemary. Adding lemon juice helped and so did Parmesan cheese.

Delicious! Comforting and easy. I didn’t have all the same ingredients but followed the recipe mostly  w black and white (great northern) beans; cooked chicken separate to add my son’s favorite seasonings (paprika garlic and onion powder salt and pepper ) and used Swiss chard. Crushing the beans a bit was a good trick to learn! Thank you Ali Slagle.
Outstanding! Made almost exactly as written. Used spinach. Use more pepper and salt than you think!

From other’s comments, I cut up chicken first, fried it in 2 Tbsp oil, 2 Tbsp butter and seasoned heavily with dried thyme, rosemary, Zatar, S&P.  Removed chicken, added more oil to pan and continued with onions and garlic, (cook 5 minutes stirring frequently), added two cans of drained and rinsed white beans (cook 5 minutes stirring frequently), added broth (5 more minutes, lid on), remove lid and add escarole, just to wilt. Off heat, add cooked chicken to soup, ladle to bowls, add Parmesan per bowl. 

Next time I will try garlic, ginger, turmeric for spices, and coconut milk instead of broth and have escarole salad on the side instead of in the soup. Not crazy about cooked greens in soup.
To make this vegan, use soy curls and vegan butter.

I consider this recipe to be a diamond in the rough.  It's very good, but it needs polishing.  I made it close to exactly as written but added some sliced mushrooms and a cup full of mixed frozen vegetables to the spinach.  For extra seasonings, I added a tsp of za'atar.  In spite of the additions, it was "tasty but bland" my spouse claimed.  Be generous with the salt and pepper and kick it up with whatever spices you think would compliment it, and finish it off with a squeeze of lemon juice.
I like to substitute bone-in chicken thighs since the bones impart more flavor.

This recipe is basically the same as one I’ve been making for years. But, along with the addition of the chicken broth, I add a piece of the rind from Parmesan Reggiano and allow it to simmer in the soup along with the onion, garlic and greens, for about an hour. Adding the beans along with 1/3 cup of sherry before serving.
Great, easy, highly satisfying dish.  As others suggested, added celery, carrots and a parm rind.  Served with farfalle pasta.  Two teaspoons of balsamic boosted the umami nicely!

I made a half recipe pretty much as written, bit I used Great Northern  instead of cannellini beans.  This dish is absolutely delicious.  I added the kale at the same time as the chicken so it would be more tender and I added a squirt of lemon juice at the very end.  The next time, I will add another can of beans so I can smash more.  It eats more like a soup than a stew, but who cares!  This is a definite keeper… so quick, so easy, and so healthy!

The flavor of this dish was terrific but the chicken texture became very tough!  The two fork shredding method was new to me and perhaps I did it incorrectly?  The chicken was inedible.  AND, I promise I had a low simmer and did not overcook the breasts.

Kim, 
Sometimes this happens to me even when I know I didn't overcook.  I think it's the "luck of the draw" with purchasing chicken breasts.  Once in a while they're so tough and rubbery, they're inedible.  Cut up and feed to the dog or throw outside for critters.
One theory is "wooden breast" - the chickens are raised so fast that the muscle and/or soft tissues don't mature properly for weight gain time-line.  Sad state of our modern day factory farmed chicken.  
Id say give it another try.

Super good. I took the advice of others in the comments and pre- braised the chicken (in the same pot as the onion mix), tossed in some mushrooms, added a dash of cayenne, cut down on broth and pre- braised the kale. Will definitely make again. Next time I’ll add a second can of beans as I added only one.
I cooked this exactly to the recipe, except that I substituted 1/2 cup of chicken stock with 1/2 cup dry red sherry. It really amped up the flavor!

Made as directed except: used thighs, added some diced pancetta and sauteed with the onions, and squeezed some lemon in each bowl when serving to my family. As a monster snow and ice storm approaches, it made for quite a cozy and delicious meal!

Super simple and delicious. I seasoned and browned the chicken first, then removed it and proceeded with the recipe as written. A generous squeeze of lemon as a finish added a lot. Will definitely make again.
Really loved this - easy to make. Agreed like the kale a little softer.

Not sure how this earned so many 5 star ratings. Mine is an overly garlicky, rubbery chicken breast, otherwise bland mess. No evidence of the rosemary sprig I used. I added some powdered ginger to try to dial back the garlic, but nothing about this is 5 stars. Would likely have been far better with a shredded rotisserie chicken or a mix of chicken breast and thigh, not to mention more than just salt and pepper as seasoning. Will not make again.
Great easy recipe. I liked it with adding additional rosemary and beans.

I was so excited about this recipe and very reluctantly disappointed in the outcome. Yes, it’s easy, but honestly a bit too simple. We added lemon, Calabrian chiles and Parmesan at the table, but also considered soy sauce & chile crisp. By itself, it was pretty boring, I’m sad to say.
Is there a crockpot version?
LOVED this. Couldn't be easier. I tried once to dress it up with some saffron and it was better as is. That'll teach me.
I think boneless thighs would be even better.
Made this last night.  Tasty but needs a little goosing.  Next time I'll add some red pepper flakes, and at least three rosemary sprigs.
My husband made this day before the snow storm.  He used leftover rotisserie chicken, spinach instead of kale, an extra can of beans and a bit of lemon.  Perfect snow day meal!

One of my recent time-saving hacks is to buy the pre-packaged, pulled breast meat from a rotisserie chicken(s). They have this at my local Kroger and at Costco. I'm going to use that in my freezer to make this tomorrow night.

This was the best comfort food that I’ve enjoyed in a while! I added a few cut up already cooked mini Yukon gold potatoes, and I used a rotisserie chicken, I also only used half of the broth stated, as I did not want a soupy soup. Fast, easy and delicious.
How do I make this in a crock pot?

I've been stuck in a hotel apartment during this recent storm.  Here's the cheap version: used boneless chicken thighs sauteed with Mrs. Dash and olive oil, can of canelinni beans, and can of low salt Ol Glory greens, dash of minced onions.  I daresay on a day with 0F windchill, my version tasted pretty good.  Oh, did I mention the Tabsco?

I made it pretty much as-is, except I used leftover chicken that I roasted yesterday. Next time I make it, I may add more veggies, and finish it off with a squeeze of lemon juice. Of I had it, I’d pair it with a slice of crusty baguette. It’s perfect for this sub-zero weather we’re having ❄️

Super easy, but unfortunately, very disappointing. I found the stew extremely one-note, even after adding a squeeze of lemon juice and grated Parmesan on top. I appreciate it for its simplicity but it won’t be making it into my regular rotation. If I made this again, I’d seriously up the spice amount (paprika or Italian spices), or add a glug of white wine to deglaze the pan. I wanted to love this, but it was a miss for me. Such a bummer!

I like to brown chicken first in olive oil for extra flavor before I use it in soups or stews. I just have a hard time putting raw chicken into the mix. Browned the chicken and did everything the same - except added some celery. Came out great.

I agree with several comments that the soup/stew is rather bland as offered. I added the parmesan rind (I often do that to a soup) and some lemon juice. I also added more chicken stock. I still think it is missing something to take it to deeper level....not yet sure what that is. Maybe a splash of Worcestershire? Please offer advice if you have found a way to boost the flavor/
@Gail try the Better than Bullion reduced salt chicken flavor. We add this for a bit of depth to lots of recipes.
subbed ghee instead of butter. used dried thyme and oregeno instead and it was delicious
Explodes with flavor!  I cooked with a quartered lemon that really enhanced the flavor.

It felt like too much broth…I would start with just enough to cover the chicken and keep adding as you see fit. Also, salt and pepper to onions and garlic in the beginning. Still great though. Added tons of lemon and parm at the end!
I wanted to like this but found it very bland. Added fresh dill and some lemon juice for a bit of acidity. Won’t make it again.

I had some chicken breasts that were getting close to their best by date, and zero inspiration or motivation. I’m so happy I found this! This recipe punches way above its weight - it was super flavorful and very easy! It was a hit with my type-2 diabetic husband and my toddler. I used shallots instead of onions, and spinach instead of kale, because that’s what I had on hand. Otherwise followed the recipe closely. This is going in the rotation for sure.
This dish as presented is over the top for someone with diabetes. A serving of beans is a 1/2 cup, so a single can of beans is plenty in my opinion.

Excellent. Made as written. The rosemary sprig adds wonderful depth and reduces the need for salt. The only change I'll make next time: add a third can of beans to increase the creaminess. The broth is a touch too watery.

LOVED THIS!! My husband couldn’t stop raving. My tips:
- must use good homemade stock, it really makes the dish.
- Make a 2-3 hours before serving to let flavors meld, if possible 
- 12 oz fresh spinach worked great
- Boneless thighs were what we had on hand, would use again next time. Brown the chicken first.
- 1 small sprig rosemary next time - 2 large was a little too much
- 3 cups (not 2) Rancho Gordo Cassoulet beans with some of their liquid - these were in my freezer and perfect in this soup 
- add 2 sliced carrots after stock comes to a simmer
- add lemon juice (1/2 large lemon) toward the end
5 stars Fabulous especially on this snowbound Saturday.

Used a rotisserie chicken, plus some skin for flavor (can remove later). Used dried sprigs of rosemary, sage and thyme and left them in longer, which added depth. Used 2 cans of rinsed beans, and added a 3rd, lightly rinsed, to help thicken. If you have an immersion blender, use it! It will puree some of the beans along with the garlic cloves and spices - yum! Topped hot individual bowls with baby spinach leaves so they wilted but remained bright green. Served with lemon wedges and parmesan.
I’m wondering if substituting olive oil for butter would work. I’m trying to limit my butter intake. Anyone tried this?

@olive oil for butter 
Should work fine. Optional: For buttery taste, add 1 T as the last step before  serving.
Absolutely the easiest meal and most delicious….

I took a chance and brought a pot of this delicious soup to a gathering of friends, where we ate it with a loaf of good, crusty bread.  Everyone loved it . . . but we all thought it had way too much chicken.  Next time I'll just use one breast (and take the advice to add some red pepper flakes).

Wow, much better than I expected, very tasty!  I used mirapoix as I always have that on hand. I put the chicken in before the broth and beans and let it sear just a bit for flavor.  Took a bit to get it up to a simmer in my dutch oven.
massaged the kale first and covered the dutch oven to make sure it very soft.  wonderful.
After reading some comments I used chicken stock, an extra can of beans and thighs instead of breasts, but otherwise followed as is. Wonderful satisfying meal.

Threw the chicken breasts atop the onions, garlic, and herbs ten minutes before adding the stock -- and used dried rosemary, because it's winter and who wants to pay for a package of sprigs of which half will go to waste? Excellent result, will make again!
A little bland but won't offend anyone!
I puréed most of the beans since my husband thinks he doesn’t like beans!  lol. Was delicious and he loved it. Super easy and very creamy.

Made this in my Instant Pot, followed recipe as written with escarole but added 2 tsp chopped fresh ginger with the garlic,because I had some and love it in some versions of chicken soup. Credit to previous commenter for the idea

This was quite easy, didn’t take too much time more than the recipe stated (one perennial issue with Times recipes), and was quite delicious. I left the rosemary in, with no ill effects, and fished the stems out at the end. Made 1.5 x the quantity; looks like we will get eight servings from it. And three hours later the kitchen still smells great!
I used less butter and added pepper flakes. I also cut the chicken tk bite sized pieces first to save a step. Browned chicken slightly with onion

Good! Made it pretty much as written, using Spinach and Rosemary rather than Kale and Sage because that's what I had on hand. I followed a suggestion or two in the comments, such as adding an extra can of beans (excellent suggestion) and lightly browning the chicken breasts (not sure.) The chicken was a little rubbery, maybe from the sautéeing then stewing? Served with rice and squeezes of lemon, with salad and hot rolls and butter on the side and everyone enjoyed it. Will tweak and try again!

I made this stew this weekend and my husband and I loved it.  I took the herb sprigs out as directed (I used rosemary and sage) but ended up chopping the leaves and adding them back in as I wanted a more savory herb taste.  The nest time I make it I will add the herbs chopped from the beginning and also toss in a parmesan rind.  We served it over rice.
So simple and so good. A new favorite in our house.
Agree that browning the chicken (thighs) first adds a lot of flavor. An old-fashioned potato masher makes smashing the beans easy.

I browned the chicken a bit before adding the beans and broth and used a mix of ground thyme, rubbed sage, dried tarragon, and crushed rosemary instead of the sprigs. Added a whole lemon's worth of juice at the end (and honestly could have added a second). Topped with parmesan and served with a side of sourdough. Delicious!

Used a whole rotisserie chicken, minus the skin and bones of course! It was about 1 pound of cooked chicken.  Used sprigs of rosemary and thyme, and a 5 ounce package of pre-washed baby spinach and arugula.  Added the chicken after mashing some of the beans.   Delicious!
This was a great use of spare rotisserie chicken!  Just sautée the cut up chicken with the butter & onions, otherwise the chickens often too bland. And grab some sourdough!

I left my sprig of rosemary in until I added the greens, Swiss chard of which the stems were chopped and added with the onions. Tossed in a piece of Grana Padano rind and, because this reminded me of white chicken chili, a can of diced Hatch chiles.

Made this one my own a bit -- it was delicious!

- Doubled the chicken
- Added 1 shallot 
- Added 3 green onions
- Added 2 cups Costco wild mushrooms (dried & rehydrated)
- Added 2 additional cups chicken broth, plus 1 cup water
- Added cayenne, cumin to season
- Topped with Parmesan

This is very good for the combo of ease and taste and healthiness   I used rotisserie chicken and threw it in at the end after having used an immersion blender to emulsify the beans somewhat.  I used Parmesan rinds and a little bit of lemon at the end.

This was excellent. A few minor changes: I seared the meat first just to brown it on both sides before cooking the onion and garlic. I added the kale with the beans and chicken instead of at the end. I tripled the rosemary, doubled the garlic (minced, not smashed), doubled the broth (homemade bone broth), and added a Parmesan rind that was hanging out in my fridge. I stashed ~8 cups in my freezer and can't wait to pull some out on a rainy day.

I gave this four stars instead of five because I think the chicken should have been prepared differently.  It would have been better if the chicken breast was pre-seasoned or brined prior to adding to the broth.  As written, the chicken comes across like an afterthought - some plain unseasoned chicken breast thrown into an otherwise delicious stew.  The rest of the flavors are great. I'll make it again with modification to the chicken.
Running late one night, so I used chopped breast from a rotisserie chicken. This is a quick, delicious and filling meal. Chopped spinach also works well as a substitute for kale. Delish!
This is my new go to chicken soup! It’s quick and easy and uses ingredients that I usually have on hand. I added an extra can of beans and it was just right.
Made as posted. Easy ,delicious and nutritious.
Has anybody froze the leftovers and if so, how did it?
marvelously.
I added some Parmesan rinds into the broth as it simmered and then removed when I took out the chicken for shredding. It added a little something to the broth. Delicious!
Very good recipe. The last time I made it, I felt it was a little bland and added about 1tsp of ras el hanout. I really liked this addition in case you’re looking for a bit of a twist.
This recipe is delicious and easy! I used lots of spinach instead of kale. Had only one can of white beans, and it worked. Served over rice with some grated parmesan. Very good!
Why is this recipe so high in sodium? It's so high I can't consider it.
It's the canned beans and chicken broth. If you use low or sodium free beans and low sodium or homemade broth it will be much lower.
@WB maybe because of the chicken stock and canned beans? Use low-salt stock, rinse the beans or cook your own, be judicious with how much salt you add, and you should be fine.
You could control sodium level by adding as much or little salt as you like, and choosing low/no-sodium broth?
think I'll try this with boneless chicken thighs. I don't mind the added fat, but does anyone think it'll hurt the flavor or texture of the soup?
I tried this with chicken thighs.  The only difference I could see is that they don't shred in quite the same way. But didn't affect my enjoyment at all
I used thighs and it was fine.
@Michelle I’m going to do the same. I think it will taste better :)
1.10.26. Used thighs & Thyme. Browned the thighs as suggested by others; good idea. However overall good but not great.
This is excellent and so easy. I add sauteed ground chicken or turkey (which I tend to keep in the freezer for quick access) to boost the protein content.
How about using turkey thigh meat? It’s what I have in freezer. I’d also finish with some parm, as another suggested.

This is THE BEST main dish recipe I have ever made from The Times. Many opportunities for modifications, including the type of beans, type of greens, type of onions. Even add red bell peppers for more color and flavor. Great over flavorful brown rice. Easy to clean up preparation bowls and the rest of the kitchen during the final simmer.
This is genuinely very easy and healthy. It’s going in the week day rotation. When I have my kids I like to start with a soup the can eat all week. This meets the needs.

Flavor was excellent, but I didn't like how the chicken turned out. Next time I will try chicken thighs with 2-3 cups chicken stock, 1/2-3/4 c white wine, and simmer that all for around 20 minutes or so, as in Sarah DiGregorio's chicken artichoke stew
I love this recipe and made the soup mostly by the book. I doubled the recipe and used both rosemary and thyme. I also left the herbs in until the chicken breasts were cooked.
Used chick peas and cannellini, all I had.  And savoy cabbage. All good on a January night. !!

Simple, easy but wonderfully delicious flavor and texture. I made exactly as written (used rosemary sprig). Sautéing the aromatics in the first step is key to building a delicious and flavorful base for the soup. I served it over rice with a squeeze of lemon. A perfect cold weather/under-the weather meal.
Very easy and minimal prep. Can be halved easily if it’s one or two of you. I served it with a ton of parm on top and that put it over the edge. 4 stars no parm, 4 1/2 stars with a lot of parm.

Simple and delicious. I added carrots and celery with the onions and garlic. I chopped the chicken into chunks instead of shredding. I didn't have fresh herbs so I added a very small pinch of thyme and cracked rosemary. I used 8 oz. of fresh spinach, which seemed the perfect amount when eating the stew. I love beans, and white beans for some reason remind me of my Italian heritage. It almost took longer to write this review than it did to make the stew. Thank you!
Used 2 cups broth from cubes and 2 cups homemade. Used  chicken from cooked breast of whole bird. Added splash of white wine and a bit of balsamic vinegar. Omg yum
@Sarah also - Tarragon
Very tasty lots of flavour I used spinach instead of kale.
Use escarole and add some crushed red pepper - now it's Italian ;-)
This is SO good and garlicky! The  Boursin gives it a super distinct flavor, so choose which one you put in wisely.
What Boursin? I don't see it in the recipe, or is that your own addition?
I don't see any Boursin in this recipe.
I'm confused - where in the recipe is there anything about Boursin a semi soft cheese spread?
A wonderful meal for a winter night. Served with a fresh, crusty bread and a mixed greens and slaw salad. A robust red wine and enjoyed by all.

This recipe looked really good for a cold winter day. Yesterday I bought some chicken-spinach-feta sausages so I used them by squeezing out balls of the meat from the casings. I browned that first, then added mirepoix as I wanted the extra veggies. Lots of garlic and red chili flakes went in next along with the herbs. I did buzz one can of the white beans in the blender with a bit of broth before adding it to the soup. It’s wonderful and satisfying!
This was delicious served over rice. I added turmeric to make it a little more pretty and nutritious, as well as carrots and celery with the onions, and loaf of parsley to finish. Delish!
Sorry I wasted ingredients on this.  Bland, kale undercooked and chicken tough.  I followed directions, so 🤷‍♀️.

This is a nice recipe made exactly as is, but it came out more brothy than stew-like and a little subtle in flavor on the first attempt.

Take 2: we used an immersion blender in the Dutch oven prior to adding the kale, as the thickening method instead of smashing some or the beans. Huge improvement in texture and flavor.

I’d also argue for adding one more seasoning component. We used turmeric the second time, which was nice. Ginger, red pepper, flakes, etc could also work. Just needs something else.

Don’t skip on the grated parm or lemon at the end, either!
This was ok.  A bit bland even with extra salt, pepper and crushed red pepper.  I would cut the chicken broth in half - way too soupy.  May add more garlic and herbs.

I added a whole handful of Italian seasonings and a healthy dash of cayenne but otherwise followed the recipe, and it was a winner! Husband has already asked for this to enter our regular dinner rotation.

I love this recipe. Quick and easy and simple and healthy and flavorful. I did add extra pepper and Kräutersalz at the end for more depth of flavor and I agree with the comments about browning the chicken a bit before adding the liquids, but overall love it. Checks all my boxes for a recipe.
Used rotisserie chicken (but shred and add at the end!) and a squeeze of lemon juice. Amazing dish.
Prepared as instructed. Delicious and easy. I added extra beans and kale. Next time maybe some carrots.
Used spinach because it was on hand. Easy recipe to follow.
Added some tomato paste to the sauté before adding liquid 😍 yum
I added a Parmesan rind during the simmer and finished each bowl with lemon juice before serving. Easy and delicious!

This was a pretty bland stew so I added the lemon zest and juice as well as Aleppo pepper and Parmesan. It was very quick and easy to make and nutritious. We’re used to more flavor packed recipes. This was fine, a little doctoring up to taste is needed though.
Great basic recipe with plenty of opportunity to add your own influence.
Delicious! My husband is a big eater so I added some orzo and a little more broth to fill it out a little more. Very lovely and easy soup!

This is a super simple recipe that is delicious and healthy. I read all these notes and agree with: browning both sides of the breasts before removing them and continuing with the recipe. The opportunity to add umami from their caramelization should not be squandered. I increased the amount of beans (pureed almost a can's worth to thicken the liquid) and chicken stock to create more of a soup. I used dried sage from my garden and left it in.
I used only 1 can Great Northern beans; I used spinach; chicken thighs (cut into pc and browned a little); more onion and ... 2 big handfuls of sauteed chopped mushrooms. great.
easy and delicious - and can be tweaked and adapted to what you have in the fridge easily. will cook again and again,
Delicious after trial and error of adding many different seasonings. Be warned that the end result is very bland as written. I’ll leave it up to the individual to choose a seasoning theme.
Living out west with a charcoal grill close at hand I always grill the chicken with lots of smoky wood before shredding. Really ups the flavor of the dish in my opinion.
Lemon squeeze.  Garnish with parm.
sub chicken thighs, added chili flakes, Orzo, parsley, dill and lemon. The beans, fat from the chicken, and butter thickened it up nicely. very tasty and simple.

Satisfying and delicious. I adapted the recipe by substituting chicken sausage, which I grill and add at the finish. This makes it even more rustic. Great with a squeeze or lemon for brightness, or soy sauce for umami. And topped with Parmesan.
Very easy and was satisfying on one of the coldest days, but overall I would say it’s good but not great.  There are better soup recipes that I would go with before this one.

I went a little rogue on this and it is fabulous! I roasted some bone-in breasts, then made stock with the bones in the instant pot. Also made beans in the IP instead of canned. Browned garlic, rosemary and red pepper flakes then threw in the shredded meat, beans and frozen kale with the stock. Omg. It took three hours of mostly hands off work, but came together really quick at the end.

This was spectacular with modifications. I dry marinated chicken cutlet strips (that was what I had on hand) in 1/4 tsp each paprika, coriander, and salt, seared it in a tbsp of olive oil, took out, deglazed the pan with a tiny bit of balsamic and red wine, then added half the butter and sauteed the onions and garlic. I also doubled the rosemary and used one less cup of broth. Added more salt andanother half tsp of the same spices while simmering. Otherwise made as directed and served with rice.

This experienced cook has been enjoying expressing creativity when cooking by utilizing ingredients on hand. I also browned the chicken first but did not cook through. Then I added it later to acquire flavor from the broth mixture. I substituted thyme for sage or rosemary and finely chopped broccoli for greens.

This dish is high on the effort: reward ratio. simple ingredients, everything to make it was in my pantry, frig  or freezer. served with crusty bread. perfect warm hearty dinner for freezing night! 
I used fresh rosemary, sage and thyme because I had them.
So tasty, so easy. Followed the recipe, but added a little turmeric for a healthy boost. Quick and yummy dinner, perfect for a cold night.
Have leftover rotisserie chicken.  Could I use this to save some more time?
@AVLDot Don’t see why not!  I’m planning to use the remains of a roast chicken I cooked and deboned yesterday!  Waste not, want not, as the saying goes!

I used (defrosted) shredded chicken from my freezer.  I'm sure I lost a little flavor but it made this a really quick dinner; sauteed the aromatics, added a little turmeric and black pepper before adding the broth.  Simmered the beans for a few minutes then added the chicken and spinach.  Heated through until the spinach wilted.  Will definitely keep in the rotation when I need a quick but warming meal.

This recipe goes in my 5 star recipe collection. Used Better Than Boullion for the broth, large bunch of lacinato kale, more chicken, more garlic and the suggested parm rind. High protein, high fiber, great taste.
Rotisserie chicken and kale salad mix from costco. Canned beans? That's where I draw the line! Gotta be dried Peruvian beans, cooked with a ham hock sometimes.

This is just like the Pasta Fagiola recipe my family has made for decades just with escarole, garlic, and parm. It is truly a keeper! I grew up with the addition of oregano, parsley, and tomato juice/sauce but my husband and daughter are allergic to tomato, so just the chicken broth now. We sometimes add in rotisserie chicken for protein, but prefer ground Italian sausage for more of a kick. Any of these options are easy, fast, and delicious. Try it!
Definitely use thighs instead of breast!!

This really worked out great! A very healthy and comforting dish that I made by cooking 3 parts separately (roasted turkey thigh, soaked and cooked gently white beans cannelloni, and the the onion garlic butter sautéed with 12 oz thawed frozen spinach) all three combined mixed and coated with1/2 cup finely grated parmigiana with just chicken stock.  The roasted turkey thigh is a game changer!lean, tasty if seasoned for a few hours beforehand and then roasted…and then sliced up and mixed in with the other two dishes.

Agreed on the need for some more flavor- so along with mushrooms, I added more garlic, 2 slabs of ginger root, fresh cilantro, extra rosemary, Thai basil (growing thru the winter inside) lemon juice, white wine and some chili peppers. Added fresh peas just before serving over jasmine rice.

Received in my inbox today, made it tonight for dinner. Very good, easy chicken soup/stew. Best thing is not needing to precook the chicken! Made as written with the following changes as noted in other reviews: Used sage instead of rosemary, spinach as greens, 2 cans whole beans, 1 can smashed, added 2 sliced carrots with the onion, at the end added juice of a half of a lemon, 1/2 cup or so of heavy cream, and some fresh grated nutmeg, and plenty of fresh pepper. Delish!

I cooked the kale in a bit of broth and olive oil and lime juice before adding it to the pot. Then because its texture was too chewy and fibrous I whizzed the kale in a food processor and added it back to the pot. I added a half cup of white wine to enhance the flavour.

I followed this recipe closely but made numerous modifications and it was so good! 
1. Used double the garlic
2. Doubled the broth amount + I had an open vegetable broth container so used it plus a low sodium chicken broth
3. Whole Foods offers a fully seasoned, whole rotisserie chicken for $5.99 in Tuesdays. Pulled the meat, chopped it up, added the meat, bones and skin. 
4. Added carrots and cooked them when sautéing the onions, etc.
5. Used red Swiss Chard
 
Finished cooking in a covered large pot for a couple of hours in the low setting. Just wow!
When you add the beans do you include the liquid in the can?

As another reader commented this is quite a bland recipe which needs a lot to lift it.  Parmesan, lemon juice/white wine, ginger and olive oil are all essential.  Chicken breast was very dry - would use bone in chicken thigh next time and cook slower and longer.
This is on the monthly menu in our house. I pick up a rotisserie chicken from BJ's for $4.99 which makes it even easier.

Found this recipe after a notification from NYT. Read all the comments and made it tonight. A big hit for hubby and me!  I made the full recipe. Two changes: sautéed the chicken first (seasoned it well ahead of time, used Penzy’s Justice spice). And added frozen corn (just because I needed to use it up). Used all the broth as recipe called for.  I used fresh collard greens and also added one carrot thinly sliced. Absolutely recommend this recipe; it’s highly forgiving for customization.

Ok- absolutely NOTHING bland about this. Ya gotta develop the onion and garlic w/ rosemary sprig. I added red pepper. I did brown the chicken first- but thanks NYT for teaching me how to GENTLY poach chicken. It’s delicious. I added lemon juice and grated parm at end because other folks suggested parm rind and I forgot… it’s seriously delicious- and I don’t like soup!!

So delicious and easy...I modified this based on what I had in the refrigerator and pantry. I substituted the kale with 1 fennel bulb and the tender tops; used shallots and green onions instead of the yellow onion, I used 4 ounces of roasted chicken from the freezer. Then I added 1 tbsp. of dried crushed New Mexico green Chile. A superb variation on an excellent, and easy recipe.

This is a simple recipe but oh so good! We’re in an ice storm, so I used what was on hand: rotisserie chicken, basil for greens. Per the comments, I added a parm rind. And because we like spicy, also added a little Calabrian chili paste. Spectacular flavor, and warmth down to the toes. Thanks, Ali!!
Was excellent with butter beans and spinach
What is a "sprig" of sage?

Very good comfort food for a cold winter night.  Loved the texture from the kale, beans, and chicken, although it was a little bland.  I always make recipes as is the first time, but next time we will try some of the suggestions from the comments.  Will definitely make again.
Super easy to make, and just plain delicious.  Made exactly as written.  Leftovers were great too!

Super yummy and quick to develop rich taste unlike most stews/soups I've made.  Chopped up collard green leaves and stems for the green leafy veg.  One change:  added collard green stems and some chopped carrots into the sautéed onions. The carrots provided nice color. Highly recommend this recipe.

I made the recipe as written, but found it a bit lacking in flavor. I'll do it again, but I may use chicken thighs instead of breasts, and I'll definitely brown the meat first, along with the onions and herbs. I used chard instead of kale, since that's what I had. I might also add a bit of paprika or hot sauce to give it a bit of zing. This would also be good with a bit of sausage, in addition to, or instead of, the chicken. It needs a little wakeup, e.g., hot pepper flakes or pepper sauce.

Solid satisfying simple meal. A bit like a cozy chicken pot pie, without the pastry (and soupier). Easy template to add what you have or want. Followed all steps and ingredients, along with these ingredient additions or changes: 
* Added a chopped fennel bulb, sliced mushrooms and cubed red potatoes to the onions and garlic. 
* used boneless skinless chicken thighs 
* 3 not 4 cups broth (plenty soupy) 
*added a spoonful of mild harissa along with the broth 
*used baby spinach for the greens 
* added 1 cup frozen peas in the last minute 
*salt, pepper, red pepper flakes at end
Solid4-star homey dinner—not dazzling but so good. 
M

An easy,good tasting meal. I followed the directions with two additions mentioned by others. I added small carrot slices to the onion mixture and used 3 cans of beans. This will definitely be a meal repeated.

The flavor , for us was just fine . The end result  seemed too thin. We ended up adding four cans of beans and spinach as the greens.
Only issue was the thinness. Would definitely make it again .
Delicious and healthy. I had a cooked chicken and its flavorful broth which made this recipe simple and easy.
Do not substitute ground turkey. It simply doesn't have enough flavor. Loved it with the ground pork, though.

Lovely recipe.  I didn't add any salt or pepper - the garlic and the herbs (I used dried thyme, garam masala and rosemary) give enough flavour.  Next time I would use less kale, not an entire bunch - a whole bunch is a bit too much.
So much flavor even without searing the chicken! Perfect weeknight, cozy meal as advertised.
Loved this!  Great chilly weeknight meal that takes no time at all.
Used pinches of dried sage and rosemary instead of fresh sprigs. Also added a pinch of red pepper flakes and squeeze of lemon. Would make again.

Not worth the minimum effort required. Have deleted this from my saved recipes, as I don't want to be disappointed again. Perhaps it's the chicken breasts in Texas. Perhaps it's the greens. Maybe it's my lack of skill. This is the first time in years I've used chicken breasts, and now I know why. Give me a whole chicken or thighs.
Cooked as written. Fantastic and simple. I added a squirt of lemon and some feta to my bowl. Delicious.
I added a can of corn (NOT creamed) (I'm a corn-aholic). Yum!

followed the recipe and some of the comments: added carrot, lemon, parmesan rind, and ginger. Fed both of us with enough for another meal. Perfect dish for the polar vortex, and maybe one of the most delicious NYT dishes I've ever made.
Delicious. Off the heat I added lemon and parmesan with a side of crusty bread. I'll be making this again
Made as directed and did try topping with some fresh parmasean, which was good. Next time I would like to try adding some white wine to brighten. Pancetta bits might also be interesting to add.
Made as written. The broth was surprisingly very buttery. We added lemon and ate on toast.

Made this last night and it was a hit! I used more broth than the recipe called for because my cans of beans were 19 oz instead of 15, and that made it a little more soupy than I'd intended. Removed some broth and beans and blended them to thicken it up, and at the last minute I threw in some farro to soak up more liquid. The lemon juice was a nice touch at the end. My teenaged sons gobbled it up. Next time I'll double the recipe for leftovers!
Have to say the chicken gets leathery and tough, especially when you reheat. Maybe cutting it in chunks instead of shredding it might help??
Really bland!

A KEEPER!  Made pretty much per instructions.  Rosemary sprig, always add extra garlic and always rinse the beans to help reduce sodium along with low sodium chicken broth. Used spinach.  Seems like it needed something...would definitely add even more garlic, maybe double?  And an extra can of beans wouldn't hurt as most got mashed.  We loved it and fairly quick to put together.
Just wanted to add that it was even better the next evening!  Flavors had more chance to meld. Spinach worked just fine.
Spinach would be a little delicate for this recipe. Kale is an excellent choice

Had bone in, skin on chicken thighs.  Pulled the skin off and put it in the oven to make chicken skin crisps while the thighs poached in the liquid. Also used up some frozen kale.  Otherwise, followed the recipe as is. Topped the bowls with crumbled chicken skin and this was a satisfying and delicious dinner.

I think I'll try this next time with ground chicken (browning it in step 1) to not have to shred the chicken.

I served this as a soup with salad, just didn't cream so many beans. I'll probably add some stock to the leftovers unless serving over rice.
Good recipe overall, I didn't get a lot of flavor from the rosemary, so I may have to up that or leave it in the sauce for a bit.
I love things that finish with 'season with salt and pepper' because I can let the diners do that.
I will definitely make this again.

Loved this but made some changes along the way. Had 1.25 pounds of chicken so added 1 extra cup of water. I added a third can of cannellini beans for body (which helped tremendously).  To amp up the flavor, I used both dried ground sage and rosemary, added some onion powder and better than bullion.  My only rookie error was using ghee instead of butter as someone had mentioned.  The flavor of ghee is too strong for this stew, so use butter. It also took longer than 15 minutes to get the chicken up to temp. Will make it again!
Love this. So easy. I used Veg broth (better than B) and threw in a parmesan rind (thanks Joe Pro). Great for the below freezing weather right now.
I only added more chicken stock and a squeeze of lemon at the end.  This is such a keeper!

Cooked last night -- will be putting this to the rotation as it's pretty easy. Added about a cup of cubed butternut that we needed to use. Increased the garlic by half, smashed them good but didn't cut. We also had some cooked small shell pasta, so I added a bit to the bowls before serving. Pasta was nice but not needed. Threw in some red pepper flake, could have added a bit more to kick up the interest. Served with sliced baguette. Husband does not like beans but got two helpings!
Added a bit of fresh ginger, finely chopped, to the onion and garlic.😋👍

Made a double batch. Was enough for dinner for three adults two nights in a row, had enough left over for three to four additional servings. Added parmesan rind after adding the broth as mentioned in previous posts, highly recommend. Seasoned the chicken with Tony Chachere's, yellow curry powder, Italian seasoning, and a drizzle of Olive Garden Italian Dressing. Sauteed the chicken first then followed the recipe mostly as written. Served with both sourdough bread and naan bread as I had both on hand, equally delicious options. Overall solid recipe with a few tweaks for enhanced flavor. Will be added to the regular rotation especially for winter time!
Perfect receipt made as is!
I made with spinach and I added diced tomatoes. The chicken was perfectly cooked but bland - I agree with Greenie, I would season and saute the chicken next time before adding it into the liquid.

This stew is what you should see next to “comfort food” in the dictionary. Highly recommend punching up the seasonings — curry powder, harissa, crushed red pepper, whatever you like — but it’s a good, hearty dish for winter.

I enjoyed this, Ali. Thank you!! We had Costco pulled chicken on hand instead of cooking the breasts in the broth. I took some of the comments here and added a can of corn, about half drained, and about 2 tsp of turmeric for color and taste. Used the rest of a tub of spinach, at least what I could salvage before compost, and it was a delicious stew! I forgot to mash the beans, but I will next time.
I used chicken thighs (in chunks instead of shredded) instead of Breast, add herb de Provence and mixed in both spinach and kale.  It turned out beautiful and I will be putting this on rotation.

I used corn-fed chicken (the one with yellow meat), a good stock concentrate (Maggi, it's the consistency of loose pudding, in small jars, for those in Europe), and 3 sprigs (with branches) of sage - it tasted phenomenal and phenomenally chickeny! Definitely a keeper and will make again.

Prefect cozy all in one meal. Subbed in Turkish oregano for sage/rosemary (didn’t have on hand), and sautéed a celery rib with the onion and garlic because I had it lying around. A big squeeze of lemon to finish adds the right touch.
A pefect recipe!

Very good and easy to make! I ended up using a rotisserie chicken, doubling the garlic, adding tomato paste to the garlic and onions, and juice from half a lemon at the end. Even my toddler loved it and asked for more!
If I have cooked chicken breast meat, could i add it later in the recpe?
@Pumpkin Eater I used rotisserie chicken and put it in at the end.
How do you prepare the chicken for this recipe?  It goes in raw?  Is it sliced into strips or some other form?

One Whole Foods organic chicken yields 1 pound of meat. I used spinach instead of kale, and added carrot and celery to increase the vegetables. The servings are huge. Added all together, one serving is between three and four cups.

So good and easy!  Also found it to be a great use of the chicken breast tenders my husband mistakenly picked up instead of regular chicken breasts.  Using the tenders solved the timing issue other commenters had noted re: chicken breasts needing additional time to cook, and the tenders shredded beautifully. Used frozen spinach for the greens.
We made it almost as is, had rotisserie chicken so shredded that and used dried rosemary...very tasty and we ate it with parmesan rosemary artisan bread :) Perfect for a cold snowy icy day!
Added a good-sized parm rind and red pepper flakes with the stock. Juice of half a lemon before serving. Would have been bland without!

So, used frozen chard from garden, cannellini beans and chicken breast.
Sautéed the onion and spices - used 1 t of cumin, adobo and Florida seasoned pepper- in my instant pot. Added and sautéed the chicken, then added the beans, chard and chicken broth. Switched off sautee and hit the soup button. Absolutely delish!

This is very good but does need changes made in comments. Brown the chicken (I used boneless thighs, too), added red pepper flakes, more garlic. Drizzled a bit of wild mushroom/sage infused EVOO on top.

Thanks for the tips on adding palm rind and a little lemon juice and red pepper chili flakes. I am using lean chicken sausage instead of the chicken breast. It smells divine. Also blended 3/4 of a cup of cannellini beans with some of the broth and will add it at the end for creaminess.
It was great I put the beans in a little later because I thought they would get soggy and they were great!!!!!!
It was great both my kids loved it but there was not a lot of beans two cans was not enough but it was still pretty good
What we made was awful! Must be the chicken which was chewy. We chose to put cannellini beans. Not sure what we did wrong… We won’t do it again!
Is 5his a soup or a stew?
Used tarragon as that's what I had in the fridge. Delicious!

Made a half recipe which was perfect for my husband and me. Used parts of a rotisserie chicken- bones for making the 2 cups of stock and the meat for the soup. In lieu of fresh herbs used some dried turkey rub which had sage and rosemary in it. For greens used lacinato kale. We agreed it was an incredibly delicious soup- 100% make again!

Wow! This was so easy and absolutely fantastic! I don’t understand the comments that it lacked flavor…I used lacinto kale and rosemary. I also added gnocchi at the end (about half of a store-bought package). A squeeze of lemon and Parmesan when served is a must!

A very tasty, healthy & quick one pot stew. I had some chicken breast tenders & some red Swiss kale in the fridge. My hubby was fighting a bad cold so I was looking for a new soup or stew to try when I found this perfect recipe. The only change I made was to add the zest of one lemon.
Rotisserie chicken, sweet onion, fresh flat parsley(no others greens in the fridge)   Absolutely yummy. Will make again.

Family agreed one of the best chicken soup recipes we've had!  I did make a few tweaks as suggested by others (doubled herbs 2 rosemary and 2 sage, seared chicken first and deglazed pan, had a 3rd can of beans that I blended with broth and added back in to thicken the soup, finished with half a squeezed lemon and drizzled some truffle oil on top and fresh shaved parm, added baby spinach to the bowls and added soup on top so it wilted better).  Simple, and my house smelled amazing!!
Sauté the chicken with the onions is a very good suggestion.   The recipe is a bit bland without that step    Or maybe I just need to add more salt.
Would adding ginger work?

Making for the 2nd time today. I used a bag of Rancho Gordo beans(can’t remember the name, but a white that was suggested for soups/stews) and soaked overnight. Used thyme sprigs because that’s what I had. I added the beans plus soaking water. Added a couple of carrots, a fennel branch removed later, Manchego rind and Lemon. So good and EASY👏

Made as written and was so surprised at how flavorful it was (for such a quick, simple recipe). I made it again and subbed some roasted tofu for the chicken. I thought it was equally good. I’m making it again tomorrow and will revert to the original recipe. It needs no alterations.
Added  a bit of dried thyme and red pepper flakes.  low on kale so put in some broccoli and mushrooms.  and some leftover brown rice.   it was good!  chicken only needed 10 min.

Super easy and delicious. I halved-ish the recipe but added extra spinach and some leftover mushrooms I had. I could see adding some white wine to it, but I didn’t feel like opening a bottle. Overall quite yummy and healthy!

I’ve made this twice in the last ten days.  Both times I 1) added a huge handful of chopped carrots with the onion/garlic/herb step 2) used the meat from a rotisserie chicken 3) used 5 cups of bone broth and 4) added 1/4+ cup dry basmati rice during the simmer step for a little more heft.  Once I used spinachas my green of choice; this time I used kale.  It’s not the world’s most exciting soup but it’s a great little recipe if you’re feeling under the weather and don’t want to work too hard.  And the leftovers are so, so good.
Used some leftover roasted turkey and that was quite good!
Adding a good size pinch of “Italian herbs,” and then adding balsamic vinegar to serving bowl added a very welcome sensation of acidity and depth of flavor.
Do I rinse the beans?

The general rule is: unless a recipe tells you to do something, you don't.  On the other hand, I've seen several recipes on here using canned bean go out of their way to affirmatively tell you to add the the liquid with the beans - as if they expected you to rinse, otherwise.  So the answer is...

NYTimes Cooking's instructions are not consistent on the matter of canned beans, and you are not likely to get an answer from them.

But I would rinse them.

I used ground rosemary and spinach for the greens because it's what I had.  I think kale would hold better and add more chew.  The taste was great but I think I would not cook the beans quite so much as they just fell apart and became mush.
I made it with chickpeas and it was awesome! Very flavorful!

I followed some commenters' suggestions - namely used plenty of salt and pepper, used spinach, and mashed a whole extra can of beans into the soup. I also added some powdered rosemary and sage, as well as Aleppo pepper and a splash of lemon juice. Yet we found it to be very lacking in flavor. I like the idea of this soup, so I think I'll give it another shot, but will add Hatch green chile powder to see if that gives it a little oomph.

This soup was absolutely delicious! I used half a chicken just under 2 lbs., 2 Adobo chilies (the heat was just right) and 1 can of beans. The cilantro and avocado are a must. The leftovers are fantastic too.
Rainbow Chard - delicious. Add one extra can of beans.

Added mushrooms and carrots; swapped out kale for spinach. I used smaller chicken breasts, so I should have cooked a couple minutes less to keep them more tender. But this recipe is as easy as it is healthy and tasty!

@Mary Fox I would soak the beans overnight and cook them for an hour or two until tender. Then proceed with the recipe.  Remember that you don't want to add salt while the beans are cooking (makes them tough) so you will need to add a lot more salt to the stew.  I use my Rancho Gordo beans like this all the time.  I do add herbs while they are first cooking.
Delicious and easy to make!

Made a half portion with home made chicken stock and fresh rosemary.  Had no fresh greens so used a lovely zucchini and, as commenters suggested, a carrot.  Simmered a bit longer.  Was delicious and seems to be highly flexible!  Will definitely make again!!

I used spinach (and some leftover iceberg salad and tomatoes) instead of kale, added a can of chickpeas, 200 grams of farfalle and some heavy cream, and seasoned it with sambal oelek.  Amazingly good, given that I was only looking for a way to use up some on-sale chicken breasts and a bunch of wilting spinach ... My teenagers finished the pot in a day.
There is no chicken shown in the ingredients.  Looks like thighs in the photo. How much or how many thighs and how to cook?
@Sharon S it literally says a pound of chicken breast

Like the combination of chicken, beans and kale a lot. Cooked chicken breast came out hard and dry for me so would highly recommend thighs instead. Also will add more beans when I give it another try.

I love this one! I used more chicken stock and two bunches of kale, along with minced shallots and leeks to add more veg. I also minced fresh rosemary and thyme and put it straight in the pot. I like a strong herb note. I also used an organic rotisserie chicken. When time to serve I dished it with lots of lemon zest and aged parm! Low effort when you're tired!

I’ve made this twice - the first time was boring. Second time delish. I used olive oil, tbl spoon of rosemary and savory, and for the broth used a good paste and one of the cups from from the beans.
