I also cooked the chicken separately with garlic salt on them because I needed to cook three pounds up. The result is delicious! Will freeze some for another day.
Made this as written and it was a little soupier than I wanted, adding some orzo solved that! Lemon zest & juice and chili flakes is to finish, lovely!

Very easy and versatile recipe. I made it as written except I had to substitute fresh thyme as I had that on hand. Also did a light pass through with my immersion blender before I added chicken (rotisserie). All my picky eaters enjoyed it. Delicious and simple.
Love this.  So easy to make.  I might add more beans and greens, but still great as is.
Really benefits from red pepper flake, splash of red wine vinegar and parm
Oh my people! Delicious, second time cooking this. I browned boneless skinless thighs, skipped the Kale, and instead added frozen carrots and broccoli. The lemon is a must.

it's the rosemary that makes this dish, so use a large sprig . This solid recipe is even BETTER the next day!  Adding Parmesan the first night helped it to bulk up a bit, but not necessary the next day. I used small white beans which was not a good idea. I think the larger ones would've made this much better. Freezes really well.

Almost 2 feet of snow outside and this was the perfect dish to warm the soul.  I didn't have white beans, so I used chick peas instead and it was delicious.  I browned the chicken first and added some lemon juice at the end.  I will definitely serve this in the regular rotation.

This is a just a kind of soup that really consistently works for me. The garlic, onion, and sage butter made my whole house smell delicious and then it got to be the base for the whole broth. I will say that since the garlic is smashed rather than minced it didn't really cook down into the broth, but that was no problem at all for me. Chicken got nice and moist and pearly, I simmered the whole thing on medium for a few minutes after shredding the chicken to set my salmonella paranoia to rest.
I liked the idea of this but 4 cups of broth seemed like a lot. I only used 2, simmered without a cover, and it was still very, very soupy. I wouldn't use more than one cup next time.

Great base recipe. Modifications were: used thighs, sautéed them with the onions, added some red pepper flakes and the parm rind, and ended with the juice of a lemon. Really delicious and going in the rotation.

It was delicious! Really enjoyed the textures and flavours - we used sage and rosemary from the garden, as well as a zucchini - large cubes. It felt like a Chicken of the Provence :)

Thank you from Australia!

I made two minor mistakes: added a parmesan rind without cutting back on added salt, and browned the chicken first but didn't cut the simmering time so it overcooked a bit. I won't bother browning next time. The stew is delicious, and the lemon adds a perfect note.
Agree that it needs a lot more seasoning. I used leftover liquid from a jar of kimchi to add some punch.

Second time making this. Came back for more but this time used some lean ground pork that I had in fridge can of garbanzos and can of white kidneys and then added chilli pepper flakes to spice it up a touch and a ton of leafy green spinach. Simple and warm. Great for a fall/winter / early spring day. Super simple and easy too.
This soup was just okay. It was a little bland and a little uninspiring. The chicken didn’t add much, and omitting it would make the recipe very affordable.
I would add more chicken broth so it is more soupy.

A friend had made it and recommended both dark and white meat for added flavor and I have now made this twice that way. I found that by the time the soup is at a simmer, the chicken is done! I used dry rosemary and one sprig of sage, and in addition to the kale, I added spinach, since I like the taste of it.Very good recipe
Chiffonade of basil to step one. Also carrots and mushrooms
Healthy, easy; delicious. This ticks all my boxes.
Simple and delicious. I used a box of baby kale for my greens and included a parm rind in Step 2 and took it out with the chicken in Step 3.

Added the mushrooms and Parmesan rind as others suggested.  Swapped in one can of red kidney beans for one of the cans of cannellini beans.  Otherwise cooked as is and it was delicious!  I would definitely make again.
Add park rind
I made the recipe as is, and came back to it a second time and cut up the chicken in long pieces. It is a bit quicker and tastes the same. Love this quick and healthy recipe.
Ideal recipe because I had everything in the house. Easy.

Delicious! Used the 1/2 function because I had only one chicken breast. A leek in my fridge that needed to be used; supplemented with a bit of frozen chopped onion. Appreciated the suggestion to add a parmesan rind. Tossed in some brown rice late in the game with a small dash of red pepper flakes. Squeeze of lemon. So good. Might try 1 anchovy at the start next time to see where that leads. There will be a next time for sure!

Made it last night and it was a quick and easy dinner.  Used some leftover rice in the bottom of the bowl and put the stew on top.  Perfect quick dinner.  Would add a bit of parmesan on top next time but, that would be my only change.
I think this is excellent as written.
Made this with escarole, only subs were poultry seasoning instead of sage sprigs and I went easy on the garlic. It was a 10/10!
unbelievably good! i added some carrot for more veg, used both sage and rosemary, and added a bunch of fresh dill at the end. SO delicious
Easy and delicious. Include the juice of a lemon and some red pepper flakes, you won’t regret it.
We really liked this.  I used kale and rosemary.  I think next time I will up it to 3 cans of beans, but it was a delicious meal.  Served it with some brown and serve hot rolls.  Yum.
This is a beautiful recipe! I was surprised how good it was because it’s so simple and easy. The sage is lovely. Low effort, very high payoff!

I use my stand mixer with the paddle attachment to pull the chicken, it's so much faster and easier than trying to do it with forks. Try it, it's a miracle! Just be aware you may have to hold the thing down a little, as this is pretty tough work for the mixer. And you'll have to clean up some splatter. But this trick has been a game changer.
Keeper.  Of course, used thighs.  Went with rosemary, but would add fried sage leaves for character.  Had a hunk of Parmesan rind that made it. Bread, crusty, was an awesome addition.

My husband and I work nights so I’m always looking for great reheat and eat recipes. This one is great, especially on a cold, rainy night. I had some leftovers from the NYT Garlicky Chicken with Lemon-Anchovy Sauce recipe that I also threw in and it worked perfectly!
For how simple this recipe is, I was a little suspect. But it really did work for me! Obviously it’s not complex, but it does taste like a fancier good old fashioned chicken soup.
As written, this recipe needs a lot more liquid. Has anyone else found the same thing?

This is a wonderful recipe.  I cook the chicken separately in the broth and add a splash of white wine.  When the chicken is finished, I remove it and then add the beans and kale.  I also add chopped carrots for some additional consistency.  Delicious.

I combined this recipe with the NYT Creamy Braised White Beans Recipe. I tweaked both recipes a bit, but not by much. After letting the milk simmer in the chicken peas, I poured the entire thing into this recipe and let them simmer as one for about 20-30 minutes.
A major winner1
Delish!  Do not skip the lemon!

Yum! We made it according to the recipe the first time and it was okay but felt a little lacking. Today we made it again but added three medium potatoes, extra rosemary, four bay leaves, and lots of pecorino romano—it was so much better!!
I added garam masala for more flavor with just a teaspoon or so of honey to round out the flavor.
Was very good, but next time I'll make it with thighs. The breasts were a little bland and easy to overcook.
Very easy to cook. Per comments, I added lemon juice. Works for me.
Delicious! Used more broth to make it soupier, and served over white rice.
I made Melissa Clark's Instant Pot hummus for lunch yesterday, and used the chickpea cooking water in place of chicken stock for this recipe last night. It was great!

I used spinach instead of Kale too and fresh basil vs. rosemary. I put fresh lemon juice in it too. I used too much chicken in the end; will cut back next time and add a third can of beans. Think that would make it perfect!

Great easy stew like dish. Good with any garden herbs. First time I made it with thyme and it was delicious.  Serves more than 4 if you make it with a side dish or yam or whatever you like. I finished it off with some smoked paprika. This dish is a keeper.

Found this while searching for recipes to use some leftover grilled chicken.  Since the chicken was cooked, I shredded it and added it last.  Also found the base a little bland, so I sliced and lightly sauted one link of andouille sausage and put sausage and brown bits in during step 3. It was delicious.
So lovely. Added a squeeze of lemon and topped my bowl with Parmesan and it’s delightful

This was a very easy weeknight dinner and SO good. I’ve never used escarole before but it is easy and a mild green. High fiber and protein a definite make-again. I served with parm on the side to top it, and used bone broth in the cooking.
Made as is and was very good. Next time I added some penne pasta leftover from a box and added it with beans. This was also very good. Round three will be Italian sausage subbed for chicken.

I’ve made this at least 5 times and haven’t been able to get the chicken to have an appetizing texture. I’ve tried different brands of chicken and every time it’s so rubbery. Any ideas what I could be doing wrong?

Delicious and hearty. I definitely use a parmesan rind, chili flakes and extra broth. I prefer chicken thighs as they are more tender and are more forgiving when reheating. Generously squeezing in lemon at the end is a must.

title: White Bean, Tuna and Kale Salad Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: This is terrific and quite simple-took me about 10 minutes total. The only thing I changed was draining the tuna, reserving the oil and then using that to dress the salad.
Could I sub spinach for the kale? I’ve never had kale that I enjoyed but am fine with spinach.
@Cathie I’m going to make it using baby arugula and I think either will be great. I may chop in a little fresh basil and/or parsley to marry flavors and add dimension..
@Cathie I was wondering the same thing. Kale is not allowed in my house 🤪

Cathie,
You may sub anything you want. That's the great thing about cooking. As a kale hater, I share your pain. I usually sub spinach when kale is called for; however, the suggestion of arugula makes my mouth water. Warning...no massaging if it is not kale.
I only had one can of tuna so used that and a can of sardines. Glad I did, it was delicious! Also added some feta.

We had this for dinner tonight and I was pleasantly surprised - I thought it was delicious, filling, and something I’ll make again. The recipe is quick, inexpensive, and so fresh!

We used red onion; tuna in water (we had it on hand) and one bunch of kale. Next time I’d increase the kale by 50% but acknowledge that a “bunch” can vary. A bit of red pepper might be a nice addition.

Hey Cristy! So you can, but don't massage it--it will turn to mush.  Perhaps marinate your onions in the lemon juice and salt while you prep any remaining ingredients, or 10 minutes (there aren't a lot of ingredients to prep!), rubbing the onions between your fingers a couple times to get the massaging effect on them.  If you use red onion it will turn bright pink and that's what you want. Toss with greens to distribute dressing, then toss with remaining.
I thought it was healthy but dull. More lemon? Next time I may add some chopped hard boiled egg, or feta, or toasted pita croutons, or some black olives, or a combination of these.
"1 large bunch Tuscan kale..." It's always useful to know how much this should be by actual weight. Eight ounces? Twelve ounces?
I steamed my “chopped kale” for about 3 minutes and still did the massage it really turns it from grass to a more salad like experience…
@Frann Bragg's sells a product called liquid aminos, find it at any health food store! Great as a dressing
Great salad to continue healthy January! My only modifications were adding 1 lime to balance flavor, diced jalapeno for a kick and mint.  I love a lemon/lime dressing.
Add amino acids to the kale for a tangier kick. This is a fire, easy, and filling weeknight dinner❤️
what is amino acids in the kitchen pantry?
liquid aminos @Frann
@Frann Trader Joe’s sells coconut aminos, also a good substitute for soy sauce for folks who can’t tolerate soy. A tad sweeter than Braggs .
Made this with baby arugula and it was absolutely delicious!
I thought this was very tasty and nutritious. The only thing I did different was add some cherry tomatoes for pops of flavour. I agree that some feta would be nice too, but I didn’t add it this time.

This is so close to a Marcella Hazan recipe that I love and make often, which happens to even be published at https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/5685-bean-and-tuna-salad. It calls for spinach rather than kale - a real pro move IMHO. I'm partial to cooking dried beans this dish, especially RG's Marcella beans.

@Mike Thanks for the link to the Marcella Hazan recipe. I’m not seeing the spinach (or any greens). Do you simply top the spinach with the tuna, bean and onion mixture or massage the greens first as in this recipe?

I ALWAYS MASSAGE KALE for a salad. I’m amazed that so many restaurants and recipes skip this step—it’s usually the roughness people object to, and massaging removes all that. Kale salads made this way usually last longer than other greens—for days, if you don’t eat it all too fast!
I'm sure I'm in the minority, but I don't like (make that hate) raw onions -- even red ones. Any substitutes you recommend to give it more flavor?
I halved the olive oil, added a dash more lemon juice, and didn’t have mint. I look forward to trying it with mint! Delicious!
It doesn't mention mint, so, I'm lost.
Not a fan of tuna but I'll definitely try this using sardines instead.
I make a variation of this salad with drained canned tuna, spinach, white beans, and linguini. A little olive oil and parmesan sprinkled on top. Why, that's what I'm making tonight!
My husband, who does not like kale, thought this salad was great! Super easy and fast to make and very nutritious. I will definitely be making this one time and time again.
Too high in sodium.  Will try using no salt tuna and no salt beans.
Not a kale fan. I used escarole instead. Yummy. Threw in a few black olives and roasted red peppers.
excellent
I made this for lunch. The only variation I made was to add some Kalamata olives. It was delicious and filling. Both of us enjoyed it and we will be making it again.
I love the ingredients in this salad but felt it lacked the rich flavor I was looking for. That said, it absolutely rocked when I put it on a slice of wheat bread with mayonnaise and a pinch of salt.

@Valerie ... I also hate raw onions, and they make me feel nauseous when I eat them.  However, I love onions that are cooked.  So, for most recipes like this I sauté the onions.  I don't care about the added time required to do that, and the sauteed onions are delicious.
I tried this heated...I sauteed the onions and kale with olive oil, added the beans and then the tuna and tossed with lemon juice right as I was serving - it was FABULOUS!
This is perfect, and now that I have learned to massage kale with lemon juice I will do it again and again!

Made it exactly as directed, with canned tuna I bought in the Azores. Fabulous! Delicious and satisfying. Interestingly, the juice from a large lemon really softened and tenderized the kale nicely. Easy, healthy, satisfying and delicious. Plus leftovers!
I veganized it by using tofu instead of tuna and use some kelp seasoning to give it some a bit of the flavor. If I had had some nori sheets on hand, I would’ve crumbled those in. It was excellent!

@Erin

I totally missed that this version doesn't serve the beans on a bed of greens. I'm pretty sure it was a serving suggestion in one of the cookbooks. I usually use baby spinach very lightly dressed with EVOO and red wine vinegar.
I loved this salad  - added a handful of golden  raisins to add a little sweetness.
I loved this with a few enhancements: rice vinegar, fish oil, and MSG. Also added thinly sliced cucumber after salting and pressing out excess water. Very satisfying.

10 to 12 oz of tuna for one bunch of Kale makes this very tuna forward. I added a second bunch of kale, 3-4 cloves of sliced garlic, and a couple more tablespoons each of lemon juice and oil (which you can get from the tuna, although don't dump the entire contents into the mix). Beefed up the portion to four full servings.
Great simple recipe. Added 1 cup of chopped castelvetrano olives, green onions, basil, and arugula. Used only one can of tuna and no red onion. Excellent
Use pre-shredded kale and added feta and a bit more lemon juice (juice of a lemon is an ambiguous command to begin with). Very delicious, and very quick and easy.
This is excellent, exactly as written.
Delicious. Used a shallot as I didn’t have red onion on hand, next time I make this I will double the kale.
Fantastic. Another bop from Ali. This is great with the white onion.
Delicious! I’ve made this with kale and decided to try with arugula and parsley. Delicious both ways!

This is one of my favorite warm-weather meals.  I’ve made a different version of this for years, with abundant parsley instead of the kale. It makes a more protein-heavy version which I often use as a main course in summer. Recently, rather than using canned beans I’ve started using the Rancho Gordo white Lima beans for this recipe. Even though it is slightly more labor intensive, the results are worth it with a hearty, creamy bean that tastes almost like potato.  Also, you can use preserved lemon +/- red chili flakes for an extra punch.

Please clarify, when instructions say “add tuna and oil, toss to combine.” Does that mean add the oil that had been drained off of the tuna? Or do I drain off the tuna, toss out the oil, and add the tuna and whatever oil is left on the pieces? Thanks
did not like. sour and mushy.
I ended up wanting extra dressing. Used a turmeric balsamic. Otherwise a good quick summer dinner.
Added some white miso, lemon zest and herbes de Provence to kick up the flavor a bit.

title: Spaghetti With Arugula and Sardines Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: This was an excellent, quick and tasty dish. I added an additional clove of garlic because I like garlic. I also added the crushed peppers into the juice and lemon zest prior to adding the noodles, arugula and sardines because my wife is a little adverse to the heat of hot peppers. By adding into the olive oil and lemon juice, I toned the crushed peppers down a little. I also added a little more olive oil as recommended because it was a little dry. I give this two thumbs up.

I do like the use of pantry ingredients and bold flavors, but I prefer about 2 oz of dry pasta per serving, so would use 1/2 lb for 4 people and double the arugula ( or add another green like parsley, watercress or spinach).

I try to make my pasta dishes heavy on sauce and light on pasta, so I use about 1.5 ounces of dry pasta per serving and add a vegetable or salad to the meal. I can't wait to make this, as I have been craving pasta lately. If my sardines are not packed in olive oil, I will simply drain them and add fresh olive oil to the recipe. I really appreciate that this dish has a reasonable amount of fat and sodium.
We make this with anchovies, which is so delicious much tastier!
@Linda how many tins of anchovies do you use? I love anchovies but they can be quite salty

Sorry, not crazy about cheese with the sardines. Take a note from Southern Italians and use olive oil tossed crunchy breadcrumbs. Sardines have sufficient umami for the dish...if you want more, add anchovies. But no cheese, please!!
@Annabella my rule is always no cheese with fish. We Americans somehow feel that every pasta dish must be finished with grated cheese. Not so.

It’s nice to see this recipe that is straight out of the pantry and uses Whole Wheat pasta. Given all the articles in the times about heart and brain healthy nutrition that emphasize whole grains I find the dearth of recipes in NYT Cooking that use whole grains really frustrating. I always substitute but cooking time and method alterations are needed. Recipes that incorporate whole grains intentionally would be helpful.
@AndrewB I heartily concur (no pun intended)! I love the wonderful pasta dishes, but I wish they always at least set forth a whole grain option

I've made a nearly identical version with tuna many times, a favorite. Olives and capers are not necessary but a delicious addition. I'm not sure the Italians would approve of the parmesan, but I won't tell.
Great quick meal. I used yellowfin tuna in EVOO and followed the rest of the recipe. Very satisfying and tasty. The lemon is a key ingredient.
Would this be as tasty with tuna?
@Kim I’m going to make it with tuna this week and report back! I don’t see why it would not be quite good.
@Kim Try Melissa Clark’s recipe for Spaghetti with TunaCapers and Scallions(and Anchovies.). It’s yummy.

Super fast and terrific. I’ll be making this on a regular basis. I just received a gift box of tinned fish which had sardines with preserved lemons. They were great in this recipe. I wouldn’t hesitate to use any other type of tinned fish.

Elegant and tasty. I put the sardines in the oven on 375 for 20 minutes and they were nice and crispy. Red pepper flakes made a difference and was an unexpected nice touch. I ran out of parm cheese and substituted blue cheese. Delicious.

I made this last night with radiatore instead of spaghetti, and chopped anchovy filets instead of sardines (didn't have any sardines handy, but they're on my shopping list now!).  I threw a poached egg in with the pasta too.  It was absolutely delicious, very much like eating a Caesar salad bowl with little pastas in place of croutons.

For the next time, I would definitely cut back on salt, won't salt the pasta water, and will keep any crunchy topping unsalted.  Not for health reasons, just because the fish, cheese, and pasta tasted plenty salty without adding extra.

Seriously good weeknight dinner though, and it really did come together in under 30 min.  Yum!
Excellent even with pasta that is not whole grain
I’ve been looking for the perfect dish to begin incorporating sardines into my diet and this is it!

This recipe and various versions of it have been a back pocket favorite for years. I have a wildly prolific Meyer lemon tree and almost always have everything on hand...flexible with additions, subtractions and amounts but the garlic, lemon and red pepper are key.  It's still great without arugula if you have none but also works with baby spinach or very thinly shredded kale or chard.
Added extra olive oil. Used garlic confit mashed instead of grated garlic. I used sardines in tomato sauce because that's what I had, and it was delicious.
Great recipe!  I added some toasted breadcrumbs on top for a little crunch and texture.  Perfect!!
I substituted tinned calamari because I didn’t have sardines on hand and it was so tasty!

Thanks for a great recipe, Ali! It was definitely worth buying better sardines from Spain: meaty and not fishy. I added extra arugula but will stay with Ali’s proportion next time. Also surprised at the difference it made to peel rather than grate the Parmesan. I’ll probably use a short pasta next just so I can enjoy it faster!
So good! My 5 year old also loves it. Omitted the chili flakes. I even like it and don’t typically like sardines. I made it bc my husband and kids do! Less fishy tasting than a tuna dish imo!
Made this last night with no modifications.  It could not have been simpler or more delicious…Surprisingly Delicious!
Has anyone made a vegetarian version of this?
A lot of people fall to realize that boneless skinless sardines are mild and far less fishy tasting than tuna.
Question: Anchovie filets?

@Annabella 
 I actually like the Parmesan in there.  I know Italian dishes made with fish or other seafood are generally served without cheese, but plenty of people add cheese anyway.  If you don't like it, feel free to leave it out.
Bravo. I halved everything and it was a perfectly delicious lunch with a nice Verdicchio to wash it down.

Loved it! I read reviews and pulled in several suggestions from those… I air fried my sardines (which took away so much of the fishy flavor!), added lots of caper and green onions, doubled and chopped up the arugula and it was just fabulous!!! Will absolutely be making again!

I had some soba noodles instead of the whole wheat pasta - worked really well with a bit of chopped caper leaves and subbed garlic powder instead of grated garlic 
Very delicious and quick!

I like cottage cheese and I am always looking for new and interesting breakfast ideas.  This fit the bill nicely.  Fast and tasty.  Good effort to reward ratio!  I will be putting this into the breakfast rotation.

I just tried this. Now, my recent go-to breakfast has been a concoction my suster has dubbed "Apple Vomit" which is cottage cheese, peanut butter, a touch of honey or maple syrup,  and some cinnamon or pumpkin pie spice,  so this is just a variation on that theme. I used a raw tahini (the sesame seeds are not toasted) and maple syrup because I'm out of honey, and topped it with banana. It scales down beautifully to one serving, and it is tasty!

@E. Parker
Sisters! Gotta love their frank and saucy comments.
Really tasty quick recipe. If anyone is in the UK, I cannot recommend the Longley Farm cottage cheese brand enough!

@GG It’s truly life changing. Not much of a cottage cheese eater but stumbled on it while on vacation. It’s like discovering French cheeses after only eating velveeta. You realize what a poor product we’ve settled for here in the US, all salt and gloopy. And I’m including good culture in that group of subpar too. British and Polish cottage cheese is transformative.
This really elevated my usual mundane cottage cheese breakfast. I’m definitely adding this to my breakfast options.
Honey + tahini = halvah. I added it to my strained yogurt. Yum
Super tasty and rich. The pinch of flaky salt is key, don’t skip that.
Subbed Greek yogurt for cottage cheese. Delicious!
Adding a squeeze of tahini and honey is such a low-effort, high-reward move! Love this!
@Lee Rogers in U.S., Good Culture.
Agree that this recipe is a keeper. But it is a bit too sweet for my taste. I always follow a recipe exactly the first time but next time will reduce the honey or omit it entirely.
Really delicious!  Made it with blackberries, dried dates and pomegranate seeds and topped with torn mint leaves.  One of the best breakfasts in recent memory!
This sounded weird to me at first, but now I can’t stop making this.

The tahini with a touch of honey adds a creamy texture to smooth out chunky curds in the cottage cheese without overpowering the natural sweetness from fruit. (I only used a couple drips of honey.) I will enjoy this again!

Was craving something warm, so made a variant on this with homemade ricotta (NYT recipe!), some Bob's Red Mill 10-grain hot cereal, lightly baked apple chunks with tahini, honey and a dash of pumpkin pie spice. Heavenly!
For the US cottage cheese - Good brand.
@Lee Rogers Good Culture is my new favorite here in Northern California
I can't wait to try this. It would make a great dinner or lunch as well as breakfast. But I will ease up on the almost 4 teaspoons of honey and skip the flaky salt to keep sodium in check.

Or just get some good quality halvah and crumble on top!
Tahini- honey is a pairing made in heaven.  I make cashew cream weekly and then make the family a cashew milk, honey, tahini shake with whats left in the blender to applause.
Genius that the article mentions persimmon. I eat a breakfast of oatmeal and cottage cheese with a Hachiya persimmon blobbed over it whenever I can get it, come December and January.
This was good, and a good excuse to stir my jar of tahini.
Outstanding! One serving: 1c cottage cheese. 3/4c mango chunks, 1tb tahini, 1tsp maple syrup

Loved this recipe, love tahini, and love Ali Slagle's recipes and her cookbook. My dietician has been wanting me to get more fruit in my diet, and this really fit the bill along with the protein in the cottage cheese. The tahini really makes this simple dish sing. The sesame seeds may seem insignificant, but they really do add crunch. Look forward to trying this recipe with many different fruits and appreciate all the suggestions in the headnotes.
Wow, some things really don't photograph well.
@KatieC I also noticed the good stylist left the leaves on the strawberries. She was definitely reaching to make it look good
Surprisingly delectable!
Strangely delicious! Especially with fresh cherries.

Take the following facts into consideration, before strongly advocating for cottage cheese.
While cottage cheeses is an excellent source of protein, it lacks dietary fiber and is high in dairy components that can slow down digestion.
Dairy proteins like casein, combined with fat, take the body longer to digest, which can slow down bowel movements.
If one have trouble digesting lactose, dairy products can cause gas, bloating, and constipation .or worsen existing symptoms.
I always check the AI generated nutrition info on New York Times recipes that seem healthy-ish. They’re almost always super calorie dense but this one is under 300. Must be all the fruit. Well done.
Cut-up strawberries dusted with Splenda, then tahini and cottage cheese added for breakfast.  Forgot the sesame seeds, maybe next time.  But this is delicious!
As soon as I came across this recipe I had to add the tahini and honey! Yum!!! I pile up all kind of fruits on the cottage cheese. For crunch and extra protein I like to add hemp hearts.

title: Seared Orange Chicken and Broccoli Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody:
This came out really good.  Faintly Asian because of the soy. Only a few points: 
1. Not enough sauce as written. Next time, I will double the sauce liquids and/or add some chicken stock. I also added some sherry.
2. Don’t slice the chicken before adding it to the sauce after cooking. Dried it out. And deglaze the pan before cooking the broccoli. I use sherry.
3. If you want some zing from the jalapeños, leave the pith with the pepper. I took it out and the flavor was good but no zing. 
Enjoy!

@BobInBonedale I agree on the chicken. Used breasts, cut in half height-wise. Once done, chopped into 1” wide by breast-width pieces and added to the sauce. I used Aleppo pepper flakes because I had no fresh jalapeños. Great recipe!

After reading the instructions, I decided to change them. With the changes, this was utterly delice! Here’s what I did. I cooked the chicken thighs - did it in the air fryer for convenience, and chopped them into bite sized pieces. I put the vinegar, soy and Clem juice in a jar and added 1 tablespoon of corn starch. I chopped the ginger and jalapeño, adding 2 chopped scallions and put in a separate bowl. Then, I heated my skillet. Added oil, then the ginger, jalapeño, scallion mix. I let that cook for a couple of minutes then added the chopped broccoli, chicken, salt, pepper, a splash of water and a splash mirin. I added the lid and simmered till the broccoli was crisp tender. Then, I removed the lid, stirred the soy slurry well, added it, and cooked for a few seconds till the sauce thickened. Finally, I seasoned with toasted sesame oil dribbled on top.
@Essie disappointed with this one.  I do like your take on the corn starch.

This seems an overly complicated way to make what is essentially a stir-fry. Made the sauce, set aside. Heated a tablespoon of oil; sauteed the chicken thighs, which I diced and s & p'd. Remove from the pan; added another tablespoon of oil, and then added chopped garlic and red pepper flakes, then the broccoli. Sauteed until tender-crisp, then added the sauce (sans peel) and a wee bit of cornstarch slurry. When it thickened up a bit, added the chicken and combined. Served with brown rice.

@CYW this is exactly what I did! Except I did not add cornstarch so sauce was too runny. Also I did not find the juice from the clementines added the sweetness I expected, so maybe add a teaspoon of sugar to sweeten it up.
This was not terribly good.  Kind of boring and salty.

I read all the notes and it turned out very well for me.  Once I cooked the chicken I put it in a warm oven with the sauce.  Then once the broccoli was cooked I added the sauce to the skillet with the broccoli and after slicing the chicken added that, mixed it all up then sprinkled the cilantro on top.  I served it with rice and served the chicken/broccoli on the rice and spooned sauce on top.

Double the sauce. At the end, slice the chicken and put  the sauce and chicken in the skillet with the broccoli, warming over low heat for two minutes. Otherwise you combine just cooked broccoli with chicken and sauce that has been sitting around for 15 minutes and is room temp. Serve with brown rice.

I like the dish, great citrusy acid flavor. But it felt like it needed rice to soak up some of the sauce.

I would consider thickening the sauce too so it sticks on better. It’s really where all the flavor is coming from.

I liked this, but modified the instructions after reading the comments. I marinated the chicken in the sauce before searing it. I put the tangerine rinds in the skillet and smashed them down to caramelize them--I love the tangerine rinds in classic restaurant tangerine chicken! Next time I'll cut the rinds into strips after squeezing the tangerines, or perhaps peel the zest. I agree with others about quickly sautéing the spices and chili. It was good enough that I'll try it again soon.

This recipe turned me into the person I usually find mildly annoying…someone who changes the recipe. Despite the high stars, the comments were brutal and made it sound so blah. So I am sorry to say, I succumbed but it was delicious. I didn’t do anything too crazy…followed the recipe except for the following: for the marinade, I just used a large glass heat resistant bowl. I used 1 big orange, all I had, zested a bit but did not put the peels in. A little bit of cornstarch-1/2 teaspoon- and warmed in microwave for 1 minute. Then instead of taking the broccoli off and dumping in the bowl, I dumped the chicken and marinade in the pan after the broccoli was finished. I also used frozen broccoli, which worked great. The marinade thickened up nicely and was hot and not cold, which sounded unappetizing to me. I served it on a bed of cauliflower rice and my husband doesn’t like spice so I eliminated the peppers and just sprinkled sesame seeds only since I didn’t have cilantro. It was delicious- we loved it and will make again.
Haven’t made this yet but seems like sauce should be warmed
Rather boring, not enough sauce and weird to create a room temperature dish
@Will Exactly. Very disappointing.

This recipe was ok. I won't make it again. Usually when I make orange chicken I coat the chicken in corn starch and use ginger and garlic in the pan then heat and thicken the sauce. This sauce was just kind of a soup that made the chicken cold. 
I kept the clementines peels in the dish. I bought them organic and washed them really well other wise I wouldn't think it would be a good idea to add them.

This was a waste of time the saucecwas never cooked, ginger not perfumed, a cold weak dressing over cold cooked chicken and greasy broccoli.

Now, if the works had been instructed to return to pan, and after heating and bubbling a cornstarch slurry was added it might work except the ginger wouldn't have been tempered, the pepper wouldn't have been temoered/cooked and the vinegar not tempered.

Ditching because it is not properly constructed and a waste of time and ingredients. Zest tangerines.
Delicious.
Not bad but, IMHO, not as good as i’d hoped given the effort involved.

Good, but needed a little something. I grated the ginger which I think was helpful. Next time I’ll put the pan with the sauce and chicken in the oven on “keep warm” while the broccoli cooks. And will add the whites of 4 green onions to the sauce, or a grated clove of garlic.

This recipe is delicious with some modifications. I added a bunch of spring onions (White parts first in Green parts later). I also added a cup of shredded carrot and instead of the jalapeño I added a tablespoon or two of Sambal for some heat. Tamari instead of soy to complement the orange. Juice of 1 full orange instead of 3 clementines. Sweetened Mirin instead of plain old white vinegar and 1 cup of vegetable broth.
I heeded the earlier comments.  Increased the sauce liquids, and heated the sauce.  Poured warm sauce over unsliced chicken.  I was pleased with the results.

the chicken should not be overcooked. I followed the recipe for the first batch of chicken but shortened the time for the 2nd and it was definitlry jucier. I also should have followed my instinct to blanch the broccoli before sautéingto get tender aflorets w/seared on outside. But the sauce is the main issue-thin and bland, needs  garlic, onions and deglazing. I ended up adding bottled teriaki sauce for flavor and thickening but I don't think I'll make this again.

It’s ok. Edible and gets better the more you eat likely because of the jalapeno/serrano pepper in the dish. I thickened (with cornstarch slurry) and heated the sauce once the chicken and broccoli were seared.

I agree with the other users that the sauce should be heated in some way. Here’s how I prepared it: Seared the chicken on both sides, not cooking all the way through. While searing the chicken, cooked the broccoli and sauce and written in a 9x13 for 10 min at 375. Then sliced the chicken, cooked at for 10 min at 400 to continue cooking the chicken. Turned out very delicious!

I have been using my NYT cooking several times a week for several years and generally love the outcomes.  Occasionally there is a meh, but this was a disaster.  I wish I had read the suggestion to cook the chicken in an air fryer.  It should at least have been cooked at a medium low temp.  Even with a screen, my kitchen is filled with grease, and the thighs had a tough exterior from being overcooked on outside to get inside at temp . Hoping for leftovers, instead I am tossing the whole mess.

So, I've quadrupled the amount of sauce, halved the amount of vinegar, added some chopped garlic and ginger to the sauce and some honey. With those adjustments it turned out way better than on the first try.

I used peeled and sectioned "cuties" -- i.e. small tangerines-- plenty of them, and added orange juice to create more liquid.  I did not use any of the fruit skin-- not sure if this might have changed the flavor, but it was delicious!  It's helpful to add a slurry of approx 1 tablespoon cornstarch stirred into 1/4 cup water toward the end of cooking to achieve a thicker sauce with a nice glazed look.  Good with Thai or other small hot chilis.  I also added some fresh garlic.

The ingredients are all these - the technique is not. 
I completely re-worked this dish according to the notes (thank you, fellow cooks).

I made this last night, carefully following the instructions of commenter Essie. The only changes I made were to cut the clementine skins into strips, throwing them in to cook with the broccoli; and adding rice vinegar in place of the mirin.  The dish came out great, with a taste very similar to Orange Chicken from a Chinese takeout. That said, next time I will add carrots, red and yellow bell peppers, and mushrooms. The pairing of chicken and broccoli just wasn't enough to keep me interested.
Could someone advise as to the amount/weight of tofu to use instead of meat?  Thanks.
@Kathleen Minder, think you could use same amount as the protein in the recipe
Would it work almost as well using left over roast chicken? I'd like to avoid the step of having to pound and then slice chicken.  Sauce is different, good!
@Judith Absolutely. No reason at all why leftover roasted chicken wouldn’t work.
Intriguing recipe until I saw that it has 29 grams of FAT per serving.
You don't have to use that much oil if you don't want to. That's where the fat grams are coming from. Use the minimum needed to sauté, and a splash of sesame oil at the end for flavor.
I would have liked a more clingy sauce.
I added the sauce back into the seared veg, then added the sliced chicken and brought back up to temp. It was too acidic for my taste. More orange juice and less vinegar next time?
Made as written except turned the sauce into traditional stir fry sauce, thickening it with flour and cooking til bubbly before tossing meat and veg back in.  Delicious!

I use some of the sauce with brown sugar to make a deliscious citrusy sauce for the dish. 
In the pan sauté garlic, ginger in butter, add a good couple spoonfuls of brown sugar and mix over medium low heat in the pan.
double the sauce

Turned out great! After reading a few comments (and based on what I had on hand) I made the following modifications:
1. I used the pre-minced ginger in the tube. Probably not as spicy/fresh, but it did combine with the sauce very well. Swapped white vinegar out and used rice vinegar. Also used broccolini instead of broccoli and it was still great.
2. After cooking the chicken, and while it rested (unsliced!) in the sauce, I deglazed the pan by squeezing a couple extra mandarins in, and added that to the glass dish with the chicken/sauce.
3. Since the chicken was primarily for my S.O. And aim not big on poultry, I crisped up some baked tofu in the pan for me, and threw that in the sauce while I prepped the broccoli. Delicious!
I loved that I could use one pan for this, and it came together quickly. I’ll absolutely be making it again.
Vinegar was over powering. Way too much in this recipe. Would not make again.

I thought this could be fairly customizable. I ended up using venison and cauliflower for the main ingredients. My husband isn't a huge fan of sesame oil, so I dropped it down to 1T and added 1T of chile oil. I also added corn starch to the marinade, and let the sliced venison marinate in the sauce before cooking. Then added the remaining marinade to the cauliflower to cook and thicken up before adding the cooked venison back in at the end.

Agree that there wasn’t enough sauce. I halved the recipe and quickly realized I shouldn’t  have. We loved the flavor. Had to use cuties because couldn’t find clementines and it was fine. I will def thicken the sauce next time. Otherwise it’s a good salad dressing?
Made this with soy curls in place of the chicken.  Worked perfectly.

I enjoyed this but diverged from the recipe a bit. I used oranges and to emphasize the fruit flavor more than the salt I added some hibiscus juice, apple cider vinegar, and chopped dried apricots which I sautéed. I didn’t want to buy what I didn’t have in the kitchen already so instead of the suggested pepper I used a chili sauce packet I had from Panda Express and some spicy red salsa. I added chopped onions & garlic with the broccoli. everything back to the pan to simmer/heat up, serve w/rice
Cooked as a stir-fry with a little cornstarch added to the liquids, which were added at the end.  Will use orange or mandarin in my stir-fry in the future!
This recipe is more work than it seems. Doesn't make much/enough sauce, which isn't really sauce, as much as a marinade. Tastes ok, not great. Doubt I'll make this again.

To make this more flavorful, don't put the citrus skin in the sauce and then have to remove it - peel the skin off with a vegetable peeler and finely dice and add that to the sauce - you can just eat that and it makes for a more fragrant and flavorful sauce...
Why not roast broccoli while everything else is being prepared and cooked? Then add hot broccoli to hot chicken and sauce?

I had my doubts about this recipe because of all the competing flavors, but it was a knock out! I cooked it as directed (except I used tamari rather than regular soy sauce because it is gluten free) I used 5 fairly large mandarins, so had quite a bit of juice for the chicken and broccoli to swim in. Next time, I will use more ginger, as that flavor got a little lost in the mix. One inch of ginger can vary a lot- it would be nice for an actual amount to be specified.

Labor-intensive but flavor was good and we enjoyed. I marinated the chicken first, increased the amounts for the sauce (as well as chicken and broccoli). Next time I would up the spice, marinate chicken, cook the broccoli first, then cook the chicken, remove to slice while the sauce cooks, add some corn starch. and then return everything to the pan to warm.

Agree, cornstarch should be considered as well as more sauce.  Used Serrano chile with a left over shallot to add to garlic/ginger.  Worked well and distributed the heat well.  In a perfect world, I’d use the little peppers found in traditional orange chicken.  Also, used thighs.  Though thighs are great, this might be a chicken breast recipe…cleaner flavor.

I took everyone's suggestions to double the sauce, but I still used 5 tangerines (locally-grown!). I used 2 serrano peppers, seeds and all, and about 4 cubes of pureed ginger. I had some Trader Joe's Aminos sauce so I used that to sub in for the soy sauce. I couldn't find my toasted sesame seed oil, but I did have Korean Perilla Seed Oil, so I used that (a Korean friend said they can be used in similar ways). I added a splash of maple syrup for extra sweetness. We thought it was great!

Wonderful! Loved the tanginess of the citrus offsetting the richness of the sesame oil. Next time I'll cook it in my wok, and cut the chicken into smaller chunks before cooking. (I used chicken breasts, and they weren't quite done when I sliced them.)

We really wanted to like this recipe. With ingredients we enjoyed mixing, we were looking forward to what we thought would be a pleasant combo of vinegary sharpness, jalapeño heat, orange acid, and nicely browned chicken, but it disappointed. Carefully working through all of the comments helped, double the sauce, add some cornstarch and chicken broth to thicken enough to coat the broccoli,  don't add salt, add sherry. Ended up being an overly complicated way to turn out a simple stir fry.
Maybe I did something wrong?! Chicken was super dry and bland. Would not make again.
Really good. Made half recipe but would better with full recipe of sauce and broccoli even with half the meat. Used thighs. Served on jasmine rice.
Seeing as how a certain percentage of folks don't like cilantro (it tastes like dirt to them, guess), does it make sense for you to include parsley or some other tasty green in your recipes?
Although I was underwhelmed by the flavor when eating this for dinner, I enjoyed mine greatly as leftovers the next day. It was like delicious leftover Chinese takeout.
1/4 cup of to heavy avocado oil?  Wowser!  Perhaps spray pan with Pam ans substantially reduce oil?  Suggestions for subs, dear readers?

It was ok. I have decided that I am really not a fan of ginger. It was all I could taste, and I did add some aleppo pepper flakes as the sauce (that I had thickened a bit) was a little too sweet. And gingery.

I made a mistake with what I grabbed from the freezer - ground chicken instead of boneless chicken.  Decided to go ahead anyway - stir fried some onion and the broccoli - added the ground chicken until almost cooked, then added the sauce - Delicious last night, then again as leftovers.  Maybe the ground chicken countered dryness concerns!
Agree with previous reviewer who said to double the sauce. It’s flavorful as written but a bit dry. Double the sauce and serve with rice.

Excellent. I used sour Chinese oranges (sour like lemons) because that's all I had, added a couple of tablespoons of sugar to the rinds and a teaspoon of agave sweetener to the juice. I also had kumquats on hand and they made a superb addition. Quartered, seeded and tossed in whole. You can eat them whole with the chicken and broccoli and if you like kumquats they are quite wonderful that way. I agree that increasing ingredients for sauce is in order. But chicken stock would dilute the citrus.

Didn’t care for this one as the sauce was never thickened up and kind of bland despite the relatively large amounts of ingredients. The NYT roast orange chicken recipe is better and the same amount of work.

We liked this a lot, but we also did plenty of modifications based on comments here. One thing I did differently than anyone else I saw was sear the chicken and then bake it in the oven in the sauce after adding a bit of a cornstarch slurry to the sauce. I’ll roast the broccoli next time alongside some onion. Zest the mandarin oranges. You know, basically make a completely different dish!

This was good, but I'll make the following changes next time. I used very fresh, ripe mandarins (green leaves were still attached!), but still couldn't taste much orange; I'll zest them first, and just add that to the sauce and toss the peels. Did use a cornstarch slurry, maybe a small splash of sherry as well. The chicken was good, but this might be a good recipe in which to velvet sliced chicken beforehand. Good base recipe with tweaks.

This was delicious! the sauce to me IS the recipe- all the other ingredients could be changed. What i love about the sauce is its bright, fresh flavors - i ended up adding more orange juice to taste so that the orange flavor was just becoming a bit more noticeable. 
I do especially enjoy that the sauce is NOT thickened. i feel it's a great Spring recipe: simple, clean, bright and fresh.
may be better with less vinegar

I prefer healthier dishes so for this, I grilled the chicken in a grill pan with only 1 tablespoon of oil. I steamed the broccoli. Then sliced the chicken and added it all together with the sauce (I also cut the toasted sesame oil in half). I found it pretty flavorful AND healthy. Will try the cornstarch idea next time though.

I loved the flavours, though agree with many comments and will change things up a bit next time.  Did anyone find the orange instructions confusing?  What exactly goes into the sauce? The squeezed juice? The orange rind? The smushed clementine pulp? All of it?  I put all three into the sauce in the end.  Gave recipe four stars because I thought this was unclear.  But will definitely make again.

I saw a lot of people complain that there wasn’t enough sauce, or they decided to stir fry everything with the sauce. I followed the recipe as directed, but with a little less chicken, because all I had were two large chicken breasts, and I thought this was fantastic! I love how the sliced cooked chicken and broccoli soaked up the sauce. I agree that this is Asian inspired, but I don’t feel like it’s mimicking a saucy stir fry at all. I would advise people to try the recipe as it is before making modifications. Also, I tried this on my Blackstone grill and it worked out perfectly! This dish was light and refreshing! Definitely adding this to our rotation of simple and yummy dinners.

title: Red Curry Chicken and Rice Bowls Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: I'd take a couple of cues from Pailin of Hot Thai Kitchen: 1) use a quality red curry paste such as Mae Ploy; and 2) start cooking the curry paste in a small amount (~2/3 c) of good coconut milk, such as Aroy-D. The coconut milk will emulsify with the curry paste and then eventually the coconut oil will break out of the emulsion and the curry paste essentially fries gently. Use the rest of the coconut milk, thinned out with the chicken broth, for the cooking liquid. Lastly, I'd be inclined to use boneless thighs, as they do cook through in the given time, but failing that, pounding the breasts to a thinner depth seems the way to go.
@Contrapunctus I took your suggestions and it yielded the best rice I’ve ever made. The slaw is trash but the chicken and rice are soooooo good. Going to add onions to the rice next time.

I really enjoy Thai food and I have overwhelmingly positive results from the NYT cooking app. But this one was a miss. The chicken takes longer to cook than the recipe states. But cooking the rice longer results in the rice becoming paste. Would recommend cooking chicken and rice separately. The zest of two limes was too much and overpowered the slaw.
@Max I agree about cooking rice and chicken separately.  But I loved everything about the slaw! I didn’t use a prepackaged slaw, just savoy cabbage with everything else.

I found this dish to be quite dry. I had to scale it for 6 people but it still could have used more of the lime juice dressing. Would consider some flavor added to the slaw as well otherwise it simply adds another dry factor. Overall taste was quite nice.

@Dave Holland I found the chicken to be dry as well. Perhaps there’s something else I could’ve done to keep the chicken from drying out. However, I found eating it with the slaw It didn’t matter too much. I personally would’ve added less lime and more of the tangerine or orange flavor. Overall I really liked it!
@Dave Holland You could always use thighs instead of breasts, if breasts are too dry for you
@Dave Holland couldn’t agree more!!!!!!

I’ve recently learned the importance of “blooming” curry paste from chef/author Kenji Lopez, so I added that step first with the oil and curry paste. I decided to use mussaman instead of
red curry and it was delicious. As other said, the chicken was a little dry. I might pound the breasts next time or filet them so they’re thinner. The dressing for the slaw was delicious, with mint as the option. I added some green onion to it as well.
Chicks took far longer to cook than recipe said. I would recommend slicing the chicken first. I would also season it as the curry paste alone did not season the chicken throughout like salt would.
@Brad I will either cook the chicken separately or slice the thick breasts horizontally. I will also salt the chicken first.

Thought it was pretty good - I had very thick breasts so I cut them in chucks - salt and peppered them, used the whole jar of curry paste, didn’t remove the breasts when resting , doubled the peanuts in salad.  Not a big cilantro or mint fan but used mint- the salad grew on me . For the citrus I used canned no sugar added mandarin oranges

@Tamra D.  I see the size of chicken breasts these days and imagine that the chicken must have looked like a monster when alive!   Ridiculously large.  I no longer buy them, regardless of recipe ingredients.
@PhyllisJL They make me think about hormones.
I’m confused.  What do you do with the dressing you make in step 3.
@Dan M the recipe says to toss the tangerines, peanuts, slaw, and herbs with the dressing.
@Dan M it’s for the slaw - add the herbs, peanuts etc and toss.
"Add the slaw, peanuts and herbs to the dressing and toss to coat."  So the dressing is what completes the slaw.
Love Ali Slagle’s recipes, but this was very bland and very dry. I tried to spruce it up to no avail. Will not be repeating.
@Meh could not agree more. My wife made coconut curry sauce to give it some life.

Alright, I just made this despite the negative comments because the flavor profile seemed fun. I also changed a lot as I went and it ended up delicious. Main changes: 
- use chicken thighs
- salt the chicken 
- bloom the curry in coconut milk and oil before toasting the rice 
- use the remainder of the coconut milk from the can instead of the chicken broth. Throw 1/2 cup of chicken broth in there. 
- add a generous splash of seasoned rice wine vinegar to the slaw 

With these changes, I cooked covered for 20 minutes and it was pretty much perfect.
I love this so much! Buy
Definitely one of the worst recipes here. Too spicey and missing more depth in the flavors.
Wondering if this would work in a pressure cooker, which would yield juicier chicken?
@Ukiedancer  A pressure cooker would work well, I think. I cooked the chicken separately in the air fryer which always yields a moister chicken breast, in my experience.
@Ukiedancer did you try the pressure cooker? I was wondering about using an instant pot.
Did anyone try doing this in a rice cooker?  I would definitely use more broth as normal ratio for cooking rice is 2:1.

Made this tonight. Mistakenly bought “red curry sauce” instead of red curry paste—which differs as paste already mixed with coconut, but still “pasty.” Probably used closer to 1/3 c than 2T—but remember the paste was already diluted a bit. Left the chicken in the pot with the rice while both rested; had moist chicken and cooked rice. Would definitely make again

@Chris…I will say I needed to add more liquid to cook the rice (1c), and about 10 min more to cook the chicken and rice. The chickeb on its own was a bit dry, but as others noted, very good with the citrus salad
I loved this. Fresh, clean and non-fancy. Easy to prepare.
This was fresh, tasty and easy to prepare. You can easily use up the small jar of Thai red chili paste.

Just don’t…

After cooking, throw out the rice and chicken and enjoy the slaw with the second round of meat you prepare.
How would this be with thighs be more juicy?
For those that tried it and it was too dry, do you think you could swap out the breasts for boneless thighs and it would be better?
Quite delicious!  Next time I will use more curry paste, more fish sauce, add some curry spices, and make a bit more dressing.  But, we like more spice :)
I might tinker a bit next time - yes, more juice for the dressing, and more curry paste - but it's a clever recipe, hearty and flavorful and easy to pull together.

I think this needs some modifications

I chopped the chicken to bite size pieces first, and added to the dutch oven with some salt. I also added a lot of red pepper flakes and ginger powder. I started with 2 cups of stock but that started running dry so i added more as needed like a risotto and it came out with that lava flowing consistency. 

I just ate the slaw and tangerine on the side and glad I did

Delicious with the slaw served right on top of the chicken (which was otherwise a bit bland). Lightly salted the chicken breasts before cooking. Makes way too much rice. Will halve the rice and broth next time, and double the slaw.
This recipe needs to be tested again n. Rice stuck to the bottom of the pot but chicken breasts were raw in the middle even after I cut them in half before cooking. Just overall really poor.
My rice wasn’t cooked through (kind of crunchy) but the chicken was juicy and delicious. We all enjoyed the recipe. Will make it again but will cook the rice a bit before adding the chicken.

The flavour is good but it doesn't look anything like the photo due to the way it's cooked. Not as pretty. I didn't like the dressing as written so added some soy sauce and brown sugar to balance the acidity of the fish sauce and lime juice - that made it quite tasty. Overall, I would do again as it was easy and tasty.

It’s always tough to cook rice and leave it over the heat without it scorching. I’d recommend bringing the rice to a boil while stirring constantly, then adding the chicken, covering, and reducing the heat.
It has potential. My rice burned on the bottom. Too much rice left over as well. Chicken should be thighs. Salad was watery so couldn’t be stored for leftovers.

Easy "5" - exceeded expectations (perhaps from reading some of the comments)

Made exactly to the recipe with a couple of minor variations, cooking for two:
- 8 oz CH breast lightly pounded to 1/2" & divided
- cooked rice + chicken per recipe maybe < 20 min (adjusting for 1/2 recipe)
- chicken aside, cooked rice a bit longer with 1/2 C coconut milk
- sliced and served

The rice was perfect; a bit spicy but my wife ate it up well
What surprised me was the chicken. Possibly the most tender ever!

Nice, muffin-like texture. However one can clearly taste the bitterness of the stout. I usually cut the amount of sugar in US recipes (generally way too sweet for European palates) but here I feel the sweetness is needed to offset the Guinness. Next time I'll try adding some chocolate chips to go with the muffin theme or cover the cake with chocolate frosting as I feel it would benefit (but loose the delightful Guinness exterior)

We loved the Red Curry Rice bowls last night. I used the full 1/4 cup of curry in the rice in a Dutch oven. Added chicken thighs and cooked for 25 minutes then let it all rest covered for another 15 minutes. The coleslaw was the bomb!  Added and finely chopped jalapeño. We couldn’t stop eating the salad.
Absolutely amazing. So many good flavors cleverly mixed to allow for sweet and savory, soft and crunchy.  Immediately added to our regular rotation.
The slaw is the star of this recipe! Truly inspired. My chicken was juicy and tender but needed salt. Other than that, a nice, easy, and delicious dish.

I read them all. Took notes, bloomed the curry paste, added salt. Cut the breasts into cutlets. It still turned out just like the reviews mention. I added a makrut lime leaf. The rice was mushy and the chicken was dry. We put it all down the disposal and we NEVER waste food. This recipe simply does not work.
Dan M:  See step 3 - slaw mix, peanuts and herbs are added to the dressing and eaten as a side dish with the chicken and rice.
Ok, there is a big difference between 2 tbsp of red curry paste and 1/3 cup.  For those of you who have made it how much did you use?
I used the small jar of Thai chili paste sold in supermarkets with no problem. Spicy but not overbearing.

I'd really like some of the little peppers by the recipe so you know how hot it is - does 1/3 cup equal 3 little peppers?  Some of the NYT recipes are just too hot for me to eat. 3-4 little peppers  = very hot, 1 = mildly hot etc.

I've bought the red curry paste at the grocery store and used a whole jar and it's still bland.  

Then I bought a similar looking jar at the Asian market and a TB made us sweat... so I can see why there would be a big disparity on how much to use. 

I'm EXCITED to make this recipe - will be using the legit Asian curry and adding some Gai Lan or BokChoy for additional veggies...
Maybe “slaw blend” is an American thing, but I’ve never heard of it — what do you put in if making your own? Cabbage, carrot, anything else?
@Guy yes, it’s a widely available American thing. Typically a mix of shredded carrots, red cabbage, and green cabbage.
@Guy. It is usually a mixture of shredded green cabbage and shredded carrots.
Any suggestions for an acceptable substitute for fish allergies?

The chicken is boring none of the sauce stays on it during the cooking. Surprisingly the rice is also kind of boring really liking in flavor.  The only thing that saves it is the slaw and the dressing, which are really good.

I thought the chicken was bland. The slaw is what saved the dish. It was AMAZING. I’m going to try the recipe again but think of ways to season the chicken more because it would be a great weeknight meal.

I didn’t look at comments until after making it for dinner tonight, following the recipe. I agree it was underwhelming and my chicken breast was dried out. The rice got mushy trying to cook it longer. 
Next time, I’ll use cut up chicken thighs that have been salt and peppered. I used the small jar of red curry paste available at regular super market… but a better quality red curry and subbing a cup of coconut milk instead of all chicken stock could improve it. 
For my family, the slaw was too sour. (I liked it ok.) I’d make again but use less lime zest and juice. The mandarin oranges and peanuts were a delightful touch.

This dish was pretty bland and the chicken was dry. Definitely needs a quality red curry paste. What we got from the grocery store was unsatisfactory. Even with good curry paste, however this dish doesn’t seem like it would work. The addition of a good coconut milk would help and blooming the curry pace. Definitely don’t do this dish.

I should’ve read the comments before I cooked. We also had a hard time getting the chicken breast cooked at the same time as the rice. And we felt like the rice could’ve used more liquid. The slaw is fantastic. Both really need some salt and perhaps a bit more flavoring. I’m going to try a different brand of Thai red curry, and cut my chicken breasts in half or thirds next time. But I wanna give it another shot. It is fast and easy. Very little prep very little cleanup.

Two tweaks: cooked rice and chicken separately; used full amount of curry paste (mixed into cooked rice) but added a half can of coconut milk to cut the spice. 
Result was delicious.
I didn’t have any peanuts so I added some peanut butter to the slaw mixture. Not many people know it’s made from peanuts!
I liked the recipe, but was wanting a little sauce with it. I’ll fix it for the next try, because I will try it again, it hit thumbs up and yums all around! Thank you.

Yeah, hey, my first comment. I took this recipe and made my family happy. Appreciate all these chefs giving us foundations to work with! Trust me, I work with the 30/45 minute recipes first. 

Anyways, I swapped out chicken breasts with chicken thighs. Tastier, but probably defeats the original aim of the dish. That's OK. I poached them in coconut milk, slowly. And made the rice as is. Added more juice for the slaw and definitely more heat: serannos and side hot sauce for my teenagers. Love!

This was delicious! I don't know what other reviewers mean by dry, except maybe overcooked chicken? I sliced my chicken breasts in half to cook them in the ~20 min timeframe. Turned out perfectly and very flavorful. The slaw is a delight and a good use for a few of my toddler's Halos.
This was very, very good.  The slaw, peanuts, fish sauce and I added some avocado were a great addition. This was a light comforting winter meal.
Brine the breasts,  and they won’t be dry

High level comment - yum and would make again with following changes: When I started the recipe, added a larger chopped shallot to some ghee in the pan. Sautéed until translucent, added rice, red curry paste and sautéed as directed and then added 1 cup broth and 1 cup coconut milk. Proceeded as specified.  I made this with boneless skinless chicken thighs. They were not cooked when rice was done so added splash more chicken broth to the rice and brought it back to a boil, covered and cooked on low for another 5 mins. Put chicken in separate pot with some coconut milk and simmered for 5 more min. Made slaw as directed but added in some chopped ginger I had on hand left over from another recipe. Also added a teaspoon of sugar to the dressing based on comments. Used only mint as that’s what we had. Some of the rice got crispy on the bottom but that was actually yummy. Served with lime wedges and sriracha.
I found the slaw to be the star here. Everything else was a bland mush, I used more red curry paste than called for and it still tasted awful.

I added a fresh herb blend (that paste you get at the store)- Thai spice. I rubbed that and the chili paste on the chicken (boneless skinless thighs) beforehand. I forgot salt which it could have used. My chicken cooked in 23 minutes but not my rice. I used short grain and I’m at altitude, that could have been my problem. The slaw was excellent, I used more of the citrus and peanuts and less slaw. 

All in all would make again, with some adjustments to address the rice issue. The slight marinade/rub was good, and the spice blend addition was great.

Made fundamental modifications based on the reviews and it turned out a weeknight hit. The herby peanut slaw is a flavor bomb.
--Jasmine rice in the rice cooker
--Chicken breast in the Instant Pot with red curry paste and 1 can of coconut milk (definitely needed salt), shredded in the curry sauce after cooking
--Chunky peanut butter in the dressing to give it some body, agave syrup for balance

I’ve got a go-to red curry with chicken thighs recipe. But wanted to make something a little different. I did bloom the red curry (1/3 cup of Mae Ploy) per someone’s else’s suggestion, because I do this with the red curry & chicken thighs recipe. Using coconut milk for this is preferred, but some heavy cream and coconut oil work, too. I butterflied the chicken breasts, and used a size 30 (le creuset) Dutch oven, and found the chicken breasts sink in, so no need to add more red curry paste. I found that boiling and simmering low covered did not dry out the chicken breasts, but didn’t cook the rice. I had to continue cooking the rice by adding more broth, boiling, and the simmering again. The cabbage slaw is delicious with this. I didn’t think the rice needed additional fish sauce. I didn’t have scallions but wish I had used these to finish the dish. I’d make this again.

I thought this dish was awesome. Following someone’s recommendation, I added a small can of coconut milk to the curry in the beginning before adding the rice. My chicken breasts were large so I cut them in half before placing on top of the rice. The rice and chicken cooked well together. With a lot of lime juice and citrus, the slaw was the perfect acid needed to balance the dish. Family loved it; will make again.
Got the ingredients to make this but this is the most confusing set of "helpful" comments I've ever seen on this site.  Totally not sure what to expect.
Bland and dry. I foolishly followed the recipe with no changes. I don’t know how the chicken is supposed to have any flavor without any seasoning or salt beyond the curry paste. Will not make again.

I would make the slaw on its own for other recipes; really tasty (but I added more lime juice, cilantro and crushed peanuts than what was called for). The rest of this recipe is nothing special, but it wasn't bad. I followed the comments about lack of flavor and added far more curry paste and additional seasoningnfor chicken. onion or shallots would have helped but I didn't have them on hand.
About half the rice was uncooked. Something very wrong with the recipe

This was not our favorite. I sliced the chicken vreasts in half, as reviewers suggested (they were big), and they were overcooked. The rice was undercooked. It had a strong flavor, but not a great flavor. The slaw was decent- I especially liked the peanut bites.
Very disappointed in this dish. I followed the recipe completely…chicken was dry and rather tasteless in spite of the curry paste. I will not make it again.
This recipe is average. You will also have to add more liquid so the rice will cook and not be chewy.
loved the slaw...the chicken was fine as was the rice but wasn't amazing- will make the slaw again though
Too much curry in the rice. Rice did not cook all the way through. I would try less curry paste next time and cook the chicken separately.

It's fair, but hardly a stellar Thai (sic) recipe. Made as instructed – though the instructions are a little spare in the prep – I didn't find the chicken to be dry at all, quite the opposite. But a really good curry paste is key here, and without it, there's nothing there. Would likely have benefited from coconut milk. And I suspect I didn't "bloom" the curry for long enough at the start.

Also, it works with a brown rice substitute if you double the cooking time (removing the chicken first).
Hot tip to make the slaw even better: double the lime and fish sauce plus a good squeeze of honey and olive oil, to taste.
The dish sounds awful like a banana split served over stuffed cabbage.

Ugh! I wanted to love this dish, but it completely fell flat. The slaw needs more moisture or an actual dressing  - Would recommend preparing the slaw with your own dressing and flavors. Hope this helps and happy cooking!

We enjoyed this recipe. I read the comments and pounded the chicken breasts to an equal thickness and sprinkled them lightly with Kosher salt and let them rest while I toasted the curry paste in a little avocado oil before adding the rice. 
I served it with the slaw and the flavors of everything were excellent together.

The flavors here were quite good. I substituted red cabbage in the carrot and some green onion and added everything else. The amount of dressing is sparse you gonna double up on the lime juice and the fish sauce and the rice needs cooked with a little bit more liquid. Everything needs chopped to precision.  It’s very light and I love how there is no oil other than in the peanuts.

I made this last night and followed directions mostly as written. I used more broth than the recipe called for. I used a whole jar of the red curry in the rice and on the chicken breas. My chicken was juicy. I made the slaw as directed but added a bit more of the lime juice to balance the fish sauce. It was a bit one note but that being said, I found it to be tasty when all the components were eaten together.

I can’t comment on the cooking technique as I used some leftover grilled chicken breast, but I thought all the flavors were delicious. The rice had an excellent level of spice - I recommend ensuring you let it toast in the curry paste on the longer side, I got better flavor results that way. I skipped the tangerines in the slaw as I just wasn’t feeling it and imo it was still so good. The amount of dressing was perfect, it flavored the slaw well without producing any excess liquid. The best part: it’s quite easy and fast to cook. Perfect for weeknights!

I love garlic, but 3 grated cloves is too much. I would reduce the garlic and add a sweet element to the slaw. The chicken took way longer than the rice and was dry and bland. Either brine the chicken and then cut in halves, use thighs instead, or even swap for brown rice.
This recipe was a bust. The flavors were poor together. I like garlic cooked rather than raw so my mistake.

This seems like a recipe that could work being made directly in a rice cooker. I generally have luck with proteins and rice being done at the same time within the rice cooker. Going to test it out. Has anyone already tried?
I just saved this (I think!) recipe after seeing it on Instagram reels. That post mentioned papaya, but I don't see papaya here! I would love some guidance about how to include it.
Can this be prepared in a rice cooker?

Many commentators remark that the chicken seems dry and takes longer to cook than the rice. I have taken to slicing chicken breasts horizontally in half and velveting them before cooking (Chinese-style). This results in faster-cooking, more tender chicken.

My husband and I love Thai food and all curries, but wow this recipe dried out the chicken AND rice, and has very little flavor. The slaw was also over powered with lime. Maybe I had some very juicy limes on my hands. But unfortunately this was a disappointing dinner.
I followed the recipe exactly. Chicken and rice cooked perfectly. The salad is delicious as part of the ‘bowl’. Definitely cook again.

So glad I read the notes first! Grateful for you all, as always. Took the advice on using coconut milk with the rice and butterflied my chicken breasts. Used about a quarter cup of chili paste from H-Mart. Cooked chicken and rice for 25 minutes. Only zested one lime, added a splash of soy sauce, and navel oranges for the salad. Put the chicken pieces under the broiler for 2 minutes at the end just to add some texture. Absolutely delicious!

Using chicken breasts in the recipe is a big mistake, it was very dry and tough!!!!
if and that's a if, I was to make this again I would use chicken thighs.

I have never written a comment on a NYTimes recipe before, though I have cooked many successfully. This just didn't work for me. The rice cooked unevenly -- and, worse than that, the rawest rice was under the chicken (which was cooked, bordering on overcooked depending on thickness); I had to remove the chicken and then try to scrape raw rice off of it. At minimum, pound the breasts and cook the rice and chicken separately. But probably just try a different recipe.
My non-stick oan is my beloved cast iron skillet. Instead of flipping the eggs, once the edges are done I stir slowly with chopsticks from the center out. It’s a wonderful ritual.
Minus the vinegar (I know, a big omission) this is what my grandfather taught me when I was a little kid.
Gave eggs more flavor.
Delicious and simple. Cooked and eaten in less than 10 minutes.
I questioned the vinegar, but I say go for it!  I used a dash of red wine vinegar with no regrets.  I had farm fresh eggs, so my dish was brilliantly orange.  Ate these out of the pan!
Tried this today just minutes after reading this recipe. Delicious!
White vinegar?

Guess what I had for dinner! The vinegar is important: tried a few bites without, then added just the tiniest schpritz using my fingers. It transformed the eggs from overwhelmingly unctuous to sublimely balanced. All that butter is keeping it off my list of weekend go-tos, but when having guests over for brunch, oh myyyyyy, yes.

The eggs had a heavy cooked butter taste. Not
What I like for scrambled eggs

The eggs had a heavy cooked butter taste. Not
What I like for scrambled eggs  I personally prefer a lighter, fresh flavor.
As for the vinegar,  I found it added nothing   Why add something to cover up the flavor of another ingredient?
I am sure glad I read this - my eggs today were so much more flavorful - without any extra work or ingredients!   Thank you.
Is there some reason not to just add the vinegar to the eggs before they're cooked?  Seems easier and more evenly distributed.

Recipe seems great. Followed to a T. Medium heat. Chocolate color browned butter. Less than 30 seconds off the heat when edges started to cook. Rubbery eggs. No real Curds. Not shiny. Will try again but bummer
My Greek Grandmother always let the butter get slightly brown before adding the eggs.  I've been doing the same for 81 years.  That nutty flavor is a winner in our home.

I've made a version of these for years and bring them to Christmas parties. I use goat cheese instead of blue cheese and mix the cheese with a touch of honey and cinnamon. They look festive arranged on a round plate.
I make a version that combines the bleu (or goat) cheese with cream cheese and it topped with a toasted walnut. No need for the nut to be candied; the date is plenty sweet.
I agree. Candied nuts on top of dates sounds cloying.
@Jennifer I use candied pecans with goat cheese, and it is actually a good balance.  Can’t say for bleu cheese.
Seems like starting with pitted dates would make this faster.

Debbie - Re your comment that starting with pitted dates seems as if it would make this faster, it might but pre pitted dates are never as fresh and good as ones that have pits in them and pitting a Medjool dates is less than a finger snap of time.
@Debbie Agreed!  I make a similar date appetizer and use pre-pitted Medjools that I find in “sundry” area of my favorite grocer’s produce section.  So easy!

I stuff them with blue cheese and wrap a small piece of prosciutto around them.  Then I bake them in a 400 degree oven for 8 minutes.  The salty prosciutto and melted cheese make a lovely combination.  Delicious!
