I made a few of these a while back, kinda too sweet for my taste. So I made some with lightly salted toasted nuts, all kinds. I think the macadamias and the pecans were best.
These are always a hit... The sharp tang of the blue cheese balances the sweetness of the date.  I tuck a toasted pecan in with the cheese.  I keep these ingredients on hand and at the ready!
I have made this combo for years, after having eaten them at a small French cafe in Victoria B.C (of all places!).  I use sliced almonds because I like them better.
i do these with gorgonzola and roasted walnuts…and often a bit of prosciutto or bacon
These look great, but I don’t understand the 2-hour advance limit.  I would think these could be made far more in advance - I really don’t want to be making anything two hours before a party!

The only thing I can think of is the salt of the blue cheese might make the dates weep a little, but I'm not sure dried dates even have enough moisture to weep! I've made them wrapped in bacon and Frozen them with no issues so who knows 😁

Delicious. For a variation on the theme, would recommend omitting the pecans, wrapping the cheese-stuffed dates in bacon, and baking them in a 400-degree oven for 30 minutes, until bacon is done. Break out the toothpicks and enjoy the acclaim.
My mother made these in the 1960’s when we lived in Portugal.  She use fresh pecans, not candied.  I make these all the time and people really like them if you like blue cheese.
@Elaine Uh…my family lived in Portugal from 64-66, in Estoril, outside Lisbon. When and where were you?
My mother made these every Christmas, with walnuts instead of pecans and cream cheese in place of blue. Dates are so sweet that the cream cheese tasted like a sugared cream filling.
...add a drizzle of honey, as well

Made as written.  Easy.  Yummy.  I think candied walnut halves would work too if you don't have pecans handy.  As serving size comment, the recipes says 14 but although it makes 14 dates, I'd say plan on 2 per person.
They're also delicious without cheese and simply stuffed with two whole almonds and one walnut half all standing upright like soldiers!
I have done a variation on this, using brie cheese and chopped mixed nuts. What a great combo! The dates are so sweet that the honey isn't strictly necessary, but it doesn't hurt, either.
Yummy! I used goat cheese as that's a little more palatable than blue cheese (depends on your crowd) and a hit.  Drizzled with honey and people kept going back for more.
Loved them just as the recipe called for.  So did everyone else I made them for.
The BEST dates come from Rancho Meladuco in California.  Once you’ve tried their dates - there’s no going back.
Easy peazy and delicious appetizer! I disagree with others who say the candied pecans make it too sweet. The blue cheese and pecans are a wonderful mix of sweet and tangy goodness!
Taking these to a party tonight. Will report back!
Stellar. These have been the hit of every event I’ve taken them to this season. And they’re so easy to make. Double win
These sound great!  I like a simpler variation.  Pit the date and open it up, add about a Tablespoon of whipped cream cheese and add 2 walnut halves on top.  So quick and so good!

I was worried about the spiced nuts being too sweet so I used plain, which I regretted. The flavour was still great and people gobbled them up, but I think the spiced nuts would have added an extra kick.
Wow!  This is a delicious dish!  A nice dessert that is easy to assemble.  10/10!!!

The Medjools I bought were quite large. and we found that halving them and using less cheese to fill made it easier and neater to eat as an hors d'oeuvre. The candied pecans I bought were not a pretty topper and added too much extra sweetness. Next time I'll make my own very lightly sweetened candied pecans or just use a roasted pecan! However, I'm happy to have discovered this recipe and all the good suggestions. Thanks everyone!

I made this recipe for Thanksgiving using a similar NYT recipe with olive oil and black pepper instead of honey/chili.  It was gone in a flash (I doubled the recipe). The one comment that was spot on is to use a mini processor, not a hand mixer.  The results are simple yet outstanding.

I made this recipe exactly as written (with Calabrian chili paste as the spice), and the moment I tasted it, I regretted not doubling it! The whipped ricotta becomes so light and airy, and the swirl of sweet and spice is sublime! I served it with baguette, and everyone from the family foodie to the pickiest eater adored it! It disappeared in moments. I’m going to make it again tomorrow. Thanks for another wonderful recipe, Ali Slagle!

Even extremely "creamed" Ricotta can still have a grainy texture.  I suggest other cheeses, especially cream cheese or a blend of cheeses instead.  Also, hot honey is good, but try Sweet Chile sauce purchased from an Asian grocer, because many of the brands from regular markets are flavorless.
@Jeanne Bishop The graininess of ricotta varies by brand.  My favorite is Galbani.  It will get smooth following this recipe.

@Jeanne Bishop definitely agree with the sweet chili sauce, for a simpler version of this one of my go-to snacks is plain whipped cream cheese with Thai sweet chili sauce with some sort of buttery club cracker, it’s delicious!
@Hamish MacBeth I do the same and sprinkle with chopped coriander, even more delish

I made this for Thanksgiving and it just seemed very bland. It was fine with a crudite and crackers but I think it might be better with an addition like pesto or raspberry puree or something to dress it up.

What if you don't have a food processor? Can you use something else?
@Dan

@Caitlin If you have one, try your blender. I do have a food processor but my blender is on the counter so I often use that instead. It works just as well. I should note that my blender has a small, bullet-style container. The standard blender container might be too big for this recipe.

I wanted to love this, but it didn't quite get there for me. The ricotta did whip up beautifully in my mini food processor, but it was also runnier than I prefer for a spread/dip. I wanted the flavors incorporated throughout, so I stirred them in fully then topped with some blobs that I swirled with a toothpick. This is what I used for flavoring: Calabrian Chili Orange spread (jam), roasted red pepper chili paste, and a smidge of Sambal Oelek for more heat. It was good, but not fantastic.
Could one just use hot honey?
I had something similar at a roadside diner that blew me away - on toast with sunny side eggs on top.  HEAVENLY.  Missy's Main Street in Rockaway, NJ.  Hope it's okay to share the restaurant!
@Nicki OMG, that’s my hometown! I’ll need to go to Missy’s when I’m back there to visit.

If you want a variation that's sweet and a little spicy (I can't tolerate spicy foods), use jalapeno raspberry jam. Simply heat in the microwave for 30 sec to make it more pourable. If you don't have a food processor, use a whip and mix it with softened cream cheese. Another alternative is adding lemon zest from 1/2 lemon. And if you want a sweet version, add the lemon and 1/2-1 c powdered sugar. The possibilities are endless!

I've made this with 1/2 mascarpone and 1/2 ricotta. The honey chili flavor from the chili crisp I used--hot!--was delicious. The texture was light and airy. The mascarpone makes it a touch sweeter than plain ricotta.   Replacing the chili crisp with jarred pesto is also delicious. Having both options, with a tray of freshly-crisped baguette chips, makes a pretty plate.
Is there any reason all of the ingredients couldn't be incorporated in the food processor?  Also, why "serve right away"?  Couldn't this be made in advance of a party, say, early in the day?
I have the same q. Why not whip the honey and chili paste into the ricotta?

@DS this was our question an and the swirling technique left picked of no flavor other than whipped ricotta which is bland. IF we made this again I would 100% just put it all in the processor and maybe just add a drizzle of honey between the two layers and on top.

@Fred I tried out the suggestion of putting everything into the food processor - though I haven’t done the original preparation to fully compare, I’m quite pleased with how it turned out. I think I’ll always do it this way :)
I used an immersion blender to whip the ricotta. On high speed, the ricotta turned into lovely fluffy creamy texture with no grains.

This received loads of compliments from my book club but next time I'll get regular Calabrian. I used Chile Spice, which kind of overwhelmed the flavor and made it taste more Asian-y -- like something you'd smear on rice and hot chicken? I ended up redoubling the sweetness to calibrate it and at the last minute stirred in a bunch of agave and cream cheese. Still a lovely and super easy treat and good on a plate of vegetables and apples!

You can also make this with the Friendship Whipped Cottage Cheese for a healthier and higher protein option.  It's delicious.  Adding a tablespoon or heaping teaspoon of apricot preserves is another adding touch - the sweet and hot honey combination - so good!
I do not have a food processor, but I do have an immersion blender with a whisk attachment. Would that whip the ricotta as well as a food processor does?
I don't have a food processor anymore - instead I have a Vitamix blender.  Could I use it instead?  Will it work?
Can you substitute cottage cheese for Ricotta?
If you want to make a totally sublime whipped ricotta dish just as simple as this one use whipped ricotta, honey and fresh basil.  Optional addition is to top it with slivered almonds.

Well, I had high hopes and I followed directions exactly, but this just didn't hit the spot for my family. My ricotta (whole milk) never became smooth and fluffy, and the flavor was underwhelming. Given all the positive reviews, I'm not sure where I went wrong, or if this is just not for us.

Very good.  Takes minutes and you can prepare it in minutes. Note instructions state "serve immediately" I layered it (first layer) and refrigerated that plus what remained in the mini processor 2 hours prior to and finished it right 10 minutes or so prior to serving. Did not seem to impact flavor, did not separate, was not grainy.
My girlfriend just made this and said it was awful. She threw it all out. Boo!
This recipe did not work for me. It was bland and too sweet for crudites or crackers. The combination just tasted odd. Very few misses with NYT recipes, but in my opinion, this was one of them.

I tried to make whipped ricotta last weekend and it didn't whip it was very liquidy. Would I have better luck if I strained it first? That step is not in these directions... I used a ninja food processor and I felt like I did it for way longer than 2 minutes.

It’s so easy to make ricotta! There’s a recipe here in NYT. It is much better than any premade ricotta. You can also regulate how dry or wet it is. And keep (freeze if you want) the whey for soup, smoothies, whatever.
Instead of chili paste, harissa is suggested. Is that harissa paste and if so, how can I make the paste? Or harissa powder - that’s in my pantry. Thx.
For those of us without a food processor...could you fake this with a blender?
Very good and simple dip! I served it with Stacy's pita chips which didn't do it justice. Will do higher quality pita chips or sourdough slices next time.
The sum product is better than it's parts on this recipe. Very good!
So tasty and very easy.  Highly recommend! We used Tin Man Carolina chili crisp.  It has peanuts in it so complemented the Tahini.
Meh we won’t make this again we thought it was largely flavorless.  The texture is great whipping the ricotta will become my new trick for whenever I use it but otherwise we found this unimpressive.
Used hot honey!

Ok, going with all of the commentary about just mixing it all together, I mixed in 2 tbsp honey and 4 teaspoons of mild Harissa. My daughter’s comment was, “I could eat a bowl of this.”  I would have gone heavier on the harissa but we have some folks here who can’t take even a little heat. Will top with chopped cashews.
This was very “meh” and not worth cleaning my mini food processor over. I actually prefer the traditional American cream-cheese-and-jam appetizer to this bland concoction.
Did not care for this at all. Had all the ingredients but thought it was tasteless
I used lactose free ricotta because of my auto-immune issues. It was wonderful.

made this for xmas eve with calibrian hot peppers and it was delicious. my nephew stirred it immediately because he thought i forgot to, kind of hilarious, but that didn't matter. everyone loved it, if they like the heat, as it was pretty spicy. will definitely make this again. the ricotta got exquisitely creamy in the food processor.
Can you make this in advance?

Too much salt! I recommend whipping just the ricotta and then adding salt to taste. Also, as other commenters have pointed out, it's runnier than what's in the picture. We even tried to add heavy cream to get it to fluff up a bit. The taste was good, but, overall, disappointing.
I mixed the honey and chili crisp with the ricotta then added a dollop of Divina’s Calabrian Chili Orange Spread in the center of the whipped mix. WOW!

Incredibly easy and super tasty! As someone else noted, I used both mascarpone and ricotta but only because I wanted to use the 1/2 container of mascarpone up before it went bad. I dotted mine with harissa paste. Served with toasted baguettes, sliced apples and sliced pears. A HUGE hit!!
This tasted like bile to me: creamy, hot, sweet, yuck.

I made this for several parties over the holidays.     First party, just my daughter and I ate it.   We both liked it.   Second party, I only got two comments it was good and half was left.    The third time, I added more chili crisp and it was gone.    I got no compliments though and I think all the kids had eaten it.    My daughter loved it, and I enjoyed it.    I think it just needs a little more pizzazz.    I read maybe add a jelly to the top???  Next time!

I should have given more attention to the instructions and read between the lines.  I made this for a cocktail party and prepared it right before guests arrived.  Given that, I wasn't able to spend much time seasoning it, but I found this extremely bland, even when mixing the chili paste and honey into the ricotta.  It didn't take long for this dip into a gloppy-looking mixture.  Not at all enticing.  I'm not sure what the appeal is for this dish.

Yum. Yum. Super extra, yum. Easy to make and the hit of the holidays. Used Momofuku chili crisp with the honey, and sprinkled chopped pistachios on top. Beautiful to see and a delightful addition to our entertainment menus. Thank you for such great recipe.
Made for a party using Harissa sauce for heat. It was okay, but nothing special and was a little runny. There are better dips out there, in my opinion.

Having read some of the comments, in order to firm this up (esp. since I was not serving right away as I was taking it to a party) I added 4oz of cream cheese to the mix. It had a good consistency (like hummus). I also found out that a good reason NOT to just combine everything - the result of doing so made the dip look like sherbet which was not particularly appetizing in my opinion.  I also doubled the recipe (for 10) but most of it was leftover, although most people said they loved it.

this is delicious.... but... I'm not getting the texture/airiness I'm seeking that I've had at restaurants (i.e. Bettina) I use my food processor and I've tried a few different brand of ricottas including one from the cheese shop. Am I not blending it long enough? Any recommendations? (The first time I used an immersion blender and it was very creamy but good. I'm afraid to over blend.) I have a delicious Italian crushed calabrian chili paste too!)
I believe the spice/veggie is chili.  The country is Chile.
use half the salt recommended

Good choice for a no-fuss appetizer. I used Trader Joe's Crunchy Chili Onion with local honey. The recipe called for 1 tsp of salt which I cut in half. Will try using a flakier salt next time and a food processor instead of the hand mixer. Served with Simple Mills Mediterranean Herb GF pita chips for book club.
Delicious appetizer with crostini!
This was delicious with breads but I don’t recommend the apple suggestion bleach

title: Pineapple, Ham and Jalapeño Bites Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: I love this combination. Might add a piece of Havarti, or smoked provolone to the skewer.
@Kevin C. My thoughts exactly!

I plated these deconstructed on a thin round of bread smothered with an herbed tomato sauce and some mozzarella cheese. Most of my guests loved it, but the ones who didn't had a hard time letting the others enjoy it, and some of them still go on about it. 

No matter, at my next dinner party I plan to lean heavily into cilantro-based canapés that everyone can agree on.
@ajs wow. What are they saying?

I made this for a cocktail party tonight. It was very well received!  Tip: not all Jalapeno slices are easy to skewer, so I ended up wrapping them in the prosciutto, then skewering. I also dusted the whole platter with a bit of Piment d'Ville powder for color, and also sprinkled a bit of the Jalapeno pepper jar liquid on the entire thing.
Tbh I was sceptical but these flew off the plate! Also added cheese (manchego). If you like Hawaiian pizza, this is your jam.
I bet making these with pepawdew would be fantastic and the bright red would be pretty.
I think these are begging for a quick broil.

I assembled this app for a Christmas Day buffet. Folks loved it. I used regular toothpicks, because that was what I had on hand. It was very difficult to keep the jalapeños intact. I repeatedly jabbed myself and the jalapeño juice seeped into my "wounds." So, I made it again for a New Year Day event, this time using toothpicks designed for apps, as shown in the recipe photo. It was much easier and equally well received. No injuries and a cleaned plate at both events.
Good idea.  Simple quick and spicy.  I think I'd substitute something else for the jalapeno, but I'm not sure what   Appetisers are SOO important.
Could I use smoked salmon instead of prosciutto?
Did I brush hot honey on the prosciutto?  Maybe!
Made these and added cream cheese

It's likely nearly impossible to find pickled jalapeños that don't fall apart when skewered, and prosciutto is so thinly sliced that it's hard to work with.  Determined, I ended up buying 3 different brands of pickled jalapenos — the Veggie Confetti Pickled Jalapenos from WF were the least mushy, but still not firm enough to be pierced and hold a toothpick. Also, serano and speck held up MUCH better. I added smoked Gouda, as suggested in a comment, but they're still a fail.
So easy to make and a very popular party fare.  Perfect combination of flavors.
Has anyone tried it with fresh jalapeños?

title: Two Roast Chickens With Citrus and Sage Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: Step 2 should come after Step 4.  Otherwise your sliced oranges and onions sit on the sheet pan 8-24 hours while the chickens marinate.

@ALC, the microbiologist in me agrees with your comment. I can’t see any benefit to the chicken having it sit on top of veg during dry brining, but the thought of raw chicken juice dripping on a carb-rich base for an extended period seems to be an invitation for a few bad bugs to multiply

Thank you for a recipe that uses normal ingredients that an average American cook would have at home, except for the fresh sage.  I will use dried which I have at home.  The fresh herbs at the grocery store are too pricey for this old lady living on a fixed income.  I love your recipes however they are often recipes for the wealthy elite.  I suspect the NYT Cooking staff have not experienced cooking with reduced financial means.

This was absolutely delicious despite the confusing directions regarding the sage leaves. And whether you make gravy from the flavorful drippings or not, the drippings are incredibly flavorful. We will make this over and over again!

Made last night as written except cutting in half for one chicken. Excellent! Meat was juicy and succulent, and the combo of onions, citrus, and sage was perfect. A delicious and comforting meal that didn’t feel heavy. One bunch sage usually contains two or three sprigs. I put one sprig of sage into the rub, one under the chicken, one in the gravy.

Needs a video. Recipe has some good ingredients but directions are confusing. For instance it says to add the sage leaves to the bowl with the oranges, lemons and brown sugar. To mix and rub this over the chicken. Later it says to place the sage leaves between the chicken and citrus on the sheet pan?
Prep time 5 minutes?! Surely that's a misprint.

I'd like to know, where do people buy 3.5-4 lb chickens? I see a lot of recipes written for this size, but the smallest whole chicken I can find at the grocery store is at least 4.5-4.75 lbs, more often 5-5.5 lbs.
@KL Trader Joe’s sells a chicken this size in CA. It’s free-range & organic, too. It’s just the perfect amount of fat; others (think Costco) are too bland, too fatty.
How do you adjust the dry brine if you're using a Kosher chicken that has already been salted?

@Lisa
I would omit the salt in the recipe

@Lisa my husband and I cook kosher meat exclusively at home and we’ve found that it always needs salt. The kosher meat (beef, lamb, chicken, etc.) is not as brined as non-kosher brined meat is. Bear in mind, we dry-brined our kosher turkey this year and the skin and drippings were a touch too salty, and we wiped off most of the salt. Happy cooking!
@Sam the idea is to feed a large group. recipe also mentions roasting one chicken for a smaller table

Step 3 says “skin side up”….Isn’t there skin on both sides? 
I am not clear on if they’re to be roasted breast side up or breast side down?
Maybe I am missing something.
Never mind, sorry, I think I get it now…once butterflied, the skin will mainly be on one side. sorry.
Grocery stores don't sell 3.5 to 4 pound chickens anymore. You're lucky if you find one below 5.5 pounds.  Let me know where you find them.
@Midwestern Joy I bought one today at Whole Foods. 3.5 lbs.
Not clear to me if the sage is added to the brown sugar mix or kept separate
@Up north there’s 2 sprigs. One goes in the brown sugar mixture and the other I think u Dee the chicken on top of the citrus?
Why 2 chickens? One seems like enough lol
If I have one chicken at 4.9 lbs, how long should I roast it for.
Can I replace whole chicken just with chicken breast and drumsticks without skin ?

This was delicious. Next time I would remove the sage leaves (from the dry brine) from the skin before roasting - they burned pretty badly and looked unappetizing. First time spatchcocking a chicken and it wasn't difficult at all! Not sure it made the roasting process faster because it took about an hour and a half for the internal temp to reach above 165. No matter! I will definitely make this again!!
Can you just buy a spatchcock chicken for this recipe? I think that's essentially cut the same way as described ("butterfly")

Excellent flavor. Definitely easy to prepare. But I think the beans to pork ratio is off. I'd go with two pounds of pork, especially if you want to feed six. And I'd opt for cannellinis over coronas next time. Coronas took much longer to cook than 8 hours at low.

@Susan I agree with increasing the meat and experience with the Corona beans. I used my Instant Pot slow cooker setting. After 6 hours I switched over to Pressure cook beans/high pressure for 10 minutes, beans were perfect.
Great recipe, followed as instructed. Needs some acid to finish, once done cooking.
@LTS Thank you! Could you please share a good option for adding acid to this recipe?
@JOK Red wine vinegar or lemon juice works great!
Can you make this with an instant pot on pressure cooking?

In the IP it will take unsoaked beans between 45 - 70 minutes, depending on their age. If you pre-soak it will take 20-ish minutes. My suggestion (and what I personally plan to do) is to soak the beans and cook the whole thing together for about 20 minutes. I would use the same amount of water to ensure the beans cook properly. If there's too much liquid at the end, just uncover and set to "saute mode" to boil it off.

@Sis I used the instant pot  to make this dish.  It worked great and only one pan to clean!. I Started by browning the meat on the sauté setting, deglazed the pan, then used the slow cook setting (low) for 6 hours. I used Rancho Gordo Corona beans. did not soak in advance. After 6 hours on the low slow cooker setting I opted to  switch to  10 minutes high pressure to finish cooking the beans. Beans perfect and meat was tender and delicious.

I'm confused about what to do with the halved garlic heads after they have been cooked "cut-sides down ... until golden brown, 2-4 minutes." Should the cooked, halved cloves be peeled before moving to Step 2?

I doctored it a bit based on what I had on hand--1lb5oz pork loin and 1/2lb of beans. Added a parm rind, because I have a few kicking around. Needed more seasoning, so I made a gremolata of more rosemary, garlic, lemon rind and pepper corns. Then it was amazing!
Question:  What is a "garlic head halved crosswise"?  Is it a head (bulb) of garlic sliced in half so that the slice is parallel to the bulb's growing position?  Or what?

It means cut the entire head of garlic (containing all of the individual bulbs) straight across the middle, horizontally (perpendicular). You’ll essentially be slicing each clove in half, while keeping the head of garlic together. When the two half-heads are added to the Dutch oven in the first step, they’re placed cut-side down, allowing the now-exposed garlic to make contact with the heat at the bottom of the pan. It’s also easier to remove the garlic (and its peel) this way before serving.

I apologize for being a bit unclear in my first response. The entire head of garlic is the bulb. The individual cloves inside are not bulbs, although I mistakenly called them that at one point. However, if you plant an individual clove of garlic outdoors in the fall, it will grow to an entire bulb by next summer and can be harvested. Garlic is very easy to grow.
Would this work in a pressure cooker/Instant pot? I have had great success with dried beans.

@David Shuler Instant pot is a perfect option for this recipe. You can saute/brown the meat, slow cook the dish and opt to use the pressure cook feature if you are short on time. I used corona beans which held up well to IP
Mushy and bland--I probably made a mistake somewhere in this recipe, but the margin of error is so slim that I won't attempt this one again anytime soon.
Just to confirm - the beans do not need to be pre-soaked?
I recently read that dry beans contain a toxin which is destroyed by cooking but that, do do so requires a temperature higher than that in a slow cooker.

Anne Asher - You are correct that certain kinds of beans, like red and white kidney beans need to be boiled to neutralize the naturally occurring toxin, lectin, in them.  Cannellini beans are white kidney beans.

Made this today, using somewhat more pork. It was ok, definitely needed something else but not sure what. Cannelini were a little more firm than I prefer after almost 9 hours. Lots of leftovers; I suspect this will be better the next day (and maybe with additional seasoning). It was super easy to throw together this morning!

I doubled the pork shoulder and cooked as written (that was the smallest piece at my store).  It’s a bit brothy for my taste - i like thicker stews and soups.  I took out some beans and mashed them up with a bit of corn starch and added them back in.  Next time I will reduce the amount of water added when it goes into the slow cooker.

Note that the beans were done in less than 6 hours - I used Low on an All-Clad slow cooker.
Did you cook it on low, med, or high for your 6 hr result?  Also Corona or Cannellini?  thanks

Really, really flavorful and satisfying dish! I added spinach for a little color and so we didn’t have to bother with a salad. Also added a bit of red pepper flakes, just because. Added a few teaspoons of vegetable “Better Than Bouillon”. Used 2 cans of Northern Beans instead of dry beans, which turned out great. I’ll definitely make this again!

Not sure what I did wrong but came out very bland. I did use more pork (2 lbs) per some others suggestion, and the beans were not fully cooked at 8 hours on low. Cooked on medium for 2 more hours and still a little bit too much bite for my taste. Adding lemon at the end helped but I’m scratching my head at how this came out so flavorless.

I typically soak dried beans overnight unless they are lentils or split peas. I often use my pressure cooker to cook them for 10 minutes or so in stock suited to the recipe. I then add them to the sautéed vegetables and protein if using. I can’t consume spices the way I once did. I find I’m enjoying the flavor of the ingredients more with a subtler seasoning than my younger self’s heavy handed peppering. It’s been a process. Yes to smoked spices/ additions and savory friends such as olives and anchovies, as well as the brightening powers of interesting vinegars or citrus peels and juices. 
I love adapting recipes with what I have on hand- stretching my resources rather than rushing out to buy ingredients.
I'm thinking that, even omitting beans as I must, I'll still be rewarded with a pretty tasty dish. Stay tuned ~

What happens to the garlic after it is cut in half and cooked cut side down? Do you put it in the slow cooker while still in the peel? Do you squeeze it out and put it in the slow cooker- when do you put it in the slow cooker?
@valerie I fished it out at the end. I assumed it was oily add some flavor sitting in the pot all day.
Do the beans need to be soaked overnight and cooked first?  Just soaked?
Hi Lynn, No need to soak the cannelini first. This was a revelation to me! Just made it, and the beans were done in seven hours or so. Very delicious!

Flavors were aromatic. Would either increase amount of pork shoulder to 1.5 # or decrease amount of beans to half pound. Add a cup of dry white wine to mix. Served on half a kaiser with center scooped out, drizzled small amount of olive oil and it was perfect. Used the braised garlic bulb to butter the kaiser before adding the beans and pork. Pasta or rice would have been overkill (carbs, color) Lovely served beside a green veg and salad. A full meal deal. Ground pepper for table. Easy receipe.

I plan to prep this tonight for the slow cooker while I work tomorrow. If you've made it, does it seem a little bland? I'll probably add some minced sauteed garlic just in case. (You can never have too much garlic.)
@Barbok it was flavorful (I did add some fennel seeds). Be sure to use salt and pepper to season meat and again when the instructions indicate adding 1-1/2 tsp more.
@Anne Asher Kidney beans. This recipe calls for white beans which don't need a hard boil to neutralize the toxin.

Kaz - Cannellini beans are a type of white kidney bean and like red kidney beans they do need to be boiled to remove the lectin.  Corona beans are a runner bean but most people will likely think of the Royal Coronas, the giant one, sold by Rancho Gordo and they take a LONG time to cook plus a pre-soak to get right. I would choose a different bean for this dish if using a slow cooker. Maybe navy or Alubia Blanca.

The 4 stars is for the recipe as written.  I agree with an earlier commenter that acid is needed to lift the dish otherwise it comes across as a bit "meh."  I added apple cider vinegar and boiled apple cider to taste and felt that made a significant improvement.  Before settling on that outcome I tested balsamic vinegar and lemon juice.  Apple cider vinegar on its own best matched the flavor profile but the addition of an equal amount of boiled cider rounded the acid notes.  Do this to taste.

I love this recipe, and the results are glorious.  But it needs more meat -- at least two pounds.  After all, it's porchetta.  And after cutting the pork into 1-inch pieces, why would you use forks to break up the pork??  It's perfect in 1-inch pieces.
0-6 servings? Seems risky.
Can I do this in the oven?
@Artist - a link to the non-slow cooker version is in the description. here is the link: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/760135278-porchetta-beans?unlocked_article_code=1.m1A.m-Tb.NYA6wg58t0M6

The recipe doesn't say but definitely soak the beans overnight or use the quick method (sort & wash beans; put in large pot & cover with 2" water; heat to boiling & boil for 2 minutes; take off heat, cover, & let sit for 1 hour; drain). Dried beans won't cook in only one or two hours, or they won't cook enough. They'll be semi-hard & undigestible. Also, skip the garlic bulb & use minced garlic instead---easier, less mess & same result.
@Carolinewhile everything was being prepped I roasted a big whole head of garlic with olive oil. When cool I squeezed it all into the simmering soup! Excellent!

This is actually untrue.  How long the beans take to soften depends on how long they've been stored. I've made plenty of pots of Rancho Gordo or other dried beans in an hour or two without soaking first. I never soak my beans, not because it's a bad idea but because I just don't plan ahead enough and I don't let that stop me from making beans.

To soak or not soak largely depends on the age of your beans. If you are buying beans from a store or supplier that regularly moves their bean product and turns it over so that you’re getting beans within a year of harvest, you generally don’t need to soak them. If you’re cooking beans that have been languishing in your pantry or the grocery store shelf for a long time, a soak will help with hem. I find, however, that larger beans like Coronas always need a soak, even better if they are soaked in salted water (1 TBS Kosher salt per pound of Coronas in 8 cups water works for me).

Another way to test for bean doneness is to blow on one of them.  If the skin peels back,, they’re done.  And yes, test more than one.

I've been using Times recipes for years and they have been great. I've been running into problems like the with The Times recipes many times in the last few months. Disappointing.

@Problem with this recipe -

I’ve cooked many braising recipes that include garlic prepared this way. It ends up adding lots of delicious flavor. 

Keep the garlic bulb whole. Remove some of the outer papery husk, but leave enough to keep the bulb intact. Cut the garlic bulb in half horizontally so that all the cloves are cut in half. 

When the recipe is finished, there is yummy garlic flavor throughout the whole pot. Depending on your preference about how garlicky you like things, you can squeeze the softened garlic cloves into the pot, or onto crusty bread, or not.

I disagree with your premise, though: this site provides recipes and directions, but it relies on the idea that we already know how to cook. To keep directions on the shorter side, it doesn’t usually include every single detailed direction (although they sometimes provide great helpful videos). When I need more information, I look elsewhere online, or I read the comments.

This is a wonderful, easy meal, satisfying and not ordinary.  Really great for a winter evening.  Prepped and put in pot around 3:30PM, ready to eat by 6:30PM. I have been cooking beans for 45 years and during this time I’ve lived in four cities. The time it takes to soften beans when cooking depends greatly on the mineral content of the water used. My first house had well water and beans would *never* get soft. City water improved softening, now I have whole house water purification that removes most minerals and beans get soft very quickly with no soaking. You might try using different water if you are having trouble softening beans. BTW I love this recipe!!

I like the concept, but am trying to figure out how the arugula can work--short of a bagel.  I see that the recipe says "bite-sized pieces" (vs. photo).  So, in theory, this should work with the suggested dipping implements?

White this is stellar with great cottage cheese - Nancy’s or Good brands are my fave, it also is delicious with Trader Joe’s 4%, even TJ’s low fat or Daisy in a pinch. It’s a wetter texture with those but I gobble it down no fail. This has become my new favorite easy dip! With the sturdier cottage cheese I love it with cucumber slices, and the wetter stuff is great with crackers.

title: Pork Tenderloin With Chickpeas, Tomatoes and Oregano Vinaigrette Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: I made this recipe as directed, except I used 18 year balsamic rather than red wine vinegar as that’s what I had on hand. I also tossed some baby yellow potatoes on the sheet pan with everything else.  Easy, delicious weeknight dinner that we loved.

I had everything home to make half this recipe, which is perfect, since I live alone and deeply appreciate leftovers. It is so easy and incredibly delicious! I did use the pork tenderloin from Trader Joe’s, which is already seasoned with garlic and cracked pepper, so I only salted and peppered the tomatoes and garbanzo beans before roasting. I roasted everything for 20 minutes, and after 5 minutes of rest, the thickest part of the pork was 150 degrees. I think if I were serving this to guests, I’d sear the tenderloin before roasting, but especially for a weeknight easy dinner, this was great. My cherry tomatoes veered on the larger side, which meant a smaller tomato to pork ratio, but crunchier garbanzo beans, since there was less juice across the pan. I’m out of couscous, so I served it over tri-color quinoa, drizzled with TJ’s creamy vegan dill dressing (SO good), alongside a Greek salad. I can imagine setting down a platter of thinly sliced pork, tomatoes, and garbanzo beans with piles of pita bread and sauces, etc., and let my guests assemble sandwiches. This is a keeper!!!

Maybe the best effort to flavor recipe I’ve made in a long time. I will use the oregano vinaigrette in many other ways. I did give the pork a quick sear but I don’t think it impacted the flavor.  I might add more cherry tomatoes but other than that this is a perfect recipe.

I loved this the second day as leftovers. The first day the chickpeas had not had enough time in the vinaigrette to have much flavor. Next time I will marinate the chickpeas in the vinaigrette for awhile first. And there will be a next time as I really do like this dish!!
Easy one-pan recipe. The tenderloin did turn out a bit grey. Would recommend browning the tenderloin in a pan first

Very easy to make and tastes great.  I only had one tenderloin about 1.6 lbs, but I kept the rest of the ingredient amounts the same. I am looking forward to the leftovers and making this again. I did need to roast about 30 minutes but that can change with the thickness of the meat.

I just made this today using just one tenderloin but kept everything else the same.  At the same time, I had a separate sheet pan with russet steak fries and pole cut red onions that I first put in a bowl with garlic salt and a tablespoon of olive oil.  I put that in the oven first.  That part was done cooking about 5 minutes after the pork sheet pan. When it was all done, I mixed it all together and added the oregano vinaigrette to all of it.  I like having more veggies and it turned out great!
We aren’t crazy about chickpeas, anyone try substituting cannellini beans?

I used canned cannellini beans and also did the few minute broil at the end. Beans were fine. Creamy texture. I’ve made this before with the chickpeas and liked that too.

I sliced leftover pork as thin as possible and packaged it in the leftover tomato and bean mixture. With some feta, that leftover mixture made delicious pita sandwiches a day or two later!
Easy one-pan recipe. Pork turned out a little grey IMO. Would brown it in a pan first next time. Would also cook the chickpeas and tomato a bit first before adding pork if you like it slightly pink.

@Zoey just made this for dinner tonight as well. 100% agree With comments about grey meat, out it under broiler for a few minutes in each side-gave the meat and the tomatoes a nice char. Definitely going into rotation
*0* to 6 servings?

Fantastic, easy meal. I seared the pork first, to good results. I also put the sheet of tomatoes and garbanzos in the oven for about 10 minutes before the pork. Also good. The oregano chimichurri-like vinaigrette really makes this dish shine.

I really wanted to follow the recipe, but I the cupboard did not support me. Not enough tomatoes, and some of my garden ones were still a little green. That started the revision. I added a few halved caltrava olives, and a tablespoon of green capers. I had only fresh oregano, so minced that with double the garlic. Not sure if my oven ran hot, but the pork was cooked and the chickpeas too crunchy. Added and some leftover crushed tomatoes to pan and cooked while the pork rested. Yummy!
I married this recipe (sans vinaigrette)  with the sheet pan tomatoes, chickpeas, feta dish and it was fantastic!

This might be my all-time favourite NYT recipe. I have made it with Trader Joes' seasoned pork tenderloins when holidaying in California, and at home in Alberta with PC Greek seasoned pork loins. Cooking times change, but the essential recipe, the oegano vinegrette drizzled over the pork, chickpeas and roasted tomatoes, is amazing. Tonight I served it with Melissa Clark's Potatoes Anna, and her Broccoli Salad with Garlic and Sesame. Our friends loved it! Tomorrow's leftovers will be sublime.

I must have done something wrong because this didn't have much flavor.  And several reviews say that people are using already seasoned pork loin. Definitely need to brown the meat...either in a grill pan or under broiler.  And cooking time depends on the size of the pork loin...mine were large and took almost double the time in the oven.

Loved this. Made one small change. Rather than searing it in a pan on the stove as some folks suggested to give it nice color. I sprinkled the tops of both tenderloins with paprika to give it a nice color before roasting.

The tomatoes weren't bursting open so I switch to the broiler for 5 minutes. This was perfect for the tomatoes and added some color to the tenderloin and the vegetables. Oh, I also added an orange bell pepper to the chickpeas and tomatoes...very nice addition.

Really easy recipe to make as directed. I tossed the veggies with some spinach so it would wilt/cook down a little, resulting in a little extra balance and color. Would probably do as others suggest and brown the tenderloin before putting in the oven Will definitely make it again since it's so easy and hands off.

I followed this recipe exactly and I’ll be honest- I didn’t love this it was a bit plain for me BUT it was super easy and def came out pretty good. I added some fresh mozzarella after reheating it and that really really helped. I think I would make some tweaks to taste if I make it again.

Really enjoyed this.  Quick and easy.  Like some others, I used cannellini beans rather than garbanzos because I prefer them, and added halved small potatoes to the beans and tomatoes.  Next time I’ll cook tomatoes and beans 
10 minutes before adding the pork so the tenderloin doesn’t overcook.
This was a great easy meal to use up the last of my summer cherry tomatoes and some cooked chickpeas I had in the freezer. The chimichurri-like sauce was great, too. Definitely a keeper.

I added some mustard and a tiny bit of honey to the glaze. Perfect! I could see a squeeze of lemon at the end being lovely too. Served with mashed potatoes and a simple aria salad. Easy and delicious!
Like another commenter, I am not a fan of chickpeas. Any suggested alternatives? I would love to add something green.

Nice dish—brown pork on grill first
Then brown under broiler at end
Had to cook 20-25 mins
Served over couscous—everyone enjoyed this 11/25

No chickpeas or white beans in the house, what’s a chef to do? Combined this recipe with Kia Damien’s Roasted Shrimp, Okra and Tomatoe. No shrimp but did sear tenderloin then roasted it with 50% more tomatoes, a cup of cubed ube,  1/2 the oregano in the vinaigrette, okra tossed with garlic and olive oil, za’atar, at 400 degrees for 25 minutes. Colorful and tasty!
Very good! Next time i'll probably season the pork more, and double the vinaigrette. very easy and tasty!

This a winner, delicious, healthy, fast, minimal
mess, affordable. I would use a little more tomatos next time!
The pork was just a little meh. The dressing was great, but we were really missing the feta from the famous tomato feta chickpea sheet pan recipe...

Tasty recipe and easily executed.  I used can of drained chopped tomatoes because it’s winter here and balsamic vinegar as that’s what I had.  Added some spinach to make a full meal. Last I also did a quick pass under the grill to brown the top.
Adding 4-5 tablespoons of Kalamata olives levels this dish right up. So good, very easy.
Trader Joe sells chickpeas in olive oil with cumin.  I used those.  I halved the recipe.  We are the entire thing.  Too good.

This was super easy, and I had everything on hand. *Personal preference* — I don’t mind oregano, but it’s not my favorite by flavor. If I make this again I’ll 1) brown the loins beforehand (if you use a roasting pan it goes from stovetop to oven just fine) and 2) reduce the amount of oregano (the dried oregano reconstituted in the vinegar just fine, but I wasn’t a fan of the resulting texture — it’s a lot of little oregano leaves) and add a little more garlic and maybe some thyme and or dill to the vinaigrette.

title: Sheet-Pan Feta With Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody:
soooo good! very adaptable recipe. 

used canned corn, tomato, and bell pepper instead of shishito, because that's what i had. also tossed some chili powder with the veggies. ended up having to bake at a lower temp for longer because things were getting burnt. 

served over brown rice with barbecued pork and avocado. the quick pickled onions & cilantro are a must! definitely adding this to the regular dinner rotation.
@alyssa what temp?
@G. I used the Sheet Pan Feta with Tomatoes and Broccoli method— lower third of the oven at 400 (maybe on convection setting, can’t remember!). And then a watchful broil. Worked out well.
Extremely easy weeknight dish.
This was a lovely weeknight dinner. The ingredients that were used are Easy to require and  the end product was savoury and delicious.
Easy to prepare and absolutely delicious; followed recipe exactly
This is so easy to come together at my family loved it. I love how quickly the broiler can transform a meal like this one.
I don't have a broiler but seem always find broiler recipes that sound yummy. Can this be made well in a hot oven, or should I just move on?
If you like more heat then add some red pepper flakes after it comes out or add some more chilies

It was just ok to me. I sautéed a seafood pack from Trader Joe’s at the end and added it to the sheet pan with the onion slices and additional lime juice. My advice if you’re adding additional items, make sure the temperature is consistent with the other components.
@Michelle H. came here to say .. i’ll be adding some green king prawns for a few minutes at the end
If you like spicy, add some pepper flakes or another pepper
My market doesn’t sell shishitos. Is there a sub?
Trader Joes
I used red bells cut up. I think any pepper you like would be great too
I made this recipe exactly as written, it was delicious!!! The pickled onion and jalapeño slices really brought everything together well. I will be making this again.

This recipe was devoured in our house.  The whole dish took 20 minutes from start to finish.  I served it on a bed of fresh spinach and added sliced tomatoes.  My family would have licked the bowl if no one was watching.  The cumin was freshly ground in the mortar and pestle which made a big impact.

This is a great way to use up veggies! Used canned white corn, cherry tomatoes sliced in 1/2, shishito peppers, & left over, uncooked snow peas. Used blue cheese crumbles (I don’t like feta).  Broiled veggies for 5 min, then put the crumbles over the top for 1 minute.  Turned out well. Had over leftover brown rice.  Snow peas were excellent for this.

Used yellow onion, instead of red (didn’t have red) & it was just meh.  I can see how they would add a lot.

Please tell us how thin slices onions should be, by a measure, in recipes. Mine were probably too thick.  I would appreciate 1/4” or 1/8” so I can use my mandolin   I think the size of the slices is really important to me, maximize the flavor balance.

Quick, easy, and flavorful. Could not find shishito peppers so subbed a mix of jalapeño and serrano. They mellowed out under the broiler and gave a nice flavor without being too spicy. Used a block of regular feta since I couldn’t find goat, and it worked fine. Otherwise I followed the recipe and it was delicious. Would serve with rice next time to soak up the yummy pan juices.
I used half the amount of feta and added a can of navy beans. It was really good! The beans toasted nicely with the corn. I’d add a bit more cumin and lime next time though.

Really delicious and easy to make.  Followed the recipe entirely except I used some jarred, pickled red onion that I had on hand already rather than slicing up an onion and adding lime juice.  I will make this again!

This looks really good.  I enjoy all the ingredients.  But - it's October!  (Actually, I'm reading it in November). Why now? Corn is gone; shishito peppers are gone; fresh basil is hard to come by....this is a late August recipe - I will calendar it for 8/26.
I used frozen sweet corn from Trader Joe's and they also have shishito peppers.  I used cilantro instead of basil and it was fantastic!
just got shishito peppers and goat feta at my local farmers market in atlanta. i have corn in the freezer - gonna make it tonight with swordfish.
Fantastic!  Don't skip the onions and the lime juice.  We will make this again.  Super easy!
fast and easy. I also made some substitutions and additions and baked. Flexible for what you have . Although I wouldn't recommend  anything canned.
Trader Joe’s shishitos didn’t cooked enough in my broiler. Trying to sauté them first this time. And may try to roast rather than broil
This was super easy and had good flavor but left my wife and I a bit underwhelmed as the main show for dinner.  I'd make it again as a side dish.
Fantastic. Prepared as directed (except used feta crumbles instead of a block) and served with a platter of pita chips for “nachos” at a potluck. Big hit
I added some sliced fennel and cherry tomatoes and they worked well. Will try zucchini and a few red peppers next time around. Overall, a great, easy, flexible recipe.

really tasty! no shishitos today at the store so subbed in some sweet peppers and it was great. served with a yogurt sauce and pita. so easy, will definitely be throwing this together again in the future for sure!

So good!  Great side dish for roasted salmon (with lemon bars for dessert).  Also makes a great taco filing.  I imagine it would be wonderful as a topping for avocado toast. Don't skip the picked onion.
Watch closely so you don't incinerate the shishito peppers.

title: Sesame Salmon Noodle Bowls With Ponzu Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody: This was so yummy! I did lettuce, sugar snap peas and radishes as the veggie base. Udon noddles and salmon with sesame oil and everything bagel seasoning. Who knew you could buy ponzu sauce at the store. What a delight.
Quick ponzu is easy to make from equal parts soy sauce and citrus plus a bit of mirin.
My husband made this dish, it was fantastic. It took just 30 minutes which totally impressed him

Does anyone have recommendations for the best store bought ponzu sauce? I haven't met one yet that I liked, relative to homemade versions? If anyone has a from scratch recipe I'd love to see that too. Thank you foodie community!
@LD any local Asian supermarket should have it! Trader Joe’s does too.
@LD. Very good question. I have used “Kikkoman® Ponzu Citrus Seasoned Dressing and Sauce” and it was GREAT!”
@Millette I believe homemade ponzu is just a few ingredients. Soy, lime and something else?
I used pork belly because I had it on hand. I made a dressing of ponzu and sesame oil. I rinsed the udon and tossed it with sesame oil to prevent sticking and let them cool.
@Sophie If you go to the NYT cooking app and search "fish" you will find more recipes for fish that isn't salmon than you will ever have time to make.

I lightly tossed the cooked noodles in sesame oil. I used leftover salmon, cucs, scallions, sprinkled with sesame seeds and furikake. Drizzled Trader Joe’s Soyaki sauce lightly over the top. Was very good!
Was there an oven temperature noted for the salmon? I read the recipe through several times and only saw a cooking time. Perhaps I’m missing it in an obvious place?
@Maureen O. Heat oven to 350 degrees. Step 1, second sentence. ;)
Delicious and easy- even my kids raved about it.
It was delicious. I made twice in two weeks, easy, light and got raves from friends and famiky. For gluten free, use rice noodles or rice, used coconut aminos and lemon to replace ponzu.
Really nice topped with arugula, cukes, and thin radish slices. I tossed the soba in the ponzu to loosen it up a little rather then pour over.
Anyone have suggestions for gluten-free Ponzu?
@LD: For store bought I like Marukan Yuzu Ponzu, if you want to make your own Mark Matsumoto has a recipe at norecipes.com/ponzu-sauce/

Good Recipe to Taste.

Thanks!

WOW. Outstanding recipe— and visually beautiful too. I made as directed, using soba noodles. For toppings I used thinly sliced cucumber, radish, Serrano pepper, and scallions. A mandoline makes light work of this. Each of us dressed our bowls with Kikkoman ponzu, a squeeze of lemon, and more sesame oil. The whole process took 30-40 minutes and dinner was on the table. Will make again and again!

This recipe was really good and was incredibly easy and quick to prepare. I didn’t have all of the ingredients so I subbed a few things by default. I found a quick ponzu recipe online and it was surprisingly good. It required boiling so I made it first so it could cool down (I may try this dish warm with the homemade warm ponzu). I roasted broccoli and cauliflower quickly at high heat to keep the crunch. I did add the peppers, like the tip suggested. I watched the video attached to the recipe and noticed she used cherry tomatoes so I threw some in too. It turned out amazing. This recipe is incredibly versatile. I’m definitely adding this to my rotation, and am going to grab the recipe ingredients and try it as written.
Pretty basic but healthy and flavorful. Made my own ponzu which kept the salt content down. Added cilantro and avocados to green onions, radishes and cucumbers. Served on a bed of spring greens.
I used extra firm tofu for the salmon and cooked it in the air fryer. I think salmon would be great too, but the tofu was fantastic. I used an avocado, carrots, and yellow bell pepper.

Quick, easy, delicious. For veggies I used radishes, cucumbers, shredded cabbage and cilantro, along with some shelled edamame beans I cooked with the noodles. The recipe calls for 2 tablespoons of ponzu per portion but this would have been too salty for us; I’d suggest starting with 1 tbsp per portion and letting people add more to taste. The adults also added some chili crisp to their bowls!

Found the salmon and noodle salad a little bland. 

Punched up the flavor with a splash of soy sauce, lemon juice, and red pepper flakes.

This is such a good  quick recipe creation. Thank you. I will add soft vegetables to the last few minutes to the noodle cooking. 
At the Asian markets. there are so many different add ins for noodle toppers as well.  The possibilities are endless. (crushed/crumbled NORI sheets to sprinkle on-top and slices of yellow radish.

Has anyone tried this with a fish other than salmon? Just curious if so, what type of fish and how did it turn out? I'm not a salmon fan at all, so when I see recipes that sound good minus the salmon, I'm always curious about the answers to the above questions. TYIA
I simply don't have room in the pantry for every sauce that NYT cooking wants me to use. Is there a decent way to approximate the taste of ponzu without buying yet another sauce?
Added a drizzle of toasted sesame oil when I added the ponzu and also included avocado slices along with sugar snap peas and Persian cucumbers.  Great luncheon and could also work for dinner!
Loved this, and it was truly done in 30 mins. I don’t always think of pairing salad greens with noodles but it made it super fresh tasting. The two six year olds at our house give it two thumbs up.

Good effort to reward ratio! I was craving something veg forward and sushi adjacent and it hit the spot. Made with carrots, snow peas, radishes, edamame and rice noodles. The ponzu makes it (I got the kikoman brand). I wasn’t a huge fan of the salmon preparation though - next time I will do my usual marinade of maple syrup and soy sauce and maybe add a little miso

This was delicious and very quick and simple to make. I felt very healthy after eating it,too! Quick comment about cooking with buckwheat soba: look for a brand that is made with wheat flour, as well as with buckwheat. The wheat flour keeps it from becoming too brittle after it is cooked.

Not a fan. The ponzu salad and soba noodles were great but my expensive organic salmon had zero flavor even with aggressive seasoning. Just the sesame oil, salt, and everything bagel seasoning was not enough to give the fish flavor.

My store bought ponzu was a bit too bland / sour, so I combined it with green onion, sesame oil, dark soy sauce, and a little Maggi seasoning. Also ended up adding furikake and crispy shallots. 5 stars — so addicting

As someone who likes to meal prep a week worth of lunches, over the years I've come to hate burrito bowls. For some reason I decided to give this one a try and was so pleasantly surprised. It's delicious! I used frozen corn. Also happened to have some leftover avocado lime cream from the crispy tofu taco recipe and it's the perfect topping, and it doesn't brown: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1026900-crispy-tofu-tacos

I wanted to make this but didn’t want to go to the store, so I made a bunch of substitutions (sorry, I know this is annoying) and it still came out very yummy, so I think this is probably a very robust recipe. Also super easy and quick! I subbed a minced red Thai chili for the jalapeño, a diced roma tomato for one of the cobs of corn (only had two cobs on hand), half a lemon for the lime, and omitted the avocado. It was still great. I look forward to trying the proper recipe next time. The cheesy bean rice itself is very tasty, so am also planning to experiment with other toppings after corn season ends

I can attest to wonderfulness of the rice and beans part of the recipe as it’s essentially Ali’s one-pot recipe with toppings. On regular rotation in our household with three kids, just prep some bowls with toppings as you see fit and let everyone go to town!

I am absolutely thrilled that I stumbled upon this recipe! It was not only straightforward to follow but also budget-friendly, which is always a bonus. The flavors were sensational and truly exceeded my expectations. My husband decided to prepare the dish exactly as the recipe suggested, and he paired it with some mouth-watering mojo-grilled chicken breast. The combination was delightful, creating a deliciously balanced meal that we both enjoyed immensely. I will definitely be making this again in the future!

I made the whole recipe. —live alone. I started eating it for dinner last night. Binged Paramount’s Ray Donovan til 2:30 am., had another serving. Just had it for lunch. Had to drag myself away from the leftover bowl— resisting a 2nd helping. I garnished it with sour cream and ketcap manjis. 
10 stars. Not 5. Thank you Ali.
How much liquid in addition to the bean liquid would be needed if I am using brown rice?
@TS I made this last night using brown rice and I used the same amount of water recommended in the recipe for the white.

@TS Somehow misread the recipe and pre cooked the 1 c of rice, then added it to the beans with some of the can liquid.  came out fantastic. If I were to make it with brown rice, I would do it the same way to avoid the rice to water question.
@TS I tried this with short grain brown rice and it took a lot more water and time… next time I will cook the rice before!

Just made this today but changed a few things:
Added 1 more jalapeño using most of the seeds from both; used 1 can of corn b/c that's all I had; cooked 1 lb. ground beef (85% lean) with hot taco seasoning. Put a dollop of commercial salsa on top with a few shakes of Cholula Chipotle hot sauce and an extra sprinkle of grated cheese. My friend and I just downed 2 helpings. So satisfying and all the textures! With avocado on top? Just the right touch! and I used 1 generous tsp of cumin. This is 🙌
Convert to cook with brown rice?  What is the new timing? Change in liquid amounts?
@JE I used brown basmati rice (less clumpy), cooked it in my rice cooker and mixed it with the rest later.
i just put the rice and rinsed beans in the rice cooker. makes everything even easier
@v I would have never thought of combining rice and beans in rice cooker! I’m going to do this just to have rice and beans in my fridge, will add sriracha or chili crisp in cooker too. Thank you

Liked this a lot, but when I (definitely!) make it again, I want to try cooking the rice and beans separately— I wasn’t crazy about the mushy texture. What an awesome dish though! Vegetarian and carnivore alike were very happy 😊
@carol I agree. Next time I will cook the to rice in my rice cooker and then combine with the beans

This is a really fun recipe and delicious. But, since I love chicken burritos, I added a cup and a half of cubed store-bought rotisserie chicken on top of the rice and bean mixture to bring it up to warm temperature and then put the cheese on top of that. Then I served it as directed. Oh yeah!

I highly suggest two alterations to the recipe:

1) Reserve an extra 1/4 cup of water while the rice is cooking. Stir the rice about every 3 minutes while it’s cooking, and if it starts to stick in the last 5 or so minutes add the water. I’ve tried this with a few different brands and varieties of beans and some have needed this extra liquid and some have not.

2) Lightly pickle the raw red onion at the start of your cook by adding the lime juice and a teaspoon of sugar to your 1/2 chopped onion. This gets rid of some of the less pleasant raw onion taste while preserving the freshness
I'm a beginner so apologies in advance for the question. Is the rice cooked or uncooked?
@Anne C uncooked!
No need to apologize, Anne. The rice is uncooked when it is added in Step 1. Enjoy your discovery of cooking. It gets easier and more fun with time.

You'll soon learn that if a recipe calls for cooked rice, the ingredient list will specify "cooked rice." Also, Step 1 instructs to "cook undisturbed until the rice is tender, 18 to 20 minutes." You'll also learn that cooked rice doesn't need 20 more minutes to become tender.

I have made many mid burrito bowls in my day, so I can confirm that this is a step above the rest! The bean and cheese rice is delicious and definitely differentiates it. You can easily add some chicken for meat eaters. I added some sliced radish and crunched up some tortilla chips over the top as well. Don’t be afraid to use the whole jalapeño. It’s not too spicy.
So easy, and delicious too. Shouldn’t have used frozen corn tho, those kernels were soft and bland. Everything else was perfection

The photo shows radishes but they are not listed in the ingredients list, which makes me wonder what else might have been left out. Also, if you add meat, I’m assuming it needs to be pre-cooked, but at what stage should it be added?
@Beverly Radish listed as suggested topping in preamble to recipe

@Beverly
No Beverly, just use raw meat…..
This was amazing!! I could have eaten the whole thing. I can’t believe there aren’t more comments on the greatness of this recipe!

I made this last night for my two-person household and got outstanding reviews. I used black beans and added radishes and rotisserie chicken. I agree that the radishes should not be hidden in the description but would be easier for those of us non-detail-oriented readers by adding them to the ingredient list. A surprise bonus was finding two complete recipes within this one for 1) a delicious corn salsa and for 2) a cheesy rice and bean component that I'm going to be sure to use again!
This is one of those where it's hard to account for how good it is based on how simple the ingredients and prep are. I'll be coming back to this all the time for work lunches.
I would definitely make this again. It was great as is, but I added some grilled TexMex chicken to stretch it out a bit. A little cilantro lime crème gave it a punch of flavour.
How can I make this dish with less calories?

@Carole Bienstock less cheese, for sure.
I add diced zucchini and carrots (cook w/onion) too
Really good! Added veggie chick’n tossed in cumin and served with tortilla chips à la everyone’s favorite burrito bowl chain.

I always have trouble making rice dishes on the stovetop. Not enough water, not long enough boiling, I’m not quite sure but I usually end up with at least 1/4 of the rice crunchy. Sometimes I panic and add more water but I usually do follow the recipe. Any tips would be appreciated.

@cookin Perhaps your pan is too thin or you haven’t stirred enough? 

My method for stovetop rice: Mix 1 cup Jasmine rice and 1.5 cups water (ratio is per package instructions) plus 1 teaspoon salt and 1 Tablespoon butter in a small nonstick pot with a lid. Turn burner to high, pot uncovered, stir a few times, scraping bottom to prevent sticking. When the liquid reaches a full boil, stir once more from the bottom, turn the heat to very low, and cover it. Set the timer for 15 minutes. When the timer goes off, turn off the heat, but leave the pot on the burner for another 10 minutes.
@cookin I have used a rice cooker for years now. Perfect rice every time.

I always cook my rice separately and then mix it in. I use brown rice and it never cooks in the time allotted, and then everything either gets mushy or some of it gets mushy and the rice is still crunchy. I've also started cooking rice like pasta so I don't have to think about water to rice ratios, like this https://www.thekitchn.com/pasta-method-rice-23719020 Brown rice takes longer than white rice and I've never had an issue with over- or under-cooked rice.

The beans and rice were super easy and delicious. I doubled the recipe and served with skirt steak. I made mango salsa because I did not have any corn. Also had guacamole, sour cream and pickled red onions as toppings. Delicious and quick dinner.
My rice didn’t come out right in the dutch oven. I used the suggested amount of liquid and kept cooking past the suggested time, even added more liquid but bits of it didn’t cook through all the way.

All rice is different (age makes a difference as well) and you can't expect cooking time to be the same. The instructions (Step 1) say to "cook the rice until tender." Sounds like you didn't do that. "Suggested time" can't take into account the exact rice you're using.

Made this last night as I wanted something that would last over to lunch the next day.  Made a few adjustments - drained black beans before adding to rice, sautéed the corn with some left over chicken, added cut up cherry tomatoes, diced the avocado, added slices of watermelon radish and a dollop of sour cream.  Was delicious.  Just a tip - test your jalapeño before adding to the rice, mine was extra spicy and way too much according to the ingredient list - good thing we had a cold beer.

We loved this! I made extra corn & used it on some lentils with cumin as a nice lunch. (Cherry tomatoes, Greek yogurt (for sour cream) & extra cilantro. Yum! I doubled the beans but will have to increase rice/beans even more to feed my teens

My kids and I loved this. I had no lime so used red wine vinegar and only had one can of corn. I also had pickled jalapeño on hand so used that in the rice and the corn salsa. I couldn’t stop eating it. We ate it with lettuce, tomato, black olives and tortilla chips.

Very tasty and easy recipe. I recommend adding a chopped tomato to the mix with the corn, onions and cilantro. I also used pepper jack cheese instead of Monterrey jack cheese. I notice the photo includes slices of radishes, which I will add next time I make this dish.
Notice, too, the last line of the head note: "Feel free to add tomatoes, pico de gallo or radishes to the bowls as well."
I am thinking you could make this in a rice cooker.

I thought this was good, especially for a pantry meal.  I doubled the recipe, which may have been part of my downfall when it came to cooking the rice.  Still soupy/mushy after 40 minutes.  We ate it anyways.  Ended up using both brown and white rice, Trader Joe's roasted frozen corn and pepper jack cheese.

Me too! My rice was so soupy and now that most of the water has mostly boiled off, the rice is super mushy. :( I was really looking forward to this one so I guess I’ll just add less water next time! Or just the bean liquid without any additional water.

I can't get enough of this rice. I subbed a poblano pepper for the jalapeno because that's what I had in the fridge, and I used the entire red onion in the rice (skipped the corn salsa topping because I only needed the rice/beans as a base for a different recipe). YUM. Super easy, super delicious. Also so good with a fried egg on top of leftovers for breakfast!
Should fresh corn be cooked or uncooked?
@Richard - uncooked

@Richard it took me some time to find this information too - found it in the description: „If your fresh corn is sweet, use its kernels raw, or cook it by microwaving the cobs in their husks for three minutes before shucking.“
Delicious and quick meal!

Really tasty base for a burrito bowl. I added some taco seasoned ground turkey on top and tomatoes to the salsa for a little extra filler. The turkey and the tomatoes were great additions. If you don't love raw red onions or the spiciness of jalapenos, you can chop them all up and saute them for the rice together. Overall, quite delicious and good for meal prep!
20/10! Everyone in my family loved it, which is a miracle. Made exactly as written and this is going I to my regular rotation.

Why publish a photo that includes ingredients not listed? I use the grocery list feature and purchase what I need trusting all the ingredients are listed. This recipe, as published, makes no mention of tomatoes or radishes that I can find. I would’ve bought them had I noticed earlier or if the grocery list were correct. The dish, as is, lacks color. I understand why it was doctored for the photo.
Tasty recipe! Made as written. It's the first recipe I've cooked that my grand-daughter asked for seconds. We adults liked it too- definitely a keeper.

I used black beans and canned corn.  I 😘 ved this dinner.  Next time I will heat the corn mix prior to serving.  Using the cold corn mixture made the burrito mix like warm.  This was a healthy and satisfying meal.
.
I made this with quinoa instead of rice and used Daiya Mexican Blend vegan cheese and it was delicious.

I missed seeing the pico de gallo and radishes at the bottom of the meal description as well.  Shopping for meals for the week, I go down ingredient lists, making sure I have everything needed for each recipe, so it would be helpful to have optional toppings listed there.  We have leftovers and I’ll pick them up as I think they’ll be the “cherry on the top” here.  Will also add more jalapeno unless I can find a good hot pico de gallo.  The meal is quick and easy…only wish I’d had all the ingredients first time around—nice crunch and tang from the radishes, and pico de gallo would give the zip it needed.

Really tasty meal prep option for weekday lunches! The only modifications I made were adding sliced radish (like shown in photo, but not written) and mixing some red jarred salsa into the rice/bean/cheese mix. Elevated the flavor with a brightness the corn salsa alone wasn't adding. Frozen corn worked perfectly in the salsa.
I make burritos for the freezer on a regular basis. I love burrito bowls, too. You won’t miss out if you forget the cheese.
As a fan of preparing food in bulk and freezing some….do you think I could double or triple the amounts in step one, and then freeze the leftovers?
I used brown rice and added the 1 3/4 cups water (1 bean filled can) along with the liquid from beans. It was a little soupy so think I will cut back next time.
Where'd those radishes come from?
Last line of the head note: "Feel free to add tomatoes, pico de gallo or radishes to the bowls as well."
Simple and very tasty! My picky toddler loved the cheesy beans and rice. Hopefully she’ll be curious enough to try the corn salsa (or even some plain corn) next time.
I loved this but would ease up on the onions. Too much for me. Otherwise it’s great. I added tortilla chips to the bowl.

Just made this! It was pretty good. The rice and beans were a little bland--definitely needed more salt. I blackened some chicken thighs to go with it, and also added some sour cream and homemade pickled onions to the top! Cheers!
Step 2 instructs: "Season to taste with salt. "

I made this this week, using different cheese. It was blah. I blame it on one of the few Trader Joe's products that was a fail for me, organic no salt pinto beans. Weird taste spoiled the whole pot. I'll have to doctor up the leftovers to make it palatable. The corn salsa turned out great, using frozen corn.
Family of bean hunts so added tomatoes from the garden and roasted chicken.  Tasty
Just made for dinner, delicious! The radishes were a great add on.
Love adding the whole can of beans, without rinsing. Adults and kids both loved this.

I am still stuck on uncle Ben's rice because everything else seems too sticky to me ( basmati the best sub but has a distinctive taste). What brands/ kinds cook up fluffy and separate, which is what want?
This is a great simple recipe.  My family loved it and asked that I make it again.
Looks very good! Seems to be short quite a bit of protein. Can you detail the protein nutrition facts?
See the Nutritional Information below the ingredient list. If it's "short quite a bit of protein" for you, you can always add more protein to the bowl, or to a separate dish.
The ingredients don't add up to the protein in the nutrition facts from what I see. Wondering if I am missing something.
The easiest and best brown rice is Alton Brown’s Baked Brown Rice https://altonbrown.com/recipes/easy-baked-brown-rice/ It’s foolproof, easy-peasy and you can freeze serving sizes.

This was yummy! I’m not a friend of spice but I still used the jalapeño, no regrets. Adds an essential complexity to the flavour. The canned beans I usually get are 18 oz not 15, so I added a bit more rice and less water than a full canful. Came out quite good, but certainly has a slop-bowl quality to it.

Fantastic recipe as is, but I luckily had some roasted pepper strips leftover from a previous meal, and added them on top. Also, I baked some large flour tortillas formed into bowls and made them the “bowl“. Gobbled up every last bite!
We loved this! I followed the directions except for using brown jasmine rice. I will make it again!

Simple and flavorful, a quick weeknight win. Only hiccup was that the rice needed an extra 10 minutes or so to cook, but otherwise a breeze. Sautéed seasoned chicken in the pan before proceeding with the rest of the recipe which added even more flavor. Looking forward to scaling this up to make for a crowd.

I made this to serve when two friends came over and none of us can believe how tasty was. It was just a great combination of foods, it’s wonderful texture and flavor. I could eat this once a week! In place of the jalapeño, I used paprika and it was delicious.

I am slowly working to convince my meat-loving husband into eating more vegetarian meals. This one might have done the trick! We made it with pearled farro, as rice is too much carb for me, and it was delicious. My avocado wasn't ripe enough to use, but it was still excellent. We've tagged it a #favorite and will be making it again!
Easy and delish.  Add a bed of lettuce

A very versatile recipe that turns out tasty and is easy to make. I add salsa and Hubby adds cooked spiced taco meat. I've used frozen corn (one bag) or canned - tastes great either way! For those who don't like a strong chile flavor, (hubby likes it mild) use 1/2 the jalapeño called for in the recipe and remove the seeds, dividing it between the corn salsa and the bean/rice dish. I also used 1/2 a red onion in the same ratio. . .
Can you use vegan cheese?
I just made the rice and beans portion of this (Step 1) and it's so delicious and easy. I prefer more beans to rice though. If I add a 2nd can of beans, would I need to adjust the water at all?
@Anna I also added a second can of beans to increase the bean:rice ratio. I just drained the second can and kept everything else the same. Worked great!

I added diced red pepper to the salsa for color, a half pound of pacific cod for the added lean protein and cut the slat by 2/3.

Simple, fast and tasty.  A new staple in our week night dinner rotation.
@bb Radishes, salsa etc al are optional add -ons mentioned in description

Fast. Delicious. Cheap. 5 stars. This one's a keeper.
 Thanks, NY Times!

This is muy bueno. Subbed brown rice and used the same amount of water, and added a few more minutes to the cook time to cook the rice through. The corn salsa is what makes this dish sing - I used frozen corn. Topped it with sour cream, green Cholula, a touch of Trader Joe's taqueria salsa - eat your heart out, Chipotle.
Threw some rotisserie chicken in...husband not a huge fan of rice but this one is a winner. Great for lunch leftovers.
Healthy delicious meal. I used 1 can of corn and 2 cans of beans. 1 black and 1 light red kidney bean. Great for meal prep!
easy to make, healthy and tasty

I didn't expect this to be as good as it was!  My husband recommended we use the rice/beans as our standard side for Mexican meals instead of rice and refritos.  I made the salsa with canned, fire-roasted corn. It needed help. I added Mexican oregano and a little granulated garlic and let it sit, stirring occasionally, for a good 30 minutes. That made a huge difference!
Easy and yummy, grounding and filling-- great for a healthy (and satisfying) weekday meal, with leftovers for lunch.  Thumbs up!
Delicious, just as recipe is written!
I added a can of beans - one pinto and one black and I’m glad I did. I was going to skip the corn salsa and just use some store bought pico but I’m glad I didn’t! Easy, economical, and delicious.

Normally I make NYT recipes as written, but this time I used frozen brown rice instead of the uncooked white rice. I made the rice separately, per package instructions, for about eight minutes, when it was al dente.
