When I added it to the beans, of course I didn't add the can of water, just a couple of tablespoons during the eight minutes I heated the beans and rice at medium for a bit, then at low.  I added one-half teaspoon of kosher salt to the corn salad but not any to the rice and beans.
This was fantastic and came together quickly.  I'd use two jalapeños next time.  Microwaving the corn in the husk was great and made it so easy to shuck.
This is an excellent recipe. I used black beans for the rice. Will be adding to my rotation!

Delicious!  Summer, fresh, bicolored-sweet corn made the dish.  Used a whole jalapeno but could have used 2.  Next time.  Garnished with lots of cilantro, radishes, cherry tomatoes, black olives and extra cheese. Looking forward to the leftovers ;-)

Reading the reviews, this sounded great. Maybe I had the world’s least spicy jalapeno pepper, but mine was bland, bland, bland. After adding a chopped tomato and a lot of salsa, it was pretty good, though not enough to make again.
An update: my daughter loved it & wants me to make it again. Go figure
This was so easy and delicious! Will definitely make again.

Delicious! Added some grilled chicken and peppers for extra protein and veg. All three kids devoured it, including my four year old who said it’s the best thing she ever ate and requested that we have it for dinner every day. Very high praise!

So so tasty! Luckily I doubled for my family. Added a shallot and lots more sliced garlic. Cooked the basmati rice in veggie broth instead of water so did not need to add salt. Did not use broccoli stems, just added lots of florets at the end. Sharp cheddar was perfect, broiled until slightly browned in spots. Huge hit with everyone, will definitely make again!

Reminded me of a less fussy, more cheesy Enchanted Broccoli Forest from Moosewood.

I loved this..used Jasmine long-grain white rice, .added some left-over cubed , cooked sweet potato..nice touch..and accented with Fly By Jing Sichuan Chil Crisp...brought it all together with flavor bursts..I wanted easy and delicious...got it.

@Fancy Nancy , excellent hacks! Thanks for sharing. My wife and I are chili crisp junkies. We like chili crisp in chili crisp. So when I make this I intend to make it with your suggestions. I’m guessing the marriage of the sweet potatoes (sugar) with the chilies (and spice) brings home the flavors in a real big way, yeah? Can’t wait to make it.

@Glenn A.
Try Mama Teav’s Hot Garlic if you haven’t already…it’s my absolute fave chili crisp! :-)

I think I need to add some little chunks of Velveeta into the rice. 

What can I say? I grew up Southern and the only cheese I knew was Velveeta. There's not many casserole-ish dishes that aren't improved with some melty, creamy Velveeta.
That broccoli will not be bright green after 8 to 10 minutes of cooking unless you consider olive green "bright".
@P mine was very nicely green and I added the extra 2 minutes.
@Judy Forkner Like yours, mine too was still bright green at 10 minutes
Can I substitute cauliflower for the broccoli?
No, that's not allowed.
Not under any circumstances, that would never work.
@JL I'm sure you can, it just won't be as colorful.

Deeply browned the onion and used chicken stock instead of water. Incredibly delicious! My 20 month old devoured it. It would be great with thyme, I think, or most any herb. Personally I felt that 4 oz cheddar was the perfect amount, and I love cheese.

This is just what the doctor ordered after a long road trip vaca, a pantry recipe. Going to make this for dinner tonight but using froz broccoli & roasted instead. Might use brown rice with chicken broth, fresh herbs too. Thanks for this easy & comforting recipe, Ali!
It's a little bland. I think the commenters who suggest swapping chicken stock for water and using more garlic and cheese are on the right track.

I made this tonight and added cannellini beans and used a Mexican cheddar we get here in Ireland that I use on Nachos. Brought a little heat and spice to the party. The next time I will put some chopped mushrooms in with the onion and garlic. I think that would add some bottom notes to the dish.

Great easy comfort food dinner for an autumn night!  I used local farm broccoli florets, basmati rice, and Speck Alto Adige IGP on top of the broccoli before Dubliner cheese.  The Speck was a delicious addition!

@srlopez
Thanks for your comment. I don’t have easy access to speck but you inspired me to add diced ham steak that I browned a bit before adding to recipe.
Any adjustments suggested for basmati or other types of rice?
@jtk I used basmati and didn’t need to make any changes to cooking time.
Great dish but wanted more substance so added a can of white beans when adding florets. Also threw in some feta with the cheddar instead of adding more salt. Delish!
Probably requires a full 8oz block of cheese to get the look shown in the picture. I feel like the version we made with the 4oz it called for looks like the diet version.

So…recipe doesn’t say to remove broccoli before adding rice and water but indicates to add the florets on top of rice after cooking rice for 10 minutes have a bad feeling the broccoli is going to be mushy after all the specified cooking time. Any insight from those who have made this dish?

It looks like you just cook the stems (not florets) w/ the onions etc., and yes, you leave them in w/ the rice. They'll get mushy, but it will be like broccoli-cheese soup. Florets stay out until the end. I think I'm going to make this tomorrow and will report back.

I haven't made this yet, but, the broccoli stems and florets are divided. 

Only the chopped broccoli stems go in with the onion and garlic. The more delicate florets are added after the initial 10 minutes of rice cooking.
The recipe says to cook the stems separately, adding the florets after cooking rice for 10 minutes. Hope this helps!

Wonderful riffable recipe.  My own riff was to use orzo instead of rice (using Ali Slagle's shrimp scampi with orzo method), toss the orzo mix when cooked with about 1 TBSP of chili crisp, before adding pre-cooked broccoli and cheddar topping.  The result was nicely saucy, which I think would not be the case with rice.  A terrific alternative to the usual broccoli dishes.  Goes with anything.
Easy, simple. Delicious. To make a full meal, I added canned salmon and corn then topped with pickled jalapenos under the cheese. Would have served with corn chips if I had any.
I’d like to make this with brown rice.  Does anybody have any recommendations on how long to cook brown rice before adding the broccoli?

@Sara Lynn brown rice typically cooks for 45 minutes or so.  Subtracting the 10 minutes of cooking after adding the broccoli florets means you should cook the brown rice for 35 minutes before proceeding as described. Hope this helps.

This recipe has become our go-to comfort food. I just feel better knowing I have the ingredients on hand to whip this up at a moment's notice. Other than being the best thing in my fridge, it also works great as a heat-and-eat drop-off for friends who are sick or struggling with a loss. I label it "Magical Rice & Broccoli: Heat, eat, heal."

It’s a very mild, gentle dish, and there’s a time and place for that. I used weak chicken stock instead of water, and decided at the last minute to stir in chunks of chicken and mozzarella, cheddar and Parmesan. Very satisfying.

This is a simple and low-budget recipe made with items likely on hand. It's mild, as you can tell from the list of ingredients, and sure to be kid-friendly (my teenager was not impressed.) I like the suggestion in the intro about serving with a rotisserie chicken or something. I'll keep this in mind for future weeknights when I have no idea what to cook.

I know I know. It’s not quite the recipe but- cut up 1 and 1/2 onions and threw it in the bottom of a class casserole pan with a bag of frozen broccoli and some cut up carrots which needed to be used. Rinsed brown rice, brown rice, and quinoa, and added some red lentils and stirred it around. I added a bunch of zaatar, salt and pepper and vegetable stock and water. I covered it and cooked it in the oven for a couple of hours. Dinner for a week!
love this, Lucy.  thanks for sharing.

This is comfort food! I added a can of tuna as the intro suggested and it was a one pot meal. I think this is a recipe where using enough salt is really going to make a difference between bland and satisfying Next time I might add a splash of white wine or lemon juice towards the end of Step 1. I sprinkled a pinch of salt on the chopped broccoli right after chopping and let it sit until I added it. 

I wish the dimensions of the skillet used while developing the recipe were included rather than just 'large'. The diameter and depth are going to affect cooking time and the surface area that the cheese will cover. I used a shallow cast iron Dutch oven (11.5"/29 cm diameter, approx. 2"/5 cm depth) and I wouldn't want to use anything much larger. The layer of cheese looked thinner than in the photo.
I mixed in a can of white beans right before the florets and it was an excellent decision.

This is now one of my favorite meals. It takes one pan. Everything is done in successive stages. Pretty much any vegetable can be used. I have used asparagus and broccoli. I preferred asparagus. I added crushed roasted bacon once and it was great. I have used brown rice and white rice. Both are good. I’ve used five kinds of cheese and they’re all good. I will make this often as comfort food.
Very nice. I loved the use of broccoli stems—to added flavor and nutrition. Most recipes have you throw them away
@Andrew Oh! I never throw them away, regardless what the recipe says. They always taste good, no matter what!

Very tasty.  The rice took longer to cook than as written.  Next time I would use 2 cups of liquid and 12ish minutes then check to see if the rice is starting to soften.  The broccoli was beautifully cooked, slightly al dente and yes, still bright green after about 12 minutes.
Trees and cheese with rice!  Now my kids won't think I'm nuts because it's in the NYT Cooking app!

I’d say this would work with basmati rice, otherwise you’ll probably overcook the broccoli (which needs about 5-7 minutes steam cooking).  Don’t put the broccoli stems in, just peel and chop into chunks. Once peeled, they require even less cooking than the florets. 

If you other tires of rice, just add the broccoli toward the end. 

If you must use frozen broccoli, add it in the last two to three minutes of cooking— it’s already cooked and flash frozen.

Made this with brown rice and just eyeballed the cooking time which was longer. Accidentally threw in all the broccoli at once because I did not read the instructions well enough.  Turned out great!  Served with buttered crusty bread.  Will try to add cooked chicken in the future.  Really lovely and easy meal. I might consider using chicken broth instead of water as well since I do not have vegetarian concerns.  Love a nostalgic inspired recipe that does not require condensed processed soup.
Shallots and onion good. Used 1 c TJ brown basmati rice and 2 1/4 c water.  Cooked 30 min then added broccoli flowerettes for 10, then broiled for 4-5 min. Very good!
American-grown rice has arsenic because there is arsenic in the soil.  Use rice from elsewhere, such as Thailand, and there is no arsenic.  I hope this helps you.
@Susan. California grown rice is also free of arsenic.
Is that a deep dutch oven? It is hard to tell from the photo--the photo looks more like a braiser or I guess a 2 or 2 1/2 quart dutch oven?

Hi Ann - It does resemble a Dutch oven, but it's shallow, like a skillet. I think it's a saute pan.  Weighs a ton, so the two handles are great.  I am dedicated to the George Carlin mentality ...I don't want "more stuff."  But last year I caved in and bought one; absolutely love it; use it constantly.
Seems like a great way to do something with the leftover rice after a weekend splurge of take-out Chinese Feast.
I skipped step 4 and just added the cheddar to the pot of (jasmine) rice and broccoli. Also skipped the hot sauce. Even TPH (the Picky Husband) liked it very much.

I'm sooooo embarrassed. I followed the recipe exactly, including the Diamond salt and a cast iron brazing pan. It looked like the photo and tasted great with a splat of hot sauce. Yes, the rice was done.
@Christian  - Absolutely!!! Made mine EXACTLY per the recipe, and it looked like the picture and was absolutely delicious!! I used a Le Creuset braising pan

Do not use a Dutch over. 
You cannot get it close enough to broiler elements to brown cheddar and broccoli. 
While very tasty, it lacks any  kind of complexity. 
Need to add other flavors to give it some punch: olives, anchovies, various herbs, capers, pepper corns, cherry tomatoes, black pepper.
Anyway you get the idea. 
Great base for riffing to elevate it to gourmet status
I had high hopes, but this wasn’t a 5 star recipe for me. I used stock instead of water and added fresh herbs and plenty of hot sauce but I still found it very bland.
Excellent. Add a little heavy cream, chili crisp and canned beans for a filling comfort meal.
Would quinoa work?
My kids said it's the best thing they've ever eaten.

I made this last night for my family, which includes 2 fairly great eaters. Feedbacks from the 11 year old was “I liked it, it was surprisingly good. But I don’t want to eat that again.” 8 year old thought it was a little weird and only ate half of the serving. I concur. Was okay, fairly tasty, a little mushy, definitely isn’t a full meal so needed a main protein- we added meatballs. Might have chosen salmon instead if I had some on hand. This recipe is giving Midwest risotto. I think maybe we’re not huge cheddar fans. We may have preferred actual broccoli risotto with parmigiana. All in all, it was fine. I like trying new recipes. Made this with ingredients I had on hand so it was low pressure and happy to have tried it. No need to make that again though.

I needed to add 2 more cups of water during the time the rice was cooking.  This isn't the worst thing I have ever eaten but it does taste bland and I agree with others about adding spices and other types of cheeses.  I used a 12" cast iron pan and everything fit inside.  Feel free to experiment but just add more water if need be.

Yes, this took me a lot longer being a beginner cook, but this was amazing. It tasted silky from the butter and I followed it exactly as a recipe stated this is definitely a recipe. I would make my 10-year-old son loved it.

The broccoli florets did not fully cook, I would suggest cutting them smaller.

I added shredded rotisserie chicken at the last step when you gentyle stir - was amazing, especially with some Franks Red Hot dashed on when served.

Also, I believe mixing some cheese in would enhance the recipe.
Great with a fried egg!
Delicious recipe. I highly recommend using a broth of your choice instead of water (and therefore no salt) and doubling the cheese!
Tastes good, but it is missing something acidic. Vinegar-y hot sauce works. I also added sundried tomatoes which helped add an acidic kick.
Sprinkle some lemon pepper in the mix. That should brighten the dish up a bit.
Agree. Hot sauce at the table was a must and made the dish perfect. I like Louisiana brand.

Made this with wild rice to add texture and fiber!  Used a little white wine to soften and flavor veggies.  Sub veg broth for water.  As suggested by another,  just tossed in the whole 8oz of cheese, half mixed in the rice when almost done cooking and the other half on top.  Amazing.
What an amazing meal. I added cubed cooking chorizo: heavenly. Bless NYT!

For any fellow allium intolerant/allergic sufferers, here are substitutions I made. Two bunches of green onion tops for the onion, 2" piece of grated ginger for the garlic, allium free chicken broth instead of water and I added a heaping teaspoon of curry powder. Right before adding the cheese I stirred in some unseasoned rotisserie chicken. I offered pickled jalapenos for topping and served with a green salad and then NYT pumpkin chocolate chip bread for dessert. Everyone loved it.
I found it to be a bit bland. Won't make it again.

Tasty and easy weeknight dinner. We will make this on the regular during winter's coldest months. Be sure to add the right amount of salt - I think this is the difference between commenters who said "meh", and those who liked it.

@Amy Exactly! I was wondering why people are saying this is bland. It is extremely flavorful and delicious. But, I am not afraid of salt. The salt grinder is one of my favorite items on my kitchen stove.

I made this the first time as written and found it tasty, but a bit bland. After an addition of plenty of chipotle tabasco, it came alive. Now I’m cooking it again, but I’m using stock instead of water for the rice, exchanging the cheddar for a blend of Italian cheeses, and will brown and stir in some fennel salssiccia and chili flakes somewhere along the line. Yum! This seems like a recipe that you could vary in all kinds of ways.
This was awesome! Used brown basmati rice from TJ’s and added 15 minutes to the simmering time, but otherwise followed directions exactly. Family loved this.
Used brown rice, took longer, excellent result.
I added 2 cups chopped, roasted chicken I had from the freezer, used chicken stock instead of water, and toasted 1 t. yellow curry powder with the onion/garlic mixture.

My 1.5 year old ate it, no one else did. Very bland. Needs more seasoning and maybe cream of chicken. If I wanted dry rice I would make cilantro lime rice which is much more flavorful and everyone in my family eats it. Not sure why this is a 5 star recipe

Disappointing. I added red pepper flakes to the onion, but it came out with just a hint of heat. I also added the breast meat of a rotisserie chicken (chopped) and 4 oz. shredded cheddar along with the broccoli stems, and splashed in some chicken stock to keep things from sticking to the bottom of the pot. Also sprinkled 4 more oz of cheddar (mixed with turmeric, as my cheese was white) over the whole thing before it went in the oven. Used brown rice. But bland, and didn’t come together.

I substituted chick stock for the water but other than that stuck to the recipe. Husband, who is somewhat of a food snob, said we needed to keep this recipe  He added of all the rice, cheese and broccoli recipes he has eaten over the last 50 years, this was the only one he liked.

When I read this, I immediately got week night soccer mom vibes. Which I thought, why not try it? We added two cans of tuna at the end when we stirred the rice, and it was amazing! The two of us ate almost the whole batch, which I just finished what was leftover from last night for lunch. Can’t wait to try it with beans!
I added edamame for vegetarian protein and the kids love it

Bland. Added chicken for substance, used chicken stock instead of water and it still came out bland and leaving me wishing for real risotto next to roast chicken and broccoli.  Though I supposes this was "one pot" easy.
Make it with quinoa instead of rice. delicious!

I was hesitant to make this based on some of the reviews but it's become a go-to meal, especially when we have a broccoli on hand. We add a bit of chicken stock to the rice as it's cooking for a little more flavor. If we have left over chicken we'll throw that in, too. Easy weeknight meal that the kids enjoy.
Did anyone try the rec to stir in a can of tuna? I'm curious what you thought.
Totally delish, I will make again!
If I wanted to add chicken would I put the cubed raw chicken in the pan and cook until golden then follow the recipe? Will it cook all the way through?
I added 1-2 Tbs of curry powder for extra flavor. Turned out mellow, but delicious.

Yum!! Made as is, with a small bit of broc crown I had left over. Sautéed some fake ground beef and added towards the end before covering with cheese and broiling. Rice was cook perfectly! Will be making again with the proper amount of broc!

I used this as a jumping off recipe and it was quite good! I chopped up 3 strips of bacon and pan fried it in the Dutch oven, took it out, and then sautéed the broccoli stems, onion, and garlic in the leftover bacon fat. I used jasmine rice and two cups of homemade chicken and veggie stock. When I stirred the rice I added in the cooked bacon, some cheddar, and some finally diced chives. Served it with grilled chicken breast and sriacha sauce. The broccoli and rice were just a little mushy, so I will cook it for less time next time. I’ll also add more cheese in the rice and on top. A good base recipe!

Stellar easy side and very satisfying. I used frozen broccoli florets that I always keep in the freezer. I cut them a bit smaller and added them in about the last 5 minutes of cooking the rice. Definitely a keeper.
Now that I’m single, I’m excited to eat this dish and not worry about having someone in the house to experience the aftermath it will cause.

Very nice recipe although I think NYT cooking has star inflation.  No way it's 5 stars.  I will say amounts and cooking instructions are spot on and it's a very visually appealing dish.  Based on other reviews I added one full teaspoon of chili crisp and cooked the rice in chicken broth - with those two changes it had a mild savory flavor with a little kick.  Otherwise, I think it would be quite bland and would require lots of hot sauce.  Will definitely make again though.

This was really bland. I'm not sure if I did something wrong, but it... just tasted like broccoli and rice? Not really sure why it has such a high rating, unless people are going really off-recipe and adding a lot of extra flavoring to it.

I needed more water and time to cook both the rice and get the broccoli soft (for the kids eating), but thought it was good and my picky kids ate it, so that was win. I used veggie bouillon to add a little flavor.  In the future, will use more broccoli and add some herbs.

So this recipe is probably fine as is for some people, but just reading through it.....it sounds bland.  I sautéing onions and garlic, so it had some color because that's where the flavor comes from. I added a little chicken base for more flavor. I did not use stems (although I do use stems in salad and stirfry).  Steamed broccoli florets separately as I didn't want to disturb the rice cooking.  I broiled the finished product in a shallow pan.  It was good, but definitely needed extra love.
Needs more seasoning, lacked any sort of flavor before adding salt, pepper, and spice blend. Would not make again with recipe as is

I didn’t expect anything mind blowing, and it wasn’t, but considering the ingredients and ease this is truly a 5 star recipe.  Only adjustments I made were to use chicken stock instead of water and finely chopped shallots instead of onion.  Oh, and some msg.  My kids made a face when they asked what was for dinner, but when the time came they cleaned their plates and asked for seconds.
My husband made a face too, but he like it very well.
Huh?  Won’t the broccoli be overcooked?  Like WAY overcooked?
@Alibre I followed the directions and the broccoli was just right. I felt the dish needs to have some additions to up the flavor, but the broccoli was perfectly al dente.

This was delightful and nostalgic! I followed as written but swapped in chicken broth for the water,  used dairy free Daiya cheese, and stirred in about 1/4 cup canned coconut milk at the end before popping in the oven. Makes for great leftovers too.
I added the tuna as suggested and it was a wonderful weeknight meal.

I used unsalted chicken broth instead of water. I also rinsed the uncooked rice several times in cold water to remove the excess starch. This is an easy recipe that could easily become a one pot meal by adding leftover cooked chicken or pork just before step 4. It would also be delicious topped with a fried egg after Step 4.
The rice was undercooked despite using the longer times. Otherwise very tasty. Next time I’ll use a slightly higher temp.

@Jean our rice was badly undercooked as well. One of the top five most frustrating things to happen in the kitchen is to end up with 50% rice that's cooked well, and 50% rice that seems like it was cooked for 5 minutes. Have no idea how this happens. Always dubious of cooking rice with stuff in it.
I had to add a little more water to the rice, but this is New Mexico and everything is very dry.
Meh. Not really worth it in my opinion. I added 6 oz of cheese compared to the 4 in the recipe and it still seemed pretty light on cheese and definitely didn’t look like the picture.
Meh. It was pretty bland. I won't bother with it again.
Add 1 extra cup water to rice. Used 1 bag frozen broccoli, defrosted in microwave. Great!

I couldn't remember this morning whether there was any of this left. There wasn't. I added veggie sausages and some chile crisp. Made it a meal and a gave it a little oomph. Otherwise, chopping up the parts of the broccoli I usually throw away was genius. Easy, fast, cheap and yummy. What more can you ask.

Delicious!  Added the broccoli stems in with the onion and softened them.  Garlic then rice and used chicken stock with my Jasmine rice so did not add salt.  I added my broccoli flowerets the last 5 minutes of cooking so they would retain their vibrancy.  Broiled with the cheese until lightly brown.  Served with a rotisserie chicken and grilled small peppers.  A complete, easy and very good meal.  Will definitely make again.
Used Morton kosher salt instead of Diamond. Tasty and less expensive
use a low salt chicken broth instead of the water.  no added salt needed.  totally improves the taste.  mix the cheddar in, no need to broil.
Has anyone used brown rice instead of white?

PR: Haven't made this yet, but would like to with brown rice. In Step 2, I'm going to try cooking the rice for about 30 minutes, waiting til 20 minutes in to add the broccoli stems. I might try adding some chopped roasted pecans under the cheese, too. Best of luck. P, I tend to agree.
Any ideas for yummy topping to use on 1 serving for a dairy-free family member?
Vegan or lactose-free cheese? Or maybe seasoned bread crumbs.
Very nice formulation

I made this tonight and served it with roasted bone in, skin on chicken thighs. I cut the quantities in half for two of us. My little skillet doesn’t have a close fitting lid so I had to fiddle with the amount of water because of evaporation. Since I had the oven at 400° convection I put the pan in it to melt the cheese. We liked it a lot!  Next time I’ll put aluminum foil under the ill-fitting lid which should solve the water problem.

I have a feeling we will be making variants of this all winter. Used diluted homemade chicken broth rather than water, which gave the fi al product a risotto-adjacent yumminess. Lovely comfort food. Might try a little Pandora on top next time for a little crunch.

I used several cheeses including pecorino romano, and finished with some chili crisp. Otherwise it would have been incredibly bland. Even with the onion and garlic, I would recommend some onion and garlic powder. And other spices you like.
Made as written. Threw a couple of chicken sausages on the griddle while I finished the rice & broccoli. Cooking times were perfect. It was nice, not amazing, definitely wanted the hot sauce!

Made a half batch for lunch today.  Easy to follow recipe.  Definitely don’t skimp on the salt and pepper as this is the main source or flavor.   I might use more cheese or a more strongly flavored cheese (pepper jack or mix of cheddar and parm) next time.
Great! Added can rinsed cannellini beans and large can Sirena chilli tuna at the stir part - and topped with combination smooth ricotta and the grated cheddar.
After all the positive comments I was disappointed. It was very bland. My husband and I added salt and pepper and I added balsamic vinegar. It was okay, but I won’t make again.
Far more flavorful than expected. A keeper to be repeated!

Fabulous! Didn’t add the chopped stems (lazy) and mixed in maybe 2oz extra cheese when stirring all together, but otherwise followed exactly. Going to make again this week with leftover rotisserie chicken and chicken broth instead of water.
i only had half a cup of rice so i used quinoa and added 2 cups of veggie stock. i also added diced carrots and celery. it was delicious!
Didn’t have any rice so used farro instead and also added mushrooms. Used half chicken broth and half water. Yummy!

Just tried this. Followed the recipe to a T. It was okay. But, I did have thoughts. I suggest use chicken or vegetable broth instead of water. Double the cheese, perhaps stirring half in when you stir in the florets.  And it was hard to judge the quantity of broccoli when the description is “a large head”. I had too much broccoli. (If that is really possible).  But, I’ll give this one more try.

Added cubed ham while stirring, before adding cheese and putting under broiler. Everyone loved it. Ate it the next day with a fried egg, pickled red onions and chile crisp for lunch... superb! Keeper.

I cooked this in a braiser. I substituted chicken stock for the water, and added a sprinkling of capers when I added the broccoli florets. For the broccoli stalks, I peeled them, and then chopped them into coins. The rice and broccoli cooked perfectly, but broiling the cheese on top at the end brought this dish to delicious. I put a fried, over easy egg on top of each dinner portion. My husband loved it and asked to have it again. Next time, I'll add toasted sesame seeds along with the florets.

I really liked this - simple, easy and tasty....and I don't even really love broccoli.  Did add 2x more cheddar than called for and next time would do the same except stir in some of it to the rice and the rest on top before broiling.
I used pepper jack cheese instead of cheddar and it was REALLY good.  No hot sauce needed.
I wonder about the cooking time for the rice...seems too short.  I buy rice in bulk at the food coop and it takes considerably longer to cook than the 18 to 20 minutes stated in the recipe.

I would love to make this dish, but I cannot "arrange a rack near the broiler" (near it??). My apartment's broiler is in a separate section below the oven. I'd have to remove the rack entirely. And, the Dutch oven would be too hot to handle, and burn being so close to the flame.

Anyway, I'd probably use instant rice and other shortcuts, like the frozen steamable Green Giant Broccoli with Cheese sauce, or microwave in the bag broccoli and use the oven to melt the cheese..
you can stack on top of a cast iron skillet to raise the surface closer to the broiler
Boring recipe. wouldn't make this again
I added a can of tuna as suggested in the recipe note. Delicious!

In our opinion, this meal fell somewhat short of its 5-Star rating. It seemed like just eating broccoli, rice, and cheese would be quite bland so we added a block of cubed, marinated tofu after the rice was finished cooking for a little protein (would recommend). Not trying to discourage anyone from making this though, it fed four well and was pretty tasty.

Delicious recipe that the whole family loved. I added shredded leftover rotisserie chicken in with the rice and used chicken stock instead of water. I did notice there wasn’t enough liquid for rice to finish cooking when adding the florets, so added more stock to the pot as well. Next time will use typical 1:2 rice-to-liquid ratio from the get go.
The methods here just don’t work.  Rice and cooks unevenly - rice gets mushy, broccoli doesn’t cook. Just trust one’s instincts and blend everything together and simmer.

Used brown-wild rice mix - more water and cooking time.  Cooked in broth, but maybe not enough (?) Rice and broccoli came out well - added broccdolli the last 8 min of the rice - which needed 1/2 hour.  Have no broiler so just put it in oven with cheese.   It was very bland indeed - meh.  I will try again if our CSI has broccoli but put in dried Iranian 'sabzi' herb mix, and a much sharper cheese - a blue cheese might be good. ???
Can someone explain to me why NYT recipes frequently do not include the water in the ingredients list? Thanks in advance.
For the same reason they don't include pots, pans, spoons, or knives in the ingredient list. It's often considered a cooking medium, rather than a structural ingredient.
this is good and easy -- I dressed it up with a little smoked paprika, dijon mustard and Worcestershire sauce.

This one was a dud. For all it's simplicity, it's fairly fussy with all the steps. Which wouldn't be a dealbreaker, but it's also just not very good. I followed the recipe, but after it should've been done (just before adding the cheese), I discovered the rice and broccoli were both still crunchy. Other similar recipes that involve baking cooked rice, broccoli, and cheese are less hands-on and more delicious.

Having read all your comments:  Added a handfull of persian dried herbs used to make Sabzi polo or such.(cilantro, fenegriek, chives, parsley) which lends a lovely aroma to almost anything.  A handfull of dried dill, a handfull of leftover favabeans (double skined) and some extra butter when adding the broccoli flowers.  Cooked in vegi-broth. I didn’t add cheese tonight but will put the rest in the oven tomorrow with cheese.  Eaten with cucumber yoghurt (Persian style) it was very good.
Yes, Mollie Katzen's Enchanted Broccoli Forest--but without the poetry.
This is delicious and easy to make.  I added extra butter at the get-go, a bit of white wine as the rice was softening, and a tad of extra cheese.  It was scrumptious.
Use romiano cheddar is amazing in this recipe

Made this as written tonight for dinner.  I should have followed my instinct to use a full 2 cups of water for the stated 1 cup of white rice.  I knew better and followed the recipe anyway.  The rice was a little crunchy — which ruins an otherwise decent dish.

This was amazing! My vegetable resistant wife gobbled it up and had the leftovers instead of pizza when I went out to dinner with a friend. 
I added minced jalapeños (half of a pepper) along with the garlic and broccoli stems and the heat was perfect—no need to add hot sauce.
I do not understand Step 4 after finishing Step 3.
You want the sprinkled cheese to melt with some brown spots from the broiler heat.
@webalc broiler is a known as a grill in the 4est of the world. and I've always called it an oven rail rather than a rack. otherwise the recipe is clear. which sentence didn't make sense?
This dish sounds wonderful, but, when I try it, I think I'll add some deli chicken for a one dish meal to serve with a tossed salad.  Has anyway tried adding chicken?
Simple but much tastier than the sum of its parts. Used a leftover cup of chicken broth for some of the liquid but otherwise followed the recipe. Comfort food!
Would Arborio rice work in this dish?

Shades of chicken divan, a 70’s casserole staple with cream of mushroom soup. This has the same elements minus the chicken and processed soup. Try pearl barley instead of rice. It’s so good. Next time I’ll add mushrooms, too.
-Great as is and can be dressed up/down depending on your guests
Added a Teaspoon of Turmeric for funsies. So good.
Made this for my boys and they went back for seconds! I did add boneless skinless chicken breast browned in bacon fat. Perfect!!!

I made this as written except substituted vegetable stock for the water and used a bit more than 2 cups (in Colorado you always need more liquid). I was sure it wouldn't work, but it did -- the rice cooked, the broccoli was perfect (I broke it into very small pieces)... but it was so bland! The family ate it, but asked me not to make it again, it was so boring. How can I spice up this otherwise great recipe?
Kids and parents loved! Hot sauce was a great idea

My broccoli took so much longer to get cooked- and I needed to add more water, twice. Wondering if this was because I added beans and maybe needed to increase the water beyond the 1 3/4 C recommended.

Added very little salt and instead tossed in a veggie stock cube when it was time to cook the rice. Skipped the broiler & put shredded cheese on top for the last 2 mins of steaming the broccoli. White aged cheddar was lovely in this recipe. Will try adding chili crisp next time!

I added a can of chickpeas per the recipe suggestion. Used a combo of sharp cheddar and Parmesan for the cheese. Otherwise cooked as written. It’s good and easy. Hot sauce adds flavor. I would add some red pepper flakes next time.
Very good recipe. I didn’t bother with the broiler, just added the cheese to covered skillet for 3 minutes.
a little dry; sauce a good option

I tried this with wild rice with the added cooking time. Must have used too much water, because even after such a lengthy simmer, there was far too much water left. And even after doubling the cheese (!), something was lacking. I suspect the water was the problem, because the cheese seemed to sort of vanish; there was no thick, gooey cheesiness as implied by the photo. It all just didn't come together.
I was really disappointed in this. Made exactly as directed and it just came out SO SO mushy. Taste was there I think but could not get past texture. Water to rice ratio way off I think.
When the broccoli is under the broiler is the Dutch oven covered or not covered?
@Judith leave it uncovered. You’re broiling to brown the top of the dish.
Really tasty! My rice browned a bit too much under the broccoli, so next time I’ll try roasting the broccoli separately and mixing it in right before adding the cheese.
As written, it seems that you are supposed to remove the broccoli florets before Step #2, and then re-add florets during Step #3. Is that correct?
@CEA No don’t add the broccoli florets at all until step 3! Just sauté the chopped broccoli stems with the onions and garlic in step 1.
note: chopped stems in step 1 vs florets in step 3
How can we scatter the brocoli on top of the rice since the recipe asks to cook it with the rice ?
@KrysaliaH, read again. You cook the stems of the broccoli with the rice, then sprinkle the more tender florets on top nearer the end of the cooking time.

This was very good, I did add part of a bouillon cube to the rice cooking water, but otherwise no changes. However, the 45 minute time estimate could only be correct after the veg are chopped and the cheese grated, which I always include in my dinner prep-time estimates. Hard to predict, I realise, given people's different skill levels etc, but still.

Instead of broccoli stems, I substituted the kohlrabi that comes in my CSA that I never know what to do with, and I felt like a culinary genius! Not as eye pleasingly green of a dish but texture and taste worked great!
So so easy and so so good.   I sprinkled Tabasco for a little zip.  Delicious 😊
I thought this was a bit dry so I tried adding a bit of ranch dressing with fresh herbs.  Great!

My broccoli was overcooked after 8-10 minutes. 
Also, I used chicken broth for added flavor. The whole dish was a bit bland in my opinion.

This is far and away the most boring thing I have ever eaten.  When my kids finally put me in a home, I will be served this twice a week.  When I complain to them, they will say, “oh it’s not so bad dad. It has broccoli it has cheese. You like those.” And I’ll be forced to say, “then you fraking eat it. Get me a cheeseburger.”

Seriously, there is no where near enough cheddar to make up for the complete lack of umami in this.
I made this with chicken Better Than Bouillon instead of plain water and added a healthy pinch of MSG. With these changes, it's not bland at all.
This was nice, but it definitely feels more like a side dish than a main.
Pro Tip for any grilled sandwich - Don’t butter the bread. Melt the butter in your skillet or griddle. Lift up sandwich before turning and add another pat for the second side. So much easier.

I'm not a pro, but I've been using that method for decades.

This recipe is way too much trouble for a gooey mess of beef and cheese. Running back and forth to a gas grill? It's just a good if you do it all inside. (Might be worth it if you used charcoal.)

@sandiom
Even better use softened butter to completely cover one side of each slice of bread. Put cheese on dry side. Sauté butter side out flipping when browned
Naw... use mayonnaise for the nicest crispist grilled bread

Out here in Middle America we put a sauce made with Mayo, ketchup and Dijon mustard, pickle juice on our Patty Melts. We also do American cheese on one side, Swiss on the other. Gives it a kinda Rueben vibe, maybe. Since our state lays claim to the invention of the Reuben, perhaps that’s why.
I made this with Fage 2% greek yogurt. I added an extra swirl of honey and it was perfect.
Lovely combo of flavors and textures! I used balsamic vinegar and was generous with the pepper.
Thanks. I am a good cook but I really love these easy recipes.  I would never have added pepper to strawberries so  it  is a great easy recipe.
@Judith Add some balsamic with the black pepper, berries, and basil and spoon over vanilla ice cream for a fab dessert!

I used balsamic vinegar instead, more honey, generous on the basil leaves. Bed of baby spinach in center of bowl. Sliced avocado around the edge. Served strawberries around the edge with slotted spoon. Blueberries on top of spinach. Added a bit of olive oil to remaining balsamic vinaigrette and drizzled over spinach. Big dollop of greek yogurt AND cottage cheese. Generous with mixed nuts. I probably won't be hungry for the rest of the day!!! YUM!
I think this would also work with ricotta.
@Maggie. It’s absolutely delicious and much better with Ricotta!
@Marianne @Maggie Yes, I can hardly wait to try this with ricotta!
Cottage cheese, good/sweet strawberries, basil, and pistachios are great together. The honey, vinegar, salt and pepper…not so much I’ll keep it simpler next time.
I like everything, but this didn’t land.  I see folks have substituted yogourt for the cottage cheese, and I would highly recommend that if the cheese/vinegar combo doesn’t work for you!
I’ll have to try this. My husband and I enjoy mandarin oranges over cottage cheese, black pepper optional.
Delicious as printed. Second time around I used almonds and I did up the spiciness using Penzey's Pico Fruita sprinkle. Next I'll try balsamic instead of red wine vinegar. I'm sold on this combo!
Made with mint instead of basil and it was good! A nice variation on a high protein snack.
More black pepper, much more basil, no salt. Balsamic instead of red wine vinegar.
Wondering how this would work with peaches since that is what I have in abundance right now… I’m thinking probably not?

@Jeanneco
I think peaches would work so well too, but I’d probably prefer a mix of sticky and white balsamic vinegars to red wine though, in keeping with the fruit’s natural sweetness.  And if you preferred a smoother texture, mix the cottage cheese with a bit of Greek yoghurt.
@Jeanneco I’m eating peaches prepared with balsamic vinegar, served over cottage cheese, and it’s as delicious as the strawberries I made last week.
Hmmm... I can see black pepper going with strawberries but honey noooo.. Maybe plain sugar would go better.
@MS I agree. I used half the honey and added date syrup which is perfect with strawberries and basil. Just the right sweet since it thick and sweet almost like balsamic vinegar. And low glycemic.

Very easy and refreshing, and I could see how it would be even better the longer you let the strawberry mixture sit. Had to sub pumpkin seeds for pistachios but otherwise followed the recipe. Thanks Diane & Alex for turning me into a cottage cheese person!!
Not usually a fan of cottage cheese, but this is delicious. I did substitute balsamic vinegar.

I like this!  Yum!  I know it's sacrilege, but I used frozen, sliced strawberries because it's January and also I'm a lazy cook; super convenient and still good.  I'm thinking maybe to try Szechuan peppercorns next time.  I've tried these with fruit before and the floral quality goes nicely and there's that spicy punch too.  Anyone tried any other sweeteners?  Maple syrup?
@Sarah yes, date syrup is perfect.  A friend turned me on to it and I use it for a lot of recipes now.  It is thick and sweet almost like a balsamic.  Much better than honey or maple syrup.

Obviously a simple recipe, but the novelty of it was for me an inspiration and a springboard for how to get a new flavor dimension out of summer fruit. I’ll be trying several of the suggestions my fellow cooks have made. In return, here’s my twist: mixed equal parts of probiotic cottage cheese and Greek yogurt, then zested a fresh lemon over mixture, stirred well, then let sit a moment before serving. I think the subtle lemon taste added a lot. As always, I really appreciate NYT recipes and all the helpful comments.
This is so so so good. The strawberries with the vinegar and basil are perfect, and such a good breakfast with cottage cheese and some toast.

This was really nice on a 100-degree day. I recommend using Trader Joe’s small curd cottage cheese, which is the closest thing to a rich European cottage cheese that I’ve found (though it’s 4% fat, and if you can find a brand with 9% fat, do it; I think Good brand may make it).
Good Culture is amazingly delicious. No garbage in it and it has live cultures. Unfortunately, lately it's been hard to find but I won't eat any other brand.
Could I use mint leaves instead of basil?
You can use anything you want. Mint would be great, if that's what you like. Basil has such a mix of flavors that I'd be surprised if mint weren't part of basil's profile.
basil is a member of the mint family.  oddly i love various mints, but hate basil.  i might try some of my chocolate mint next summer.

There is a a small ice cream chain here in the Pacific Northwest, as well as in Florida, Salt and Straw, that has a rotating menu of very inventive flavors. One of my favorites is Strawberry Honey Balsamic Black Pepper. It’s a GREAT combination! So like others have commented, I would use nice syrupy balsamic vinegar in this recipe. But I’ll have to wait another month or so for the good local berries to arrive.
If anyone tries this over plain yogurt rather than cottage cheese, let me know. Sounds delicious otherwise
We serve this at home as dessert except with the strawberries being the star of the dish and a small scoop of mascarpone on top instead of the cottage cheese.
Simple easy and delicious for lunch breakfast or snack. Pepper and basil harmonize with the berries. Used Sherry vinegar
I’m eating it right now. I’ll lose the basil and pistachios next time. Meh.

I had no strawberries as it is September, so I made this with ripe nectarines.  This recipe made fat free cottage cheese (which is what I had) taste delicious.  The vinegar and black pepper really rounded out the flavor of the sweet fruit.
Ooh, really interesting!  I’m going to make it with homemade vegan ricotta & see how that goes!

I made it. After two bites, I strained the basil and pistachios out, washed the salt and pepper off, and had a fine breakfast. I have no idea why anyone would want to cripple the taste of fresh strawberries with that other stuff.
can you use frozen strawberries?
Would this deliver as much protein if you substituted yogurt for cottage cheese?
Pretty disappointed with this. Too vinegary and the basil doesn’t go well with any of it. Not a fan.
I wanted to like this but it is a no go.  I would rather eat a bowl of cottage cheese and then one of strawberries.
Made this as per the recipe and it tasted faintly like pickled herring.

@MerfromMaberly

Wait, so is that a positive or a negative?
I don’t like basil but used chives and that worked well. I did not anticipate liking this as much as I did plus to fits with my online diet app as a healthy breakfast ! It’s a keeper!
I've used mint instead of basil, but I prefer the taste of the basil.

This is all the right notes - taste, visual appeal, texture and endlessly adaptable, both in yield and optional ingredients, like ricotta or yogurt for cottage cheese (I recommend Nancy's organic probiotic 2 %), or balsamic for red wine vinegar, peaches for strawberries  I would not substitute fresh basil (be generous) nor the black pepper - both are essential. Ali - thanks for great recipe.

I was hesitant to make this but wanted to try some new protein options for breakfast. It is delicious! 
If you're on the fence I would encourage you to try it.

This was delicious. I used thawed frozen strawberries so that I could use organic berries. The berries and juices were wonderful over Daisy cottage cheese. A very satisfying meal for breakfast, lunch, or dinner.
This has been a real hit throughout my pregnancy!
This is a bit on the herby side and light on the sweetness...doesn't quite go with cottage cheese as well as tangy yogurt. I could have done without the basil and had a little more sugar.

I personally think the basil funks this up a little bit. And a little bit more honey than suggested (or maple syrup) would improve this. OK, and greek yogurt instead of cottage cheese. I realize this just made a different dish!
I really liked this though I imagine it’s not for everyone. I forgot the pistachios once but still good. A great way to add protein to your diet
Oh how I wanted to love this dish.  The flavors just didn't come together for me.

I made this today & it was delicious! Mind you I like cottage cheese, but what a great savory breakfast bowl. I used 1/2 red wine vinegar and half balsamic vinegar, because I knew that would appeal to me. Don’t skip the pepper, it really adds something special.

I loved this, but I also really like cottage cheese. Other members of the family find the texture of cottage cheese a little weird, so this didn't work for them. Regardless, great, healthy dessert alternative. I added some blueberries and reduced the honey to not be too sweet.
Tried this minus the vinegar (I strongly dislike vinegar even in tiny quantities) and thought it was great!
Some cottage cheese has probiotics so try to look for that kind (unfortunately more expensive) but makes breakfast extra healthy!
this is insanely delicious. made it with white sugar instead of honey

I blitzed up the cottage cheese til smooth and added some sour cream. My husband hates cottage cheese and he didn't know the difference! I can't wait to do this with other fruits. I see apple pecan and raspberry in my future.
Simple and surprisingly amazing.
I made this with blueberries instead of strawberries and it was really good. A healthy and filling breakfast!
I love Ali Slagle's recipes and have her cookbook, and this recipe was terrific: pistachios would have been lovely, but I used sunflower seeds as that was what I had.

I’ll cut back a bit on the basil quantity next time (maybe 2-3 leaves only..?) but overall this was a quick, tasty breakfast. It was even better the next day after the leftovers had marinated in the strawberries, so if your schedule allows make this one a day in advance.

Used ricotta and soaked chia seeds as the base. Followed the remainder of the recipe for one bowl, and it felt like almost too many flavors? For the other bowl, I used pecans instead of salted pistachios and omitted salt and pepper. Got a great light, sweet treat. Servings are filling!
This is absolutely delightful!
Absolutely delicious and easy! I use a little extra honey and put it over cottage cheese (good culture and kalona supernatural are my favorites).
current hyperfixation breakfast

@nandini

And worth every fixate!

I make this with  maple syrup, red wine vinegar and the strawberries. I add the pinch of salt to help make it juicier. Serve it with chia pudding, yogurt or cottage cheese. It's makes the supermarket strawberries taste great.
The vinegar, honey, cottage cheese combo was not a good one.  Vinegar and cottage cheese is a bit unpleasant.
Made as directed, and it was delicious.
Use allulose instead of honey to make this lower carb
A little too acidic for my taste. I may try with a different vinegar or maybe lemon juice.
This was a little too acidic for my tastes - more honey and/or less vinegar might be a good idea. I would also like to try it with white wine vinegar, or maybe with lemon juice.
Not a fan of honey, so I used course Demerara sugar. Did everything else by the book. Delicious!
Made a smaller batch to test. I liked it right in the middle of sweet vs. savory. It would be easy to serve extras so people could move in either direction.

I love cottage cheese, it was a staple growing up in Sweden. I ate it most days as an after school snack mixed with mashed banana and topped with crushed almonds (try it!). I find the American ones I’ve tried much too salty and the curds are harder, not as creamy. Anyone have a suggestion of a creamy, non-salty brand for this recipe?

I made this as written (except didn't have pistachios so skipped them)--delicious!  Today I tried a not-quite-ripe peach and threw in a few pieces of diced mango to add sweetness.  I live in Atlanta, so peaches are abundant right now.  Used mint instead of basil with the peach, but otherwise used red wine vinegar, honey, salt and pepper.  So so good!  This is an easy and sophisticated summer breakfast.
I made this for  my lunch, today it’s 100F in Kentucky! Perfect cool and delicious!

Excellent and easy to make night before. Used balsamic vinegar instead of red wine vinegar. Next time maybe add a bit more vinegar to increase the tartness to our preference. Used lots of basil and it was good. Otherwise followed the recipe exactly. A very nice change for breakfast. Did not have pistachios but toasted up pecans and crushed them and it  was excellent. 
Excited to use leftovers to serve over vanilla ice cream tonight.

title: Miso-Grilled Shrimp with Corn and Shishito Peppers Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody:
Really darn good. Scaled it down to 1 lb of shrimp, two ears of corn and a bag of locally grown shishito peppers. Two of us polished off the shrimp and half the veggies. Pretty good considering one of us “doesn’t like shrimp”. 

We’ll definitely be making this again. Thanks for a winner!

1.  Don’t add extra salt, miso/soy is salty enough. 2. Double or triple the sauce. 3. Grilled two lime-halves to squeeze on the tray before serving. 4. Added some hoisin sauce at the table. 5. Maybe skewer the shrimp.
Made with zucchini instead of peppers. Don’t add extra salt, miso is salty enough.  A little miso heavy for my taste, but husband liked it.  I added some hoisin sauce at the table.

Big hit with the family. I fully endorse the tail-on shrimp, but only had peeled, frozen wild shrimp, so I skewered it. I doubled the shishitos, threw in mini bell/sweet peppers from the farm box, and grilled two lime-halves to squeeze on the tray before serving. Beautiful late-summer spread!
This got rave reviews from my family. I made it as instructed in the recipe except that I didn’t add extra salt per @Judy. It will definitely go in the rotation. Thank you Ms. Slagle and @Judy.
Fun dinner you eat with your hands.  Double or triple the sauce.
This dish was waaaayyy too salty for us. The soy miso overpowered the fish. If I make it again I’ll definitely omit the soy sauce.

Fantastic. I haven't used miso in a lot yet, so I wasn't expecting this kind of flavor. If I had a real grill instead of cats iron skillet, I feel like the touch of smokiness would have been a huge hit. Go make this.
Way too salty even without adding that extra salt to the shrimp. This recipe is just meh! Not sure I get all the 5 star reviews, I guess to each their own.
Delicious and simple. Would 1.5x the sauce so there’s more for dipping.
Really good, just used the marinade for shrimp and zucchini and grilled. Super tasty.

Delicious! Spouse doesn’t eat corn on the cob so made a charred skillet corn with red onion and jalapeños .  Do not skip corn with this dish! Grilled zucchini with the shishiitos.  This is a keeper. Didn’t salt the shrimp but overall not too salty.

title: Slow Cooker Chickpea Stew With Lemon and Coconut Recipe
author: Ali Slagle

comments.commentBody:
I have a 6-in-1 Instant pot, and this is what I did.
Note that this recipe makes a lot of food! 
-1lb chickpeas   1 can coconut water, 1/2 the lemon peels, salt, and 1.5 cans of water. The chickpeas need to be completed covered by the fluid with some room to spare or else the top layer will dry out
-seal and cook on bean setting for 20 minutes 
-chickpeas will be al dente, not entirely cooked
-add cauliflower, 2 small yellow onions roughly chopped, and the following:
stock cube
2-3 tbsp curry powder 
2-3tsp cumin powder 
1 can coconut cream
1 jalapeno 
1 inch of ginger 
1 large clove of garlic (mash jalapeno, ginger and garlic in mortar and pestal before adding or roughly chop)
1 can of water
-the cauliflower will release some water, but not enough to make it runny
-seal and put on chili setting for 5 minutes 
-safely open and add 1 cup cashews and 2 handfuls of spinach
-stir spinach (should wilt immediately), add salt and pepper to taste
-add a splash of coconut cream if it's too spicy for you, but remember that the instant pot will water down the spices
I served it with brown basmati coconut rice and it was delicious. The sweetness and creamy flavor of the coconut rice will also help with any spice. The chickpeas are umami and the flavours are great. Cauliflower is perfectly cooked, soft with a hint of crisp. enjoy!
Kind of veers from the author's intended simplicity, but def a good curry recipe.  I also use IP almost exclusively, sometimes agonizing that a slow cook would yield richer textures, deeper flavors.

@SDC not sure why people feel the need to comment on a recipe that differs quite a lot from the original, making it a totally different recipe. I’m going to try the simple original which sounds delicious and so easy.

@Mo Kevany
I think it is because some of us love to share what new taste delight we have discovered and some of us love to learn what that new recipe is. I plan to save the original recipe and the variation.

Making my first comment because it will be first. This is a dish that will only taste better with time as the flavors blend. And it’s endlessly riffable - add chicken or pork shoulder for the non-vegans. I used to make a variation of this for our office parties. It’s easy to scale up to feed a hungry horde.

@Bret Bradigan
Making it now. It sounds great!

@Bret Bradigan
Slow cooking cauliflower for hours sounds awful. It doesn’t overcook?
@Anon O. Miss it crumpled into oblivion.

Made this according to the directions with one exception:  I added a tablespoon of smoked paprika along with the turmeric.  I’ve been on a smoked paprika kick lately and couldn’t resist.  It was absolutely delicious!  I think it would have been delicious even without the smoked paprika, but I enjoyed the nice smoky flavor it added to the mix. 

The coconut milk makes it so smooth and silky

The lemon zest and juice really add to the flavor. I probably will add more lemon at the table.

Easy peasy and delish!

Thank you Ali Slagle!
Excellent!  Cauliflower held up great in the slow cooker.  I added fresh corn and a teaspoon of cayenne pepper.
I have a pressure cooker but not a slow cooker. Any recos on adapting the recipe for an InstantPot or stovetop method? I have a cauliflower to use up and this looks delicious!

@Jess

Most InstantPots have a slow cook function, so you should be able to use that without any modification.
@Jess You can use your oven at a very low setting. I have put a Dutch oven in at 225, and it behaved just like a slow cooker

@Shelby yes it’s the same but the slow cook setting should be on medium. At 6 hours the chickpeas are al dente and the cauliflower just right. I might add a little time next time. Also considering blending half of the soup to make it thicker.

This was one of the blandest dishes I’ve ever made.  
So, I started experimenting.  I had added a chopped onion before cooking the garbanzos and waited to add the cauliflower until the end to avoid overcooking it.  Afterward, I added about 2 Tbl Better Than Bouillon Chicken Stock concentrate, about 1/4 c. Worcestershire, a bit of soy sauce, and some Johnnie’s Seasoning Salt.  Ah, much better.  But after a day in the fridge, it’s downright delicious.
@Rosie feeling the same way about the recipe and all your adds sound just right. Will taste the original in the morning to see if my mind changes.

@Rosie agree 100% this recipe as written is ok but pretty bland. We doused it with chil crisp and added some fresh Thai basil and it brought a *lot* of life to the dish. As written this is a 1.5-2/5 stars max but yes with some additions it can become amazing. Will try your ideas next time (I was thinking about the need for sauteed onions almost immediately as well)!
Made as written, except I added a few teaspoons of vegetable Better Than Bouillon because I was worried about it being bland.

I made this as written, mainly to see if it would be a suitable vegan dish for my son and family to add to their recipes for those busy days- it was absolutely delicious and the cauliflower had nice chunks to it. Paired with brown rice and a cucumber dill yogurt salad, it was perfect! I would make again and maybe vary the spicing just for the heck of it.  But overall a great recipe!

This is really a lovely, subtle dish,  we served it over white rice. I added a smidge of Aleppo pepper and Garam masala, but not enough to alter the flavor.  The lemony flavor adds so much.  Had only one can of coconut milk so used chicken broth. Less rich and less fattening. We had it as a side dish with grilled pork tenderloin and cooked zucchini but it is a meal unto itself. People who are complaining about lack of flavor are looking for more strongly spiced Indian food, which this is not.
May be unpopular opinion but it hit for me. I topped it with my choice of hot sauce and I had rotisserie chicken from Whole Foods for added protein
I too am curious about cauliflower. Also, I suppose this defeats the recipe’s purpose, but how can I make this without a slow cooker?
Has anyone made this with coconut milk lite? I have two cans and was wondering if it would be almost as good...lol
Just ok…I agree with a few other reviewers that this is a good base, but needs some other additions.  To the leftover soup I added chopped spinach and some shredded chicken which made it better….

Mo Kevany: Many different types or people (and eaters) read these recipes. It would be the easiest if everyone recognized that some comments may land for you and it may land for someone else. If it doesn't land for you...move on. I just read a comment on a recipe that said the chicken treatment works great for eggplant. I thought GENIUS! The very next comment was someone who chastised the writer for making a vegetarian comment on a chicken recipe. Leave space for everyone to enjoy the comments.
I love the sound of this but I really don't like cauliflower. Any suggestions for a replacement veg that won't get soggy?

Maybe chopped - not sliced - cabbage?  If you use a red one, the color would be a pleasant enhancement.  Or cook the chickpeas alone and when done stir in a goodly amount (at least 8 oz) of baby arugula.  Neither option would change the flavors considerably, although the textures would be different.
@Yolanda, maybe eggplant? Root vegetables also hold up well, especially parsnips and carrots, maybe kholrabi
@Yolanda I would try sweet potato. Always pair as well with chickpeas in a curry.

Made this with the Instant Pot… added all ingredients as directed, set the lid on, used the slow cook function. Time = 7 hours, heat = medium. Was a little watery so I used the sauté function for about 5 minutes then turned the pot off and made brown basmati rice to accompany. Garnished with fresh cilantro. Easy and delicious. My advice if using the instant pot is reduce the water somewhat, it apparently does not need to cover the cauliflower completely.

I added a chopped onion and a diced jalapeno at the beginning. Used about 4C water, not enough to cover the cauliflower. I accidentally cooked for almost 10 hours. Chick peas were good but cauli was overdone. But once I garnished with chopped tomatoes, whole cashews, and some plain yogurt and served over a mix of basmati rice and quinoa, it was pretty tasty. I used the juice from both lemons.

Made this as directed! I'm glad I was home - it was done (chickpeas and cauliflower tender) MUCH faster than written, after maybe 3 hours on low. (These were old, dried beans, not soaked.)

I really like the flavor, but it is pretty subtle, and the cauliflower was bland when it first finished. I added more salt to taste, stirred, and let it sit off heat for a couple hours, and the cauliflower tastes better now.

If you don't like the thin broth, add some split yellow peas with the dried beans!
@Elijah B. Or red lentils for thickening and added protein.
This was delicious! I used canned chick peas and cooked on the stove instead of the slow cooker.  Added cilantro before serving.  Hearty but also light and clean.  A new staple for my family.
The trouble is that the smell of slow-cooking cauliflower is very off putting.

I made a half batch since some of the comments were discouraging, but I like cauliflower and chick peas. It was really good. I soaked the beans overnight. I also used less than 3/4 (coconut milk) can of water because of the comments about bland taste and “soupiness.” I figured the cauliflower that wasn’t submerged would steam. I made sure to salt well, and used juice of half a lemon - didn’t need extra. It was flavorful, easy to make, and I’ll make it again.
I slow cooked this recipe for over 8 hours.  The chick peas were still hard and unsatisfactory.  I recommend soaking them overnight or pressure cooking them first.

I liked this but here's what I would do different: add cauliflower about 1/2 way through the cooking process rather than at the beginning. Sub chicken broth for one of the cans of coconut milk (less thick and fewer calories). Add some more spices - garam masala? Curry? Red curry paste? Something else. More salt! Also, a can of crushed tomatoes would be nice too. 
I had no problems with the chickpeas cooking through. 
This felt like the basis for a great recipe but as is it's sort of bland.
The lemon zest overpowered my entire dish. If I read well, 2 lemons, big stripes. Everything tastes like lemon zest, no coconut, no chickpea, no cauliflower. Anyone had this happen?

I had canned garbanzo beans, so I just boiled everything together for about 25 minutes, added a handful (or two) of whole black peppercorns for depth; threw in some garlic, onion, and half a fresh habanero pepper as well. Topped with cilantro to serve. Amazing.

Very disappointed .The cauliflower cooked nicely.  I served it with basmati  rice and corn chips.  The soup was thin and bland. Cilantro topping would have helped.   Adding chicken would have helped. I wonder how my friend liked the quart I gave her..(before I had served it to my family).

Did anyone else have issues with this drying out? I needed to add a substantial amount of additional liquid -- an entire third can of coconut milk, and a little water on top of that. Maybe my slow cooker's low setting runs a little hotter... 
Otherwise, we loved it. I did add some extra spices for more depth, but will absolutely add this to the repertoire.

Prepared this recipe as written. It is really underwhelming. It takes so long to cook the chickpeas that it causes the coconut milk to split. As others have mentioned, slow cooking cauliflower will leave your poor dog getting blamed for the atrocious odor until you recall oh yes, I’m SLOW COOKING CAULIFLOWER. Furthermore, as much as I love turmeric, it is not a flavor that is pleasant on its own — it really begs for other spices with which to harmonize. I’m left wondering how in the world this recipe made the cut to be featured in the NYT.
sorry, this recipe was downright disgusting. the chickpeas were crunchy, the cauliflower was mushy, and the flavor profile was unpleasant. at the very least, you should soak the chickpeas

Made as written except on stovetop & emulsified because I was hiding the cauliflower. Delicious!! What a nice surprise!!! I really enjoyed the lemon. The turmeric wasn’t strong. Hearty yet light, is that possible?! I’ll definitely make this again!

I cooked this as written and was surprised by the deliciousness of it. Such a simple and satisfying dish that felt almost magical for a dinner party (served with a 5 ingredients baked salmon from NYT cooking, and basmati rice). A winner with all my guests.

Unfortunately, this is one of those recipes that I think vegans who have eaten vegan for a looooong time and forget what other food tastes like thinks is good. And/or if you’re a purist that likes unflavored, ingredient only food? Couldn’t force myself to eat the leftovers and it’s been years since a recipe failed that hard for us.
This dish is really magical in the way it delivers flavor and texture from a few ingredients AND with little cooking. Simplicity at its finest.
Could I use canned chickpeas instead and than cock everything on my stove the old way?

This was the most underwhelming dish I’ve made from NYTCooking. Not sure how it left the test kitchen. Made exactly as written and lacked any flavor, texture, umami. Tried to salvage based on other comments with cayenne, a corn starch slurry, and nada. I’ll keep for nutrition reasons, but def not making again. Shame cause I did want this to work. Move along to another dish.

It was delicious, but I used the Instant Pot on slow cook function (high), and my chickpeas weren't done even though I brought them to a boil first to jump start cooking. Ended up pressure cooking for a few minutes to finish it off, which worked but the cauliflower broke down. I would make it again, maybe soaking and/or parcooking the chickpeas first on high pressure. 
It's not bland. Just season it well—and go ahead and use the full fat coconut milk.

Ugh. I love cauliflower, chickpeas, and Ali Slagle recipes, and this is the first time they have disappointed me. Came home to the house reeking of dirty diapers only to discover it's the smell of dinner. The coconut milk curdled and cauliflower turned to mush. Chickpea texture is fine but the recipe is bland and needed a LOT more salt and could use more flavor. Somehow the lemon zest got a gross scent too. I'm throwing this out and airing out the house while I make something else ;_;
Made this with carrots instead of cauliflower since that’s what I had on hand. I also added fresh grated ginger and some dried spices. Turned out great!

Made this recently and LOVED it. I found the basic recipe to be a little bit lacking in flavor, so toward the end I added fresh ginger, garlic, and some tamari. For serving, ladled it over rice and added some marinated & broiled tofu, fresh cilantro, toasted cashews, and chili crisp. So, so good and very easy for a nutritious weeknight meal. The next day tossed in some leftover chicken breast. It's a great base to work with whatever else you have on hand.

We made this today and, as many have stated, it has a lovely taste, albeit a bit bland. I added 1 1/2 tsp of Ras el Hanout and voila! Instant level up! I do think it needs some sort of green - fresh spinach, arugula, Lacinato kale? Unfortunately, I don’t have any of these on hand to try, but that’s my personal note for next time. Thanks to the chef who created this! The lemon is exceptionally lovely.
If you forget the lemon strips, microplane some lemon zest in there at the end and call it good.  So delicious - the lemon juice is a great addition.

nice, but a little bland and a little thin. i put about a quarter cup of rice in a bowl and then a couple of ladles of the soup, and that helped body-wise. i also added a little more lemon and that enhanced the flavor.

I wanted to like this, it looked so easy and fresh with the lemon. I followed the instructions to a t and it looked like the one in the photo. It didn't smell amazing but I trusted the process. When it was done I took a few tastes of the soup then ordered pizza. It was bland and not too appetizing to look at it. This one was a miss.
To save time, would it work with can chickpeas? What would be changes to the amount of ingredients (coconut milk, Cauliflower etc)? Thanks

The recipe looks wonderful as-is (though I also wonder about slow-cooking cauliflower for 6 - 8 hours).

Wondering about adapting to an IP, I would think that letting the chickpeas cook to about 3/4 done-ness and then adding in the cauli should do it.  I can always simmer longer afterwards, to allow the coconut milk to keep developing.  Maybe also add ground coriander, because.  

L/O's could easily be go into a more complex curry - add sliced onion and chunks of sweet potato.
Made this evening. Started with sautéed onion and garlic. Added the Better than Boullion as suggested by others. Still lacking so added Aleppo pepper and a squeeze of lime. Pleased with the result.
I want to cut this in half. How will that affect the cooking time?
We made this as written. At 7.5 hours the chickpeas were still quite firm. We agree with the description that it is sweet and floral and we just didn’t care for the floral part of the flavor profile.
Very tasty and so easy to make! This makes a lot of food - has anyone tried freezing leftovers and have any tips?

I did it with ingredients as above (except subbed 1 c. chicken broth for water to fully cover) in Instant Pot. Cooked 40 minutes with natural release and the chickpeas came out perfectly. Cauliflower only a little mushy. Agree with some other commenters that it's quite bland and hope as someone said that the flavors will meld for tastier leftovers. As to someone else's comment about the consistency, mine came out perfect somewhere between a soup and a stew.

This sure was underwhelming. Even after an extra 10 hours of slow cooking, the chickpeas were still crunchy and the flavor was bland, excepting bites that had large chunks of lemon peel in them. I now have a lot of this left and don't look forward to eating it.
Anyone use canned chickpeas?  Its all I have currently, wondering how it comes out.

I found it watery and bland with too soft cauliflower. I ended up doing a quick sauté of garlic and onions added a little red curry paste and white miso then puréed the whole thing and topped it with yogurt and a little green fig leaf oil!

As much as I wanted to like this recipe, I think it's flawed.   Not enough liquid to cover the chickpeas - so not all cooked at the same rate.  Found them still underdone. Cauliflower fell apart (although I cut in large florets, which I thought would preserve their integrity.  Also, kinda bland - even though I added a healthy amount of fresh ginger (3+ T) and a diced jalapeno.  
Not inedible, just not great enough to make again
Sad.

My kid doesn't like/ won't eat cauliflower( if he can see it).
If I cut the florets small, won't they disintegrate...meaning the nutrition stays but the evidence is gone??

@Esther the cauliflower dissolved completely in mine. I ended up with a thick mush. I added garlic, a jalapeño and some shallots to mine, along with a little basil. The taste is good but the texture is not. Oh well. Filling and nutritious!

This was indeed an easy, straightforward recipe - the most difficult preparation step was abiding the smell of cooking cauliflower for several hours. The result was interesting - intriguingly unlike anything else, hearty and filling, delightful with small mint leaves, but just not quite enjoyable enough (for me). 

I’ll struggle through the leftovers with lots more salt, mint leaves, and hot sauce. I’m not sorry to have tried it, but probably won’t make it again.

As it's written, the flavor relies largely on the quality (and age) of the turmeric. Using top-shelf turmeric makes all the difference! Plus I subbed chicken broth for some of the water which didn't hurt.
Great ratio of prep time to yumminess. It is so easy to make that I had my 5 year old help out. You could add stuff to it, sure, but the basic recipe is so good that I'm not inclined to mess with it.

Very good!  I used chicken broth instead of water, but vegetable broth would work also if you wanted to keep it vegan.  I also skipped the salt and add some better than bullion at the end to add both salt and umami.

Made a variation on the stovetop:
* cook soaked chickpeas in 6 cups water, 1tsp salt, 5 cloves garlic, zest from a lemon.  ~1hr
* when the chickpeas mostly done, add cauliflower, turmeric.  cook until the cauliflower is falling apart. ~45 min
* add 1 can coconut milk & lemon zest
* salt & pepper to taste.

The chickpeas took longer than usual-- wonder if it's from cooking with the lemon zest.

There's a great dal recipe by George Alagiah of the BBC which has a similar flavor profile.

This was disappointing. After 6 hours in the slow cooker both chickpeas and cauliflower were like rocks. I put a bunch in the microwave to make them edible, and added a lot of spices to help the blandness. Then let it all sit over night, simmered an hour on the stove, and applied the immersion blender. At least it’s edible now!

Made it exactly like the recipe called for. It never turned into a stew. Even after 8 hours, the chickpeas were still pretty firm, edible but firm. The broth was watery, and tasted fine by itself, but got lost with the cauliflower when eaten together. This will be a pass for me.

Made in the instapot on slow cook setting for 8 hours and it was good.  I wanted some other colored veggies on the side so cooked up some broccoli and carrots.  The carrots were especially good added to the soup and I might try putting them in at the beginning next time.  I think I would like it spicier so I will experiment with that (there are leftovers so I will do that before reheating).

This was so easy, and a hit with my husband. I did make three minor changes: I put the cauliflower in two hours before the end; I went with one can full fatted coconut milk and one can lite coconut milk instead of two full fatted cans; and I used Penzey's Hot Curry powder instead of Turmeric because I like a little kick.

Did not care for this at all. Didnt get much flavor and not a fan of texture. Maybe I added too much water because the coconut milk was diluted and this was less of a stew and more of a bland, watery soup. One commenter shared a curry version using an IP. Might be worth testing that approach instead.  Also - note that this makes a LOT of food.
Any substitute for turmeric?  It took forever for the stains to come off of my teeth.
I'm a super taster, and this was a bit too subtle for me. Textures are nice, and the color is lovely.

The chickpeas didn't cook all the way through in eight hours.  I ended up having to remove the cauliflower and pressure cooking the beans.  If I make it again I would pressure cook the beans first to avoid this issue!

Ok, made this overnight for a work potluck. I wanted to make sure there was a vegan and gluten free option. I had resolved to follow the recipe exactly through to final tasting. My findings: Stew was watery looking and the cauliflower did not break down so it was overly chunky. I solved that by blitzing with an emersion blender in bursts until it was smoother, but still had small chunks. It tasted kind of meh, so I added the juice of both lemons.
