Very good, but still needed something so I added 1/2 tsp salt and a good shake of pepper. This was a mistake. The salt blunted the lemon almost completely. Sad. I added in more lemon juice but it still was less lemony than before the salt. I also added a healthy squeeze of sriracha to kick it up. I think it’s good. Hope my colleagues agree! Not sure if I will make again, but it was a very good overnight cook option.
Another comment: I wish I had cut the lemon peel into smaller pieces. I kept them in, but they did resemble used bandaids. Not ideal.
This wasn’t a hit in my house. I didn’t like the texture of the overcooked cauliflower.

I'm joining those who feel that the recipe, as is, is bland. When it was finished, I kept tasting and adding: more turmeric, more lemon, then paprika, garam masala, and finally fenugreek. It's pretty good now. We'll enjoy it, but I won't make it again.

Easy to prepare and was quite a warming and aromatic meal with good texture as well as taste. I didn't depart from the recipe, other than adding a touch of cornstarch to add some body to the sauce. It turned out well, and paired with some rice it was enjoyable to eat. I cut the lemon zest into thin strips before adding them to the pot.
I made this as directed but did use chicken stock instead of water. I think I put a bit too much in as my result was more soupy than stewy, but it was delicious!

I was so excited to try this even as I was assembling it, as I love cauliflower and chickpeas and curries. I always follow the instructions as given the first time… but I have to say, my husband and I both disliked it. I found the lemon overpowering, maybe I used too much juice but I assume “half the juice” meant the juice of one lemon with the second in reserve; I added some salt, but didn’t have a good idea how much and 3/4 tsp plus more pepper did not help. I put some in the fridge to see if it improves and threw most of it out.

I used my instant pot and set it for 2 minutes. My husband said the 2nd lemon wasn’t necessary, but I love sour and disagreed. Yummy for a weeknight, especially if you’re wondering why you bought that cauliflower in the first place.
My husband made this and it really has no flavor. I think it would benefit from some aromatics like garlic and onion.

The lemony flavor is indeed floral and quite lovely, although it makes for something of a one-note dish.  I had cooked chickpeas on hand so I went stovetop.  Cooked the cauliflower in the coconut milk with lemon peel and turmeric.  When done, added chickpeas and simmered for a while.  Since I virtually live on legumes, a welcome addition to my collection.

I was excited to make this, bought all the ingredients. Then… I read the comments. Although I was looking forward to the ease of making this I did not want my house to smell like farts….

So I made the recipe this way:
-pressure cooked the chickpeas
-air fried the cauliflower 
-then added the chickpeas to the coconut milk & turmeric & lemon zest and brought to a boil and simmered for about 20 minutes then added the cauliflower & lemon juice.

So far so good, and our house does not smell like farts.

As others have mentioned the original recipe is a bit bland, I countered this by adding a lot of salt, pepper, refreshing lemon juice with each bowl, adding a decent amount of red pepper flakes. it’s an easy recipe and creates a pretty high volume amount. i also added 2 chicken breasts and some barley but that veers off the og recipe a bit more.

This was one of the most disappointing NYT recipes I’ve made. It was too rich, thick, and flavorless to be called a stew; it was more like a bland coconut curry(ish). Epic fail. We threw out the leftovers.
@Horn Agree. And I’m usually a big big Ali Slagle fan. I managed to salvage it by adding spices and garlic to my taste and using an immersion blender to turn it into a cauliflower chickpea soup.
I really enjoyed the flavors here, but wasn’t thrilled with the texture. Liked it a lot better after I puréed it with the immersion blender.
Nice idea - I might try that with my leftovers.

Fried bacon ends and onions and added canned chickpeas and canned white beans with rest of ingredients to that. Cooked an hour on the stove. The shaved lemon was delicious when cooked. Used vegetable broth instead of water. Added half and half left behind by a visitor after I turned it off. Used submersion blender. Sprinkled with sweet curry before serving. It was a keeper

I was nervous to make this because there were a lot of reviews saying that it was tasteless. But my family really enjoyed it. The lemon peel does add another layer of flavor. And the coconut milk really adds a creaminess. I did add sauteed shallots and garlic as well as chicken broth in place of water. But I find this an easy and delicious recipe.

What an easy way to make a tasty, special dinner out of an inexpensive bag of chickpeas! Despite my doubts, the chickpeas cooked up nice and tender, and the lemon zest as promised was surprisingly delicious. I served it over rice, offering the garnishes of lemon wedges, cilantro, and almonds, which added interest and complexity. I can see making this dish for company, although I'd add another dish - some kind of green salad I think. Needed  the extra lemon juice and more salt before serving.

So disappointing. I made this in slow cooker for eight hours and followed the recipe exactly. When it was done I followed the instructions to squeeze in  juice of half a lemon or more. I squeezed the lemon in and the soup curdled.  I have not seen a review reporting this. I threw it out. A ton of soup. Very disappointing.

I don’t think I’ve ever given 3 stars before. Made according to recipe. It cooked properly but had a disappointingly boring taste that was too lemony, and I like lemon. I used 2 small ones for the peels and the juice of one of them at the end. I assumed “half the lemon juice” meant the juice of one lemon but why not just say the juice of one lemon? Or better yet, an approximate measurement with “adjust for taste?”Seems to be a source of some confusion. I may at least use this basic idea without lemon and add red curry paste or a lot of spices to make a good curry.

So unbelievably bad. I can’t believe this recipe has more than one star. After 6 hours in the slow cooker, the overcooked cauliflower and lemon peels combine to create the most revolting stench. My house was almost uninhabitable until I aired everything out. The smell made my wife and son gag. I chocked down a few bowls and found the taste less offensive than the smell, but it came out incredibly bland. I followed the recipe exactly.

💙 Each Thursday brings me such joy to deliver home cooked, love filled food the the Community Resource Center of Mamaroneck.  Sharing this week lemon chickpea + cauliflower stew which has warm turmeric and coconut meal for healing the heart, soul and body.  The CRC shared that each week their members feel the love and that is what Love is.
Be forewarned: six hours of cooking cauliflower requires opened windows. My daughter came home and provided a graphic description of her olfactory experience.

I liked this quite a bit when I added shredded chicken thighs, but still feel like it’s missing some dimension of flavor.
Does get better after it sits in the fridge a day or so?

Anybody have any simple ideas for enhancement?

Following the recipe exactly, I found this bland, not bad, just blah.  I sauteed 8 cloves of garlic, a giant leek, and an onion, with some red pepper flakes and paprika.  To the pan, I added maybe half the cooked amount of chickpea stew.  I will try other additions to the second half of stew.
Needed a little something, I will add a couple of onions next time. Also my turmeric is old so that probably did not help.

This was not good.

I’m wondering if it was because I have a crock pot that is rectangular rather than round - which spread the liquid out too much?

The dish was dry, the chick peas weren’t fully cooked. We ended up throwing it out. I’m glad it’s working better for other folks! It sounded delicious!

My neighbor liked the quart I gave her.  I didn't like the texture or thin broth, After a. night in the refrigerator, it had thickened up; I put it in a blender with some chicken broth and it was. good. Not sure that I will make it again, but trying to include more healthy beans in my diet and garbanzos packed.
what if I use canned chickpeas? Does that reduce the cook time or coconut milk measurement?

When I made this, I was concerned about the cauliflower not softening up much after 3 hours, so I increased the temperature to High. While the broth was a bit watery as described by others, the flavor is quite pleasant. (Though yes, the smell is something else!)  I think I will puree this to make a nice lemony soup for these dog days of summer.
Are those mint leaves floating on top?
I used canned chickpeas and cooked on the stove top was still very good.
Can you substitute canned chickpeas for the dried ones?

This was good, but I agree with the others that the chickpeas need more time than the cauliflower. If I make this again, I will cook chickpeas for 2-3 hours on high and then turn to low and add cauliflower for last 4 hours. I also agree that this dish is a bit bland and sauteed onions and garlic in the beginning helps. It also helps mask the odor of the cauliflower simmering which is a bit unpleasant.

Made half portion of this recipe in a 6 quart Instant Pot set at slow cooker setting for 8 hours.  The chickpeas were still crunchy at the end of cooking.  The is the only recipe from the NYtimes that I have composted after cooking.

Some of the problem was user error (my coconut milk was not the best and I think I left it in the slow cooker too long- 6 hours would have been plenty) but this was not very interesting. We added a lot of extra salt and hot sauce to round out the flavors.

I read all the comments on this recipe before cooking, and made a few changes as a result. 1)Soaked the chickpeas, and only used 1lb of the soaked beans. 2)A large head of cauliflower instead of 2lbs. Amounted to 1.5 lbs on my scale. 3) Only 1 lemon available, and it was enough. 4)Used better than bouillion in warm water to cover. 5)Added: 1tbsp minced ginger, 1tsp curry powder, dash of cayenne, dash of red pepper flakes, 1.5 tsp garlic powder and 1 tbsp onion powder. Took 8 hours in my 8 quart slow cooker on low. Then I purèed about 2/3 of it--incl lemon rinds. It is very good as I prepared, and might add shredded chicken or rice for a nutrition boost on later servings. Take the recipe as a great base, home chefs, and make it to your taste.
Rather bland taste, even with addition of lemon. I wish the recipe would specify how many cups of water to add. I added enough to cover cauliflower and ended up with a soup instead of stew.
I added the juice of probably 1 and a half lemons and a lot of extra salt at the end. I used green beans and zucchini instead of cauliflower. Great and EASY!
Made it as per recipe.  While experts may say that the flavors of coconut and lemon go together nicely, I discovered that they do not so that for me.  Won't make this again.
I found it bland.  Others might like it.

I individually like all the ingredients in this recipe but did not like the result. There is a bitterness on the back of the tongue. I'm eating leftovers on the second day and the lemon juice (from one lemon) is really overpowering. Other than the lemon, it has little taste.
i'm concerned that strings of lemon peel will have an offputting texture. Did anyone have that issue, or do they melt into the soup?

@rcmapp I was concerned about that, too, so preemptively removed the most visible lemon peel pieces prior to serving. However, happily, I couldn’t remove all of them and discovered that when I hd a bite that contained a bit of peel it was not off putting at all. The texture was barely noticeable and the soft hint of extra lemon flavor was nice. Good luck!

This was good. It was definitely on the more "subtle" side flavour wise, but the limited, cheap ingredients and ease of method made up for it. I used soaked dried chickpeas because I have an instant pot which notoriously runs cool. My cauliflower was also small with a lot of leaves, so I just chopped the leaves up and threw them in with the rest of the ingredients. It's good with hot sauce, but it would also be a nice meal for a sick day.
This was an easy and delicious dish, but we felt it needed a little more depth to the seasoning. We added 1-1/2 tsp of Penzey’s Now Curry — wow! A new favorite recipe!

This was delicious! I only had one can of coconut milk. I used 4 cups of veggie broth instead of all water. At the end I added baby spinach and fresh herbs for greens, and a dollop of Greek yogurt to compensate for my missing can of coconut milk. Served it with red pepper flakes and homemade naan! Will definitely make again!

Ignore the haters and try this. It could not be easier, and the payoff is quite nice and very riffable. 

Made as written, but added garam masala, cumin, and fresh garlic (we love spicy). Used 1 can full fat coconut milk + 1 can lite. Served with cilantro and a dollop of Greek yogurt. Really tasty. 

Next time I'll add a bit more water. The sauce really thickens up in the fridge and you'll want more of it to spoon over your rice.
I dont have a slow cooker or an instant pot. How can this be adapted with a dutch oven? Thanks!
Ali, this is a lovely recipe. Simple, economical and delicious. Wouldn't change a thing .
The finished product had a good aroma, but the chick peas stayed hard. Not edible but soft enough for the disposal to handle it. I’d try it again but would soak over night.

Slow cooking cauliflower for hours produced quite the unpleasant odor throughout the house, and after 8 hours, the chickpeas were still not quite soft. Had to give in and pressure cook for 10 minutes, which produced what I think was very adequate results. If I make this again I will just pressure cook.

Questions:
I do not like the taste of turmeric, plus it gives me indigestion in significant amts. May I omit it from the recipe or is there a substitute?

Many have commented that the chickpeas were still too firm at end of cooking. Could canned chickpeas be successfully used? If yes, should slowcooking time be reduced?
Has anyone tried replacing the garbanzo beans with lentils?

Surprisingly tasty! Others have said comforting, yet light at the same time and this couldn't be more accurate. Like others, I made some adjustments. Used reduced-fat coconut milk, added cumin, garam masala, and cayenne. Totally missed the part about adding water, but added about two cups halfway through cooking. I kept the cauliflower pieces quite large and they didn't get mushy and the chickpeas were cooked through. In my opinion, both the lemon juice and rind bumped this out of ordinary.
Very good, had leftovers for breakfast with medium boiled eggs on top

I loved the lemon peels- so delicious to eat the finished peels. I would probably add some spinach right at the end next time to lighten it up a bit, but I also made the mistake of using a few too many chickpeas and not adjusting the liquid.....
substitute salt with fish sauce as a finishing touch instead to give a bit more depth.
This was surprisingly delicious! I was dubious about slow-cooking cauliflower, but decided to chance it since I had a head on its last legs. I’m glad I did.

I made this because I’m always curious about easy new recipes and so far it’s OK thin I’m thinking of maybe taking my immersion blender to it to get it a little bit more body and some bouillon as stated in some of the other comments.

I made this using the recipe in my InstantPot. After 4 hours on low (=185F)and the chickpeas seemed far from being cooked, I increased to medium (=194F). Now, after 4 more hours, the chickpeas are still not cooked (same texture as biting into an undercooked potato). I tried pressure cooking for 15 min. Since they're still not done, I'm pressure cooking going to pressure cook them in high for 30 minutes and hope that does it.

After slow cooking on low for 4 hours, medium for 4 hours, and pressure cooking for 40+ minutes, it turned out great. Don't skip the final step of lemon juice - it gives it just the right acidic balance.
Also, I used frozen cauliflower rice to make it more kid friendly. That worked well.
Added a raw boneless chicken breast at the beginning. Came out great. Next I’ll going to add carrots.

I was worried about blandness so I used broth made with better than bouillon instead of water, and I also added 1 Tbsp mustard power and 1 Tbsp cumin. It was delicious and still very easy/hands-off — ready in about 6 hours.
I'm not a cauliflower fan. Any suggestions for what to use instead?

the lemon is important to this
i think turmeric alone is sad so added cumin
i tried starting everything at the same time but with soaked chickpeas and the cauliflower still disintegrated by the time chickpeas were done (ok, but not what i was hoping for), so i cant imagine starting with dry.

It ended up being more of a soup than a stew. Fixed by adding a a tablespoon of corn starch towards the end which thickened it up nicely. The cumin colored the dish nicely but a small pinch of saffron adds a beautiful color profile to the dish.

I made it is but got worried as I  read comments after it was in the pot. It turned out perfectly. After slow cooking for a little over 5 hours I did 30 minute of pressure cooking and it went from a thinner soup to thicker. It’s delicious. Can I freeze it?
The coconut milk broke/curdled for me—was that the intended result? Looks like it’s a bit broken in  the recipe photo, but not as badly as mine

Made this last night as wanted a simple meal to host family prior to Thanksgiving. After reading the comments I decided to add 1/8 tsp baking soda to avoid curdling and it worked great. My other edits were: decreased the turmeric to 2 tsp., added 1 tsp. ground curry, 1 clove of minced garlic, added 3 tbs. of maple syrup (please don’t judge), measured the lemon juice and used 2 tbs., swapped out the full fat coconut milk for low fat. I made it on the stove-top in my Staub pot. I did a quick soak of the beans by bringing to a boil for 2 minutes and then covered off stove for 1 hour. I drained the beans, then proceeded with the recipe. I used a quart of water. I brought everything to a simmer, then let it cook covered for 2 hours. It was fabulous!

Really good. Responding to some folks’ concerns: The chickpeas were throughly cooked with a nice firmness. The cauliflower held up well — also had a nice bite to it. The dish is quite mild but tasty. I am adding salt and pepper now. I think I would add a couple of jalapeños or other green peppers next time for some nice heat.

I am making this right now, and following the recipe exactly as stated, including generous pinches of salt and freshly ground black pepper. After two hours on low I looked into the cooker and noticed that the cauliflower had risen to the top and was no longer submerged. I took a spoon and got a little taste (it was delicious!) and also one chickpea that was of course nowhere near done. Still, the flavor was lovely. Should I add more water?

Someone inquired about cauliflower over cooking and yes, the cauliflower was mush well before the chickpeas were fully cooked. That may have been purposeful-it helps thicken broth, but I think I would next time add cauliflower late in the game so you still get the nutty crunchiness of cauliflower.  The lemon flavor paired with coconut though is AMAZING   I  only had 1 can of coconut milk so did use flax milk to make up diff
Unbelievably tasty for the near zero effort you need to put into making this. I added an extra teaspoon of turmeric and quite a bit of black pepper at the end and it turned out amazing
Loved it! Simple and easy meal. I liked chopped scallions to finish and the cooked lemon peel tastes delicious. Don’t bother to discard it.
This is quite delicious but does need some time for the flavors to get acquainted. Did blend some for some texture and finished with steamed broccolini. Recipes are a guide!
It was okay, I found it a little bland overall. Adding MSG helped. Also it will make your entire house smell like cauliflower.
It tasted ok but not worth the smell of my house when I returned home after 6 hours of cooking cauliflower :(

I love so many NYT Cooking recipes - but not this one.  It's very (very) bland.  Tomorrow I'll do some significant doctoring to give it more flavor.  I followed this recipe to a "t" and the result was very disappointing. Agree with mc below, my house smelled horrid after about 4 hours in the slow cooker. Had to air it out!
Can you use canned chick peas?
i added two tablespoons of Thai Curry Powder and the recipe turned out quite well

Ok this recipe didn’t work for me as written, perhaps because I cut the florets too small and didn’t check on it until 6 hours had passed. The main problems were that the cauliflower was way too mushy and the flavor was too lemony-sweet, maybe because I used meyer lemons. 

To salvage this recipe as more of a minestrone, I:
1. Added mushy cauliflower to 7 c. garlic broth. Immersion blend until smooth.
2. Sautéed diced onions, carrots, celery, maybe 2 c. total. Add garam masala (1 tbsp.) roasted garlic from Penzey’s, (1 tbsp.) seasoned salt (1 tsp.) to taste. I did this to offset the lemony excess. 
3. Add 1/8 c water, 2 tbsp. tomato paste, 2 tbsp. minced ginger. Stir until incorporated. 
4. Add 2 c. diced red potatoes and sautee til tender. Adding this starch was another attempt to downplay the lemon. 
5 Add  and Sauté 1 c. Diced zucchini til tender. 
6. Add 2 c. Diced arugula from my garden as well as 2 tbsp. Chives. Sauté till greens have c
hanged color. Add sautee to soup.
7. Add 1 c. Frozen corn and 3/4 c. Frozen peas

Like several, I added fresh minced ginger and garlic to bump the flavors. I like a little heat so I topped with szechuan chile sauce and then some finishing salt. We had Rancho Gordo garbanzos, so they were the perfect bean. The cauliflower slow cooked is a wonderful earthy thickener in this stew. So nice.

This did not turn out well. there was not enough liquid so after 4 hours the chickpeas were still not done. I thought it seemed light on the liquid side, but I like to follow the recipe the first time. I added water and cooked several more hours until the chickpeas were an ok texture. ultimately though we didn't like the flavor or texture and we eat a lot of chickpeas.

I can’t believe I found a NYT recipe that takes only 30 minutes and tastes this good! I only made minor modifications:
-1 can coconut milk and 1 can veggie broth (instead of 2 cans coconut milk)
- Added 1 chopped yellow onion
- Added 2 dried chipotle peppers (discarded the skins after cooking)
- increased the turmeric to 1.5 Tablespoons 
- topped with chopped cilantro (good flavor and color)

Otherwise made as directed.
We both loved this. There is so much stew leftover that I plan to try different spice modifications each time I reheat it. Thanks, NYT.
I had some peas in the freezer, so I added about a cup of those at the end! I really just needed to use them but they went well with the stew.

Tasty base, but really bland - our family required a whole lot more spices .... beyond turmeric, we added curries and chilies .... Added kale and served over rice with roasted poblano, fresh mint, chili onion, cilantro, lime, lemon and a side of partha.
For me - not repeatable - but a decent one off.

I made this as directed and it turned out well. Chickpeas were tender without pre-soaking. It’s not a strong flavor but it’s nice. We added some chili crisp when serving to give a little kick. The downside is the smell in the house when cooking cauliflower and chickpeas and for that reason I’m not sure if we will make it again (kind of shame because it is simple and hearty)

This was very good and surprisingly easy! The cauliflower was very soft, but I didn't think it was a problem. I made one minor change -- I added chicken bone broth I had leftover from another recipe instead of some of the water.

I approached this recipe with some trepidation given the polarizing comments, and I halved the recipe in case I didn't like it. I think this recipe serves its purpose of being simple and quick to put together, but I don't think I would make it again except when in need. The chickpeas were soft enough but not creamy and the cauliflower almost completely disintegrated. (I used frozen cauliflower.) I'd maybe try again with cooked chickpeas in a pressure cooker and perhaps add curry powder.
An excellent slow cooker recipe! For the spices, I opted for 1 Tbsp red curry powder plus 1/2 tsp turmeric. It’s delicious served with cilantro, yogurt and brown rice.
This was super easy and delicious- I added some crushed garlic cloves and handfuls of spinach near the end which I’d recommend

This was good, and I probably would make it again, but I would probably try to change some things. I thought it would be creamier than it was, so I would probably add some yogurt of heavy cream next time I make it, and I might use a bit less of the lemon peel. I know the recipe said that it wouldn't be super lemony, but more flowery, but it didn't seem to be that way for me. Don't get me wrong, I LOVE lemon, but it kind of overpowered everything else in this recipe for me.
